approach: Lock down the anonymous credential-dump endpoint: add [Authorize] to the ChatList controller and stop returning the User entity — project to a Password-free DTO ({ Id, Name, Permission }, which is all the chat UI uses). Add [JsonIgnore] on User.Password as defense-in-depth so no other endpoint can ever serialize it, and drop the unused password field from the frontend type.
steps: In Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` (only the Authorization one is strictly needed here) and decorate the class `public class ChatList : ControllerBase` (line 15) with `[Authorize]` directly under the existing `[ApiController]`/`[Route]` attributes (lines 13-14). This matches the per-controller pattern already used in ReportLookupsController.cs and the Report controllers — there is no global FallbackPolicy in Program.cs, so the attribute is what actually enforces auth.

In ChatList.cs change the memory-cache value type and the query so Password is never materialized or serialized. Replace `List<User>? chatList` with a list of an anonymous/record type and replace `chatList = await _context.Users.ToListAsync();` with a projection `await _context.Users.Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission }).ToListAsync();`. Use a `var`-typed cache via the object overload (cache the projected list as `object`/`var`) since anonymous types can't be a generic type argument — see the patch for the concrete shape using a private record `ChatUserDto`.

Add a `ChatUserDto` record (Id, Name, Permission — no Password) inside the Backend.Controllers namespace (either a nested type in ChatList.cs or a small Dtos file) so the cache can be typed `List<ChatUserDto>?` and the projection is strongly typed. Note the cache key 'ChatListCache' previously stored `List<User>` with plaintext passwords in process memory — switching the projection also purges that sensitive data from the in-memory cache.

In Backend/Model/User.cs add `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` and annotate the Password property (line 21) with `[JsonIgnore]` as defense-in-depth, so even if any other controller returns a raw User entity the password is never emitted on the wire. Keep `[Required]` for EF.

In Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatListItem.ts remove the unused `password: 'string';` field from the `ChatListItem` interface (line 6) so the TS type matches the new DTO. The UI (ChatList.tsx / ChatListMobile.tsx) only reads `item.id`, `item.name`, `item.permission`, so no rendering changes are needed.

Follow-up (track separately, do not block this fix): passwords are stored and compared in plaintext — JWTManagerService.Authenticate (Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32) does `x.Password == users.Password`. Until passwords are hashed, ANY read of the Users table is a credential leak. File a hardening task to hash passwords (e.g. ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher / BCrypt) and update the login comparison accordingly.
verification: Anonymous request must now be rejected: `curl -i http://localhost:<port>/api/ChatList` (no Authorization header) returns 401 Unauthorized instead of 200 with a JSON body.

Authenticated request must not leak passwords: `curl -s http://localhost:<port>/api/ChatList -H "Authorization: Bearer <valid-jwt>" | grep -i password` returns nothing; the JSON contains only id, name, permission per item.
Grep the response/DTO: confirm `ChatList.cs` no longer references `_context.Users.ToListAsync()` and the cache type is `List<ChatUserDto>`.
Build/run backend (`dotnet build` in Backend) — confirm it compiles with the new record and the [JsonIgnore] import.
Smoke-test the chat UI: open the Chat page while logged in; the contact list still renders names and permission (titles/descriptions), confirming the DTO covers the UI needs.
Defense-in-depth check: temporarily hit any other endpoint that returns a User (or write a quick unit test serializing a User) and confirm Password is omitted due to [JsonIgnore].
pitfalls: Anonymous types cannot be used as the generic type argument to IMemoryCache.TryGetValue/Set, so you must introduce the named `ChatUserDto` record (or a class) — don't try `out List<anonymous>`.; Ordering: [Authorize] only works because the request pipeline calls app.UseAuthentication()/app.UseAuthorization() (Program.cs lines 166-167). Do not remove or reorder those; there is no global FallbackPolicy, so removing the attribute re-opens the hole.; Cache invalidation: the old 'ChatListCache' entry may already hold a List<User> with plaintext passwords in process memory for up to 1 hour. Changing the cached type means a fresh value is computed, but if you hot-reload without restarting, restart the API to flush the stale sensitive entry. A cold restart fully clears it.; [JsonIgnore] uses System.Text.Json (the ASP.NET Core default). If any code path serializes User with Newtonsoft.Json instead, add Newtonsoft's [JsonIgnore] too — but this project uses the default System.Text.Json.; This change stops the dump but does NOT fix plaintext password storage (JWTManagerService.cs:32). Don't close the underlying credential-storage risk based on this fix alone; the hashing follow-up is required for true remediation.

approach: Treat the leaked `sa` password (and the weak JWT key) as compromised: rotate at the DB and replace `sa` with a least-privilege login, externalize all secrets into environment variables / user-secrets (no code change needed — .NET's default config already overlays them over appsettings.json), untrack `Backend/appsettings.json` (it is gitignored but still committed), and purge the secret from git history.
steps: DB-side first (do before touching git, so the live secret is dead the moment it could leak further): connect to SQL Server at 203.81.66.111,14330 and run `ALTER LOGIN [sa] WITH PASSWORD = '<new-strong-random>';` to rotate. Then create a least-privilege application login instead of using sysadmin sa: `CREATE LOGIN [tradenet_app] WITH PASSWORD='<random>';` and in BOTH databases run `CREATE USER [tradenet_app] FOR LOGIN [tradenet_app];` plus `ALTER ROLE db_datareader ADD MEMBER [tradenet_app];`, `ALTER ROLE db_datawriter ADD MEMBER [tradenet_app];` (grant EXECUTE on the report stored procs the app calls). The app only needs read/write + EXEC on TemplateDB and TradeNetDB, never sysadmin.

Externalize the three connection strings and the JWT key. No code change is required: Program.cs:65-67 already call builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("TemplateDB")/("TradeNetDBTest") and Program.cs:74 + JWTManagerService.cs:41/71/111 read builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"], and CreateBuilder already layers environment variables and user-secrets ABOVE appsettings.json. In production set env vars (double-underscore = config nesting): `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest`, `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection`, and `JWT__Key` (a 32+ byte random value, NOT 'This is my supper secret key for jwt'). Set these in the systemd unit / Docker env / AWS secret store the API runs under.

For local dev, move secrets into the dotnet user-secrets store instead of the file: from Backend/ run `dotnet user-secrets init` then `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB" "<conn>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest" "<conn>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<random>"`. (Confirm/add a <UserSecretsId> to Backend/API.csproj — `dotnet user-secrets init` adds it.)

Scrub the working-tree file so no real secret remains. Edit Backend/appsettings.json: blank the JWT:Key value (line 10), and replace the TemplateDB (line 26) and TradeNetDBTest (line 27) connection-string values with placeholders that contain no host/password (or remove the production entries entirely and keep only the harmless localhost DefaultConnection). Commit this as a template/example.

Untrack the file from git (it is already in .gitignore at line 162 but remains committed, which is why it still leaks): `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` then commit. The .gitignore line 162 (`Backend/appsettings.json`) already prevents re-adding it. Optionally rename the scrubbed template to Backend/appsettings.json.example and keep IT tracked so new devs have a shape to copy.

Purge the secret from history. With the repo mirrored/backed up, run BFG or git-filter-repo to remove the password (and the old JWT key) from every commit, e.g. create replacements.txt with `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022==>REMOVED` and `This is my supper secret key for jwt==>REMOVED`, then `git filter-repo --replace-text replacements.txt` (or `bfg --replace-text replacements.txt`). Force-push all branches/tags (`git push --force --all && git push --force --tags`) and have every collaborator re-clone. Note this rewrites SHAs including 07d95d8 and 0453b61.

Restrict network exposure of 203.81.66.111,14330 — the DB port should not be reachable from the public internet; lock it to the API host(s) via firewall/security group so a leaked string is not directly usable from outside.
Treat any GitHub fork/PR/backup as still holding the old secret even after history rewrite; the rotation in step 1 is what actually neutralizes it.
verification: `git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (no longer tracked).
`git grep -I 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022' $(git rev-list --all)` returns no hits after the history rewrite (also check for the old JWT string).

Run the API with env vars set (ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB / __TradeNetDBTest / JWT__Key) and hit a report endpoint via Swagger — data loads, confirming GetConnectionString picks up the env-supplied strings (Program.cs:65-67).
Authenticate and call a [Authorize] endpoint to confirm token issue/validation still works with the new JWT__Key (JWTManagerService + Program.cs:74-89).

Attempt to log in to the DB with the old sa password and confirm it is rejected; confirm the app's new least-privilege login can read/write the report tables but cannot perform sysadmin ops (e.g. xp_cmdshell denied).
pitfalls: Ordering: rotate the sa password (step 1) BEFORE the git-history rewrite/force-push — the rewrite does not invalidate clones/forks/backups, only the live rotation does.; Any environment that depends on the TemplateDB/TradeNetDBTest strings will break until the env vars / user-secrets are configured (verifierNotes confirms DefaultConnection is only a fallback for local Windows-auth dev, so rotating sa won't break trusted-connection local dev).; git-filter-repo / BFG rewrites every commit SHA (including 07d95d8 and 0453b61) and detaches existing remotes — coordinate a force-push and have all collaborators re-clone; back up the repo (mirror clone) first.; If you keep a tracked Backend/appsettings.json.example, make sure it contains ONLY placeholders so you don't re-introduce a secret.; Granting the new app login: don't forget EXECUTE on the report stored procedures (the reports run procs, not just table reads) or report endpoints will 500.
estimatedTime: Half a day (DB rotation + least-priv login ~1-2h, env/user-secrets wiring ~1h, untrack + history purge + force-push coordination ~2-3h)

approach: Replace the committed low-entropy JWT signing key with a cryptographically random 256-bit secret loaded from environment/secret store (never source control), enforce a minimum key length at startup, then stop tracking appsettings.json and purge the leaked secrets (JWT key AND the sa DB password on lines 26-27) from git history and rotate them. Token rotation invalidates existing tokens, which is acceptable since all prior tokens must be assumed forgeable.
steps: Generate a new random 256-bit key: run `openssl rand -base64 48` (produces a 48-byte/384-bit base64 string). Keep the output for the deployment secret store; do NOT paste it into any tracked file.

In Backend/appsettings.json line 10, remove the real secret. Either delete the JWT:Key line entirely or set it to an empty placeholder "" so the existing guard in Program.cs:75-78 (`if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(jwtKey)) throw ...`) forces the value to come from config providers/env. The key MUST NOT be a real value in any committed file.

Provide the key at runtime via an environment variable. ASP.NET Core config binds `JWT__Key` (double underscore) to `JWT:Key` automatically — no code change needed for the lookup itself. For local dev, use the already-wired User Secrets (Backend csproj has UserSecretsId f930729b-3190-42a6-9201-49e396edac05): run `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<base64-from-step-1>"` from the Backend project dir. For UAT/prod set the `JWT__Key` env var in the host/container.

Harden the startup guard in Program.cs (currently lines 74-79). After reading `jwtKey` and before `Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes`, add a minimum-length check: HMAC-SHA256 needs >= 32 bytes (256 bits). This prevents re-introducing a weak key. Use IsNullOrWhiteSpace instead of IsNullOrEmpty so an all-whitespace placeholder is also rejected.

Apply the same defensive read in Service/JWTManagerService.cs. Lines 41, 71, and 111 each do `iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? ""` — the `?? ""` silently falls back to an empty key and would sign tokens with a zero-length secret if config is missing. Extract a single private helper `GetSigningKey()` that validates non-empty/length and reuse it in Authenticate (line 41), AuthenticateTradenet2 (line 71), and RefreshToken (line 111).

Stop tracking the secret file. NOTE: `.gitignore` line 162 already lists `Backend/appsettings.json`, but the file is still tracked (committed in dadadc6 before the rule was added), so `git check-ignore Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing and the rule is currently inert. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` so the ignore rule takes effect, and commit a sanitized template `Backend/appsettings.example.json` (placeholders only) for onboarding.

Same-effort secret purge (per verifier note): the DB sa credentials on appsettings.json lines 26-27 (`User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` for both TemplateDB and TradeNetDBTest on 203.81.66.111,14330) are also leaked. Move those connection strings to env vars / secret store the same way (ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB, ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest) and rotate the sa password.

Rewrite git history to purge the file from every commit (secrets appear from Initial commit dadadc6). Use git-filter-repo: `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths`, then add a sanitized version back. Coordinate a force-push and have all collaborators re-clone, since this rewrites commit SHAs.

Rotate the exposed secrets: the new JWT key from step 1 is the rotation (the old key is dead the moment appsettings is changed and the env value is used), and rotate the sa DB password. After deploy, all previously issued tokens become invalid — communicate that users must re-authenticate.
verification: Build: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds with the new guards.

Negative startup: run with no JWT key configured (unset JWT__Key, empty appsettings value, no user-secret) and confirm the app throws the InvalidOperationException at startup instead of booting with a weak/empty key.
Weak-key startup: set `JWT__Key` to a short string (e.g. "short") and confirm startup throws the 'at least 32 bytes' error.

Happy path: set `JWT__Key` to the step-1 base64 value (env var or `dotnet user-secrets set`), start the app, POST to the login endpoint, confirm a token is issued and an [Authorize] report endpoint accepts it.
Forgery check: take any token signed with the OLD key 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' and confirm it now returns 401 against an [Authorize] endpoint.

Git hygiene: `git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (untracked); after the history rewrite `git log -S"supper secret" --all` and `git log -S"Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022" --all` return no commits.
pitfalls: Rotating the key invalidates every currently issued token (Authenticate 1-day, AuthenticateTradenet2 and RefreshToken 31-day tokens) — all users must re-authenticate; schedule a maintenance window. The history rewrite (git filter-repo) changes commit SHAs and requires a coordinated force-push plus re-clone by all collaborators; do it after the deployment is staged. appsettings.json is ALREADY in .gitignore (line 162) yet still tracked because it was committed first — you must `git rm --cached` it or the rule stays inert and re-edits keep getting committed. JWTManagerService signs with HmacSha256Signature (lines 50/80/119) while Program.cs validates HmacSha256 — leave the algorithm as-is; only the key changes. Editing appsettings.json alone is insufficient: the old secret stays recoverable from git, so rotation (a brand-new value) is mandatory, not optional.
estimatedTime: 2-3 hours (code change ~30 min; secret provisioning, git history rewrite/force-push coordination, and DB password rotation make up the rest)

approach: Stop storing/comparing passwords in plaintext: hash with ASP.NET Core's PasswordHasher<User> on write, and on login look up the user by Name then verify the hash (never query by password). Separately, lock down / DTO-project the anonymous ChatList endpoint so it can never serialize the password column.
steps: Add a hashed-password column to the model. In Backend/Model/User.cs add `public string PasswordHash { get; set; } = "";` and keep `Password` only as a transient input field. Decorate the plaintext `Password` property with `[NotMapped]` (add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.Schema;`) so EF no longer persists raw passwords, OR add `PasswordHash` as the real column and drop `Password` from the table via migration. Recommended: keep `Password` as `[NotMapped]` (it is the login form input bound in AuthController.Login) and add a mapped `PasswordHash`.

Register the hasher in Backend/Program.cs DI, next to the existing JWT registration around line 92: `builder.Services.AddScoped<Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.IPasswordHasher<API.Model.User>, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher<API.Model.User>>();`. PasswordHasher<T> ships in the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App shared framework, so no new NuGet package is required for this .NET 8 web project.

Inject the hasher into Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;`, add a `private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _hasher;` field, and add the parameter `IPasswordHasher<User> hasher` to the constructor (line 21-29), assigning `_hasher = hasher;`.

Rewrite the login comparison in JWTManagerService.Authenticate (line 32-39). Replace the `Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password)` query: first load the user by Name only (`var tempUser = await _userService.Retrieve.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);`), return null if it is null, then `var verify = _hasher.VerifyHashedPassword(tempUser, tempUser.PasswordHash, users.Password); if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.Failed) return null;`. Remove the old `AsParallel().Any()` block. Use the already-loaded `tempUser` for the token claims below (it is non-null at this point).

Optionally handle the SuccessRehashNeeded result: if `verify == PasswordVerificationResult.SuccessRehashNeeded`, re-hash and persist (`tempUser.PasswordHash = _hasher.HashPassword(tempUser, users.Password); await _userService.Update(tempUser.Id, tempUser);`) before issuing the token.

Create a hashing path for any user creation/seeding so PasswordHash is populated with `_hasher.HashPassword(user, plaintext)` and the plaintext is never written. There is currently no registration controller in this repo (only Service/JWTManagerService.Authenticate consumes credentials), so seed/admin-provisioning code that inserts into ApplicationDbContext.Users must call the hasher; never set User.PasswordHash from a raw value.

Lock down the ChatList endpoint in Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs. It currently has no [Authorize] and returns the full `List<User>` (including Password) from `_context.Users.ToListAsync()` (line 31, served via _memoryCache). Either delete this controller (it appears to be leftover chat scaffolding unrelated to reports) or: add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `[Authorize]` on the class, and project to a DTO that omits the password, e.g. `await _context.Users.Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission }).ToListAsync();` and cache/return that anonymous-typed/DTO list instead of `List<User>`.

Add an EF Core migration for the schema change: from the Backend project dir run `dotnet ef migrations add AddUserPasswordHash` then `dotnet ef database update` against the TemplateDB connection (ApplicationDbContext owns DbSet<User>). Note Backend/DBContext/TradeNetDbContext.cs line 447 also has a DbSet<User> for the external TradeNet DB; do NOT migrate or alter that one — only ApplicationDbContext owns the admin login Users table.

Migrate existing plaintext rows: write a one-off backfill that reads each existing Users row's plaintext value and sets `PasswordHash = hasher.HashPassword(user, plaintext)`, then clears/drops the plaintext column. If plaintext is unrecoverable or you prefer zero plaintext-at-rest risk, force a password reset for all admin accounts instead of backfilling.
verification: Run the API and POST to /api/Auth with a valid Name/Password JSON body; confirm a 200 with a TokenModel (token issued). POST with a wrong password and confirm 404/null (login fails).

Query the Users table after a successful login or seed and confirm PasswordHash contains a non-reversible hash (PasswordHasher v3 output starts with base64 beginning 'AQAAAA...') and that no plaintext Password column value remains.
GET /api/ChatList without a bearer token and confirm 401 (after [Authorize]); with a valid token confirm the JSON payload contains only Id/Name/Permission and NO Password or PasswordHash field.
Confirm the project still builds: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds (PasswordHasher<T> resolves from the shared framework, no missing-package error).
Confirm `dotnet ef migrations list` shows the new AddUserPasswordHash migration and `dotnet ef database update` applied the PasswordHash column to the ApplicationDbContext Users table.
pitfalls: Ordering: add and apply the PasswordHash column (migration) BEFORE deploying the new Authenticate code, otherwise login throws on a missing column. After deploy, existing rows have empty PasswordHash and every login fails until you backfill (hash existing plaintext) or force resets — do the backfill in the same release. AuthController.Login binds the incoming JSON straight to the User model, so the request still sends `Password`; keep that property (as [NotMapped]) — do not rename the inbound field or the frontend login breaks. Do NOT touch the second DbSet<User> in TradeNetDbContext.cs (external TradeNet DB) — only ApplicationDbContext owns the admin login table. ChatList is cached in IMemoryCache for 1 hour; if you only add [Authorize] without changing the projection, the cached List<User> still holds passwords in memory and the DTO change is what actually removes them — apply both. Rollback: the schema change is additive (new column) so reverting code is safe, but once plaintext is dropped it cannot be recovered, so keep a backup of the Users table before dropping the plaintext column.

approach: Stop comparing plaintext passwords and stop misusing PLINQ on an EF query: inject ASP.NET Core's built-in PasswordHasher<User>, fetch the single user server-side with FirstOrDefaultAsync, and verify the supplied password against the stored hash. Because there is no in-app user-creation path (users are seeded directly into SQL), pair the code change with a one-time migration that rehashes existing plaintext rows, plus on-the-fly upgrade of legacy plaintext logins.
steps: In Backend/Model/User.cs, the Password column currently holds plaintext (nvarchar(max), Migrations/20250622052201_userAndToken.cs). Keep the property named Password to avoid a schema migration, but treat its contents as a hash going forward. Update the XML/intent comment so future devs know it now stores a PasswordHasher hash, e.g. add `// stores Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher hash, never plaintext` above the Password property at line 21.

Register the hasher in Backend/Program.cs next to the existing auth registrations (lines 92-93). Add `builder.Services.AddSingleton<Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.IPasswordHasher<API.Model.User>, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher<API.Model.User>>();`. No NuGet package is needed — PasswordHasher<T> ships in the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App shared framework already referenced by this Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web / net8.0 project.

In Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;` (EntityFrameworkCore is already imported on line 3), add a private readonly field `private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _passwordHasher;`, add the constructor parameter `IPasswordHasher<User> passwordHasher` (lines 21-24) and assign `_passwordHasher = passwordHasher;` in the body (lines 25-29).

Rewrite the credential check in Authenticate (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32-39). Remove the plaintext predicate `&& x.Password == users.Password` and the `.AsParallel().Any()` call. Fetch the candidate by Name server-side with `FirstOrDefaultAsync`, then verify the supplied password against the stored hash with `_passwordHasher.VerifyHashedPassword`. Return null if the user is missing or verification fails. Reuse the fetched entity as `tempUser` so the existing token-building block below (lines 40-64) is unchanged.

Handle the existing plaintext rows already in the DB. Option A (preferred, no downtime): write a one-time idempotent script/console step or an EF migration `Up()` that loads each User and replaces Password with `new PasswordHasher<User>().HashPassword(user, user.Password)` for any row whose value is not already a hash (PasswordHasher v3 hashes are Base64 and start with the 0x01 format marker; cheaply detect legacy rows by length/`!value.StartsWith("AQAAAA")`). Run it once before deploying the new Authenticate. Option B (transparent upgrade in code): in Authenticate, when `VerifyHashedPassword` returns `PasswordVerificationResult.Failed`, also compare `user.Password == users.Password` as a legacy fallback; on a legacy match, rehash and persist via `await _userService.Update(user.Id, user)` then continue. Ship Option B only as a short-lived bridge and remove it after all rows are migrated.

Confirm AuthController.Login (Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:25-43) needs no change — it already awaits `_jWTManager.Authenticate(data)` and maps null to 404. Note that the catch on line 41 returns `ex.Message` to the client; that is a separate finding and out of scope here.

Separately remediate the hardcoded 'sa' connection string called out in the impact (move it out of appsettings into a secret/managed identity) — tracked as its own finding; the hashing change above limits blast radius but does not by itself fix the credential exposure.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` in Backend/ — confirm IPasswordHasher resolves with no new package and the AsParallel/plaintext lines are gone (`grep -n AsParallel Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs` returns nothing; `grep -n 'x.Password == users.Password' Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs` returns nothing).
Run the migration step, then inspect the Users table: every Password value should be a Base64 PasswordHasher string (starts with 'AQAAAA'), with no readable plaintext.
POST /api/Auth with a valid {Name, Password} returns 200 with a token; POST with a wrong password returns 404 (NotFound); POST with an unknown Name returns 404.

Enable EF SQL logging (or SQL Profiler) for one login and confirm the emitted query filters by Name on the server (SELECT TOP(1) ... WHERE [Name] = @__) and that the password is NOT part of the WHERE clause and no full-table client enumeration occurs.

If the legacy bridge (Option B) is enabled, log in once with an old plaintext credential, confirm success, then re-query that row and confirm Password is now a hash; a second login still succeeds via the hash path.
pitfalls: Ordering: deploy/run the rehash migration BEFORE the new Authenticate goes live, OR ship the Option B legacy bridge in the same release — otherwise every existing user is instantly locked out because VerifyHashedPassword will reject their stored plaintext.; Idempotency: guard the migration so re-running it does not hash an already-hashed value (which would double-hash and lock users out). Detect already-migrated rows by the 'AQAAAA' prefix / length before hashing.; There is no in-app user-creation or password-reset endpoint, so any future tooling that inserts users must call PasswordHasher.HashPassword — do not let new plaintext rows reappear. Consider adding a guarded admin endpoint or a documented seeding helper.; Keep the Password property name to avoid a schema/migration churn; renaming to PasswordHash would require an EF migration and updating the User() constructor default at Model/User.cs:12.; Rollback: the code change is safe to revert, but once rows are rehashed you cannot recover the original plaintext — back up the Users table before running the migration.; Out of scope but related: the hardcoded 'sa' connection string and AuthController returning ex.Message are separate findings; do not conflate them with this fix.

approach: Rotate the leaked SQL `sa` password and JWT signing key out-of-band first, then move all three secrets (JWT:Key, TemplateDB, TradeNetDBTest) out of the committed Backend/appsettings.json into environment variables / .NET user-secrets / deploy-time injection, untrack the file from git, and purge it from history. The code already reads via builder.Configuration so no consumer code changes are required once the config providers supply the values.
steps: ROTATE FIRST (out-of-band, before any commit): on SQL Server 203.81.66.111,14330 change the `sa` password (ideally disable `sa` entirely and create a least-privilege login per database, e.g. `tradenet_app` with only the rights the report API needs on TemplateDB and TradeNetDB). Generate a fresh >=256-bit JWT key, e.g. `openssl rand -base64 64`. Treat the old `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` password and the literal 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' as permanently compromised.

Strip the secrets out of the committed file. Edit Backend/appsettings.json so JWT:Key, ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB and ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest are empty placeholders (keep the keys present so structure is documented). Program.cs:75-78 already throws InvalidOperationException when JWT:Key is empty, so a misconfigured deploy fails fast rather than running with a blank key.

Provide the real values locally via .NET user-secrets (the project already has <UserSecretsId>f930729b-3190-42a6-9201-49e396edac05</UserSecretsId> in Backend/API.csproj, so no extra setup). From the Backend folder run: `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<new-random-key>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB" "Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<new>;..."`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest" "Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<new>;..."`.

Provide the real values in production via environment variables injected at deploy time (the default ASP.NET host already loads env vars and user-secrets, so no Program.cs change is needed). Use the double-underscore section delimiter: `JWT__Key`, `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest`. Set these in the container/orchestrator (Docker env, compose, or the host's secret store) — the csproj already targets Docker (DockerDefaultTargetOS/DockerfileContext).

Untrack the file from git so future edits to local secrets are not committed. The root .gitignore already lists `Backend/appsettings.json`, but `git check-ignore -v Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing and `git ls-files --error-unmatch Backend/appsettings.json` succeeds — confirming it is STILL TRACKED and the ignore rule is inert. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` then commit; the gitignore entry then takes effect for the working copy. Add a committed `Backend/appsettings.example.json` (placeholder values only) so other developers know the required keys.

Purge the secret from history. `git log --all -- Backend/appsettings.json` shows it lives in 5 commits (dadadc6 initial, 0453b61, 73edb0d, 07d95d8, f97eee7). Removing it from HEAD is not enough. Use git-filter-repo (preferred) or BFG to scrub it: `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths` (or `bfg --delete-files appsettings.json` / `bfg --replace-text` with the leaked strings), then force-push all branches and tags and have every collaborator re-clone. Coordinate this rewrite with the team.

After rotation, also close the supporting auth gaps the verifier flagged: in Backend/Program.cs set ValidateIssuer=true and ValidateAudience=true (lines 83-84) now that JWT:Issuer/JWT:Audience are configured, so a leaked key alone cannot mint a universally-accepted token; and stop storing plaintext passwords — JWTManagerService.Authenticate (line 32) compares `x.Password == users.Password`, so introduce password hashing (e.g. ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher / BCrypt) and verify the hash instead of equality. Track these as follow-ups if out of scope for this finding.
verification: `git ls-files --error-unmatch Backend/appsettings.json` should FAIL (file no longer tracked) and `git check-ignore -v Backend/appsettings.json` should now print the matching .gitignore rule.

`grep -R "Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022\|supper secret key" .` returns nothing in the working tree; after history rewrite, `git log --all -S 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022' --oneline` and `git log --all -- Backend/appsettings.json` return no commits.

With user-secrets set, run `dotnet run` from Backend/ and confirm the app starts (no InvalidOperationException 'JWT:Key configuration value is missing.' from Program.cs:77) and that report endpoints querying TemplateDB/TradeNetDB succeed — proving the connection strings resolve from config providers.

Confirm rotation took effect: the OLD sa password no longer authenticates against 203.81.66.111,14330 (e.g. `sqlcmd -S 203.81.66.111,14330 -U sa -P 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022'` fails), and a JWT signed with the OLD literal key is rejected (401) by an authorized endpoint.
If the auth-hardening follow-ups are done: a token with a wrong/absent issuer/audience is rejected once ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience are true, and stored user passwords are non-plaintext hashes.
pitfalls: Rotate credentials BEFORE the history rewrite/force-push, not after — the values are already public to anyone who cloned, so removal alone does not protect them. The root .gitignore listing is misleading: it does NOT ignore the file today because git was already tracking it; you must `git rm --cached` for the ignore to apply. History rewrite (filter-repo/BFG) changes every commit SHA — coordinate with the team, force-push all branches+tags, and have everyone re-clone or hard-reset; open PRs/forks must be rebased. Do not delete the JWT/ConnectionStrings keys outright from appsettings.json — keep them as empty placeholders so Program.cs:65/67/74 still resolve the config sections (env-var/user-secret values override empties). If you switch off `sa` to a least-privilege login, verify the new login has every permission the report stored procs and Excel-export queue use against both databases before cutover, or report endpoints will 500. Rollback: revert the appsettings.json edit and re-set the (rotated) values in config providers; never revert the credential rotation.
estimatedTime: 3-4 hours (incl. credential rotation, least-privilege login setup, config wiring, and coordinated history rewrite)

approach: Introduce a validated JwtOptions class (mirroring the existing ExcelExportOptions pattern), bind it with AddOptions().Bind().ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart(), and have both the JWT validation (Program.cs) and signing (JWTManagerService) read from it instead of raw IConfiguration; move all secrets (sa connection strings, JWT key/issuer/audience) out of the tracked appsettings.json into environment variables / user-secrets, untrack the file from git, rotate the leaked sa password and JWT key, and purge git history.
steps: Create Backend/Service/Auth/JwtOptions.cs (new folder) mirroring ExcelExportOptions: `public sealed class JwtOptions { public const string SectionName = "JWT"; [Required, MinLength(32)] public string Key {get;set;}=""; [Required] public string Issuer {get;set;}=""; [Required] public string Audience {get;set;}=""; }` Add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;`. The MinLength(32) enforces a >=256-bit HMAC key.

In Backend/Program.cs, before the AddAuthentication block (line 68), register and validate the options: `builder.Services.AddOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>().Bind(builder.Configuration.GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName)).ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart();`

In Program.cs AddJwtBearer (lines 72-91), replace the raw `builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"]` read and the null-check (lines 74-79) with the bound options. Resolve via `var jwt = builder.Configuration.GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName).Get<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>() ?? throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT options missing");` then `var Key = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwt.Key);`.

In Program.cs TokenValidationParameters (lines 81-90), flip `ValidateIssuer = true`, `ValidateAudience = true`, keep `ValidateLifetime = true` and `ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true`, and set `ValidIssuer = jwt.Issuer`, `ValidAudience = jwt.Audience`, `IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Key)`.

CRITICAL signing-side fix in Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs: the three token descriptors (lines 42, 72, 112) currently emit NO Issuer/Audience claims. If you enable ValidateIssuer/Audience without this, every login token fails validation. Inject options: change the field/ctor from `IConfiguration iconfiguration` to `private readonly API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions _jwt;` taking `Microsoft.Extensions.Options.IOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions> jwtOptions` and assigning `_jwt = jwtOptions.Value;` (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Options;`).

In each of the three SecurityTokenDescriptor blocks in JWTManagerService.cs, replace `var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? "");` with `var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(_jwt.Key);` and add `Issuer = _jwt.Issuer, Audience = _jwt.Audience,` to the descriptor so emitted tokens match the now-enabled ValidIssuer/ValidAudience.

Strip secrets from Backend/appsettings.json: set `JWT.Key` to "", and for ConnectionStrings keep only non-secret shape or empty placeholders (do NOT leave the sa credentials or literal key). Real values come from env vars / user-secrets at runtime via the options binder and GetConnectionString (which still reads ConnectionStrings__* env vars).

Provide the real secrets out-of-band. Dev (user-secrets, UserSecretsId f930729b-... already in API.csproj): `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<new 32+ char random>" --project Backend/API.csproj`, same for `ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB` and `ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest` with a NEW least-privilege login (not sa) and ROTATED password. Prod: set env vars `JWT__Key`, `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest` (double-underscore) in the host/container/secret store.

Stop tracking the file that .gitignore (line 162) already lists: `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` and commit. (appsettings.Development.json is also tracked but holds no secrets after this change; consider untracking it too.)

Rotate and purge: change the SQL 'sa' password (and create the new least-privilege login) on 203.81.66.111,14330, generate a brand-new JWT signing key, then scrub git history of the leaked values with `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths` (or BFG) and force-push, coordinating with all clones. Treat the old key as compromised — any tokens signed with it are forgeable.
verification: Build: `dotnet build Backend/API.csproj` succeeds with the new JwtOptions and updated JWTManagerService ctor.

Fail-fast check: run the app with an empty/short JWT:Key (no user-secret set) and confirm ValidateOnStart throws an OptionsValidationException at startup (proves validation + >=32-char rule fire) instead of silently running with a weak key.

Set a valid 32+ char key + connection strings via user-secrets, start the app, POST to the login/authenticate endpoint, decode the returned JWT (jwt.io) and confirm `iss` = configured Issuer and `aud` = configured Audience are present.

Call an [Authorize] report endpoint with that fresh token -> 200. Then forge/replay a token signed with the OLD literal key 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' -> expect 401 (proves the key was rotated and the old key no longer validates).
`git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (file untracked); `git grep -n 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022\|supper secret key' $(git rev-list --all | head)` returns nothing after history purge.
`grep -rn 'JWT:Key' Backend --include=*.cs` returns no matches outside this change (raw IConfiguration reads eliminated).
pitfalls: Ordering trap: do NOT enable ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience (Program.cs) without also adding Issuer/Audience to the three SecurityTokenDescriptors in JWTManagerService.cs in the same change — otherwise all newly issued tokens lack iss/aud claims and every authorized request 401s. Rotating the JWT key invalidates ALL currently issued tokens (login tokens last 1 day, the Tradenet2/refresh tokens up to 31 days) — every user must re-authenticate; schedule accordingly. The new sa-replacement SQL login must have the same schema/exec rights the reports need (the app currently runs as sysadmin); test report + Excel-export queries before cutover. GetConnectionString still works with env vars only if you use the ConnectionStrings__<Name> double-underscore convention. History purge (git filter-repo/BFG) rewrites SHAs and requires a coordinated force-push; treat the leaked sa password and JWT key as compromised regardless of purge — rotation is mandatory, not optional. appsettings.Development.json is also git-tracked; leaving it tracked is fine since it now holds no secrets.
estimatedTime: 3-4 hours (code change ~1h; secret rotation + least-privilege SQL login + env/user-secrets wiring ~1h; git history purge + coordinated force-push ~1-2h)

approach: Close the exposure at the source by adding [JsonIgnore] to the User.Password entity property so it is never serialized into any API response, and remove 'password' from the UserList table's displayData. The login path keeps working because it reads Password server-side from the DB, not from the JSON response.
steps: Backend root-cause fix: open Backend/Model/User.cs. Add a using for System.Text.Json.Serialization at the top, then annotate the Password property (line 20-21) with [JsonIgnore]. This stops Password from being serialized by the default System.Text.Json serializer used by AddControllers() in Program.cs. Because the UserController inherits BaseAPIController<User> and returns ApiResult<User> (whose Data is the raw List<User>), this single attribute removes the password from the list endpoint, the Get(id), and the Put/Post echo responses all at once.

Confirm the login flow is unaffected: JWTManagerService.Authenticate (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32) queries x.Password == users.Password against the database via _userService.Retrieve. It reads Password from the EF entity column, not from a JSON response, so [JsonIgnore] on the property does NOT break authentication (JsonIgnore only affects (de)serialization, not EF column mapping). Note: the inbound login still deserializes Password from the request body via the auth/login endpoint's bound User model — verify that endpoint binds to the same User type; if it does, the request-side Password binding will also be ignored and login will break (see pitfalls).

Frontend fix: open Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx line 29 and remove 'password' from the displayData array, changing it to ['name', 'permission', 'isActive', 'id']. This removes the visible column and stops the value from being placed in the DOM even if a stale/cached response still contains it.

Rebuild and redeploy the backend (the deployed binary, not just the .cs file). The Password column was previously flowing through the generic ApiResult<User>.CreateAsync projection in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs, so a source-only change with no rebuild leaves the live API still leaking.

Forward-looking (recommended, separate ticket): the verifier confirmed passwords are stored and compared in cleartext (JWTManagerService.cs:32). Plan migration to a salted strong hash (bcrypt/Argon2) and rename the column to PasswordHash. [JsonIgnore] stops the over-the-wire leak now; hashing removes the at-rest cleartext risk. Do this AFTER the JsonIgnore + frontend fix is shipped, as it requires a data migration.
verification: Run the backend, authenticate as an operator, then call GET /api/User (e.g. curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" "https://<host>/api/User?pageIndex=0&pageSize=10") and confirm each object in .data no longer contains a password field. Also hit GET /api/User/{id} and confirm no password in the body.
Verify login still works after the change: POST credentials to the auth/login endpoint and confirm a token is returned (proves Password is still read from the DB for comparison).
Open the User list page in the browser; confirm the Password column is gone from the table and that the password value is absent from the Network tab response and from the rendered DOM.
grep the User model to confirm the attribute is present: grep -n "JsonIgnore" "Backend/Model/User.cs".
pitfalls: "Critical ordering/binding check: [JsonIgnore] from System.Text.Json applies to BOTH serialization (responses) and deserialization (request binding). If the login endpoint model-binds the incoming credentials to this same API.Model.User type, the inbound Password will be ignored and login will silently fail (Password arrives empty, the x.Password == users.Password query never matches). Before shipping, confirm how the login endpoint receives credentials. If it binds to User, either (a) use a dedicated LoginRequest DTO for login input, or (b) replace [JsonIgnore] with [JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)] only if that still blocks all reads — safest is a separate login DTO so the entity can stay write-protected. Also: this is a source change to a compiled entity — you MUST rebuild/redeploy; the .sql/source drift trap noted in project memory applies (a stale deployed binary keeps leaking). No DB migration is needed for the JsonIgnore fix itself."

steps: ROTATE FIRST (do this before touching git, because the old values are already public in history). On the DB host 203.81.66.111,14330: change the 'sa' password, and create a least-privilege login the app will use instead of 'sa' (e.g. CREATE LOGIN tradenet_app WITH PASSWORD='<new strong>'; map a user in TemplateDB and TradeNetDB with only the db_datareader/db_datawriter + EXECUTE on the report stored procs the app calls — NOT sysadmin).

Generate a new JWT signing key with at least 256 bits of entropy (e.g. `openssl rand -base64 48`). This replaces 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' (Backend/appsettings.json:10, also read in Backend/Program.cs:74 and Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:41/71/111). Note: rotating this key invalidates all currently-issued tokens — coordinate so users re-authenticate.

Stop tracking the file: from the repo root run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json`. The existing .gitignore:162 entry `Backend/appsettings.json` then keeps it out of future commits. Keep the physical file on disk locally.

Blank the secret values in the working-tree Backend/appsettings.json so the template that remains carries no live secrets: set JWT:Key (line 10) to "", and ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB (line 26) and ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest (line 27) to "". Leave the non-secret keys (Issuer, Audience, Logging, ExcelExport, DefaultConnection which is Trusted_Connection local-only) intact.

Create a committed, secret-free template Backend/appsettings.Example.json mirroring the structure with empty/placeholder secret values, and add it to git (`git add Backend/appsettings.Example.json`). This documents the required keys without exposing secrets. Add a README note that real values come from user-secrets/env.

Inject dev secrets via the already-configured user-secrets store (Backend/API.csproj already has <UserSecretsId>f930729b-3190-42a6-9201-49e396edac05</UserSecretsId>). From the Backend folder run: `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB" "Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<new>;..."`, the same for `ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest`, and `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<new base64 key>"`. No code change needed — Program.cs already calls builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString(...) and builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"].

Inject prod secrets via environment variables on the deploy host (double-underscore maps to the colon-nested keys): `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest`, and `JWT__Key`. Set these in the systemd unit / container env / orchestrator secret store, not in any committed file.

Purge the file from git history. With BFG: `bfg --delete-files appsettings.json` then `git reflog expire --expire=now --all && git gc --prune=now --aggressive`. Or with git-filter-repo: `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths`. Then force-push all branches/tags (`git push --force --all` and `git push --force --tags`) and have every collaborator re-clone.
Restrict the DB firewall on 203.81.66.111 so port 14330 is reachable only from the app server's IP, not the public internet.
Verify no other tracked file still carries the secret string before declaring done: `git grep -n "Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022\|supper secret key"` must return nothing across HEAD.
verification: `git ls-files --error-unmatch Backend/appsettings.json` returns a non-zero exit (file no longer tracked).
`git grep -n "Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022" $(git rev-list --all)` (or after history purge, `git grep -n "Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022" -- $(git rev-list --all)`) returns no matches — confirms history is scrubbed.

With JWT:Key/ConnectionStrings supplied via user-secrets, `dotnet run` from Backend starts cleanly (Program.cs:75-78 throws 'JWT:Key configuration value is missing.' if the key is unresolved — so a clean start proves the override is being read).
Old SQL 'sa' login with password Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022 no longer authenticates against 203.81.66.111,14330; the new least-privilege login does and can run the report procs.
A JWT minted with the OLD key is now rejected (401) by any [Authorize] endpoint, and a token minted via JWTManagerService with the new key is accepted.
From an IP other than the app server, `nc -vz 203.81.66.111 14330` fails (firewall restricted).
pitfalls: Ordering matters: rotate the 'sa' password and JWT key BEFORE the history purge/force-push — the old values are already exposed, so removing them from git does nothing for credentials that are still live. Rotating JWT:Key invalidates every issued token, forcing all users to re-login; schedule it. The history rewrite (BFG/filter-repo + force-push) changes commit hashes for everyone — every collaborator and any CI/mirror must re-clone or hard-reset, and any open PR branches will need rebasing. Do not delete the local Backend/appsettings.json file (git rm --cached keeps it on disk); the app still loads it as the base layer that env/user-secrets override. If you switch from 'sa' to the new least-privilege login, confirm it has EXECUTE on every stored proc the report controllers call, or reports will fail at runtime with permission errors even though the app starts. The Issuer/Audience are not validated (Program.cs:83-84 ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience = false) so they are not secrets, but the JWT:Key is the only thing protecting token signing — treat it as a secret.
estimatedTime: Half a day (about 4 hours), most of it coordinating the credential rotation, the history rewrite/force-push, and collaborator re-clones

approach: Give OFFSET/FETCH a stable, fully-unique sort everywhere it pages: add a deterministic tiebreaker key to the generic ApplySort fallback so the generic fast-page path can no longer page an unordered query, and carry the per-item composite PK into the _Fast Import Licence projection so its CreatedDate ordering breaks ties deterministically.
steps: In Backend/Model/APIResult.cs, fix ApplySort (lines 251-273) so it never returns an unordered source. When sortColumn is null/invalid, instead of `return source;` return `source.OrderBy(DeterministicSortKey())` so OFFSET/FETCH (the Skip/Take in CreateAsync line 74-76 and CreateFastPageAsync line 119-120) always has a stable order. ApplySort already uses System.Linq.Dynamic.Core (imported at line 5), so OrderBy("PropA, PropB") is valid.

Add a private helper DeterministicSortKey() to APIResult.cs next to ApplySort. It reflects typeof(T).GetProperties(BindingFlags.Public|BindingFlags.Instance), prefers a natural key if present ("Id"/"UniqueId"/"LicenceNo"/"CreatedDate", emitting "Id, UniqueId" together when both exist for composite-PK shapes), and otherwise falls back to every simple/comparable public property name (value types + string) joined by comma so ties cannot occur. Cache the computed string per typeof(T) in a static ConcurrentDictionary to avoid reflecting on every request. Call IsValidProperty with throwExceptionIfNotFound:false inside ApplySort's guard so an invalid client sortColumn falls through to the deterministic key instead of throwing.

In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs, carry the item composite PK into the projection. Item entities ImportLicenceItem and BorderImportLicenceItem both declare [PrimaryKey("Id", "UniqueId")] (Id is string, UniqueId is int) and neither is currently projected. Add two properties to the private ImportLicenceDetailFastRow class (around lines 802-848): `public string ItemId { get; init; } = null!;` and `public int ItemUniqueId { get; init; }`.

Populate the two new fields in all three projections building ImportLicenceDetailFastRow: OverseaRows (`select new` at line 575), BorderPaThaKaRows (line 651), BorderIndividualTradingRows (line 730) — add `ItemId = item.Id, ItemUniqueId = item.UniqueId,`. The `item` range variable is db.ImportLicenceItems in OverseaRows and db.BorderImportLicenceItems in the two Border methods; both expose Id and UniqueId.

Change Rows() at line 441 from `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);` to `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate).ThenBy(row => row.ItemId).ThenBy(row => row.ItemUniqueId);` so the per-item fan-out (many rows sharing one CreatedDate) is fully ordered. This source feeds the Skip/Take at lines 48-51 plus the Excel (line 94) and stream (line 122) paths.
No change needed for SectionGroups (line 272): its `.OrderBy(SectionName).ThenBy(Currency)` is unique per group, and AggregateInSqlAsync materializes full groups with ToListAsync (no OFFSET).

Audit the ~60 StoredProcedureToLinq/*.cs Query() methods with no orderby (e.g. sp_ReExportReport.cs Query lines 70-97 and ReExportDetailQuery 159-185 build `select new sp_ReExportReportResult{...}` unordered). The ApplySort fallback (steps 1-2) makes these deterministic automatically via ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync (70 controllers use it). For the high-traffic detail reports, also add an explicit `.OrderBy` on a meaningful unique column (e.g. project reExport.Id/registration.Id into the result and order by it) so the SQL is index-friendly rather than relying solely on the reflective all-columns fallback.

Run `grep -rn "OrderBy(" Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/` and confirm every paged Query()/Rows() either ends in a unique key (Id/UniqueId/LicenceNo) or is fully materialized (ToListAsync without Skip).
verification: Build: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds (the new dynamic OrderBy strings are validated at runtime by System.Linq.Dynamic.Core, so also smoke-test a real request).

Determinism test for the _Fast report: pick a date range with many same-CreatedDate bulk-issued licences, request page 1 then page 2 with a small pageSize (?pageIndex=0&pageSize=5 then pageIndex=1) on the Import Licence detail endpoint, and confirm no LicenceNo/HSCode row repeats across pages and none is skipped vs a full unpaged pull; repeat the two requests several times and confirm identical order each time.

Generic path: hit any ReportQueryService-backed endpoint (70 controllers, e.g. ReExportDetailReportController) paging through all pages with pageSize=5, concatenate all pages, and compare row count and set against an IncludeTotalCount=true total — must match with no dupes/gaps.

Inspect generated SQL via EF logging or ToQueryString to confirm every paged query emits `ORDER BY ... OFFSET ... FETCH` ending in a unique key column rather than a bare OFFSET or ORDER BY on CreatedDate alone.
Run `grep -rn "OrderBy(" Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/` to confirm remaining paged sources terminate in a unique key or are fully materialized.
pitfalls: The item PK is composite (string Id + int UniqueId) — ordering by ItemId alone is NOT unique across the fan-out; you must add BOTH ThenBy clauses. In ApplySort, call IsValidProperty with throwExceptionIfNotFound:false (the public default is true, relied on elsewhere) so an invalid client sortColumn falls through to the deterministic key instead of throwing. The deterministic fallback must apply to BOTH CreateAsync (exact-count path, used when IncludeTotalCount=true) and CreateFastPageAsync (estimate path); they share ApplySort so one fix covers both, but verify both paths now use the SAME ordering or their page sets won't line up. Ordering by all columns when no natural key exists adds SQL sort cost on wide projections — for the hottest reports add an explicit single indexed-PK OrderBy instead of relying on the all-columns fallback. Rollback is safe: reverting APIResult.cs and the _Fast projection restores prior behavior; no schema/data migration involved.
estimatedTime: 3-4 hours (core fix ~1h; auditing/spot-fixing high-traffic detail SP Query()s and cross-endpoint verification the rest)

approach: Lock down the public UploadController by requiring authentication and eliminating path traversal: add a class-level [Authorize], ignore the caller-supplied query `filename` for the on-disk path and instead derive a server-side-safe name (Guid + validated extension, no path separators), and validate against the loop file (`file`) actually being written. The exception-leak item is moot per the verifier (the dict is never serialized), so just log server-side and keep the existing 500.
steps: In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, add `[Authorize]` to the UploadController class (line 23), directly above/below `[ApiController]`, matching ExcelExportController/UserController. The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` is already present (line 12), so no new import is needed.

Stop using the raw query-string `filename` to build the disk path. Validate the extension of the file ACTUALLY being written (`file`, the loop variable) rather than `postedFile`, since the write uses `file`. Replace the `var ext = postedFile.FileName...` block (lines 63-64) and the AllowedFileExtensions check to operate on `file.FileName`.

Generate a server-side-safe name and reject any path separators. After the size/extension checks pass, in the `else` branch (lines 81-87), compute a safe base name: `var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + extension;` and build the path with the OS-correct, root-anchored directory: `var imageDir = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot", "Image"); var filePath = Path.Combine(imageDir, safeName);`. Then add a defense-in-depth check that the resolved full path stays under imageDir: `var fullDir = Path.GetFullPath(imageDir); var fullPath = Path.GetFullPath(filePath); if (!fullPath.StartsWith(fullDir, StringComparison.Ordinal)) return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);`.

Because SaveAsWebpAsync always rewrites the output to `<base>.webp` (lines 113 and 136 do `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"`), the real persisted name is `safeName` without its original extension plus `.webp`. Capture the returned path from `SaveAsWebpAsync` and return the basename to the caller so the frontend can reference it: `var savedPath = await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath); dict.Add("success", Path.GetFileName(savedPath));`. (The current code passes the raw query `filename` into SaveAsWebpAsync's split logic; after this change it passes the Guid-based path, removing the traversal entirely.)

Harden SaveAsWebpAsync's filename derivation: replace the fragile `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"` (lines 113 and 136) with `Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp")` so a Guid name containing no dot (or one accidentally containing multiple) is handled correctly.

Remove the dead commented-out members (lines 25, 27, 28 and the commented constructor params/body on lines 31, 33, 36, 38, 39) and the commented `using Amazon.S3` lines 8-9, leaving only the `IWebHostEnvironment _hostingEnvironment` field, constructor param, and assignment.

Exception handling (lines 94-100): per the verifier, nothing leaks today because `dict` is never serialized and only a bare 500 is returned. Do NOT spend effort scrubbing a response body that doesn't exist. Optionally replace the discarded `var res = string.Format(ex.Message...)` with a proper server-side log (inject `ILogger<UploadController>` and call `_logger.LogError(ex, "Upload failed")`) so the error is recorded rather than swallowed, then keep `return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError);`.

Verify whether this endpoint is still wanted: it is only called from Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx:221 (AntD Upload `action`) and writes to wwwroot/Image, while the report-export feature stores files outside wwwroot (Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportOptions.cs). If the Chat feature is dead, prefer deleting UploadController.cs and the ChatBox uploader entirely instead of hardening it.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` the Backend project succeeds with no new warnings about unused fields.
Unauthenticated request is rejected: `curl -i -X POST 'https://<host>/api/Upload/Postupload?filename=x.png' -F file=@test.png` returns 401 (was 201/200 before).

Path traversal is neutralized: with a valid JWT, POST with `?filename=..%5C..%5Cappsettings` and a real image part; confirm the file is written ONLY as wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp and that NO file appears outside wwwroot/Image (e.g., check ContentRoot for a stray `appsettings.webp`). The response `success` value should be the guid-based .webp name, not the attacker-supplied filename.
Happy path still works for the Chat uploader once the frontend sends the bearer token (see pitfalls): uploading a .png from ChatBox produces a wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp and the chat image renders.
Static serving sanity: GET the returned wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp succeeds; GET of any path outside Image is unaffected.
pitfalls: Frontend breakage from [Authorize]: ChatBox.tsx:219-223 uses an AntD `<Upload action={...}>` which does a bare browser POST and does NOT attach the axios JWT Authorization header. After adding [Authorize] this upload will 401. You must wire auth on the uploader too (add `headers={{ Authorization: 'Bearer ' + token }}` and/or `withCredentials`), or convert it to a `customRequest` that uses the existing axios instance. Coordinate this change with the backend change or the chat image upload feature will stop working.; Returned filename contract changed: previously the endpoint echoed back the caller's `filename` (and ChatBox derives the webp name client-side at ChatBox.tsx:203 via `filename.split('.')[0] + '.webp'`). Now the server owns the name (a Guid). The client-side derivation in ChatBox is unreliable anyway because SaveAsWebpAsync rewrites the extension; if you keep the feature, read the actual saved name from the response body rather than reconstructing it from the original filename.; Path.ChangeExtension expects the extension with a leading dot ('.webp'); keep the dot. With a Guid name (no dot) `filePath.Split(".")[0]` happened to work, but for names containing dots it truncated — ChangeExtension is the correct, non-lossy replacement.; Ensure `_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath` resolves to the app root containing wwwroot; if the deployment runs with a different working directory, the old relative `Path.Combine("wwwroot", ...)` and the new absolute path could point at different folders — verify the served static path matches in the target environment.; Rollback: revert UploadController.cs (and any ChatBox.tsx auth wiring) in one commit; no DB migration or data changes are involved.
estimatedTime: 2-3 hours (backend ~1h; frontend Upload auth wiring + manual verification ~1-2h)

approach: Make endpoints secure-by-default by adding a global FallbackPolicy (RequireAuthenticatedUser) in Program.cs, remove the class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, add [Authorize] to ChatController and UploadController, and stop serializing the plaintext Password on API.Model.User by marking it [JsonIgnore] so the generic Get on UserController can never leak it.
steps: In Backend/Program.cs, register an authorization fallback policy so every endpoint requires a valid JWT unless it explicitly opts out with [AllowAnonymous]. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` to the usings, then after the JWT bearer registration block (after the `.AddJwtBearer(...)` call that ends around line 91) add: `builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options => { options.FallbackPolicy = new AuthorizationPolicyBuilder().RequireAuthenticatedUser().Build(); });`

Confirm the pipeline order in Program.cs is correct for the new fallback policy: `app.UseAuthentication()` (line 166) then `app.UseAuthorization()` (line 167) already run before `app.MapControllers()` (line 168) — no change needed there. The fallback policy is enforced by the routing/authorization middleware, so it applies to all mapped controller actions automatically.

Verify the only intentionally-public endpoint stays public: AuthController.Login already carries `[AllowAnonymous]` (Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:23), so the new FallbackPolicy will not break login. Do NOT remove that attribute.

In Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs, delete the class-level `[AllowAnonymous]` attribute on line 15. The per-method `[Authorize]` attributes (lines 28, 50, 65, 90, 100, 113) can stay (harmless/explicit) or be removed since the FallbackPolicy now covers them — leaving them is fine. This closes the fragile [AllowAnonymous]-class + [Authorize]-method pattern where a newly added method would otherwise be silently public.

In Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs, add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` (it is not currently imported) and put `[Authorize]` on the class, e.g. directly above `public class ChatController : ControllerBase` (line 14), alongside the existing `[ApiController]`/`[Route]` attributes. This stops anonymous read/write of chat messages.

In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, add `[Authorize]` to the class above `public class UploadController : ControllerBase` (line 23). The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` is already present (line 12). This stops anonymous file uploads to wwwroot/Image. (Defense-in-depth beyond auth: Postupload writes using the caller-supplied `filename` at Path.Combine("wwwroot","Image", filename) — line 83 — so also call Path.GetFileName(filename) before combining to prevent path traversal, but the auth fix is the in-scope change for this finding.)

Stop serializing the plaintext Password on the model that the generic UserController Get returns. In Backend/Model/User.cs (namespace API.Model — this is the type used by `UserController : BaseAPIController<User>`, NOT Backend/Model/TradeNet/User.cs), add `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` and annotate the Password property (line 21) with `[JsonIgnore]` so `ApiResult<User>` returned by BaseAPIController.Get never emits the password. Keep the `[Required]` attribute.

If any login/write flow needs to bind Password on the way IN (AuthController.Login binds API.Model.User), prefer `[JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)]` is NOT sufficient — instead, if inbound binding of Password is required, replace the read path with a dedicated UserDto for GET responses rather than [JsonIgnore] on the entity. Verify AuthController.Login still receives Password after applying [JsonIgnore]; if login breaks, introduce a UserReadDto (Id, Name, Permission only) and have UserController/BaseAPIController project to it instead of returning the entity.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ — confirm no compile errors after the using/attribute changes.

Anonymous is now blocked: `curl -i https://localhost:7xxx/api/Chat?room=a-AND-b` (no Authorization header) must return 401 (was 200). Same for `curl -i -X POST .../api/Upload/Postupload?filename=x.png` — must return 401 (was 201/200).
Login still works: `curl -i -X POST .../api/Auth -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"...","password":"..."}'` returns 200 with a token (AllowAnonymous on Login still honored).
Authenticated report endpoints still work: call any existing report controller with a valid Bearer token and confirm 200 (FallbackPolicy doesn't break already-[Authorize]'d controllers).
Password no longer leaks: with a valid token call `GET /api/User` and confirm the JSON `data` items contain Id/Name/Permission but NO `password`/`Password` field.

Regression: confirm no controller that legitimately must stay public lost access — only AuthController.Login should be reachable anonymously (grep: `grep -rn AllowAnonymous Backend/Controllers` should show only AuthController.cs after BaseAPIController's is removed).
pitfalls: FallbackPolicy applies ONLY to endpoints with no authorization metadata; existing [Authorize]/[AllowAnonymous] still win. After removing [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, the inherited methods fall back to RequireAuthenticatedUser (they already had per-method [Authorize] anyway), so behavior tightens without breaking authenticated callers.; Static files: app.UseStaticFiles() (Program.cs:169) serves wwwroot publicly and is unaffected by the fallback policy. Files already written by the previously-anonymous Upload endpoint remain publicly readable — locking the endpoint does not retroactively secure existing uploaded files; audit wwwroot/Image if abuse is suspected.; Two different User types exist: API.Model.User (Backend/Model/User.cs, has plaintext Password — this is the one UserController exposes) and API.Model.TradeNet.User (Backend/Model/TradeNet/User.cs). Apply [JsonIgnore] to the API.Model one; the TradeNet one is a separate EF entity.; [JsonIgnore] on Password blocks BOTH read and write serialization. AuthController.Login binds API.Model.User from the request body and reads data.Password — verify login still binds Password after the change. System.Text.Json [JsonIgnore] (no condition) suppresses deserialization too, which can break login. If login breaks, do NOT keep plain [JsonIgnore] on the entity; instead introduce a UserReadDto for GET responses and leave the entity intact for inbound binding.; Order: add AddAuthorization in the service-registration section (before var app = builder.Build()), not after — registering services after Build() has no effect.; Rollback: revert the four files; the changes are additive attributes plus one service registration, no DB migration involved.

approach: Remove the class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController<T> so it is no longer anonymous-by-default (which currently short-circuits the per-action [Authorize] and exposes the User credentials table), make authenticated access the secure default via a fallback authorization policy in Program.cs, and introduce explicit request/response DTOs for UserController so the Password/Permission columns can no longer be over-posted.
steps: Open Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs and DELETE the class-level [AllowAnonymous] attribute on line 15 (the line directly above `public class BaseAPIController<T> : ControllerBase where T : class`). This is the single most important change: in ASP.NET Core [AllowAnonymous] beats [Authorize] no matter where it sits, so while it is present the inherited Get/Get(id)/PutData/PostData/PostDataList/DeleteData actions on /api/User are reachable anonymously despite their per-action [Authorize].

Keep the per-action [Authorize] attributes already on every action (lines 28, 50, 65, 90, 100, 113). UserController.cs already declares [Authorize] at class level (line 13), so once [AllowAnonymous] is gone, /api/User is fully authenticated. No edit to UserController's attributes is required for the auth fix.

Add a fallback authorization policy as defense-in-depth so any future action that forgets [Authorize] is still protected. In Backend/Program.cs, immediately after the `builder.Services.AddAuthentication(...).AddJwtBearer(...)` block ends (after line 91), add an AddAuthorization call with a FallbackPolicy of RequireAuthenticatedUser(). This makes 'authenticated' the default for every endpoint that has no explicit auth attribute.

Verify the public login still works: Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs already has an explicit [AllowAnonymous] on its login action (line 23). An explicit per-action [AllowAnonymous] overrides the FallbackPolicy, so login remains public. Do not remove that attribute.

Stop over-posting on the User credentials table. Create Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs with a UserCreateDto / UserUpdateDto (Name + Password + Permission, no Id allowed on update from client beyond the route id) and a UserResponseDto that omits Password. Then override the generic write/read actions in UserController so the wire contract is the DTO, not the raw User entity.

In UserController.cs, replace the empty body with overrides that hide the generic PostData/PutData/Get-by-id from the inherited base for the User type: add `[HttpPost]` Post(UserCreateDto), `[HttpPut("{id}")]` Put(string id, UserUpdateDto), and a Get-by-id that maps to UserResponseDto. Inside each, hash/validate as needed and map only the whitelisted fields onto the User entity before calling SaveChangesAsync via the injected ApplicationDbContext (add a `using API.Model.Dtos;` and inject the context the same way the base does). Because these new actions share the same [HttpPost]/[HttpPut("{id}")] routes, also mark the corresponding base actions to not be exposed for User — simplest is to make the base PostData/PutData/PostDataList `protected` (non-action) and re-expose only the DTO-based ones in concrete subclasses; alternatively keep them public but ensure the subclass routes win — prefer making base CRUD `protected virtual` so subclasses opt in deliberately.

If, after review, the generic CRUD base is not actually needed for User (the app's real user management may go through AuthController), delete BaseAPIController.cs and convert UserController to a plain [Authorize] ControllerBase exposing only the specific, DTO-based actions it needs. This removes the entire over-broad attack surface (Put/Post/PostDataList/Delete on the credentials table) in one move and is the recommendation's preferred end-state.
verification: Before the fix, confirm the exposure: `curl -i https://<host>/api/User` (or http://localhost:5173-proxied API) with NO Authorization header returns 200 + a JSON list including Password/Permission values. After removing [AllowAnonymous], the same anonymous request must return 401 Unauthorized.

Anonymous write/delete must also be blocked: `curl -i -X DELETE https://<host>/api/User/<someId>` and `curl -i -X POST https://<host>/api/User -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'` with no token must each return 401 (previously 200/Created/Ok).

With a valid JWT in the Authorization header, `GET /api/User` still returns 200 and the data, proving the per-action [Authorize] + class-level [Authorize] on UserController still permit authenticated access.

Public login is unaffected: `POST /api/Auth` (the [AllowAnonymous] login action) with valid credentials and no prior token still returns a token (the FallbackPolicy does not apply to actions that carry explicit [AllowAnonymous]).

Over-posting closed: with a valid token, `POST /api/User` with a body containing an `Id` or extra fields no longer lets the client set arbitrary primary keys/fields, and `GET /api/User/{id}` response (UserResponseDto) contains no Password field. Inspect the response JSON to confirm Password is absent.
Build check: `dotnet build Backend` from the repo root succeeds with no missing-using errors (AuthorizationPolicyBuilder requires using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization in Program.cs).
pitfalls: "Adding the FallbackPolicy makes EVERY endpoint require authentication by default, including any endpoint that was previously (incorrectly) open and relied on the base class being anonymous. Before deploying, grep all controllers for actions that legitimately need to be public; AuthController's login already has explicit [AllowAnonymous] (line 23) so it is safe, but verify there are no other intentionally-public endpoints (e.g. health checks, swagger in prod) that would suddenly start returning 401. None other were found in Controllers/, but confirm against any newly added controllers. Rollback is trivial (re-add the attribute / remove the AddAuthorization block) but should not be done since it re-opens the credentials table. The DTO change is a wire-contract change for /api/User Post/Put — coordinate with the React frontend (Frontend/src) if it currently posts the raw User shape, otherwise those calls may fail validation."

approach: Add a root-level GitHub Actions workflow `.github/workflows/backend-tests.yml` that runs `dotnet test` on the existing `Backend.Tests` project on every PR/push to `main`, split into a CI-safe tier (translation/pure-logic/InMemory tests, run unconditionally with no SQL Server) and a DB-integration tier (gated behind the `TRADENET_REPORT_TEST_CONNECTION_STRING` secret / a SQL Server service container). Tag the three DB-dependent test classes with `[Trait("Category","RequiresDatabase")]` so the tiers select reliably instead of relying on brittle class-name string filters.
steps: Create the repo-root workflow directory (it does not exist today — only `Frontend/.github/workflows` is present): `mkdir -p "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.github/workflows"`.

Tag the three DB-dependent test classes so the CI tier split is robust (not based on class-name substrings). Add `[Trait("Category", "RequiresDatabase")]` to the class declarations of: `Backend.Tests/ReportEndpointSmokeTests.cs` (uses `ReportSqlServerFixture` -> `EnsureCreatedAsync`, needs a live SQL Server), `Backend.Tests/ReportSeededDatabaseSmokeTests.cs` (needs the seeded `TradeNetDBTest` database), and `Backend.Tests/TempSectionValidation.cs` (its `NewDb()` throws if `TRADENET_REPORT_TEST_CONNECTION_STRING` is unset). xUnit's `[Trait]` is in the `Xunit` namespace, already globally imported via `<Using Include="Xunit" />` in `Backend.Tests.csproj`, so no extra using is needed.

Create `.github/workflows/backend-tests.yml` with two jobs.
