Job `ci-safe-tests` runs on every PR and push to `main` and executes `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln --filter "Category!=RequiresDatabase"` — no database, no secrets. Job `db-integration-tests` runs `dotnet test --filter "Category=RequiresDatabase"` against a SQL Server service container (behind the connection-string secret), allowed to be non-blocking initially. Use `actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` pinned to `dotnet-version: 8.0.x` (the projects target `net8.0`). Set `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`.

In the `ci-safe-tests` job, the steps are: checkout; setup-dotnet 8.0.x; `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`; `dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln --no-restore --configuration Release`; `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln --no-build --configuration Release --filter "Category!=RequiresDatabase" --logger "trx;LogFileName=ci-safe.trx" --results-directory ./TestResults`. The job must fail the build on any test failure (default `dotnet test` behavior — do NOT add `continue-on-error`).

In the `db-integration-tests` job, add a `services:` block with `mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest` exposing port 1433 with `MSSQL_SA_PASSWORD` and `ACCEPT_EULA=Y`, then set `env: TRADENET_REPORT_TEST_CONNECTION_STRING` to point at that container (Initial Catalog=TradeNetDBTest, User Id=sa, TrustServerCertificate=True, MultipleActiveResultSets=true) and run `dotnet test ... --filter "Category=RequiresDatabase"`. Note `ReportSeededDatabaseSmokeTests` also needs the `TradeNetDBTest` schema + seed data and specific stored procedures present; until a seed/restore step exists, mark this job `continue-on-error: true` so it informs but does not block, while `ci-safe-tests` is the required gate.

Make `ci-safe-tests` a required status check: GitHub repo Settings -> Branches -> add a branch protection rule for `main` requiring the `ci-safe-tests` check before merge. (The existing Frontend workflows use `branches-ignore: [main, master]` and would never gate merges to `main`; this backend workflow triggers ON `pull_request` to `main` so it actually gates.)

Before flipping `ci-safe-tests` to a required check, fix the 2 pre-existing CI-safe failures that already break the suite today (see pitfalls): `ReportEndpointPayloadFixtureTests` fails because `CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumberController` is auto-discovered by `ReportTestHelper.ControllerTypes` (the `.Where(...)` at ReportTestHelper.cs lines 24-31) but does not implement the expected non-public `TryCreateReportRequest` method. Either add that method to the controller to match the convention, or temporarily exclude that controller from `ReportTestHelper.ControllerTypes` discovery. Otherwise the new gate is red on day one.
patch:
name: Backend Tests

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  ci-safe-tests:
    name: CI-safe backend tests (no database)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      - run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      - run: dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln --no-restore --configuration Release
      - name: Run CI-safe tests
        run: >
          dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln
          --no-build --configuration Release
          --filter "Category!=RequiresDatabase"
          --logger "trx;LogFileName=ci-safe.trx"
          --results-directory ./TestResults
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: ci-safe-test-results
          path: ./TestResults

  db-integration-tests:
    name: DB-integration backend tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    continue-on-error: true   # informational until TradeNetDBTest seed/restore exists
    services:
      sqlserver:
        image: mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest
        env:
          ACCEPT_EULA: "Y"
          MSSQL_SA_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.SQL_SA_PASSWORD }}
        ports:
          - 1433:1433
    env:
      TRADENET_REPORT_TEST_CONNECTION_STRING: "Server=localhost,1433;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDBTest;User Id=sa;Password=${{ secrets.SQL_SA_PASSWORD }};TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;"
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      # TODO: restore/seed TradeNetDBTest schema + stored procs before running.
      - run: >
          dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln
          --filter "Category=RequiresDatabase"

# --- Trait added to the three DB-dependent classes, e.g. ReportEndpointSmokeTests.cs ---
# [Trait("Category", "RequiresDatabase")]
# public sealed class ReportEndpointSmokeTests(ReportSqlServerFixture sqlServerFixture)
#     : IClassFixture<ReportSqlServerFixture>
verification: Locally reproduce the CI-safe tier exactly as the workflow runs it (no DB needed): `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln --filter "Category!=RequiresDatabase"` from the repo root — it should build and execute the translation/payload/branch-defaults/InMemory-cache/Excel-writer tests with no SQL Server connection. (Verified during analysis: the equivalent FQN-exclusion filter ran 327 tests offline; the three DB classes were correctly skipped and never attempted a connection.)

Confirm the gate catches failures: today it reports `Failed: 2, Passed: 325` because `CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumberController` is missing `TryCreateReportRequest` — i.e. the workflow correctly exits non-zero on real breakage. After the controller is fixed/excluded, the same command should report `Failed: 0`.
Push a branch with a deliberately broken assertion in a CI-safe test, open a PR to `main`, and confirm the `ci-safe-tests` check goes red and blocks merge; revert and confirm it goes green.

Validate the DB tier wiring by running `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln --filter "Category=RequiresDatabase"` with `TRADENET_REPORT_TEST_CONNECTION_STRING` pointed at a seeded `TradeNetDBTest`; the `ReportSeededDatabaseSmokeTests.TradeNetDBTest_database_is_available` fact should pass.
pitfalls: BLOCKER — the CI-safe suite is already red on `main` today: `ReportEndpointPayloadFixtureTests` (2 tests) fails because `ReportTestHelper.ControllerTypes` auto-discovers every `*Controller` in `Backend.Controllers.Report`, and `CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumberController` does not implement the expected non-public `TryCreateReportRequest` method (`GetTryCreateReportRequest` throws). Fix or quarantine this BEFORE making `ci-safe-tests` a required check, or every PR will be blocked on a pre-existing failure unrelated to its change. Classification note: `ReportQueryTranslationTests` calls `CreateSqlServerDbContext()` against `(localdb)\\mssqllocaldb` but only invokes `.ToQueryString()`, which translates LINQ->SQL WITHOUT opening a connection — it is genuinely CI-safe and must NOT be tagged `RequiresDatabase`; only the three classes that actually open a connection (`ReportEndpointSmokeTests`, `ReportSeededDatabaseSmokeTests`, `TempSectionValidation`) get the trait. The DB job needs more than a connection string: `ReportSeededDatabaseSmokeTests` asserts specific seeded row counts (e.g. `MemberRegistrationReportController` == 52) and requires the stored procedures to exist, so a bare empty SQL container will fail it — keep that job `continue-on-error: true` until a real schema+seed restore step is added. Do NOT add the workflow under `Frontend/.github/workflows`; only workflows at the REPO-ROOT `.github/workflows` are picked up, and the existing frontend `test.yml` uses `branches-ignore: [main, master]`, which would never gate merges to `main`. Pin `setup-dotnet` to `8.0.x` (projects target `net8.0`); the local SDK is 10.x, which builds net8.0 fine but CI should match the runtime target.
estimatedTime: 2 hours (CI-safe tier + traits + branch protection); +half a day for a fully seeded DB-integration job

approach: Add behavioral xUnit coverage for the auth pipeline: a fake ICommonService<T> (backed by EF Core InMemory, already referenced, so JWTManagerService's await FirstOrDefaultAsync works) drives unit tests of JWTManagerService.Authenticate / RefreshToken, asserting issued-token validation, claims, expiry, and null-on-bad-credentials; plus an AuthController test proving Login returns NotFound for unknown users and never echoes raw exception text on a thrown error.
steps: Create Backend.Tests/Fakes/FakeCommonService.cs implementing API.Interface.ICommonService<T> for arbitrary T. Back Retrieve with an EF Core InMemory DbContext DbSet<T>() (use Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.InMemory, already in Backend.Tests.csproj) so JWTManagerService's `await UsersRecords.FirstOrDefaultAsync()` and `_userService.Retrieve.Where(...)` resolve against a real IAsyncQueryProvider. Implement Create as a context.Add + SaveChanges (return 1), and record created entities in a public List<T> Created so tests can assert TokenModel persistence; Update/Delete can be minimal (return 0/1).

Create Backend.Tests/JWTManagerServiceTests.cs. Build IConfiguration with an in-memory provider: new ConfigurationBuilder().AddInMemoryCollection(new Dictionary<string,string?>{["JWT:Key"]="This is my supper secret key for jwt"}).Build() — reuse the exact key from Backend/appsettings.json so issued tokens validate under the same SymmetricSecurityKey.

Test 1 (valid credentials issue a usable token): seed FakeCommonService<User> with new User{Id="u-1", Name="alice", Password="pw", Permission="Admin"}. new JWTManagerService(config, new FakeCommonService<TokenModel>(), userService). Call await Authenticate(new User{Name="alice", Password="pw"}). Assert result is non-null, result.UserId=="u-1", result.Permission=="Admin". Then validate result.Token with new JwtSecurityTokenHandler().ValidateToken(token, new TokenValidationParameters{ ValidateIssuer=false, ValidateAudience=false, ValidateLifetime=true, ValidateIssuerSigningKey=true, IssuerSigningKey=new SymmetricSecurityKey(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(key)) }, out var validated) — mirrors Program.cs lines 81-90. Assert principal has ClaimTypes.Name=="u-1" and ClaimTypes.Role=="Admin", and ((JwtSecurityToken)validated).ValidTo is ~1 day out (Assert.InRange around DateTime.UtcNow.AddDays(1) +/- a few minutes). Also assert the TokenModel was persisted (the TokenModel FakeCommonService.Created has Count==1).
Test 2 (wrong password -> null): seed the same user, call await Authenticate(new User{Name="alice", Password="WRONG"}); Assert.Null(result). Add a second case for unknown Name -> null.

Test 3 (RefreshToken happy path + rotation): seed FakeCommonService<TokenModel> with new TokenModel{Token="old-tok", RefreshToken="rt-1", UserId="u-1"}. Call RefreshToken("rt-1"); assert non-null, UserId=="u-1", a fresh Token issued, and that Delete was invoked for the old token (track deleted ids in the fake). Add RefreshToken("missing") -> Assert.Null.

Create Backend.Tests/AuthControllerTests.cs. Use a tiny stub IJWTManagerService (hand-rolled, no Moq in repo): StubJwtManager with Func<User,Task<TokenModel?>> for Authenticate. (a) Authenticate returns null -> new AuthController(stub).Login(new User{Name="nobody",Password="x"}) returns NotFoundResult (matches AuthController.cs:36). (b) Authenticate throws new Exception("DB secret: connection string ...") -> Login returns ObjectResult with StatusCode 500, and Assert that the serialized value does NOT contain "DB secret"/the raw message — this test will FAIL against current AuthController.cs:41 which returns ex.Message, documenting the info-leak; then fix the controller (next step) so it passes.

Fix the info-leak the test surfaces: in Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs change the catch block to log the exception and return a generic message instead of ex.Message — return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, "An error occurred while processing the login request."). Inject ILogger<AuthController> and call _logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed"). This makes Test 3b green and removes the real vulnerability the audit flagged.
Run dotnet test from the repo root and confirm the new tests execute (they need no SQL Server / live DB).
verification: From repo root run: dotnet test "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj" --filter "FullyQualifiedName~JWTManagerServiceTests|FullyQualifiedName~AuthControllerTests" — all new tests pass with no SQL Server connection (they use EF InMemory and stubs).
Confirm the info-leak test (Login_does_not_echo_raw_exception_message) RED before the AuthController.cs catch-block edit and GREEN after — proving the test actually exercises the leak path.
Run the existing suite (dotnet test on the whole Backend.Tests project) to confirm no regressions in ReportEndpointSmokeTests etc.

Optional manual check: POST /api/Auth with a bad password returns 404 (NotFound) and POST that triggers a server error returns 500 with body "An error occurred while processing the login request." — no connection string / stack trace in the response.
pitfalls: "Do not use new List<User>().AsQueryable() for the userService mock — JWTManagerService.Authenticate calls `await UsersRecords.FirstOrDefaultAsync()`, which throws 'does not implement IAsyncQueryProvider' on a LINQ-to-Objects source; that's why the fake is backed by an EF Core InMemory DbContext. Authenticate also calls .AsParallel().Any() which works on either, but FirstOrDefaultAsync is the constraint. The AuthController fix adds an ILogger<AuthController> constructor parameter — update the test (use Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions NullLogger<AuthController>.Instance) and confirm DI still resolves (ILogger<T> is auto-registered by the host, so Program.cs needs no change). Keep using the exact appsettings key string 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' so the validate step matches; if the production key is later rotated, the test's hardcoded key is independent and still valid. Scope these tests as unit tests only — do NOT also remediate the plaintext-password storage here (separate finding); these tests merely document, not fix, that design flaw."

approach: Stop persisting the JWT in localStorage where any JS/XSS can read it. Move the bearer token into an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie that AuthController sets on login and the API reads via JwtBearer's MessageReceived event; keep only non-secret UI state (userId/permission) and an isAuthenticated flag in React state, with logout clearing the cookie server-side.
steps: Backend - have login set an httpOnly cookie instead of returning the raw token to JS. In Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs Login(): after `var result = await _jWTManager.Authenticate(data);` and the null check, write the cookie before returning Ok. Add `Response.Cookies.Append("access_token", result.Token, new CookieOptions { HttpOnly = true, Secure = true, SameSite = SameSiteMode.Strict, Path = "/", MaxAge = TimeSpan.FromDays(1) });` (MaxAge should match the 1-day Expires in JWTManagerService.Authenticate at line 49). Return a body WITHOUT the token, e.g. `return Ok(new { result.UserId, result.Permission });` so the JWT never reaches JS.

Backend - teach JwtBearer to read the cookie. In Backend/Program.cs inside the `.AddJwtBearer(o => { ... })` block (lines 72-91), after setting `o.TokenValidationParameters`, add an event: `o.Events = new JwtBearerEvents { OnMessageReceived = ctx => { if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(ctx.Token) && ctx.Request.Cookies.TryGetValue("access_token", out var c)) ctx.Token = c; return Task.CompletedTask; } };`. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer;` is already present; ensure `using System.Threading.Tasks;` is available (add if missing). This keeps the existing Authorization-header path working while also accepting the cookie.

Backend - add an authenticated logout endpoint that clears the cookie. In AuthController.cs add `[Authorize] [HttpPost("logout")] public IActionResult Logout() { Response.Cookies.Delete("access_token", new CookieOptions { HttpOnly = true, Secure = true, SameSite = SameSiteMode.Strict, Path = "/" }); return Ok(); }` and add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` (already imported).

Backend - allow cookies cross-origin. The CORS policy in Program.cs (lines 119-156) already calls `.AllowCredentials()` with an explicit `WithOrigins(...)` allowlist, which is correct for cookies. Do NOT add `AllowAnyOrigin()` (it is incompatible with credentials). No change needed here beyond confirming the frontend origin (http://localhost:5173 / reportuat.myanmartradenet.com) is in the list — it is.

Frontend - send cookies and stop attaching the localStorage token. In Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts: set `withCredentials: true` on the instance (`const axiosInstance = axios.create({ withCredentials: true });`) and DELETE the lines `const token = localStorage.getItem('token');` and `config.headers.Authorization = \`Bearer ${token}\`;` (lines 10-11). Keep the baseUrl prefixing logic.

Frontend - stop writing/reading the JWT in AuthContext. In Frontend/src/context/AuthContext.tsx: in login() (lines 50-54) remove `localStorage.setItem('token', temp.token);` and `setToken(temp.token);`; the API no longer returns a token, so read `temp.userId`/`temp.permission` from the new response body. Drop the `token` field from AuthContextType/state entirely (no consumer reads `auth.token` — verified via grep; ChatBox/ChatList/App/ProtectedRoute use only user/isAuthenticated/logout).

Frontend - make logout call the server. In AuthContext.tsx logout() (lines 67-73) await `axiosInstance.post('Auth/logout')` (catch errors) before clearing local state, and remove the `localStorage.removeItem('token'|'userid'|'permission')` calls. Since isAuthenticated depends on `user` state which is now in memory only, a full page refresh logs the user out; if you need refresh-survival, add a `GET api/Auth/me` endpoint that returns userId/permission from `User.Identity` (the cookie) and call it on AuthProvider mount to rehydrate — do NOT rehydrate from localStorage.

Frontend - remove the localStorage rehydration. In AuthContext.tsx the useState initializers (lines 22-34) read `localStorage.getItem('userid'|'permission'|'token')`. Replace with `useState<User|null>(null)` and remove the token state; rely on the `me` endpoint from the previous step (or accept re-login on refresh). userId/permission are not secret but should now come from the cookie-authenticated `me` call, not localStorage.
verification: Log in via the SignIn page against the running backend, then open DevTools > Application > Local Storage: confirm there is NO 'token' key. Application > Cookies should show 'access_token' flagged HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict.
In the browser console run `document.cookie` and confirm 'access_token' is NOT listed (httpOnly cookies are invisible to JS) — this is the core proof the XSS-read window is closed.

Hit a protected report endpoint from the UI after login and confirm it returns 200 (cookie auth works). Then call the same endpoint from curl WITHOUT the cookie and confirm 401: `curl -i https://localhost:5001/api/<someReport>` should be 401, while `curl -i --cookie "access_token=<jwt>" ...` returns 200.
Click logout, then re-call a protected endpoint and confirm 401, and confirm the access_token cookie is gone in DevTools.
Network tab: confirm requests carry `Cookie: access_token=...` (with withCredentials) and no longer carry an `Authorization: Bearer` header sourced from localStorage.
pitfalls: SameSite=Strict + cross-subdomain: the SPA (reportuat.myanmartradenet.com) and API must be same-site for the cookie to be sent. If the API is on a different registrable domain, the Strict/None cookie will not flow — use SameSite=None;Secure and ensure both are HTTPS, and keep the explicit CORS WithOrigins+AllowCredentials (never AllowAnyOrigin with credentials).; Cookie auth introduces CSRF exposure that the old localStorage+Authorization-header scheme did not have. Because state-changing report endpoints exist (POST/PUT/DELETE in CORS), add CSRF protection: either rely on SameSite=Strict (sufficient for same-site only) plus a double-submit CSRF token header for any cross-origin POST, or keep SameSite=Strict and verify no cross-site state change is needed.; Secure=true cookies are dropped over plain HTTP, so local dev must use the HTTPS profile (the app already calls UseHttpsRedirection). Don't test over http://.; Ordering: deploy backend (cookie-set + OnMessageReceived) BEFORE frontend, since OnMessageReceived still honors the Authorization header during the transition — old clients keep working until the new frontend ships.; Removing localStorage rehydration means a page refresh logs users out unless you add the GET api/Auth/me rehydration endpoint; decide this before shipping to avoid a UX regression.; This change reduces but does not eliminate XSS impact for in-page actions (an attacker can still ride the cookie via fetch on-origin). Pair with the separate strict-CSP finding for defense in depth.

approach: Introduce a lazy boundary so the 159 report pages + 336 KB reportConfigs.ts are not statically reachable from the login chunk: convert every static page import in reportRoutes.tsx to React.lazy(() => import(...)), wrap the /Report route children in Suspense, and add a Vite manualChunks rule so the report config/vendors land in their own chunks. No React/router upgrade needed — react-router-dom is already v7 and supports React.lazy with element + Suspense.
steps: Open Frontend/src/Report/reportRoutes.tsx. Replace the 159 static default imports (lines 1-159, e.g. `import AccountSummaryReport from './Page/AccountSummaryReport';`) with React.lazy dynamic imports. Add `import { lazy } from 'react';` at the top and rewrite each line as `const AccountSummaryReport = lazy(() => import('./Page/AccountSummaryReport'));`. All 160 page files have a default export, so the default-export form of lazy works as-is with no `.then(m => ({default: m.X}))` shim.

Leave the `export const reportRoutes = [...]` table (lines 161-321) exactly as-is. The JSX `element: <AccountSummaryReport />` references stay valid because `lazy()` returns a component, so keeping the variable names identical means zero edits to the 159-row route table.

Add a Suspense boundary so the lazy report pages have a fallback (mandatory under the data router). Edit Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx: change line 17 to `import React, { ReactNode, Suspense, useEffect } from 'react';` and add `import { Spin } from 'antd';`. On the `/Report` route object (lines 161-170), wrap each child route element in Suspense by mapping over reportRoutes: `children: reportRoutes.map((r) => ({ ...r, element: <Suspense fallback={<Spin size="large" .../>}>{r.element}</Suspense> }))`.

Add a Vite manualChunks rule so the report config and heavy vendors get their own cacheable chunks. Edit Frontend/vite.config.ts: add `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks(id)` that returns 'report-config' for ids under 'src/Report/config/', and separate vendor chunks for antd/@ant-design, react-router, dayjs, and react/react-dom. This keeps the 336 KB config out of the login/entry chunk and prevents a single report edit from busting the vendor chunk.

Because pages are now lazy, the `import { reportConfigs } from '../config/reportConfigs'` in each Page/*.tsx (e.g. Frontend/src/Report/Page/AccountSummaryReport.tsx using `config={reportConfigs.AccountSummaryReport}`) is no longer reachable from the login chunk; with the manualChunks rule it loads as a separate report-config chunk only when the first report opens. No per-domain config split is required for this finding.

Run `npm run build` in Frontend/ and confirm dist/assets now contains ~159 individual page chunks plus a report-config-*.js chunk and vendor-* chunks, and that the entry/index chunk shrank substantially.
verification: Run `npm run build` in Frontend/. In dist/assets confirm ~159 individual page chunks (e.g. AccountSummaryReport-<hash>.js) plus report-config-<hash>.js and vendor-* chunks now exist instead of one giant entry chunk, and the main entry/index chunk is much smaller.
`npm run dev`, open the signin page with DevTools Network filtered to JS, and confirm NO report page chunk and NO report-config chunk are downloaded before login.
Navigate to /Report/AccountSummaryReport and confirm the page chunk + report-config chunk load on demand, the Suspense Spin briefly shows, and the report renders with no console errors.
Navigate to a second report and confirm its own chunk lazy-loads (new .js in Network) while report-config is reused from cache.
Optionally run `npx vite-bundle-visualizer` to confirm reportConfigs is isolated in report-config and no longer co-located with the entry/login chunk.
pitfalls: react-router-dom is v7 (not v6) and createBrowserRouter (data router) is used — lazy elements MUST have a Suspense boundary above them or the app throws 'A component suspended while responding to synchronous input'; that is exactly why the routes.tsx Suspense wrapper is mandatory, not optional. Keep the lazy variable names identical to the old import names so the 159-row reportRoutes table (lines 161-321) needs zero edits. Every Page/*.tsx must keep its `export default` (verified: all 160 do) for the default-export lazy form. Do not leave both a static `import X` line and a `const X = lazy(...)` for the same page — that is a redeclaration compile error. In manualChunks, order matters: the broad `node_modules/react` check must come AFTER the react-router/react-dom checks because 'node_modules/react' also matches those paths. Rollback is trivial: revert the three files; no data/migration impact.
estimatedTime: 3-4 hours (mostly the mechanical conversion of 159 imports; scriptable with sed/regex)

approach: Wire the already-declared sort/search API inside BasicTable.tsx (backend already accepts the params); make headers clickable to sort and add a per-column search box driven by real setState.
steps: Open Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx. The component already imports SortOrder, declares column.sortKey/sortable/searchable/filterKey, and threads sortColumn/sortOrder/filterColumn/filterQuery into the query object and buildLegacyUrl — only the rendered UI and state are missing. Destructure the two currently-ignored props in the function signature (after initialPageSize = 10 around L135): add `initialSortColumn = '',` and `initialSortOrder = 'desc',`.

Replace the value-only filterQuery state at L176 (`const [filterQuery] = useState('');`) with a real setter: `const [filterQuery, setFilterQuery] = useState('');`. Add two new state hooks right after it: `const [sortColumn, setSortColumn] = useState(initialSortColumn);` and `const [sortOrder, setSortOrder] = useState<SortOrder>(initialSortOrder);`.

In the `query` useMemo (L191-202), stop hardcoding sort. Change `sortColumn: '',` -> `sortColumn,` and `sortOrder: '',` -> `sortOrder,`, and extend the dependency array from `[filterColumn, filterQuery, pageIndex, pageSize]` to `[filterColumn, filterQuery, pageIndex, pageSize, sortColumn, sortOrder]`.

Add a sort toggle handler above the return (e.g. after renderCell, ~L281). It resolves the backend column name from sortKey, then dataIndex, then key: `const handleSort = (column: BasicTableColumn<T>) => { if (column.sortable === false) return; const colKey = (column.sortKey ?? column.dataIndex ?? column.key).toString(); setPageIndex(0); if (sortColumn === colKey) { setSortOrder((prev) => (prev === 'asc' ? 'desc' : 'asc')); } else { setSortColumn(colKey); setSortOrder('asc'); } };`. Reset pageIndex so sorting starts at page 1.

Make the column-header <th> interactive in the header row (L378-391). Compute `const colKey = (column.sortKey ?? column.dataIndex ?? column.key).toString(); const isSorted = sortColumn === colKey; const sortable = column.sortable !== false;` inside the map. Add `onClick={() => handleSort(column)}`, `style={{ width: column.width, cursor: sortable ? 'pointer' : 'default', userSelect: 'none' }}`, and render a sort indicator after the title: `{sortable && (isSorted ? (sortOrder === 'asc' ? ' ▲' : ' ▼') : ' ⇅')}`. Guard the click with the sortable flag so non-sortable columns stay inert.

Add a per-column search box. Use the existing `searchable` prop (PropsType L59) plus per-column `column.searchable`/`filterKey`. Below the column-header <tr> (before </thead> at L395) add a filter row that renders only when `searchable` is true and the column is searchable: `<input value={filterColumn === (column.filterKey ?? column.dataIndex ?? column.key).toString() ? filterQuery : ''} onChange={(e) => { setPageIndex(0); setFilterColumn((column.filterKey ?? column.dataIndex ?? column.key).toString()); setFilterQuery(e.target.value); }} placeholder="Search" />`. (Single active filterColumn matches the backend, which takes one filterColumn/filterQuery pair.) Debounce optional — keep simple for first pass.

In GenericReportPage.tsx the props are already forwarded at L800-801; no change needed there except confirming the value flows. Since config.initialSortColumn holds the backend SQL column name (e.g. 'VoucherDate', 'Date' in reportConfigs.ts) it is fed directly as sortColumn, which is what the stored proc's @SortColumn expects (Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_VoucherReport.cs L160) — so the seed value is correct as-is.

To make a column's clickable header sort by the right backend column when it differs from camelCase dataIndex, add an optional `sortKey?: string;` to ReportColumnConfig in Frontend/src/Report/config/reportTypes.ts (after fallbackDataIndexes, L27). BasicTableColumn already has sortKey (BasicTable.tsx L36) and toTableColumn spreads the config (GenericReportPage.tsx L326-340 `{ ...column }`), so a sortKey set on a config column will propagate automatically. Populate sortKey only where dataIndex !== the SQL column name; otherwise the dataIndex fallback in handleSort is sufficient.

Add minimal styling in Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/style.css near the existing `.table-container thead th` rule (L132): a `.table-container thead th.sortable { cursor: pointer; }` helper and a `.table-container thead .column-filter-row input { width: 100%; }` rule so the search inputs fit the column width.
verification: cd Frontend && npm run build (or tsc --noEmit) — confirm no type errors after destructuring initialSortColumn/initialSortOrder and adding sortKey to ReportColumnConfig.

Run the app (npm run dev), open a high-volume report (e.g. Voucher report, config initialSortColumn 'VoucherDate'). Confirm a sort arrow renders on every header and the active column shows the seeded descending arrow on first load.

Open browser DevTools Network tab, click the 'Date'/'Amount' column header, and confirm the POST to config.apiRoute body now contains a non-empty sortColumn plus sortOrder 'ASC'/'DESC' (GenericReportPage.buildRequest uppercases sortOrder, L309); click again and confirm sortOrder flips and the visible row order reverses.

Type a value into a column search box (with searchable enabled) and confirm the request body carries filterColumn = that column and filterQuery = the typed text, pageIndex resets to 0, and the grid narrows to matching rows.
Confirm pagination still works after sorting/searching (total count updates, page 1 is reselected).
pitfalls: initialSortColumn in reportConfigs.ts holds BACKEND SQL column names (PascalCase like 'VoucherDate', 'Date', 'SakhanId'), NOT the camelCase dataIndex — do not lowercase or remap it when seeding sortColumn; it must be passed through verbatim to match the stored proc @SortColumn (sp_VoucherReport.cs L160). For header-click sorting where the displayed column's dataIndex differs from the SQL column, you MUST set a per-column sortKey in the config, otherwise the proc will receive an unknown column name; the proc decides what an unknown/empty @SortColumn does, so verify a few reports rather than assuming all columns are sortable — consider only marking columns sortable where the backend supports them, or default sortable:false and opt columns in. The backend takes a single filterColumn/filterQuery pair, so the per-column search must be single-active (selecting a new column replaces the prior filter) — do not try to send multiple column filters. If unsure whether every report's proc validates @SortColumn safely, gate the feature by leaving search off (searchable defaults undefined/false) and rolling sort out report-by-report. Rollback is trivial: revert BasicTable.tsx and reportTypes.ts; no data migration involved.

approach: Stop exposing credentials on the User grid by removing 'password' from the table's displayData (this also kills its leak into the client-side Excel export), then harden the backend so GET /api/User never serializes Password — without breaking the login path that still needs Password during deserialization.
steps: Frontend (primary, trivial): In /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx line 29, remove 'password' from the displayData array so it reads displayData={['name', 'permission', 'isActive', 'id']}. This removes the on-screen column AND, because UserList passes no onExcel prop, prevents exportClientTableToExcel (BasicTable.tsx:236-244, XLSX.utils.table_to_book on the visible DOM) from including passwords in the .xlsx.

Backend (defense-in-depth — the field is still in the JSON payload even if hidden in the grid): the generic GET in /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs (Get action, lines 27-47) serializes the full User entity, and Backend/Model/User.cs exposes Password ([Required] string, plaintext). Do NOT add a bare [JsonIgnore] to User.Password: AuthController.Login (Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:25) binds a User from the request body and JWTManagerService.Authenticate compares x.Password == users.Password (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32), so the default [JsonIgnore(Condition=Always)] would stop the login password from deserializing and break auth.

Preferred backend fix: stop returning the raw User entity from the list endpoint. Override Get in UserController (Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs) to project to a password-free shape, or change the User screen to a dedicated UsersController endpoint that selects new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission } (the frontend grid only uses name, permission, isActive, id). Note: isActive is not a property on API.Model.User (User.cs has only Id, Name, Password, Permission), so the grid already shows N/A for it — keep that behavior or drop the column.

Minimal alternative backend fix (if you must keep the shared User model on the response): annotate Password with System.Text.Json so it is write-only — accepted on deserialize (login) but never serialized (responses). Add `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` to Backend/Model/User.cs (this attribute namespace is already used in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:7) and decorate Password with [JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)] ONLY if you also ensure the entity's Password is reset/cleared before returning (WhenWritingDefault hides empty strings, not populated ones); since DB rows have non-empty passwords this will still serialize them — so this alternative is NOT sufficient on its own and the DTO projection in the previous step is the correct fix. Prefer the DTO.

Per verifier note (out of original scope but flagged High-aggravating): passwords are stored in plaintext (User.cs Password set to "" in ctor, no hashing; Authenticate does a plaintext equality compare at JWTManagerService.cs:32). Open a follow-up to hash credentials at rest (e.g. ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher<User> or BCrypt) and change Authenticate to verify a hash instead of x.Password == users.Password. This is a separate migration, not part of this finding's quick fix.

Per verifier note: BaseAPIController is decorated [AllowAnonymous] at the class level (BaseAPIController.cs:15) while every action has [Authorize] (the action attribute wins, so the list endpoint is gated). Remove the misleading class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController so the auth posture isn't fragile/ambiguous; all CRUD actions already carry [Authorize].
verification: Frontend: run the User list page; confirm the grid shows only Name / Permission / Active / Id and no Password column. Click the Excel button and open the downloaded .xlsx — it must not contain a password column.
Backend: with a valid JWT call GET /api/User?pageIndex=0&pageSize=10 and assert the JSON 'data' items have no 'password' property (e.g. curl ... | grep -i password returns nothing).

Regression: confirm login still works — POST /api/Auth/Login with body {"name":"<user>","password":"<pw>"} must still return a token (proves Password still deserializes on the request side after the change).
Auth gate: call GET /api/User with no Authorization header and confirm 401 (action-level [Authorize] still enforced after removing class-level [AllowAnonymous]).
Build: dotnet build the Backend solution and run the existing Backend.Tests to ensure the new UserController.Get override and UserListDto compile and don't break report tests.
pitfalls: Do NOT slap a default [JsonIgnore] on User.Password — AuthController.Login binds Password from the request body and JWTManagerService.Authenticate (line 32) compares it, so a default-condition JsonIgnore breaks login. Use a read DTO/projection instead.; The frontend grid references item.isActive in transformUserData, but API.Model.User has no IsActive property (only Id, Name, Password, Permission) — it already renders N/A. The DTO above intentionally omits it; if you want to keep the column, leave the 'isActive' key in displayData (harmless N/A) but don't fabricate the field on the backend.; Using `public new` to shadow the base Get: ensure the route still resolves to UserController's action and not the base. Confirm with the GET verification above; if ambiguity arises, give the override a distinct name and add [HttpGet] without changing the route, or remove the inherited Get from the User route surface.; This change only stops display/export and response serialization. Plaintext-at-rest passwords remain a separate High-priority issue (verifier note) — schedule the hashing migration separately; it requires a data migration and a change to Authenticate's comparison logic.; Rollback is low-risk: reverting the one-line displayData change and the UserController override restores prior behavior; no DB migration is involved in this fix.
estimatedTime: 30 min (frontend + DTO backend fix); hashing follow-up is a separate ~1 day task

approach: The strategic fix is to move the session token to an httpOnly+Secure+SameSite cookie so JavaScript (and thus any XSS) cannot read it; that requires a coordinated backend change. The immediate, low-risk hardening that lands now: guard the axios request interceptor so it only attaches `Authorization` when a real token exists (stop sending `Bearer null`), and add a strict Content-Security-Policy to shrink the XSS surface that makes localStorage exfiltration possible. Server must remain the source of truth for authorization since `isAuthenticated`/`permission` are derived purely from client-editable localStorage.
steps: IMMEDIATE FIX (interceptor guard) — Edit Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts in the request interceptor (currently lines 9-11). Replace the unconditional `config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;` with a guarded version that reads the token, and only sets the header when the token is a non-empty string. This stops the `Bearer null` / `Bearer undefined` headers being sent on unauthenticated requests (e.g. the POST to 'Auth' login itself).
Keep the existing baseUrl rewrite (`config.url = envConfig.baseUrl + config.url`) and the `else { return Promise.reject('') }` branch unchanged — only the Authorization assignment changes.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH (CSP) — Add a Content-Security-Policy. Since this is a Vite SPA served as static files, set the policy at the web server / reverse proxy that fronts Frontend (preferred, because it can set it as a real HTTP response header and also set frame-ancestors). If no proxy is editable, add a `<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy">` tag in Frontend/index.html <head> as a fallback (note: meta CSP cannot express frame-ancestors). Start in Report-Only mode to find violations, then enforce. The policy must allow the app's real origins: fonts.googleapis.com / fonts.gstatic.com (already used in index.html), the API baseUrl from src/config, plus the MQTT (wss) and Firebase endpoints this app uses — inventory them via grep before enforcing so you don't break MQTT/Firebase.

AUTHORIZATION HARDENING (backend, audit-driven, not a code change in this file) — Confirm every protected ASP.NET Core endpoint enforces `[Authorize]` (and role/policy checks) server-side rather than trusting the client. The client `permission` value in localStorage is user-editable and is only a UI hint; ProtectedRoute.tsx gates on `auth?.isAuthenticated` which is just `!!user` derived from localStorage, so it provides no real security. Add a follow-up ticket to audit controller authorization attributes.

STRATEGIC (token storage migration — separate larger ticket) — Change the /Auth endpoint to issue the JWT as an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict (or Lax) cookie instead of (or in addition to) returning it in the JSON body. Then in AuthContext.tsx stop calling `localStorage.setItem('token', ...)` and in AxiosInstance.ts set `axios.create({ withCredentials: true })` so the cookie is sent automatically and drop the manual Authorization header entirely. Keep a non-sensitive `userid`/`permission` in memory or localStorage only as a UI hint. This requires CORS `AllowCredentials()` + an explicit allowed-origin (not `*`) on the backend and CSRF protection (SameSite plus an anti-forgery token) — scope it as its own PR; do NOT bundle it with the interceptor guard.

Do NOT add 401 key-clearing — it already exists. The response interceptor in Frontend/src/layouts/app/App.tsx:134-151 calls `auth?.logout()` on HTTP 401, and logout() in AuthContext.tsx:67-73 already removes 'token', 'userid', and 'permission'. Leave it as is.
verification: Build the frontend (`cd Frontend && npm run build`) — TypeScript must compile cleanly after the interceptor edit.

Open the app logged OUT (no 'token' in localStorage). In DevTools > Network, trigger the login POST to 'Auth' and confirm the request has NO `Authorization` header (previously it sent `Authorization: Bearer null`).
Log in, then make any report request and confirm `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` is present and correct — i.e. authenticated traffic is unchanged.
Clear localStorage while logged in (simulate expiry) and make a request: confirm no Authorization header is attached and the app still functions (no `Bearer null`).
Confirm the existing 401 flow still works: force a 401 from the backend and verify the App.tsx (layouts/app) response interceptor logs out and removes 'token'/'userid'/'permission' from localStorage.

After adding the CSP in Report-Only mode, exercise MQTT, Firebase, fonts, and API calls; check the console/`report-uri` for CSP violations and widen the policy only as needed before switching to enforcing mode.

For the cookie migration (when done): confirm the Set-Cookie on /Auth has HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite, that `document.cookie` in DevTools does NOT show the token, and that authenticated requests succeed with `withCredentials: true`.
pitfalls: The interceptor guard is safe and self-contained; do it first and ship it independently. The httpOnly-cookie migration is a breaking, cross-cutting change (backend cookie issuance + CORS AllowCredentials with a fixed origin + CSRF protection) — keep it in a separate PR to avoid coupling a risky migration to a one-line hardening.; The localStorage key is 'userid' (lowercase) and is used consistently in AuthContext.tsx read (line 23), write (line 51), and logout (line 69) — do not 'fix' it to 'userId'; that would silently break session restore.; Do NOT re-add 401 key clearing — App.tsx (layouts/app) already does it via auth.logout(); adding a duplicate could double-fire logout/messages.; CSP risk: this app loads Google Fonts and uses MQTT (wss) + Firebase. A too-strict connect-src/font-src/style-src will silently break those features. Inventory the real origins (grep src/config and the MQTT/Firebase setup) and roll out CSP in Report-Only first. Meta-tag CSP cannot set frame-ancestors or use a nonce easily — prefer an HTTP header at the proxy if clickjacking protection is needed.; Until the token leaves localStorage, the core XSS-exfiltration risk remains only mitigated, not eliminated — make sure the cookie-migration ticket is actually scheduled, not dropped after the quick interceptor fix.
estimatedTime: Interceptor guard: ~30 min (incl. verification). CSP report-only rollout: ~half a day to inventory origins and tune. Full httpOnly-cookie migration: ~1-2 days (separate PR, needs backend + CSRF work).

approach: Rewrite Frontend/dockerfile as a true multi-stage build: a Node build stage that runs a full `npm ci` (so devDependencies vite/typescript/plugin-react-swc are present) and copies Vite's real `dist/` output, plus an nginx stage that serves it with an SPA-routing nginx.conf. Pin Node explicitly for reproducibility.
steps: Open `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend/dockerfile`. Note the spaces in the path and the lowercase filename `dockerfile` (referenced verbatim by `Frontend/docker-compose.production.yml:5`).

Replace line 2 `FROM node:lts-alpine` with a pinned, named build stage: `FROM node:20-alpine AS build`. Pin a concrete major (20) rather than `lts` so the image is reproducible; React 19 / Vite 6 require Node >=18, so 20 is safe.

Line 11: change `RUN npm ci --only=production` to `RUN npm ci`. This is the core bug — `--only=production` omits devDependencies, but `typescript`, `vite`, and `@vitejs/plugin-react-swc` are all in devDependencies and are required by the `build` script `tsc && vite build` (see Frontend/package.json).

Keep `COPY . .` (line 14) and `RUN npm run build` (line 17). The build now succeeds because the toolchain is installed. Vite writes to `dist/` (default — `vite.config.ts` sets no `build.outDir`, and `Frontend/dist` exists while `Frontend/build` does not).
Line 20-21: in the nginx stage, fix the copy source from `/app/build/` to `/app/dist/` and reference the named stage: `COPY --from=build /app/dist/ /usr/share/nginx/html`.

Create `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend/nginx.conf` with a `try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;` directive so client-side react-router-dom routes (deep links/refresh) resolve to index.html instead of 404. Then add `COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf` in the nginx stage.

Add `dist/` to `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend/.dockerignore` (it currently contains only `node_modules/` with no trailing newline) to keep stale local build output out of the build context; harmless functionally but speeds the build.
Optional hardening: pin the nginx base too, e.g. `FROM nginx:1.27-alpine`, for the same reproducibility reason.
verification: From `Frontend/`: `docker build -f dockerfile -t tradenet-frontend .` completes without the previous `tsc: not found` / `vite: not found` errors and without a 'COPY failed: /app/build: no such file or directory' error.
`docker run --rm -d -p 3000:80 tradenet-frontend` then `curl -s http://localhost:3000/ | grep -i '<div id="root"'` returns the built index.html (non-empty served content).
Deep-link / SPA check: `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/some/react-route` returns 200 (served index.html via try_files), not 404.
Compose path: `docker compose -f docker-compose.production.yml up -d --build` brings up the react-app service on host port 3000 and the site loads in a browser.
`docker run --rm tradenet-frontend ls /usr/share/nginx/html` shows index.html and the hashed assets/ directory.
pitfalls: Build context grows with `COPY . .` — ensure `.dockerignore` excludes `node_modules/` (it does) and add `dist/` so the local build artifact isn't shipped into the build stage.; Do not keep `npm ci --only=production` 'for image size' — the devDependencies only live in the build stage and never reach the final nginx image, so the slim runtime is preserved automatically by multi-stage.; If `npm run build` fails on real TypeScript errors after this fix (the `tsc` step now actually runs and may surface pre-existing type errors that were never caught because the build never ran in-container), those are genuine type errors to resolve — they are not a regression from this change.; The filename is lowercase `dockerfile`; `docker-compose.production.yml` and `docker-compose.yml(.dev)` reference it explicitly. Do not rename it to `Dockerfile` without updating those compose `dockerfile:` keys (case matters on Linux).; Rollback: revert the single `Frontend/dockerfile` (and remove `Frontend/nginx.conf`); the active deploy path is `deploy.ps1` robocopying `Frontend/dist`, so reverting causes no production outage.

approach: Upgrade the frontend's axios dependency from the vulnerable 1.9.0 to the latest 1.x line (currently 1.17.0), re-pin the caret range in Frontend/package.json, and regenerate the lockfile so a fresh install no longer resolves below the patched line. This is a non-breaking minor upgrade within the 1.x series and also clears the transitive form-data 4.0.2 Critical.
steps: Work in the Frontend directory: all commands below run from "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" (quote the path because of the spaces).

Edit Frontend/package.json line 26: change the dependency entry from "axios": "^1.9.0" to "axios": "^1.17.0" (the current latest 1.x; do NOT pin to ^1.16.0 as the audit recommendation text suggested — the verifier notes the NO_PROXY incomplete-fix advisory GHSA-pjwm-pj3p-43mv still affects 1.15.x, so target the latest 1.x).

Install/upgrade and regenerate the lockfile by running: npm install axios@^1.17.0  (this rewrites the node_modules/axios entry at package-lock.json:6563 from 1.9.0 to 1.17.0 and bumps the transitive node_modules/form-data entry at package-lock.json:8872 from 4.0.2 to a patched 4.0.4+).
Run npm audit fix to pull any remaining transitive patches, then re-run npm audit to confirm the axios and form-data advisories are gone.

Confirm the bundle still builds: npm run build (script is "tsc && vite build"). No source changes are needed — src/services/AxiosInstance.ts only uses axios.create() + a request interceptor (Authorization header from localStorage and baseUrl prefixing), which is unchanged across the 1.x line.
Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together so the pinned version and the resolved lockfile stay in sync.
patch:
// Frontend/package.json  (line 26)  BEFORE -> AFTER
  "dependencies": {
    ...
-   "axios": "^1.9.0",
+   "axios": "^1.17.0",
    ...
  }

# Then regenerate node_modules + lockfile:
#   npm install axios@^1.17.0
#   npm audit fix
#   npm audit          # expect 0 axios / form-data advisories
#   npm run build      # tsc && vite build still passes

# Frontend/package-lock.json effect (node_modules/axios ~line 6563):
-      "version": "1.9.0",
+      "version": "1.17.0",
# and node_modules/form-data (~line 8872): 4.0.2 -> 4.0.4 (patched)
verification: Run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --omit=dev`) in Frontend and confirm no High/Critical advisories remain for axios or form-data.
Run `npm ls axios` and confirm the resolved version is 1.17.0 (>=1.16.0), and `npm ls form-data` shows >=4.0.4.
Grep the lockfile: `grep -A1 '"node_modules/axios"' Frontend/package-lock.json` should show "version": "1.17.0".
Run `npm run build` and confirm tsc + vite build succeed with no type or runtime errors against the new axios.

Smoke-test the running SPA: load a report page (e.g. via src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx) and confirm authenticated API calls through src/services/AxiosInstance.ts still send the Bearer token and resolve normally.
pitfalls: Always edit package.json and regenerate package-lock.json in the same change — bumping only package.json leaves the vulnerable 1.9.0 pinned in the lockfile, so CI/clean installs (`npm ci`) would still install the vulnerable version. Do not hand-edit version strings in package-lock.json; let `npm install` rewrite it so integrity hashes and the transitive form-data bump are correct. The verifier confirmed the most alarming items (NO_PROXY/proxy SSRF, Proxy-Authorization redirect leak, maxContentLength/maxBodyLength stream bypass, formDataToStream CRLF) are Node HTTP-adapter-only and NOT exploitable in this browser Vite/React bundle — so this is routine dependency hygiene, not a live-exploit emergency; the genuinely applicable in-browser residual risks it closes are the prototype-pollution gadgets and DoS/ReDoS items. Rollback is trivial: revert the two files and run `npm ci`. The 1.x upgrade is non-breaking, so no source migration in AxiosInstance.ts or any consuming page is required.

approach: Move CI to the repo root and add automated supply-chain scanning: a root .github/dependabot.yml covering both the npm (Frontend) and nuget (Backend/Backend.Tests) ecosystems, plus a CI workflow that gates on `npm audit` and `dotnet list package --vulnerable`. Modernize the existing Frontend workflows by replacing `npm install --force` with `npm ci` against the committed lockfile and bumping the Node matrix off EOL 16/18 to supported LTS (20.x/22.x).
steps: Create the repo-root directory `.github/workflows/` (currently the only `.github` is under `Frontend/`; the backend has no CI at all). All new files go at the absolute repo root: `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.github/`.

Add `/.github/dependabot.yml` with three update blocks: `package-ecosystem: npm` with `directory: "/Frontend"` (where `Frontend/package-lock.json` lives), `package-ecosystem: nuget` with `directory: "/Backend"` (API.csproj) and a second nuget block for `directory: "/Backend.Tests"` (Backend.Tests.csproj) — or a single nuget block at `directory: "/"` since `tradenet-admin-report.sln` is at root and Dependabot's nuget resolver follows the solution. Use the per-project form (shown in patch) to be explicit. Set `schedule.interval: weekly` and `open-pull-requests-limit: 10` on each.

Add a root CI workflow `/.github/workflows/security.yml` that runs on pull_request and push to main with two jobs. Job `frontend-audit`: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4` with `node-version: 20.x` and `cache: npm` / `cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json`, then `npm ci` and `npm audit --audit-level=high` run with `working-directory: Frontend`. Job `backend-audit`: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` with `dotnet-version: 8.0.x`, `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, then fail-on-vulnerable using `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` piped through a grep gate (see patch — `dotnet list` exits 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so you must grep the output and fail explicitly).

Fix the existing `/Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml`: change the matrix `node-version` from `['16.x','18.x']` to `['20.x','22.x']`, bump `actions/checkout@v3`->`@v4` and `actions/setup-node@v3`->`@v4`, replace `run: npm install` with `run: npm ci` (deterministic install against the lockfile), and DELETE the `Test` step (`run: npm test`) — there is no `test` script in `Frontend/package.json` (scripts are dev/build/lint/preview/changeset/storybook/build-storybook/prettier:write), so that step would always error. Keep `npm run build`.

Fix `/Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml`: replace `run: npm install --force` with `run: npm ci` so the release build installs the exact audited lockfile tree instead of bypassing peer-dependency resolution. Bump `actions/checkout@v3`->`@v4` and `actions/setup-node@v3`->`@v4` (keep `node-version: 20.18.0`). Leave the `changesets/action@v1` step intact — changesets is genuinely configured (`Frontend/.changeset/config.json` exists), this is not pure template leftover.
Optionally pin the supported runtime by adding `"engines": { "node": ">=20" }` to `Frontend/package.json` so local installs also warn on EOL Node.

Commit all four files (new `.github/dependabot.yml`, new `.github/workflows/security.yml`, edited `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml`, edited `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml`). Push on a branch and open a PR so the new `security.yml` runs on its own PR event and you can confirm the gates execute.
patch:
# 1) NEW FILE: /.github/dependabot.yml
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "npm"
    directory: "/Frontend"            # Frontend/package-lock.json
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/Backend"             # Backend/API.csproj (net8.0)
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/Backend.Tests"       # Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10

---
# 2) NEW FILE: /.github/workflows/security.yml
name: Dependency Security
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
jobs:
  frontend-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: Frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20.x
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm audit --audit-level=high
  backend-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      - run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      # dotnet list exits 0 even with vulns -> grep + fail explicitly
      - name: Scan for vulnerable packages
        run: |
          dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive 2>&1 | tee audit.log
          if grep -q -E "Critical|High" audit.log; then
            echo "::error::High/Critical vulnerable NuGet packages found"; exit 1
          fi

---
# 3) EDIT: /Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml
   strategy:
     matrix:
       node-version:
-        - '16.x'
-        - '18.x' # whichever node versions you support
+        - '20.x'
+        - '22.x'
   steps:
-    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
+    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
     - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }}
-      uses: actions/setup-node@v3
+      uses: actions/setup-node@v4
       with:
         node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
-    - name: Clean install dependencies
-      run: npm install
+    - name: Clean install dependencies
+      run: npm ci
     - name: Build application
       run: npm run build
-    - name: Test
-      run: npm test        # DELETE: no "test" script exists in Frontend/package.json

---
# 4) EDIT: /Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml
-      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
-      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
         with:
           node-version: 20.18.0
-      - run: npm install --force
+      - run: npm ci
verification: Open a PR with these files and confirm the new `Dependency Security` workflow appears in the PR Checks tab and runs both `frontend-audit` and `backend-audit` jobs (the existing `test.yml` runs on `branches-ignore: [main, master]`, so it also fires on a feature-branch PR).

Run the frontend gate locally: `cd Frontend && npm ci && npm audit --audit-level=high` — exit code 0 means no high/critical advisories; nonzero reproduces the CI failure (the 32 advisories from the companion finding should now be visible).

Run the backend gate locally from repo root: `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln && dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` and confirm the output lists transitive packages and that High/Critical entries cause the workflow step to exit 1.

Confirm `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml` no longer contains `npm install --force` (`grep -R "install --force" Frontend/.github` returns nothing) and that `test.yml` no longer references `16.x`/`18.x` or `npm test`.
After merge, verify Dependabot is active: GitHub repo > Insights > Dependency graph > Dependabot tab should list the npm + nuget update configs and begin opening update PRs on the weekly schedule.
pitfalls: Enabling `npm audit --audit-level=high` and the dotnet vulnerable-package grep will turn existing (currently invisible) advisories into red CI on the very first run — that is the intended behavior, but coordinate so it doesn't block an unrelated in-flight PR; if you need a soft rollout, temporarily append `|| true` to the `npm audit`/grep step (or use `continue-on-error: true`) and remove it once the companion vulnerable-dependency finding is remediated. `npm ci` requires `Frontend/package-lock.json` to be in sync with `package.json`; if a contributor hand-edited package.json without updating the lock, `npm ci` fails where `npm install` would have silently rewritten the lock — run `npm install` once locally to resync and commit the lockfile before merging. `dotnet list package --vulnerable` returns exit code 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so the explicit grep-and-exit-1 in the patch is load-bearing — do not replace it with a bare `dotnet list`. Dependabot nuget on a directory containing only a test csproj is harmless but will open separate PRs; if you prefer fewer PRs, collapse the two nuget blocks into one at `directory: \"/\"` (it resolves via the root .sln). Rollback is trivial: delete `.github/` and revert the two Frontend workflow edits.
estimatedTime: 1-2 hours (plus triage time for whatever advisories the new gates surface)

approach: Lock down `UploadController` by requiring authentication and never trusting the client-supplied `filename`: generate a server-side GUID name, write under a fixed resolved root validated to prevent traversal, and fix the validation loop so checks apply to the file actually being saved. Return the server-generated name so the frontend can reference the stored asset.
steps: In `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs`, add a class-level `[Authorize]` attribute on `UploadController` (line 23), matching the convention used in `Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs:19`. The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` import is already present (line 12), and there is no global fallback authz policy in `Program.cs`, so this attribute is what actually closes the anonymous hole.

Rewrite the body of `Postupload` (lines 49-92) so it no longer iterates `httpRequest.Form.Files` while always re-reading index 0. Take a single file via `httpRequest.Form.Files.GetFile("file")` or `httpRequest.Form.Files.FirstOrDefault()` into a local `postedFile`, and run the extension/size validation against that exact `postedFile` before saving (the current loop validates `Form.Files[0]` but passes the loop variable `file` to `SaveAsWebpAsync`).

Stop using the caller-supplied `filename` for the path. Generate `var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + ".webp";`. Build the destination root from the injected `_hostingEnvironment`: `var root = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.WebRootPath ?? Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot"), "Image");` (kept under wwwroot because these chat images are intentionally served back via `app.UseStaticFiles()` in `Program.cs:169`). Compute `var filePath = Path.Combine(root, safeName);`.

Add a defense-in-depth canonical-path guard before writing: resolve `Path.GetFullPath(filePath)` and `Path.GetFullPath(root + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar)`, and if the resolved file path does not start (OrdinalIgnoreCase) with the resolved root, return `BadRequest`. This makes traversal impossible even if `safeName` generation is ever changed.

Validate file extension/size on `postedFile` (not the trusting `filename`). Keep `MaxContentLength = 1024 * 500` but note the message text says 1 MB while the limit is 500 KB — align them (recommend keeping 500 KB and fixing the message to 'up to 500 KB'). Tighten `AllowedFileExtensions` to image types actually accepted by the chat upload (`accept="image/*"`): `.webp,.jpg,.jpeg,.png,.gif,.svg` — drop `.PDF/.pdf` unless PDFs are genuinely needed, since the save path always re-encodes to `.webp`.

In `SaveAsWebpAsync` (lines 104-147), replace the fragile `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"` in both branches (lines 113 and 136) with `Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp")`. Because `filePath` is now a GUID name, this is safe, but `Split(".")` mishandles any name containing dots and should not survive. Keep the existing `Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.GetDirectoryName(filePath)!)` (line 107) — it is now creating only the validated `Image` root.

Return the server-generated name to the caller instead of echoing the client filename. Replace `dict.Add("success", filename)` with returning `safeName` (e.g. `return Ok(new { success = safeName });` after refactoring the action to `Task<IActionResult>`), and update `Backend/Controllers/Chat`/frontend consumers if any read the echoed name. Note `HttpResponseMessage` return type never actually serializes `dict` to the body today; consider switching the action signature to `Task<IActionResult>` returning `Ok(...)`/`BadRequest(...)` so the success name is genuinely sent.

Update the frontend caller in `Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx:219-223`: it currently appends the raw client file name as `?filename=...`. Since the server now ignores `filename` and returns the stored name, remove the query param (or keep it ignored) and read the returned `safeName` from the response in the `onChange` success branch to reference the uploaded image.
verification: Anonymous request is rejected: `curl -k -i -X POST "https://localhost:5001/api/Upload/Postupload" -F "file=@test.png"` returns 401 Unauthorized (it previously accepted the upload).

Authenticated happy path: repeat the curl with `-H "Authorization: Bearer <valid-jwt>"` and a small PNG; expect 200 with body `{"success":"<32-hex>.webp"}`, and confirm a file named with a GUID (not the client name) appears in `Backend/wwwroot/Image/`.

Path traversal is neutralized: with a valid token, attempt `?filename=..%5C..%5Cappsettings.json` style values (now ignored) and confirm nothing is written outside `wwwroot/Image` and no file overwrites `appsettings.json` — verify via `git status` / file listing of the repo root and `wwwroot`.
Multi-dot name handling: upload a file named `my.photo.v2.png`; confirm exactly one GUID `.webp` file is created (regression test that `Path.ChangeExtension` replaced the broken `Split('.')[0]`).

Build/static-serve check: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds, and the returned GUID image is retrievable at `/Image/<guid>.webp` via `app.UseStaticFiles()`, confirming chat image display still works end-to-end through `Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx`.
pitfalls: Per the verifier note, the save path forces a `.webp` extension on every write, so this is NOT a config/executable overwrite vector — scope the risk to arbitrary `.webp` writes (asset overwrite, disk-fill DoS, publicly servable planted webp); do not claim you fixed arbitrary-file overwrite. Frontend coupling: `ChatBox.tsx` currently sends the file name in `?filename=` and relies on a 200/201 status; if you change the action signature to `IActionResult`/`Ok(...)` the success status still returns but the response body shape changes, so update any code that reads the echoed name to read the returned `safeName`. Changing `AllowedFileExtensions` to drop `.pdf` will reject PDFs that may have worked before (they were re-encoded as webp via SkiaSharp and likely failed decode anyway) — confirm no flow depends on PDF upload first. The existing message says "1 mb" while the limit is 500 KB; keep the limit and fix the text, or raise both together, but don't leave them inconsistent. Order: add `[Authorize]` and the path guard together — deploying the name/path change without `[Authorize]` still leaves the endpoint anonymous (writable webp + DoS).

approach: Turn on issuer/audience validation in the JWT bearer setup in Program.cs, and make the token-minting paths in JWTManagerService stamp matching iss/aud claims so every issued token still validates. Both must change together or every existing/new token will be rejected.
steps: In Backend/Program.cs (AddJwtBearer delegate, lines 81-90), change the TokenValidationParameters so ValidateIssuer = true and ValidateAudience = true. Leave ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["JWT:Issuer"] and ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["JWT:Audience"] as-is (they already point at the appsettings values "https://sakbran.github.io" and "sakbran.github.io").

In Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs, read the issuer and audience once from configuration inside each issuing method (or add private readonly fields set in the constructor from iconfiguration["JWT:Issuer"] and iconfiguration["JWT:Audience"]).
In JWTManagerService.Authenticate (tokenDescriptor at lines 42-51), add Issuer = iconfiguration["JWT:Issuer"] and Audience = iconfiguration["JWT:Audience"] to the SecurityTokenDescriptor initializer.

Apply the same two properties to the SecurityTokenDescriptor in AuthenticateTradenet2 (lines 72-81) and in RefreshToken (lines 112-120) — all three mint tokens with the same signing key, so all three must carry the iss/aud or they will fail validation after step 1.

Confirm the same JWT section keys exist in every environment's config. appsettings.json has JWT:Issuer/Audience; appsettings.Development.json has no JWT section, so it inherits from appsettings.json — verify the deployed/production config (and any environment-specific override) supplies JWT:Issuer and JWT:Audience, otherwise ValidIssuer/ValidAudience become null and validation throws.
Rebuild the Backend project and restart the API so the new validation parameters take effect.
verification: Log in to obtain a fresh token, then paste it into https://jwt.io (or decode locally) and confirm the payload now contains "iss": "https://sakbran.github.io" and "aud": "sakbran.github.io".
Call an [Authorize] endpoint with the new token -> expect 200. This proves tokens minted after the change still validate with issuer/audience checks on.

Forge a token signed with the same key but a different iss or aud (e.g. via a quick JwtSecurityTokenHandler test) and hit a protected endpoint -> expect 401 Unauthorized, whereas before the change it would have been accepted.
Verify any token issued BEFORE the change (no iss/aud claims) is now rejected with 401, confirming validation is active.
pitfalls: Ordering/coupling: do NOT enable validation in Program.cs without also adding Issuer/Audience to all THREE SecurityTokenDescriptors (Authenticate, AuthenticateTradenet2, RefreshToken) in the same deploy — otherwise newly issued tokens lack iss/aud and every request fails with 401. Breaking change for live sessions: all tokens minted before this change have no iss/aud and will be rejected, so deploying invalidates existing sessions (users must re-authenticate); roll out during a low-traffic window. Config dependency: if a deployed environment lacks JWT:Issuer/JWT:Audience, ValidIssuer/ValidAudience resolve to null and the middleware throws on validation — confirm those keys are present everywhere before enabling. This change does not address the related weak/exposed signing key ("This is my supper secret key for jwt") — that is a separate finding and remains the larger risk. Rollback: revert both files (set the two flags back to false) and restart.

approach: Move the existing `app.UseSwagger()` / `app.UseSwaggerUI(...)` block in Backend/Program.cs inside the already-present `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` guard so the interactive API explorer and OpenAPI spec are only served in development, not in production/UAT.
steps: Open Backend/Program.cs. Locate the request-pipeline section starting at line 99 (comment 'Configure the HTTP request pipeline.').

The dev/prod branch at lines 100-107 currently only wraps UseDeveloperExceptionPage (dev) and UseHsts (prod). The Swagger calls at lines 109-115 sit AFTER this block, unconditionally. Cut the entire block: `app.UseSwagger();` plus the full `app.UseSwaggerUI(options => { ... });` (lines 109-115).

Paste that Swagger block inside the existing `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` body, right after `app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();` (line 102). Keep the SwaggerUI options exactly as-is: SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1"), RoutePrefix = "swagger", DisplayRequestDuration().

Leave the `else { app.UseHsts(); }` branch unchanged, and leave the service registrations (AddEndpointsApiExplorer / AddSwaggerGen at lines 27-63) unchanged — those build the spec but do not expose any endpoint, so they are harmless to keep.
Confirm placement: the Swagger block must remain AFTER `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97) and BEFORE `app.UseHttpsRedirection();` (line 117). Inside the dev `if` is the correct spot.

Optional hardening if QA/ops need Swagger in UAT: instead of the IsDevelopment guard, gate on a config flag (e.g. read builder.Configuration.GetValue<bool>("Swagger:Enabled") into a local before app.Build(), then `if (enableSwagger)`), and keep it false in the production appsettings. Do not enable it in the public production environment.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend (no compile errors).

Production behavior — run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production (e.g. `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run`) then `curl -ik https://localhost:<port>/swagger/index.html` and `curl -ik https://localhost:<port>/swagger/v1/swagger.json` — both must return 404 (not 200).

Development behavior — run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development; `curl -k https://localhost:<port>/swagger/v1/swagger.json` returns 200 and the spec, and /swagger UI loads, confirming dev tooling still works.
Confirm normal report endpoints still respond in Production mode (e.g. a JWT-authenticated report request) so the move did not disturb the rest of the pipeline.
pitfalls: Environment is driven by ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT. Verify the actual UAT/production hosts set this to Production (or anything != Development); if a server is misconfigured to Development, Swagger will still appear. Check the deployment env var, not just the code.; UAT host reportuat.myanmartradenet.com is in the CORS allow-list — if QA relies on Swagger there, use the optional config-flag approach (Swagger:Enabled) rather than IsDevelopment, but keep the flag off in the truly public production appsettings.; This finding is recon-only. The verifier flagged a separate, likely higher-severity issue: unauthenticated ChatList/UploadController endpoints. Hiding Swagger does NOT secure those — track and fix them independently by adding [Authorize].; Rollback is trivial: move the two Swagger calls back outside the if/else block.

approach: Stop echoing raw exception text on the anonymous login endpoint: inject ILogger<AuthController>, log the full exception server-side with a correlation id, and return a generic 500 body. Leave UploadController alone — its dict is discarded and the 500 body is already empty (no leak, per verifier).
steps: Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs.
Add a logging import at the top: `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;` (the default WebApplication host already registers ILogger<T> in DI, so no Program.cs change is needed).
Add a private readonly field `private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;` alongside the existing `_jWTManager` field.

Update the constructor signature from `AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager)` to `AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager, ILogger<AuthController> logger)` and assign `this._logger = logger;` in the body.

In the `catch (Exception ex)` block on the Login method (currently line 39-42), replace `return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, ex.Message);` with code that generates a correlation id, logs the full exception with that id, and returns a generic message carrying only the correlation id.

Do NOT modify UploadController.Postupload — the verifier confirmed its `dict` (which holds ex.Message) is discarded and the returned `HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError)` has an empty body, so there is no message leak there.
Build the Backend project to confirm it compiles: `dotnet build` from the Backend folder.
verification: Build: from /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend run `dotnet build` — it should compile (ILogger<AuthController> resolves from the built-in DI container).

Force an exception on the anonymous login path (e.g. stop SQL Server or send a malformed body) and POST to /api/Auth. Confirm the 500 response body is the generic `{ "message": "An error occurred while processing your request.", "correlationId": "..." }` and contains NO SQL/EF text, table/column names, or connection details.
Confirm the full exception (with the same correlationId) appears in the server logs/console, so operators can still diagnose.
Confirm a successful login (valid credentials) still returns 200 with the token and an unknown user still returns 404 — the happy path and NotFound branch are unchanged.
pitfalls: Scope: only AuthController.Login leaks. Do not 'fix' UploadController.Postupload for a leak — its dict is discarded and the 500 body is empty (verifier-confirmed). (Its missing [Authorize]/[AllowAnonymous] plus no FallbackPolicy in Program.cs is a separate access-control concern, out of scope here.); The constructor signature change is a breaking DI change only if AuthController is manually instantiated anywhere; it is resolved by the MVC container, so adding the ILogger parameter is safe — no Program.cs registration needed.; Out of scope but worth flagging separately (verifier's tangential notes): JWTManagerService compares passwords in plaintext (x.Password == users.Password) and the JWT signing key falls back to an empty string when unconfigured. Do not bundle these into this finding.; Rollback is trivial: revert the catch block to the single StatusCode(... ex.Message) line and drop the logger field/parameter.

approach: Stop exposing the generic CRUD base over the User entity: override the write endpoints in UserController with explicit DTOs that never bind Password/Permission from the client, gate user-management behind [Authorize(Roles="Admin")], enforce ownership (a non-admin may only edit their own record), and hash passwords on write. Leave BaseAPIController generic for other entities but harden it where cheap.
