steps: Confirm the exposure: UserController.cs:15 declares `public class UserController : BaseAPIController<User>` with an empty body, so it inherits BaseAPIController.cs:67 `PutData(string id, T obj)` and :92 `PostData(T data)` which bind the whole `User` (Password + Permission from Model/User.cs:21-23) and write it via `Entry(obj).State = Modified` / `AddAsync`. Permission is the JWT role source (JWTManagerService.cs:47), so a low-priv user POSTing/PUTting Permission='Admin' is privilege escalation.

Add DTOs. Create `Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs` (namespace `API.Model`) with `UserCreateDto` and `UserUpdateDto` as shown in the patch. `UserUpdateDto` MUST NOT contain a `Permission` property; password change is an explicit optional `NewPassword`. Add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;`.

Rewrite `Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs`: stop inheriting `BaseAPIController<User>`. Change the declaration to `public class UserController : ControllerBase`, inject `ApplicationDbContext` (already imported via `using API.DBContext;`), and add `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` and `using System.Security.Claims;`. Implement only the vetted Get/Create/Update/Delete methods from the patch. This removes the inherited generic `PutData`/`PostData`/`PostDataList`/`DeleteData`/`Get` routes for the User entity entirely.

Gate privileged operations with `[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]` on Create and Delete. The role string must match the `Permission` value stored for admin rows in the `Users` table (there is no HasData seed in code — rows are inserted directly in the DB; verify the actual admin Permission value via the DB and use that exact string). Note `AuthenticateTradenet2` (JWTManagerService.cs:77) issues role `"User"`, so those tokens will correctly be denied admin operations.

Enforce ownership in `Update`: read the caller id from `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value` (the JWT sets ClaimTypes.Name = user Id at JWTManagerService.cs:46) and allow the write only if `User.IsInRole("Admin")` or `callerId == id`; otherwise return `Forbid()`. Bind only `Name` and optional `NewPassword`; never assign `Permission` from the request in this method.

Never write the client-supplied object. In Update, load the tracked entity with `_context.Users.FindAsync(id)`, mutate allowed fields on it, and save — do not do `Entry(clientObj).State = Modified`. In Create, construct a new `User` server-side and only copy whitelisted fields.

Hash passwords on write (ties into the related plaintext-password finding): use `BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(...)` for `Create` and for the optional password change in `Update`. Add the `BCrypt.Net-Next` NuGet package if not already referenced (`dotnet add Backend package BCrypt.Net-Next`). If the password-hashing remediation is handled in a separate finding, coordinate so both use the same hashing API and the login path (`JWTManagerService.Authenticate`, currently comparing `x.Password == users.Password` at line 32) is updated to verify the hash.

Harden the base class for the remaining entities (defense in depth): in `BaseAPIController.cs`, remove the class-level `[AllowAnonymous]` at line 15 (it is already overridden by method-level `[Authorize]`, so this is cleanup that prevents a future write endpoint from being added without auth). Consider also confirming no other security-sensitive entity (e.g. TokenModel) is exposed through a generic `BaseAPIController<T>` subclass.

If anything in the frontend calls `POST /api/User` or `PUT /api/User/{id}` with a full User object, update those callers to send the new DTO shape (search `Frontend/src` for `/api/User` / axios calls). The `CreatedAtAction("GetData", ...)` in the old base referenced a non-existent action name; the new code uses `nameof(Get)`.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` in `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend` succeeds; confirm UserController no longer derives from BaseAPIController<User>.

Escalation attempt blocked: log in as a non-admin (token role 'User'), then `curl -X PUT https://<host>/api/User/<ownId> -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"Name":"me","Permission":"Admin"}'` — the Permission field must be ignored (DTO has no such property) and the stored Permission must remain unchanged. Query the Users row to confirm.
Cross-user edit blocked: as a non-admin, `PUT /api/User/<someOtherUserId>` returns 403 Forbidden.
Admin-only gates: as a non-admin, `POST /api/User` and `DELETE /api/User/{id}` return 403; as an admin (Permission matching the Roles string) they succeed.

Password hashing: after Create or a NewPassword update, inspect the Users.Password column — it must be a BCrypt hash (`$2a$...`), not plaintext; and login via `POST /api/Auth` still returns a token (after the matching login-verification change).
Route removal: confirm `POST /api/User/PostDataList` and the generic `PutData` route no longer exist for User (404), proving the generic write surface is gone for this entity.
pitfalls: Inheritance trap: as long as UserController inherits BaseAPIController<User>, the inherited [HttpPut]/[HttpPost] routes remain registered even if you add new methods — you must drop the inheritance (or override+hide every write action) or the vulnerable endpoints stay live.; Role string must match DB data: admin Permission values live in the database, not in code seeds. If you gate with Roles="Admin" but the DB stores e.g. "admin" or "Administrator", admins get locked out. Confirm the exact stored value first; ClaimTypes.Role is fed verbatim from Permission (JWTManagerService.cs:47).; Password hashing must be rolled out together with login verification — if you start hashing on write but leave `Authenticate` doing `x.Password == users.Password` (JWTManagerService.cs:32), every newly-saved/changed user will be unable to log in. Existing plaintext rows also won't match a hash; plan a migration/rehash-on-next-login. Coordinate with the related plaintext-password finding.; Frontend coupling: any existing client code posting a full User object (including Permission) will break against the new DTOs; update callers in Frontend/src.; Token claim source: ownership check relies on ClaimTypes.Name holding the user Id (set at JWTManagerService.cs:46). The RefreshToken path (line 116) also sets Name=UserId, so that holds; just don't assume the username is in ClaimTypes.Name.; Rollback: revert UserController.cs and remove the DTO file; the base class [AllowAnonymous] removal is independent and safe to keep.
estimatedTime: 3-4 hours (1-2 hours if password hashing is tracked/handled by the separate plaintext-password finding)

approach: Lock down the chat endpoints by adding [Authorize] and deriving participant identity from the JWT (ClaimTypes.Name holds the user Id) instead of trusting client-supplied values: on GET, verify the caller is one of the two room participants; on POST, force the Room/Sender to the authenticated user. Apply the same [Authorize] to the sibling ChatList controller (and the empty Chat.cs) so the whole chat surface requires a token.
steps: Open Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `using System.Security.Claims;` to the existing usings block.

Add the `[Authorize]` attribute on the `ChatController` class (line 14, alongside the existing `[ApiController]` / `[Route("api/[controller]")]`). The app already wires JWT bearer in Backend/Program.cs (AddAuthentication/AddJwtBearer at line 68, UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization at lines 166-167), so [Authorize] takes effect immediately.

In the `Get([FromQuery] string room)` action: move the existing `string.IsNullOrEmpty(room)` BadRequest check to the top (before the Split calls), then after computing FirstValue/SecondValue read `var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;` and return `Forbid()` unless `callerId` equals FirstValue or SecondValue. Note: ClaimTypes.Name carries the user's Id (set in Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs line 46: `new Claim(ClaimTypes.Name, tempUser?.Id)`), and the room format is `{userId}-AND-{otherUserId}` (built in Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatList.tsx line 26).

In the `Post(ChatModel message)` action: read `var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;`, `Forbid()` if it is null/empty or if it is not one of the two `-AND-` halves of `message.Room`, then set `message.Sender = callerId;` (overriding any client-supplied Sender) before the existing `message.Timestamp = DateTime.Now;` and AddAsync/SaveChangesAsync. The ChatModel.Sender property is the field the frontend sends from auth.user.id (Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx lines 76/98) and renders 'isMine' from (line 144), so forcing it server-side is safe.

Apply the same fix to the siblings called out in verifierNotes: add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `[Authorize]` to the `ChatList` class in Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs (line 15) so GetChatList (which returns the full Users table) requires a token. Also add `[Authorize]` to the empty `Chat` class in Backend/Controllers/Chat.cs (line 11) to harden it by default for any future action.
Build the backend: from the repo root run `dotnet build Backend/Backend.csproj` (or the solution) to confirm the new usings/attributes compile.
verification: Without a token: `curl -i "https://<host>/api/Chat?room=1-AND-2"` returns 401 Unauthorized (was 200). Same for POST /api/Chat and GET /api/ChatList.
With a valid token for user A: GET /api/Chat?room=A-AND-B returns 200 with that room's last 10 messages; GET /api/Chat?room=X-AND-Y (where A is neither X nor Y) returns 403 Forbidden.

With user A's token, POST /api/Chat with body {"room":"A-AND-B","sender":"B","type":"text","content":"x"} succeeds but the persisted row's Sender is 'A' (server-forced), not 'B'. POST with room 'X-AND-Y' returns 403.

Smoke-test the UI: log in, open a chat thread (route /Test/Edit/{myId}-AND-{otherId}), confirm messages still load and send (axiosInstance already attaches `Bearer <token>` per Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts lines 10-11), and that own messages still render right-aligned via the `msg.sender === auth?.user?.id` check.
pitfalls: Forcing message.Sender = callerId server-side is the desired behavior, but verify nothing relies on a Sender different from the room participants. The frontend ChatBox does not set an Authorization header for the MQTT broker (broker.emqx.io) — only the REST calls go through axiosInstance, which already adds the bearer token, so adding [Authorize] will not break the existing UI; any non-UI/integration caller hitting these endpoints anonymously will start getting 401 and must send a token. Order: add the using directives before referencing ClaimTypes/Authorize or the build fails. ChatList's GetChatList currently caches and returns the entire Users table to anonymous callers — adding [Authorize] is the key win there. Rollback is simply removing the [Authorize] attributes and the participant checks.

approach: x
steps: Open Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs. Add `using System.Security.Claims;` to the using block (it is NOT currently imported; the file only has System, System.Linq, System.Threading.Tasks, API.* and Microsoft.* usings).

Add a small private helper to read the caller's username the same way the enqueue path records it. Right after the constructor add: `private string? CurrentUserName => User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;`. Note: the JWT (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:46,76,116) issues `new Claim(ClaimTypes.Name, ...)`, and every report controller enqueues with `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value` (e.g. ImportPermitVoucherReportController.cs:84), so this matches stored ExcelExportJob.RequestedByUserName exactly.

In Download(Guid id), after the existing null check (`if (job == null) return NotFound();`), add an ownership guard that returns NotFound (not Forbid) so the job's existence is not leaked: `if (!string.Equals(job.RequestedByUserName, CurrentUserName, StringComparison.Ordinal)) return NotFound();`. Keep this BEFORE the status/file-existence checks.

In Delete(Guid id), add the identical ownership guard immediately after the `if (job == null) return NotFound();` line, before the `_fileStore.Delete` / `_db.ExcelExportJobs.Remove` calls. This is the most important one (it removes the file and DB row).
In GetJob(Guid id) (the polling endpoint), add the same ownership guard after the null check so a user cannot poll/inspect another user's job metadata via its DTO.

Leave GetJobs() returning all jobs ordered by CreatedAtUtc (the `<summary>All exports ... (shared visibility)</summary>` is a deliberate shared-drive design per the verifier). Optionally, if the team decides exports must NOT be shared, also filter it with `.Where(j => j.RequestedByUserName == CurrentUserName)` — but document that decision; the audit only requires Download/Delete (and ideally GetJob) to be scoped.

Defensive note: if CurrentUserName resolves to null/empty (it should not for an [Authorize]-gated endpoint, but legacy tokens at JWTManagerService.cs:46 store an empty string when tempUser is null), the StringComparison.Ordinal check still fails closed for any job whose RequestedByUserName is a real username, which is the safe behavior.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from the Backend project root — confirm no compile error (the new `using System.Security.Claims;` resolves ClaimTypes).

Manual two-user test: log in as user A, enqueue an export from any report controller's POST .../Excel, note the returned job GUID. Log in as user B (different username), then `GET /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}/download` and `DELETE /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}` with B's bearer token — both must now return 404. With A's own token they still return the file / 204 NoContent respectively.
Confirm `GET /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}` (GetJob) returns 200 for A and 404 for B.
Confirm GetJobs (`GET /api/ExcelExport/jobs`) still lists all jobs (unchanged shared list) unless the team chose to also scope it.
Optional integration test: assert that Delete by a non-owner does not remove the row — query ExcelExportJobs after the 404 and verify the job still exists.
pitfalls: Return NotFound (not Forbid/403) for non-owned jobs so you don't reveal that a given GUID exists. Do NOT change how RequestedByUserName is stored — it is set from User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value at enqueue (ExcelExportJobService.cs:103 via the report controllers), so the check must read the same claim; using User.Identity.Name also works since the default handler maps ClaimTypes.Name to it, but FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name) is the exact match. Any export jobs already in TemplateDB created before this change keep their original RequestedByUserName, so they remain accessible only to their original requester — no data migration needed, but be aware reused/dedup'd "closed period" jobs (ExcelExportJobService.cs:69-88) are matched by FilterHash and may have been created by a different user, so a second user who triggers the same closed-period export will reuse the first user's job GUID and would now get a 404 on download; if that breaks the reuse UX, either also stamp the current user on reuse or relax Download to a role check instead of strict ownership. There is no role-based authorization anywhere in the backend, so do not attempt a role-bypass branch unless you first add a real admin role/policy.

approach: Add a short-lived (e.g. 60s) per-filter-hash IMemoryCache layer over the two re-run hot paths: (1) cache the grouped List<ReportAggregateResult> produced by AggregateInSqlAsync (the GROUP BY + Daily FX query that ~47 aggregate controllers re-run on every page turn / footer render), and (2) cache the detail-report total COUNT in sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync (BasicTable always sends includeTotalCount=true, so it re-runs per page). Reuse the existing AddMemoryCache() registration and the GetOrCreateAsync idiom already established in ReportLookupCache; the OFFSET/FETCH-in-SQL rewrite the finding mentions is skipped per the verifier (minor win, and the grouped set is already SQL-grouped not the detail fan-out).
steps: Add a cache-key helper for the aggregate request. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;` and a private static method that builds a stable string key from sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest + dimension + includeSakhan, e.g. BuildAggregateCacheKey(request, dimension, includeSakhan) returning $"ImportLicAgg:v1:{request.Type}:{request.FromDate:O}:{request.ToDate:O}:{request.PaThaKaTypeId}:{request.ExportImportSectionId}:{request.ExportImportMethodId}:{request.ExportImportIncotermId}:{request.SellerCountryId}:{request.CompanyRegistrationNo}:{request.SakhanId}:{(int)dimension}:{includeSakhan}". Include every field of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest (defined in sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:11-23) so two different filters never collide.

Thread IMemoryCache into GetAggregateRowsAsync (sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:165) and CreateAggregateResultAsync (:128). Add an `IMemoryCache cache` parameter and, inside, wrap the existing `await AggregateInSqlAsync(db, request, dimension, includeSakhan)` call in `cache.GetOrCreateAsync(BuildAggregateCacheKey(...), async entry => { entry.AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(60); return await AggregateInSqlAsync(db, request, dimension, includeSakhan); })` and fall back to an empty list if null — exactly the pattern in ReportLookupCache.GetAsync (ReportLookupCache.cs:111-125). This caches the GROUP BY result AND the Daily FX fill (AggregateInSqlAsync :402-405 runs ReportUsdConversionService.FillDailyUsdValuesAsync before returning, so the cached list already carries TotalUSDValue). Keep CreateAggregateExcelWorkbookAsync (:148) uncached since it streams the full set for export.

Update the ~47 aggregate controllers that call GetAggregateRowsAsync / CreateAggregateResultAsync to pass the cache. For ImportLicenceDailyReportNewLicenceReportController.cs: inject IMemoryCache via the constructor (currently TradeNetDbContext + IExcelExportJobService only, :27), add `private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;` and `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;`, then pass `_cache` to the GetAggregateRowsAsync call at :43-44 (and the WriteRowsAsync call at :95-96 may stay uncached or share it). IMemoryCache is already DI-registered (Program.cs:23 AddMemoryCache()), so no Program.cs change is needed. The Daily controller's footer at :53-58 keeps summing the SAME `groups` list it already holds, so the footer no longer triggers a second DB round-trip.

Cache the detail-report COUNT. In sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync (:25) add an `IMemoryCache cache` parameter (mirroring the Export twin sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync at sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:25-27 which already takes IMemoryCache). Replace the line `var totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount ? await CountRowsAsync(db, request) : (int?)null;` (:44-46) with a GetOrCreateAsync over a count-specific key (e.g. $"ImportLicCount:v1:{...same request fields...}") and the same 60s AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow, so only the FIRST page of a paging session pays the second full COUNT scan; subsequent page clicks within 60s reuse it. CountRowsAsync (:466) itself is unchanged.

Wire IMemoryCache into the two detail controllers that call CreatePagedResultAsync: ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs (constructor at :28, call at :48-49) and BorderImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs. Add `IMemoryCache cache` to each constructor, store `_cache`, add the `using`, and pass `_cache` into CreatePagedResultAsync. (StreamResolvedChunksAsync at :108 / the Excel path do not use the COUNT, so leave them.)
Do NOT change BasicTable.tsx:199 (includeTotalCount: true stays). The server-side count cache makes the repeated COUNT cheap without altering the frontend paging contract or the total-row display.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ must succeed after the signature changes (compiler flags every un-updated call site so none are missed).

Run the API, open the Import Licence Daily report over a wide date range, enable SQL logging (or SQL Server Profiler / EF Core `LogTo`). Click through pages 1->2->3 within ~60s: confirm the GROUP BY query and the ExchangeRate+Currency FX query execute ONCE (first page) and are NOT re-emitted on subsequent page clicks; the Total footer also fires no new query.
For a detail report (Import Licence Detail), page forward several times and confirm the CountRowsAsync COUNT query runs once per filter set within the TTL, not once per page.
Change a filter (e.g. date range) and confirm a fresh GROUP BY / COUNT runs (new cache key), proving stale results are not served for a different filter.
Confirm displayed totals (NoOfLicences, TotalValue, Total USD Value) and pagination total are unchanged vs. before the change.
pitfalls: "Cache key correctness: the key MUST include every filter field of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest and the dimension+includeSakhan flag, or two different reports/filters will collide and show wrong numbers in this sensitive gov system. Use ISO 'O' date formatting to avoid culture issues. Keep TTL short (~60s) so newly-approved licences appear quickly; the aggregate values are not real-time-critical but should not be cached for minutes. IMemoryCache is per-process — fine for a single instance, but if the API is ever scaled out, different instances will have independent short-lived caches (acceptable; results still consistent within a session). Ordering: change the static method signatures first, then fix all ~47 aggregate call sites and the 2 detail call sites (the compiler enumerates them) before running. Do not cache the Excel-export paths (CreateAggregateExcelWorkbookAsync, StreamResolvedChunksAsync) — they intentionally stream the full set. Rollback is trivial: revert the GetOrCreateAsync wrappers to the direct AggregateInSqlAsync/CountRowsAsync calls and drop the added parameters."

approach: Add a deterministic tiebreaker to the single shared ORDER BY in Rows() so OFFSET/FETCH paging is reproducible across all Types and across the Border UNION ALL. Carry a unique per-item key (the item.Id PK) plus a source discriminator into ImportLicenceDetailFastRow, then order by CreatedDate, then SourceOrdinal, then RowKey. This fixes the cross-page skip/dupe bug (shared with offset-without-stable-order-by) without changing the result set or column output.
steps: Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs.

In the private sealed class ImportLicenceDetailFastRow (starts line 802), add two new properties used only for ordering (not surfaced in ToResult): `public string RowKey { get; init; } = null!;` (the item primary key, which is unique per fan-out row) and `public int SourceOrdinal { get; init; }` (a branch discriminator: 0 for the PaThaKa/Oversea branch, 1 for the Border Individual Trading branch). These are NOT added to ToResult() so the response shape is unchanged.

In OverseaRows (projection starting line 575), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 0,` to the `select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow { ... }` initializer. item is the ImportLicenceItem (item.Id is the string PK).
In BorderPaThaKaRows (projection starting line 651), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 0,` (item here is BorderImportLicenceItem; item.Id is its string PK).

In BorderIndividualTradingRows (projection starting line 730), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 1,` so the two Border branches never collide on the tiebreaker even if a CreatedDate and an item.Id were ever shared.

Change the ORDER BY in Rows() (line 441) from `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);` to `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate).ThenBy(row => row.SourceOrdinal).ThenBy(row => row.RowKey);` so OFFSET/FETCH has a fully deterministic key. This is the single shared sort for Oversea and Border, so it also fixes the sibling offset-without-stable-order-by instability on the Oversea path.

Build the Backend project to confirm the EF query still translates (the added ORDER BY columns must be in the SELECT projection over a UNION ALL, which EF Core 9 supports because RowKey/SourceOrdinal are now real columns in both branches).

Do NOT change CountRowsAsync / CountBorderPaThaKaAsync / CountBorderIndividualTradingAsync — the two counts are an inherent property of the UNION (count = countA + countB) and the verifier confirmed severity is bounded by MaxPageSize=1000; reducing count cost is out of scope for this finding.
verification: dotnet build the Backend project (from /Users/.../Backend) to confirm the EF Core 9 query translates with ORDER BY over the UNION ALL.

Call the Import Licence Detail report endpoint that drives CreatePagedResultAsync with request.Type='Border' and a date range that returns more than 2 pages. Request page 0 then page 1 with the same PageSize; capture the LicenceNo+HSCode+RowKey set. Confirm no row appears on both pages and no row is missing across the page boundary (the previous behavior could skip/dupe rows whose CreatedDate ties).
Repeat the two-page check with request.Type='Oversea' to confirm the shared-ORDER BY fix also stabilizes that path.
Enable EF Core query logging (or capture via SQL Server Profiler) and confirm the generated SQL ends with ORDER BY [CreatedDate], [SourceOrdinal], [RowKey] before OFFSET ... FETCH NEXT.
Confirm the JSON response shape is unchanged (no RowKey/SourceOrdinal leak) since ToResult() was not modified.
pitfalls: RowKey must be a column present in BOTH Border branches of the Concat/UNION ALL, otherwise EF Core cannot order the combined query — that is why the property is added to all three projections (Oversea, BorderPaThaKa, BorderIndividualTrading), not just Border. SourceOrdinal is a constant per branch, so it sorts the two Border branches into contiguous reproducible blocks but does NOT reorder within a branch. Keep RowKey/SourceOrdinal out of ToResult() and out of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportResult so the API contract and front-end columns are untouched. This change is read-only and additive (no DB migration, no schema change); rollback is reverting the OrderBy chain to the single .OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate) and removing the two row fields. Note item.Id is a string PK, so the ThenBy(RowKey) is a string sort — that is fine for a tiebreaker (only determinism matters, not numeric ordering).

approach: placeholder
steps: LIVE FIX — Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.cs. Root cause: the private `Rows(...)` method (line 137) is declared `IEnumerable<WineImportationFastRow>`. Even though `DefaultRows` and `WineImportationDetailRows` return `IQueryable`, the upcast to `IEnumerable` makes `rows.Count()` (line 49) and `rows.Skip(...).Take(...)` (lines 52-54) bind to `System.Linq.Enumerable`, so EF Core materializes the ENTIRE filtered multi-join into memory before counting/paging. The Summary path is genuinely in-memory but returns at most 5 rows, so it is fine.

In `CreatePagedResultAsync`, branch the Summary path (small, in-memory) away from the detail path (must page in SQL). Replace the single `var rows = Rows(db, request);` block (lines 47-55) so that for Summary you keep the current in-memory `Count()/Skip()/Take().ToList()`, but for the detail paths you obtain an `IQueryable<WineImportationFastRow>` via a new `DetailQuery(db, request)` helper and run `await query.CountAsync()` and `await query.Skip(...).Take(...).ToListAsync()` (add `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` is already present at line 6).

Add a private `IQueryable<WineImportationFastRow> DetailQuery(TradeNetDbContext db, sp_WineImportationReportRequest request)` that returns the same three branches that are currently detail (not Summary): `request.ApplyType == Valid` -> `WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate > request.Date)`; `request.ApplyType == Invalid` -> `WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate < request.Date)`; else `DefaultRows(db, request)`. These three already return `IQueryable`, so no query body changes are needed — only the static type that flows to Count/Skip/Take.

Keep `private static IEnumerable<WineImportationFastRow> SummaryRows(...)` exactly as-is (it concats 5 `GroupBy` projections via `.AsEnumerable()`), and route only `request.Type == Summary` through it.

Update the Excel and streaming paths the same way so they page/stream against `IQueryable` for detail and the small in-memory enumerable for Summary: in `CreateExcelWorkbookAsync` (lines 98-100) and `StreamResolvedChunksAsync` (lines 119-129), call `DetailQuery(...)` and use `await ...ToListAsync()` / `.AsAsyncEnumerable()` for detail (matching `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateExcelWorkbookAsync` line 94 and `StreamResolvedChunksAsync` line 122), while Summary keeps the in-memory enumeration. Resolve the WineType CSV via `ReportLookupCache.ResolveCsv` in `ToResult` AFTER materialization — it already does this (line 424), so do not push it back into SQL.

DEAD-CODE DELETION — delete Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs entirely. Verified: no controller or other class references the type `sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report` (grep returned only its own declarations). It is the SOLE caller of `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.Query` (line 86 of that file).

DEAD-CODE DELETION — delete Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs entirely (this is the legacy non-Fast class containing the `.AsEnumerable().Concat(...).OrderBy(...).AsQueryable()` in-memory union at :221-226 and the correlated `string.Join(from country in db.Countries ... Contains ...)` at :289-296). Once the Daily class above is removed it has zero references — all live import-licence controllers use `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast`. Removing it also deletes the now-orphaned `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.Query` slow path.
Build the backend to confirm nothing else referenced the deleted symbols: `dotnet build` from the Backend project directory. A clean build proves both classes were dead.

OPTIONAL (verifier-confirmed minor) — Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ReExportReport.cs: the `Query(...)` result is paged in SQL by `ApiResult.CreateFastPageAsync` (OFFSET/FETCH), so it is NOT a memory issue — do NOT add AsNoTracking (no-op on DTO projections) and do NOT 'remove buffering' (there is none). The only valid sub-claim is OFFSET/FETCH without a stable ORDER BY. If desired, add a deterministic `orderby reExport.ReExportNo` (and for the detail/summary helpers) so page boundaries are stable; this is cosmetic for correctness, not a perf fix.
verification: Build: from Backend run `dotnet build` — must succeed after deleting sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs and sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs (proves they were dead) and after the Wine refactor.

SQL translation check: enable EF logging (or attach SQL Server Profiler / set Logging:LogLevel for Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command to Information) and POST to the Alcoholic Beverages Importation Detail report endpoint (AlcoholicBeveragesImportationDetailReportController Post, which calls sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync) with a wide FromDate/ToDate. Confirm the emitted SQL contains `OFFSET @__p ROWS FETCH NEXT @__p ROWS ONLY` and a separate `SELECT COUNT(*)`, and that a single page returns only ~PageSize rows from the DB (no full-set materialization).
Behavioral check: request page 2 with PageSize 10 and verify the same rows/totalCount as before the change (parity), and that the Summary report (Type=Summary) still returns its 5 aggregate rows.
Regression grep: `grep -rn "sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport\." Backend --include=*.cs` and `grep -rn "sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report" Backend` return no hits after deletion.
pitfalls: "Do NOT route the Summary path through IQueryable/ToListAsync as one query — SummaryRows is built from `.AsEnumerable().Concat(...)` of 5 separate GroupBy projections and is intentionally composed in memory; forcing it to a single IQueryable will not translate. Keep Summary on the in-memory branch (it is only ~5 rows). Order of deletion matters: remove sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs first (or together) — deleting sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs alone while the Daily class still references its .Query will break the build. The fast-page sentinel still relies on Take(pageSize + 1) when totalCount is null (CreateFastPageFromRows), so preserve the `+ (totalCount.HasValue ? 0 : 1)` term in both branches. ResolveCsv(WineTypeIds) must stay post-materialization in ToResult — do not move it back into the SQL projection. Per the verifier: do not add AsNoTracking to these DTO-projection queries (no-op) and do not attempt to 'de-buffer' sp_ReExportReport.Query — it already pages in SQL."

approach: Introduce one abstract generic base, ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest>, that owns the duplicated skeleton (the Excel action, IStreamingExcelReport plumbing, shared FromDate/ToDate validation, paging clamp, and Excel-enqueue) and require each report to supply only ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, a MapProcedureRequest projection, and the report-specific data-fetch/streaming. Migrate controllers incrementally (Amendment-style first, then the country-cache style), preserving each report's own paging/projection so behavior stays byte-for-byte identical.
steps: Create a new file Backend/Controllers/Report/ReportControllerBase.cs declaring `public abstract class ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest> : ControllerBase, IStreamingExcelReport where TRequest : ReportQueryRequest where TProcRequest : class`. Keep it in namespace Backend.Controllers.Report so it sits with the controllers and is covered by the same assembly scan.

In the base, add the abstract members each report must supply: `protected abstract string ReportKey { get; }`, `public abstract string ExcelWorksheetTitle { get; }`, `protected abstract TProcRequest MapProcedureRequest(TRequest request);` (the per-report projection + FormType/Type literal — this is the BorderExportLicence-vs-ExportPermit `FormType` and the ImportLicenceDetail `Type` null-coalesce / BySection hardcoded `"Oversea"` variation point), and `protected abstract Task FetchRowsAsync(TProcRequest procedureRequest, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken ct);` for the streaming body.

Move the identical validator into the base as `protected bool TryCreateReportRequest(TRequest? request, out TProcRequest? procedureRequest, out ActionResult? errorResult)` — copy verbatim the null check, FromDate/ToDate `== default` checks and the `ToDate < FromDate` check from ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:102-124, then call `procedureRequest = MapProcedureRequest(request)` in place of the inline `new sp_...Request { ... }` block. Because TRequest:ReportQueryRequest does not declare FromDate/ToDate (each concrete DTO redeclares them), add `protected abstract DateTime GetFromDate(TRequest r);` and `protected abstract DateTime GetToDate(TRequest r);` so the base can validate dates without reflection (cheapest, zero-migration option).

Implement IStreamingExcelReport plumbing once in the base: `public Type ExcelRequestType => typeof(TRequest);` and `[NonAction] public Task WriteRowsAsync(object request, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken ct) { TryCreateReportRequest((TRequest)request, out var pr, out _); return FetchRowsAsync(pr!, sink, chunkSize, ct); }` — this replaces the duplicated `[NonAction] WriteRowsAsync` shim + private overload pair in all 157 controllers.

Provide a concrete base Excel action so reports don't repeat it: `[HttpPost("Excel")] public async Task<IActionResult> Excel([FromBody] TRequest? request) { if (!TryCreateReportRequest(request, out _, out var err)) return err!; var result = await _excelExportJobs.EnqueueAsync(ReportKey, request!, GetToDate(request!), User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value); return Ok(result); }`. Inject TradeNetDbContext and IExcelExportJobService via a protected base ctor (`protected ReportControllerBase(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService jobs)`); concrete controllers that also need ICountryCache (e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReportController) add it to their own ctor and pass context/jobs to `base(...)`.

Add a shared paging helper `protected (int pageIndex, int pageSize, string? sortColumn, string? sortOrder) ResolvePaging(TRequest request)` that centralizes the DefaultPageSize=10 / MaxPageSize=1000 clamp and the SortColumn/SortOrder whitespace-to-null normalization (the block at ExportPermitAmendmentReportController.cs:48-54). Do NOT push the `sp_AmendReport.ExecuteAsync(...).ToResult()` projection or the country-cache `CreatePagedResultAsync` call into the base — those are report-family specific and stay in the concrete `[HttpPost] Post`.

Migrate the ExportPermit/BorderExport Amendment pair first as the proof-of-concept: each concrete controller shrinks to ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, GetFromDate/GetToDate, MapProcedureRequest (only the `FormType = "Export Permit"` vs `"Border Export Licence"` literal differs), a report-specific `[HttpPost] Post` using ResolvePaging + the existing ApiResult.CreatePageFromRows/CreateFastPageFromRows branch, and FetchRowsAsync wrapping the existing `sp_AmendReport.ExecuteQueryable(...).AsAsyncEnumerable().ChunkAsync(...)` loop. Confirm the diff between the two migrated controllers is now ONLY names + ReportKey + title + FormType.

Migrate the country-cache style (ImportLicenceDetailReportController) next: its FetchRowsAsync calls `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.StreamResolvedChunksAsync(_context, _countryCache, pr!, chunkSize, ct)` and its Post calls `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync(_context, _countryCache, pr!, request!)`. Keep MapProcedureRequest with the `Type = string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(request.Type) ? "Oversea" : request.Type` default; ImportLicenceBySectionReportController keeps its hardcoded `Type = "Oversea"` in its own MapProcedureRequest override. These per-report differences must remain in the overrides, not be flattened.

Roll out the remaining ~155 controllers in small batches grouped by data-fetch family (_Fast country-cache reports, sp_AmendReport reports, plain paged reports), rebuilding after each batch. This is the same chore tracked in the CLAUDE.md 'roll out the remaining ~155 report controllers' memory — reuse that batching cadence.

Verify auto-discovery still works without code changes: ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.RegisterReportHandlers (Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:36-39) scans `t is { IsClass: true, IsAbstract: false } && typeof(IStreamingExcelReport).IsAssignableFrom(t)`. The new generic ReportControllerBase<,> is abstract so it is excluded, and concrete controllers stay non-abstract and assignable. ControllerStreamingExcelReportJobHandler.cs:39 uses ActivatorUtilities.CreateInstance(context.Services, _controllerType) on the concrete type, which still resolves ctor deps (context, jobs, and optionally countryCache).
verification: Build the Backend project: run `dotnet build` from the Backend folder — it must compile with zero new errors after each migration batch.

Functional parity (response shape unchanged): POST api/BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReport and api/ExportPermitAmendmentReport with a valid FromDate/ToDate body before and after migration and diff the JSON — identical rows/paging/total-count. Repeat for api/ImportLicenceDetailReport (country-cache path) to confirm ConsignedCountry/CountryofOrigin still resolve.

Validation parity: POST each migrated endpoint with (a) empty body -> 400 'Request body is required.', (b) missing FromDate -> 400 'FromDate is required.', (c) ToDate < FromDate -> 400 'ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.'

Excel queue still discovers handlers: at startup verify one IExcelReportJobHandler is registered per migrated controller (log/breakpoint in ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.RegisterReportHandlers, or assert the registered handler count equals the IStreamingExcelReport controller count). Then POST api/<Report>/Excel and confirm an EnqueueResult (Queued/Ready/Processing) returns and the background worker produces the file.
Confirm the base class is excluded from scanning: assert `typeof(ReportControllerBase<,>).IsAbstract` is true so RegisterReportHandlers never instantiates it.
Diff sanity: after migrating the Amendment pair, `git diff` the two controllers — the only differences should be class/request names, ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, and the FormType literal.
pitfalls: Do this incrementally and rebuild per batch — it touches ~155 files; a single bad base signature breaks every controller at once. Preserve per-report variations the verifier flagged: ImportLicenceDetailReportController's `Type = IsNullOrWhiteSpace ? \"Oversea\" : request.Type` default vs ImportLicenceBySectionReportController's hardcoded `Type = \"Oversea\"`, and the country-cache paging path (sp_..._Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync / StreamResolvedChunksAsync taking _countryCache) vs the inline sp_AmendReport.ExecuteAsync + ToResult() path — keep these in the concrete overrides, do not flatten into the base. The base reads FromDate/ToDate, which are NOT on ReportQueryRequest (each DTO redeclares them); use the GetFromDate/GetToDate abstracts shown (zero migration), OR promote FromDate/ToDate onto ReportQueryRequest only if you also remove the shadowing declarations from all DTOs (riskier, do not mix). Keep the [HttpPost(\"Excel\")] and [NonAction] WriteRowsAsync on the BASE only — leaving stale copies on a concrete class double-registers the Excel route. ActivatorUtilities.CreateInstance (ControllerStreamingExcelReportJobHandler.cs:39) resolves the concrete ctor, so any controller adding ICountryCache must keep it in its own ctor signature and pass context/jobs to base(...). Migration is purely mechanical with no DB/schema change, so rollback is per-file `git revert`. CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumberController does not implement IStreamingExcelReport and is intentionally out of scope.
estimatedTime: 2-3 days (base class + Amendment proof-of-concept pair in ~half a day; the remaining ~155 controllers migrated in batches with per-batch build/verify make up the rest)

Then optionally triage the remaining unreferenced superseded-by-`_Fast` variants after a routing check.

approach: Stop the genuine client-facing message leak in AuthController, replace the bare swallowing catch in ApiResult.ApplyFilter with upfront type detection so it never silently masks errors, and add ILogger plus a single global IExceptionHandler/ProblemDetails so all ~167 controllers get consistent logging and generic error bodies instead of each reinventing try/catch.
steps: Fix the real leak in Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs. Inject ILogger<AuthController> via the constructor (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;`, add a `private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;` field, accept it as a second ctor parameter and assign it). In the catch block at lines 39-42, log the exception with context (the username from `data`) and return a generic Problem result WITHOUT ex.Message.

Replace the AuthController catch body with: `_logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed for user {UserName}", data?.UserName); return Problem(detail: "An unexpected error occurred while processing the login request.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError);`. Confirm the property name on the API.Model.User type used for the username; if it is not `UserName`, adjust — and never log the password field.

Fix the swallowed exception in Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs lines 94-100. The verifier confirmed ex.Message is NOT returned here (it is put into `dict` which is never sent), so this is not a client leak — but it is silently swallowed. Inject ILogger<UploadController> (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;`, add a field and ctor parameter alongside the existing IWebHostEnvironment) and replace the catch body with `_logger.LogError(ex, "Image upload failed for file {FileName}", filename);` before returning the existing `new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError)`. Remove the dead `dict.Add("error", res)` line that builds a response which is never returned.

Fix the bare catch in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs ApplyFilter (lines 235-248). ApiResult<T>.ApplyFilter is a `private static` method on a generic type, so injecting ILogger is awkward and unnecessary — instead remove the try/catch entirely and decide the operator upfront by inspecting the property type via reflection (the class already uses reflection in IsValidProperty). Only use `.Contains(@0)` when the target property is a string; otherwise use equality. This eliminates the silent fallback that could mask a genuinely broken filter as an equality match.

In ApplyFilter, replace the try/catch: `var prop = typeof(T).GetProperty(filterColumn, BindingFlags.IgnoreCase | BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance);` then `return prop?.PropertyType == typeof(string) ? source.Where(string.Format("{0}.Contains(@0)", filterColumn), filterQuery) : source.Where(string.Format("{0}==@0", filterColumn), filterQuery);`. filterColumn is already validated by the IsValidProperty guard at line 216 so it is safe to interpolate.

Add a single global exception handler so the remaining ~167 controllers stop needing per-method try/catch and never leak ex.Message. Create Backend/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs implementing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.IExceptionHandler (built into the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App framework in net8.0 — no extra package): inject ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler>, in TryHandleAsync call `_logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception for {Method} {Path}", httpContext.Request.Method, httpContext.Request.Path);`, set status 500 and write a generic ProblemDetails (Title "An unexpected error occurred.", no exception detail), return true.

Wire it up in Backend/Program.cs: after `builder.Services.AddControllers();` (line 22) add `builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();` and `builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Infrastructure.GlobalExceptionHandler>();`. In the pipeline, add `app.UseExceptionHandler();` inside the non-Development branch at lines 104-107 (next to app.UseHsts()), keeping app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage() for Development so developers still see full detail locally.

Optional but recommended for the report-layer observability gap: WebApplication.CreateBuilder already adds Console/Debug logging by default, but add a Logging section to Backend/appsettings.json (e.g. `"Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning" } }`) so the new _logger calls surface at the intended level in production.
verification: Build: from repo root run `dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj"` and confirm it compiles (verifies the new ctor params, the IExceptionHandler signature, and the reflection-based ApplyFilter).

Login leak gone: POST to /api/Auth with a payload that triggers an exception in JWTManagerService (e.g. force a DB/connection error). Confirm the 500 body is the generic ProblemDetails ('An unexpected error occurred...') with NO stack trace or SQL/exception text, and that a structured 'Login failed for user {UserName}' error line appears in the app logs/console.

Global handler: in a non-Development environment, hit a report endpoint that throws (e.g. an invalid filterColumn that throws NotSupportedException from IsValidProperty) and confirm the client gets a generic ProblemDetails 500 plus an 'Unhandled exception for GET {path}' log entry — not the raw message.

ApplyFilter behavior: call a paginated report endpoint with filterColumn = a numeric/date-like column and a non-matching value (equality path), then with a string column and a partial value (Contains path); confirm results are correct and no exception is silently swallowed. Compare row counts for a known string-column filter against pre-change behavior to confirm Contains still works.

Logging coverage: grep the running console/container output to confirm log lines now originate from AuthController/UploadController/GlobalExceptionHandler, not only the two Service/ExcelExport workers.
pitfalls: Confirm the username property on API.Model.User before logging it — use the real property name and NEVER log the password. If unsure, log a fixed message instead of the object.; Order in Program.cs matters: AddProblemDetails() + AddExceptionHandler<>() must be registered before builder.Build(); UseExceptionHandler() must run early in the pipeline (before MapControllers) and only in the non-Development branch so it doesn't conflict with UseDeveloperExceptionPage, which should stay for local detail.; UploadController.Postupload returns HttpResponseMessage directly and swallows its own exception in the catch, so the global IExceptionHandler will NOT fire for it — that is why the per-method ILogger call there is still required.; ApplyFilter is generic/static: keep BindingFlags.IgnoreCase on GetProperty to preserve the current case-insensitive filterColumn tolerance (matching IsValidProperty). Nullable string columns are still typeof(string), so the Contains path is preserved for them.; Rollback is low-risk and isolated per change. If the global handler changes the response shape for clients that expected plain-text 500 bodies, remove only the UseExceptionHandler/AddExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails lines while keeping the AuthController and ApplyFilter fixes.

approach: In ApiResult<T>.ApplyFilter (Backend/Model/APIResult.cs), replace the two unguarded Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery) calls with a single DateTime.TryParse that bails out (returns the unfiltered source) on a bad value, and replace the brittle filterColumn.Contains("Date") name heuristic with an actual property-type check via reflection so only real date columns take the date branch.
steps: Open Backend/Model/APIResult.cs. The class is `ApiResult<T>` in namespace `API.Model`. The target method is the private static `ApplyFilter` at lines 209-249; the unguarded date block is lines 221-233.

Add a private static helper `IsDateProperty(string propertyName)` (place it in the #region Methods block, e.g. right after `IsValidProperty` which ends at line 316). It looks up the property on `typeof(T)` with the same BindingFlags as IsValidProperty, unwraps `Nullable<>` with `Nullable.GetUnderlyingType`, and returns true only when the underlying type is `typeof(DateTime)` or `typeof(DateTimeOffset)`. This replaces the substring heuristic that wrongly matches names like `UpdateDate`/`Validate`.
In `ApplyFilter`, change the guard `if (filterColumn.Contains("Date"))` (line 221) to `if (IsDateProperty(filterColumn))`.

Inside that date branch, replace the two separate `Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery)` calls (lines 223 and 228) with one guarded parse: `if (!DateTime.TryParse(filterQuery, System.Globalization.CultureInfo.InvariantCulture, System.Globalization.DateTimeStyles.None, out var filterDate)) return source;`. Then build the range from `filterDate`: use `filterDate.AddDays(1)` for the `< @0` upper bound and `filterDate` for the `> @0` lower bound, keeping the existing `source.Where("ColumnName < @0", value)` dynamic-LINQ calls. Use InvariantCulture so behavior is deterministic regardless of server locale and matches the ISO dates the SPA date picker sends.

Note that `IsDateProperty` must NOT throw when the property is missing or non-date — it just returns false, so a non-date filterColumn correctly falls through to the existing Contains/== string filter below (lines 235-248). `IsValidProperty(filterColumn)` is already called earlier in ApplyFilter (line 216) and throws if the column doesn't exist, so the column name is still validated against injection before IsDateProperty runs.
Build the backend: `dotnet build` from Backend/. Confirm no nullable/usage warnings around the new helper.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ succeeds.

Negative case (the actual fix): call any paginated report endpoint with a date column and a junk value, e.g. `GET /<report-route>?filterColumn=<aDateColumn>&filterQuery=not-a-date` with a valid bearer token. Before the fix this returns HTTP 500 (FormatException); after the fix it returns 200 with the filter ignored (full unfiltered page).
Positive case: same endpoint with `filterQuery=2024-01-15` still narrows results to that day (the > startDate / < startDate+1 range), proving the date branch still works.

Heuristic case: a non-date column whose name contains 'Date' (e.g. a string column) now goes through the Contains/== string-filter path instead of attempting a date parse — confirm such a filter returns 200 and filters as text.
pitfalls: If any report DTO models a date column as a `string` (some report projections do), the old code would have date-filtered it via the name match, but the new IsDateProperty returns false for strings, so it now falls to the Contains/== branch. That is correct behavior for a string column, but if a particular report relied on string-typed date-range filtering, verify that report still filters as expected. No data migration or DB change; change is isolated to APIResult.cs and trivially reversible. Both CreateAsync and CreateFastPageAsync call ApplyFilter, so both pagination paths are fixed by this single edit.

approach: Fix the one genuine runtime defect first — UploadController.Postupload returns System.Net.Http.HttpResponseMessage, which ASP.NET Core MVC serializes as a JSON object with HTTP 200 (so failures never reach the frontend) and builds a `dict` it never returns. Convert it to a proper IActionResult (Ok/BadRequest/StatusCode) returning a small JSON envelope. Then standardize the remaining controllers on IActionResult/ActionResult<T> with ProblemDetails for errors so the frontend stops special-casing every endpoint.
steps: PRIORITY 1 (functional bug): In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, change the action signature `public async Task<HttpResponseMessage> Postupload(String filename)` to `public async Task<IActionResult> Postupload(string filename)`.

Inside Postupload, replace every `return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.X)` with the MVC equivalent that carries the message you were stuffing into the unused `dict`: the validation branch at line 71 (bad extension) and line 79 (too large) -> `return BadRequest(new { error = message });`; the success branch at line 86 -> `return Ok(new { success = filename });`; the no-file fallthrough at line 92 -> `return BadRequest(new { error = "No file uploaded." });`; the catch at line 98 -> `return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, new { error = ex.Message });`. Delete the now-pointless `Dictionary<string,object> dict` and its `.Add(...)` calls since the messages are returned inline.

Remove the now-unused `using System.Net;` and `using System.Net.Http;` at the top of UploadController.cs (lines 5-6) and add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;` (for StatusCodes) — confirm it compiles with no HttpResponseMessage references left.

Note: the frontend at Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx (the antd Upload onChange handler, lines 198-217) keys off `info.file.status === 'done' | 'error'`, which antd derives from the real HTTP status. Once the controller returns real BadRequest/500 instead of an HTTP 200 wrapping an HttpResponseMessage body, the existing `Modal.error({ title: 'Fail' ... })` path will finally fire on rejected uploads — no frontend change required, but smoke-test it.

STANDARDIZE ERRORS (consistency): In Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs Login (line 25), the bare `return NotFound();` returns an empty body the frontend must special-case. Make failures structured: `return Problem(detail: "Invalid username or password.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status401Unauthorized);` (401 is more correct than 404 for a failed login) and in the catch (line 41) prefer `return Problem(detail: ex.Message, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError);` so all errors are ProblemDetails. Keep `return Ok(result)` for success.

In Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs Get (line 30), the error path already uses `BadRequest("Room parameter is required.")` (a bare string). For consistency change it to `return Problem(detail: "Room parameter is required.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status400BadRequest);` and add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;`. Note the GET success already returns `Ok(List<ChatModel>)` (an un-enveloped list, not a raw `List<ChatModel>` return type as the audit text loosely says) — leaving the list shape as-is is fine; only the error encoding needs unifying.

ReportLookupsController.cs already uses `ActionResult<T>` and `Ok(...)`/`NotFound()` correctly (lines 43, 89, 116) — its `NotFound()` for an unknown lookupName (line 74) is acceptable; no change required there beyond optionally returning `Problem(statusCode: 404)` if you want every error to be ProblemDetails. Treat as low priority.

OPTIONAL (broader consistency, do as a follow-up): decide on one collection contract. The report controllers already return `ApiResult<T>` (Backend/Model/APIResult.cs, with Data/TotalCount/HasNextPage). Either (a) leave non-paged lookups/chat as plain `Ok(list)` and document that paged endpoints use ApiResult<T>, or (b) wrap lookups in ApiResult<T> via `ApiResult<ReportLookupOption>.CreatePageFromRows(...)`. Do NOT migrate report endpoints — the frontend BasicTable already binds to ApiResult<T>.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` from the Backend project dir — must compile with no `HttpResponseMessage` / `System.Net.Http` references remaining in UploadController.cs.

Reject path (the real bug): POST a >500KB image (or a .exe) to `POST /api/Upload/Postupload?filename=test.png` with multipart form-data. BEFORE the fix you get HTTP 200 with a serialized HttpResponseMessage JSON blob (so antd shows success); AFTER the fix you get HTTP 400 with body `{ "error": "..." }`. Example: `curl -i -F "file=@big.png" "http://localhost:5000/api/Upload/Postupload?filename=big.png"` and confirm the status line is `HTTP/1.1 400`.
Success path: POST a small valid .png and confirm HTTP 200 `{ "success": "<filename>" }` and the .webp lands in wwwroot/Image.

In the running app, open the Chat box (Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx), upload an oversized image, and confirm the antd `Modal.error({ title: 'Fail' })` now appears (it previously never fired because every response was HTTP 200).
Auth: POST bad credentials to `POST /api/Auth` and confirm HTTP 401 with a ProblemDetails JSON body instead of an empty 404.
pitfalls: Do NOT migrate the report endpoints off ApiResult<T> — the React BasicTable binds to Data/TotalCount/HasNextPage and changing that shape breaks every report grid. The AuthController status-code change from 404 to 401 is more correct but is a behavior change; check that the frontend login error handler in Frontend/src (axios interceptor / login thunk) treats 401 the same as the old 404 (i.e. shows 'invalid credentials' rather than redirect-on-401 logout looping) before shipping — if a global 401 interceptor force-logs-out, keep the login failure as a non-401 (e.g. 400) or exempt the /api/Auth route. The Upload reject path was effectively broken before (always 200), so making it return real 4xx/5xx is the intended behavior change, not a regression.

approach: Replace the in-memory cross-join in ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.Post with the existing SQL-grouped aggregation, exactly mirroring the sibling BorderExportLicenceBySectionReportController: delegate to sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateAggregateResultAsync(..., ReportAggregateDimension.Section, includeSakhan: false, includeColumnTotals: true). This makes the interactive path group/page in SQL, deletes the quadratic BuildRowsAsync and the bespoke 5-minute IMemoryCache, and guarantees the UI matches the Excel path (both now run through the same SectionGroups SQL).
steps: Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs.

Rewrite the [HttpPost] Post method (lines 41-97) so that after the TryCreateReportRequest guard it returns: `var result = await sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateAggregateResultAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, request!, ReportAggregateDimension.Section, includeSakhan: false, includeColumnTotals: true); return Ok(result);` — identical to BorderExportLicenceBySectionReportController.Post (lines 43-47). includeSakhan must be false here because this is the Oversea report (the source TryCreateReportRequest sets Type = "Oversea" and the legacy RDLC has no Sakhan column).

Delete the entire BuildRowsAsync method (lines 99-163), including the in-memory `.ToList()` query, the CurrencyList/SectionList loads, and the `foreach (section) foreach (currency)` cross join. CreateAggregateResultAsync -> AggregateInSqlAsync already performs the per-(Section,Currency) GROUP BY in SQL and only emits non-empty groups, so the old `if (cell.NoOfLicences > 0 || cell.TotalValue > 0)` filter is preserved by construction.

Remove the now-unused IMemoryCache field and caching: delete `private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;` (line 28), the `private static readonly TimeSpan RowsCacheDuration = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5);` constant (line 32), the `_cache` assignment in the constructor (line 37), and drop `IMemoryCache cache` from the constructor parameter list (line 34). Also delete the now-unused `New`/`Approved` constants (lines 30-31) since the filter constants now live in the _Fast class.

Remove now-unused usings at the top of the file: `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` (line 15) and `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;` (line 16). Keep `using API.Service.Reports;` (it provides ReportAggregateDimension) and `using API.StoredProcedureToLinq;`. The local `ImportLicenceBySection` cells model is no longer referenced from this controller — leave that type definition wherever it is declared unless it has no other references; do not delete it blindly.

Leave the existing TryCreateReportRequest, Excel, and WriteRowsAsync members untouched — WriteRowsAsync already uses sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync (backed by the same SectionGroups SQL), so after this change the interactive and Excel paths share one grouping definition.

Build the backend (`dotnet build` in /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend) and fix any leftover references to the removed _cache/BuildRowsAsync.
verification: dotnet build the Backend project — it must compile with the IMemoryCache field, RractoCacheDuration, BuildRowsAsync, and the two usings removed (no CS0103/unused-symbol errors).

POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport with a wide date range and a known PaThaKaTypeId/section filter. Confirm: (a) the rows array is one page of Section+Currency groups ordered by section then currency, (b) result.columnTotals.noOfLicences and result.columnTotals.totalValue are present and equal the grand totals, (c) only non-empty (Section,Currency) combinations appear.

Parity check: compare the same request's interactive response totals/rows against the Excel export (POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport/Excel via the queue). They must now match exactly, since both run through sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast SectionGroups.

Capture the generated SQL (enable EF Core query logging) for the Post request and confirm it contains a GROUP BY on Section/Currency with OFFSET/FETCH paging — no full detail set is materialized in app memory.
pitfalls: "Pass includeSakhan: false — this is the Oversea Import Licence By Section report (TryCreateReportRequest hardcodes Type = \"Oversea\"); passing true would add a Sakhan group key and change the row counts vs the legacy RDLC. The ColumnTotals keys produced by ReportAggregationService.BuildColumnTotals are exactly noOfLicences and totalValue (same as the old manual code), so the frontend footer needs no change. Do NOT switch to CreateSectionPagedResultAsync — although it exists and uses the same SectionGroups SQL, it has zero callers and does not emit ColumnTotals, so the TOTAL footer row would disappear; the verifier confirmed CreateAggregateResultAsync(..., Section, ..., includeColumnTotals: true) is the correct precedent. Removing the IMemoryCache means each Post hits SQL directly; this is fine because the work is now a single grouped/paged query, but if a stress test shows hot repeated identical requests you can re-add caching at the ApiResult level later. NoOfLicences in the SQL path counts DISTINCT non-empty licence numbers (group.Select(...Distinct().Count())), whereas the old in-memory code used a plain .Count() of item rows; this is the intended/correct count and matches the RDLC and the Excel path, but it can legitimately differ from the old buggy number — treat that as the fix, not a regression."

approach: Add a centralized error path: register the built-in ProblemDetails service plus a custom IExceptionHandler that logs every unhandled exception with a correlation id and returns a sanitized RFC-7807 response, then wire app.UseExceptionHandler() at the top of the pipeline in Program.cs (keeping UseDeveloperExceptionPage strictly for Development). No NuGet package is needed — IExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails/UseExceptionHandler ship in net8.0.
steps: Create Backend/Service/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs declaring namespace API.Service. Make it a class implementing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.IExceptionHandler with a constructor injecting ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> and IProblemDetailsService. In TryHandleAsync generate a correlation id (e.g. Activity.Current?.Id ?? httpContext.TraceIdentifier), log the exception at Error level with that id, set httpContext.Response.StatusCode = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, and write a sanitized ProblemDetails (Title "An unexpected error occurred.", Status 500, an Extensions["correlationId"] entry) via IProblemDetailsService.TryWriteAsync. Never put ex.Message or ex.StackTrace in the response body. Return true.

In Backend/Program.cs, in the service-registration section (right after builder.Services.AddControllers(); on line 22, or anywhere before line 97 var app = builder.Build();), add builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(); and builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Service.GlobalExceptionHandler>(); (add a using API.Service; or use the fully-qualified name).

In Backend/Program.cs replace the if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); } else { app.UseHsts(); } block (lines 100-107) so the non-Development branch registers app.UseExceptionHandler(); before app.UseHsts();. UseDeveloperExceptionPage() stays only inside the IsDevelopment() branch. This block already sits before app.UseRouting() (line 160), so the handler runs early enough to catch downstream exceptions.

Leave the AccountSummaryReportController.cs:70 catch (SqlException ex) when (IsMissingPaginationProcedure(ex)) block intact — it is a deliberate functional fallback to the non-paginated LINQ query when the *_pagination proc is missing, not error-swallowing. The new global handler only catches exceptions that escape the controller, so the two do not conflict. (Optional, separate cleanup: if you later introduce a typed MissingPaginationProcedureException, map it in the handler; do not do this as part of this finding.)

Confirm Swagger exposure is unaffected: app.UseSwagger()/UseSwaggerUI (lines 109-115) run after the exception handler registration, so they keep working; the audit's separate concern about Swagger-in-prod is out of scope here.
verification: Build: dotnet build on Backend/Backend.csproj succeeds (no missing-package errors — IExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails/UseExceptionHandler are in net8.0).

Force an exception in a non-Development run (e.g. set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production, point ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest at an unreachable host so sp_AccountSummaryReport throws a non-2812 SqlException, then POST /api/AccountSummaryReport). Expect HTTP 500 with Content-Type application/problem+json, a body containing title "An unexpected error occurred." and a correlationId, and NO stack trace or SQL text.
Confirm the same request emits one Error-level log line with the matching CorrelationId and request Path.
Confirm the normal happy path and the existing missing-_pagination SqlException fallback in AccountSummaryReportController still return 200 (the global handler must not intercept them).
grep -rn "UseExceptionHandler\|AddExceptionHandler\|AddProblemDetails" Backend --include=*.cs now returns the three new registrations.
pitfalls: Register app.UseExceptionHandler() before app.UseRouting()/UseAuthentication()/UseAuthorization() (it currently sits at lines 100-107, well before line 160, which is correct) — placing it after routing would miss exceptions thrown in routing/auth. AddProblemDetails() is required for IProblemDetailsService to be resolvable; without it TryWriteAsync returns false and you fall back to an empty 500. Keep UseDeveloperExceptionPage() inside the IsDevelopment() branch only; do not move it to the shared path. Do not remove or weaken the IsMissingPaginationProcedure SqlException catch in AccountSummaryReportController — it is a functional fallback, and removing it would break reports whose *_pagination proc is not yet deployed (recall: SP .sql files are not auto-applied). Rollback is a clean revert of Program.cs plus deleting GlobalExceptionHandler.cs; no data migration involved.

approach: Harden Backend/Program.cs with three built-in/standard operability features: a per-user partitioned rate limiter applied to the report endpoints, MapHealthChecks("/health") with AddDbContextCheck for both DbContexts, and Asp.Versioning on the /api surface; separately, push the ImportLicenceBySectionReport aggregation into SQL instead of materializing the whole join with .ToList() in app memory.
steps: Add NuGet packages to Backend/API.csproj: `Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore` (v8.0.x to match the net8 runtime) and `Asp.Versioning.Mvc` (v8.x). Run from the Backend dir: `dotnet add API.csproj package Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore --version 8.0.17` and `dotnet add API.csproj package Asp.Versioning.Mvc --version 8.1.0`. Rate limiting (System.Threading.RateLimiting / Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting) is already in the net8 shared framework — no package needed.

In Backend/Program.cs add the usings: `using System.Threading.RateLimiting;`, `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting;`, `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;`, `using System.Security.Claims;`, `using Asp.Versioning;`.

Register services in Program.cs after `builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);` (line 95) and before `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97): add `AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>("TemplateDB").AddDbContextCheck<TradeNetDbContext>("TradeNetDB")` — both context types are already imported via `using API.DBContext;` and registered via AddDbContextPool at lines 64-67.

In the same block add `builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(...)` with a named policy `"per-user"` that partitions on the `ClaimTypes.Name` claim (the identifier already used in 160 places, e.g. ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:177 `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value`), falling back to remote IP for unauthenticated calls; use a FixedWindowRateLimiter (PermitLimit 30 / 1 min) and set `RejectionStatusCode = 429`.

In the same block add `builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(...)` with `DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1,0)` and `AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true` so the existing 166 controllers (all `[Route("api/[controller]")]`) keep responding on the current URLs without immediate route changes.

In the request pipeline, insert `app.UseRateLimiter();` AFTER `app.UseAuthorization();` (currently line 167) and BEFORE `app.MapControllers();` (line 168) so the authenticated user claim is available to the partition function.

After `app.MapControllers();` add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health").AllowAnonymous();` so the endpoint is reachable by load-balancer liveness/readiness probes without a JWT. Place it before `app.UseStaticFiles();` (line 169).

Apply the rate-limit policy to the heavy report endpoints. Either decorate the report controllers' POST actions with `[Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting.EnableRateLimiting("per-user")]`, or — to cover all 166 controllers at once — add a global limiter as the default in AddRateLimiter via `options.GlobalLimiter = PartitionedRateLimiter.Create<HttpContext,string>(...)` using the same per-user key. For a focused first pass, add `[EnableRateLimiting("per-user")]` to ImportLicenceBySectionReportController and the other StoredProcedure-backed report controllers under Controllers/Report.

Fix the in-memory materialization called out in the finding: in Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs, the `BuildRowsAsync` method (lines 99-163) ends the LINQ query with `.ToList()` at line 125 then groups/counts in app memory (lines 132-151). Replace this path with the SQL aggregation that the Excel path already uses — `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync(_context, procedureRequest)` (already called in WriteRowsAsync at line 197) — so the section/currency grouping happens in SQL Server, not after pulling every joined row into memory. Map its rows to `ReportAggregateResult` and keep the existing IMemoryCache wrapper (lines 64-68) and ColumnTotals footer (lines 90-94).

Optionally centralize the per-call `db.Database.SetCommandTimeout(120)` (currently in only 3 places, e.g. sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:45) by setting `sqlOptions.CommandTimeout(120)` inside `UseSqlServer(...)` for both AddDbContextPool registrations (Program.cs lines 64-67) so every report query has a bounded timeout.
verification: `dotnet build Backend/API.csproj` compiles with the new packages and usings.

Run the app, then `curl -i http://localhost:<port>/health` returns 200 with body `Healthy` when both DBs are reachable; stop/block the TradeNetDB connection and confirm it returns 503 `Unhealthy` (proves AddDbContextCheck covers both contexts) — and that it works WITHOUT a JWT (AllowAnonymous).

With a valid bearer token, fire >30 POSTs in one minute at a report endpoint that has the policy (e.g. `POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport`) and confirm the 31st returns HTTP 429; a second distinct user's token is unaffected (proves per-user partitioning on ClaimTypes.Name).

Hit a versioned route / send `X-Api-Version: 1.0`; confirm the response carries the `api-supported-versions` header (ReportApiVersions=true) and that the existing `/api/...` URLs still resolve (AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified).

For ImportLicenceBySectionReport, run the report with a broad date range and confirm the response rows and ColumnTotals (noOfLicences, totalValue) match the previous in-memory result, while a SQL profiler / EF logging shows aggregation executed server-side (no full-join row stream into the app).
pitfalls: "Ordering: UseRateLimiter MUST come after UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization, otherwise User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name) is null and every request falls into one shared anonymous partition. MapHealthChecks MUST be AllowAnonymous or the LB probe will get 401 from the JWT pipeline. Package versions: pin the HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore package to 8.0.x (not 9.x) to match the net8 TargetFramework, even though EF Core itself is 9.0.6 — the 9.x health-check package targets net9 and will warn/fail to restore. AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified=true is essential on first rollout so the React client's existing /api/* axios calls don't break before any URL changes ship. PermitLimit=30/min is a starting value — tune per real report cadence; too low will throttle legitimate dashboard users who open several reports. For the ImportLicenceBySection SQL refactor, verify sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync returns the same section/currency cell shape (including the `cell.NoOfLicences>0 || cell.TotalValue>0` filtering at line 145) before swapping; rollback is trivial since the old LINQ path can be kept behind a flag. Rate limiting and SQL refactor are independent — ship rate-limit/health/versioning first (low risk) and the aggregation rewrite as a separate change."

approach: Thread HttpContext.RequestAborted into every synchronous JSON report Post action by adding a `CancellationToken cancellationToken` parameter (model binding supplies it), then plumb that token through the sp_* helpers and the shared ApiResult paging methods down to each terminal EF call (ToListAsync / CountAsync / SqlQueryRaw), so an aborted client request cancels the running SQL instead of letting it run to the 120-180s timeout. Do NOT touch the existing WriteRowsAsync Excel-streaming path — it already receives and uses a token.
steps: Add the optional token to the shared paging helpers in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs first (everything below depends on them). Change the two terminal helpers: `private static async Task<int> CountAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct)` -> call `await source.CountAsync(ct)`; `private static async Task<List<T>> ToListAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct)` -> call `await source.ToListAsync(ct)`. Add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` as the last parameter to `CreateAsync(...)` and `CreateFastPageAsync(...)`, pass it through to the CountAsyncSafe/ToListAsyncSafe calls and from CreateFastPageAsync into its `await CreateAsync(...)` call. Add `using System.Threading;` at the top.

In Backend/Service/Reports/ReportQueryService.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` to `CreatePagedResultAsync<T>(IQueryable<T> query, ReportQueryRequest request, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)` and forward it: `return ApiResult<T>.CreateFastPageAsync(query, pageIndex, pageSize, null, null, request.FilterColumn, request.FilterQuery, request.IncludeTotalCount, cancellationToken);` Add `using System.Threading;`.

In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_AccountSummaryReport.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` (add `using System.Threading;`) as the last parameter of `ExecuteAsync(...)` and change line 91-92 to `return await ExecuteQueryable(db, request, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount).ToListAsync(cancellationToken);` Leave ExecuteQueryable (returns IQueryable, no terminal call) and Query untouched.

In Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken` as the second parameter of `Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken)`. Pass it to the sp call: `await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount: true, cancellationToken)`, and to the SqlException fallback: `await ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync(query, request, cancellationToken)`. (`using System.Threading;` is already present.)
