approach: Make the `no-explicit-any` lint rule an error so new `any`/`AnyObject` usages are blocked, then thread real `<T>` generics through the report data path (`BasicHttpServices` → `useFetchData` → `GenericReportPage` → `BasicTable`) and replace `AnyObject` with handwritten DTO interfaces on the report (non-template) paths. Leave vendor dashboard template `any` sites alone per the verifier.
steps: Open Frontend/.eslintrc.cjs. The repo uses the CLASSIC eslintrc config (there is no flat eslint.config.* file even though eslint ^9.28.0 is installed). It currently has NO explicit no-explicit-any rule — it only inherits the default 'warn' from 'plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended'. ADD an explicit error rule to the `rules` object: `'@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'error'`. (Do NOT switch to a flat config; the verifier confirmed the classic config is correct here.)

Run `npm run lint` (script: `eslint . --ext ts,tsx ...` in package.json) to enumerate every offending site now that it's an error. Expect ~66 `: any` reports plus the AnyObject definition file. Use this list to scope the burn-down to src/Report and src/services first.

Fix the genuine type-mismatch bug in Frontend/src/services/BasicHttpServices.ts: `GetSingle` is annotated `Promise<AnyObject>` but its body constructs and returns a `PaginationType` (lines 14-19). Decide the real contract: GetSingle is called only from Frontend/src/hooks/useFormload.tsx:20 to fetch one record. Re-type it as a generic `GetSingle = async <T = unknown>(url: string): Promise<T>` and return `resp.data as T` instead of the bogus JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...)) round-trip cast to PaginationType. Apply the same `<T = unknown>` generic + `resp.data as T` pattern to Post, Put, Delete, ToggleUserActive so callers can supply the response DTO; keep `Get` returning `PaginationType` (it is list-shaped).

In Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx, change the hook to a generic `const useFetchData = <T = unknown>(url: string)` and type the state as `useState<T[]>([])` (or `useState<T | undefined>(undefined)` depending on usage) and `useState<unknown>(null)` for error — removing both `: any` at lines 4-5. Note the existing missing-dependency bug: fetchData's useCallback deps array is `[]` but it closes over `url`; add `url` while you are here (this is why the effect re-fetches via the separate `[url]` effect).

In Frontend/src/types/AnyObject.ts, keep the type only as a generic-default fallback but stop treating it as the data contract. Add a doc comment marking it deprecated for report rows. Do NOT delete it yet — PaginationType, BasicTable, and BasicTableColumn all use it as their `<T extends AnyObject = AnyObject>` default constraint, so removing it breaks compilation until all generics are supplied.

Create per-report DTO interfaces. ReportColumnConfig (Frontend/src/Report/config/reportTypes.ts:22) keys columns by string `key`/`dataIndex`, so define interfaces whose field names match the backend response JSON property names (camelCased .NET DTO members) for the reports being migrated, e.g. `interface MemberRegistrationRow { companyName: string; registrationNo: string; companyCount: number; ... }`. Put them next to each report page (ListOfDirectors.tsx, MemberRegistrationReport.tsx, CompanyProfile.tsx) or in a shared Frontend/src/Report/config/reportRowTypes.ts.

Thread the generic through GenericReportPage.tsx. Replace `PaginationType<AnyObject>` in fetchRows (lines 653-654) and the `<BasicTable<AnyObject>>` instantiation (line 788) with the report-specific row type. Since GenericReportPage is config-driven, add an optional type parameter to the page or carry the row type on ReportPageConfig (e.g. `ReportPageConfig<TRow extends Record<string, unknown> = Record<string, unknown>>`) and instantiate `<BasicTable<TRow>>`; for reports without a DTO yet, fall back to `Record<string, unknown>` rather than `AnyObject`.

Change the BasicTable generic default from `AnyObject` to a non-`any` shape. In Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx (lines 33, 48, 121) and Frontend/src/types/PaginationType.ts (line 3), change `<T extends AnyObject = AnyObject>` to `<T extends Record<string, unknown> = Record<string, unknown>>`. This keeps dynamic-key indexing working (string render keys) without an `any` index signature, so field typos in `dataIndex` against a concrete `T` are caught.

Burn down the remaining flagged report/service sites surfaced by lint: NameConvert.ts:2 `let character: any;` → type it `let character: string;` (it indexes a string). Audit the AnyObject imports in src/Report/Page/ListOfDirectors.tsx, MemberRegistrationReport.tsx, CompanyProfile.tsx and the hooks (useFormActions, useFormload, useFileUpload*, BasicStepForm) and replace `AnyObject` with the new DTO/`Record<string, unknown>` as appropriate.

Leave the vendor dashboard template files (e.g. Frontend/src/components/dashboard/default/CampaignsCard/CampaignsCard.tsx:1 and other src/components/dashboard/* any sites) as-is; per the verifier these are template noise and low-value. If lint now errors on them and you want green CI immediately, add a scoped `overrides` entry in .eslintrc.cjs for `src/components/dashboard/**` that sets `'@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'warn'`, so the error gate protects src/Report and src/services without forcing a template rewrite.
verification: Run `npm run lint` in Frontend/ — it must report 0 errors for src/Report and src/services (no `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` violations there). With the rule at 'error', a freshly added `: any` anywhere outside the dashboard override should fail lint.

Run `npx tsc --noEmit` in Frontend/ — must still exit 0. Because BasicTable's default changed from `AnyObject` (any index) to `Record<string, unknown>`, any report whose `dataIndex` references a field absent from its DTO `TRow` will now fail compilation; fix those (that is the intended bug-surfacing).

Negative test: temporarily mistype a `dataIndex` in one migrated report config (e.g. `companyName` → `compnyName`) against its concrete DTO `TRow` and confirm `tsc --noEmit` now errors where it previously compiled clean.

Manually load a migrated report (e.g. Member Registration) in the app, apply filters, and confirm rows + the columnTotals footer still render — proving the generic re-type did not change runtime behavior.
Confirm `grep -rn ': any' src/Report src/services` returns nothing (NameConvert.ts:2 fixed) and `GetSingle` no longer claims to return PaginationType.
pitfalls: Do the .eslintrc.cjs rule flip and the generic threading together in one branch — flipping the rule to 'error' alone will turn ~66 warnings into build-breaking errors across the whole tree (including vendor dashboard files), so either land the dashboard `overrides` exclusion in the same change or keep the rule at 'warn' until the burn-down is done. Order matters: change PaginationType/BasicTable defaults from AnyObject to Record<string,unknown> BEFORE deleting/deprecating AnyObject, otherwise every generic default breaks at once. The GetSingle return-type change is behavioral: its sole caller (useFormload.tsx:20) does `JSON.stringify(await GetSingle(...))`, so returning `resp.data` directly instead of a PaginationType wrapper is what was always intended, but smoke-test the form-load path. AnyObject cannot be deleted until all 14 importing files are migrated; rollback is a single git revert of the branch since no data/migration is involved.

approach: Add a monotonically increasing request-id (staleness) guard inside BasicTable's load effect so a late-resolving response for an older filter/page can no longer overwrite the grid; this one guard covers both the fetchData and legacy fetch paths.
steps: Open Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx and locate the load effect at lines 204-234 (the `useEffect` that calls `fetchData(query)` / `fetch!(buildLegacyUrl(api!, query))` and ends with `}, [api, enabled, fetch, fetchData, query, refreshKey]);`).

Add a stable per-component request counter. Just below the existing `useState` declarations (after line 179, `const [data, setData] = useState<PaginationType<T>>(emptyPage<T>());`) add `const requestIdRef = useRef(0);` and add `useRef` to the React import on line 1 (`import React, { useEffect, useMemo, useRef, useState } from 'react';`).

Inside the effect, before any async work, capture the id for this run: `const requestId = ++requestIdRef.current;`. This must be incremented synchronously in the effect body (not inside the async `load`) so every new effect run claims a strictly higher id than any in-flight request.

Guard every state-mutating call that happens after the `await` with `if (requestId !== requestIdRef.current) return;`. Specifically: after `const result = ...await...`, only `setData(result ?? emptyPage<T>())` if this is still the latest request; in `catch`, only `setError(...)` if latest; in `finally`, only `setLoading(false)` if latest. A stale response must not flip `loading` off either, or it would clear the skeleton while the newer request is still running.

Keep the early-return branches (`!enabled` and `!fetchData && (!fetch || !api)`) as they are — those run synchronously before any await, so they are inherently the latest run and need no guard. The `setLoading(true)`/`setError(null)` before the try are also synchronous for the current run and stay unguarded.

Leave GenericReportPage.tsx unchanged. `fetchRows` (lines 652-662), `applyFilters` (711-715), and `resetFilters` (717-722) need no edit: bumping `refreshKey` / changing `filters` already re-runs the BasicTable effect, and the new guard handles the out-of-order resolution. Do not add a signal param to `fetchRows` — that would force every report to thread it through.
Run `npm run lint` and `npm run build` (or `tsc -b`) in Frontend/ to confirm the `useRef` import and types are clean.
verification: Throttle the network to slow 3G in DevTools. On a heavy report (e.g. a Daily report) click Filter, then immediately change the page size from 10 to 1000 (or jump pages) while the first request is in flight. The grid must end up showing the data for the LAST action; the earlier (slower) response must not flash in or overwrite it.

In the Network tab, confirm both requests still complete (no cancellation expected with this approach) but observe that the grid only ever reflects the most recent query — verify by comparing visible rows/totalCount against the active page/filter.
Add a quick temporary `console.log(requestId, requestIdRef.current)` in the post-await guard to confirm stale responses hit the `return` branch and never call setData; remove the log after.
Run `npm run build` in Frontend/ — it must compile with the new useRef import and no TS errors.
Regression: a single Filter click still loads data, pagination still works, and the loading skeleton still clears normally when only one request is in flight.
pitfalls: Increment the id synchronously in the effect body (`++requestIdRef.current`), NOT inside the async `load` callback — if you bump it inside `load` after an `await`, two rapid effect runs can both read the same id and the guard fails.; Do NOT use a `useState` counter for the id; state updates are async/batched and a stale closure would compare against an outdated value. A `useRef` is required so the comparison reads the live latest id.; The `finally` must also be guarded — if a stale response sets `loading=false` it would clear the skeleton while the genuinely-latest request is still loading, showing an empty/stale grid momentarily.; This guard does not cancel the HTTP request (no AbortController), so backend load from in-flight queries is unchanged — that is acceptable for the correctness fix. If you later want to also cancel, layer an AbortController + axios `signal` on top, but keep this guard because axios cancellation still allows a late `.then` unless guarded.; Rollback is trivial: revert the single file (remove the useRef, requestId capture, and the three guards) to restore prior behavior.

approach: Adopt RTK Query (already shipped via @reduxjs/toolkit ^2.8.2 and already wired through <Provider store>) with one shared baseQuery that reuses the existing token-injection + baseUrl logic from AxiosInstance.ts. Migrate the highest-value shared fetches first — the ReportLookups endpoints in GenericReportPage — so lookups are cached/deduped across all ~134 report pages and survive navigation, then move report rows and Excel polling onto the same slice incrementally; also fix the latent useFetchData url-staleness bug the verifier flagged.
steps: Create Frontend/src/redux/api/baseQuery.ts: an axiosBaseQuery() that wraps the existing axiosInstance (Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts) so token injection and baseUrl prefixing stay in one place. Signature: ({ url, method, data, params }) => async (...) returning { data } or { error: { status, data } }. Reusing axiosInstance (not fetchBaseQuery) keeps the Authorization header + envConfig.baseUrl behavior identical to AxiosInstance.ts.

Create Frontend/src/redux/api/reportApi.ts with createApi({ reducerPath: 'reportApi', baseQuery: axiosBaseQuery(), tagTypes: ['Lookup','CompanyName'], endpoints }). Define getLookup: builder.query<LookupOption[], string>({ query: (lookupName) => ({ url: `ReportLookups/${lookupName}`, method: 'GET' }), providesTags: (r,e,name) => [{ type:'Lookup', id:name }] }) and getCompanyName: builder.query<CompanyNameLookupResult, string>({ query: (companyRegistrationNo) => ({ url:'ReportLookups/company-name', method:'GET', params:{ companyRegistrationNo } }) }). Move the LookupOption and CompanyNameLookupResult interfaces (currently inline at GenericReportPage.tsx:60 and :72) into Frontend/src/types/ReportLookup.ts and import them in both files.

Wire the api slice into Frontend/src/redux/store.ts. Because the root reducer is wrapped in redux-persist, do NOT persist the RTK Query cache: add [reportApi.reducerPath]: reportApi.reducer to combineReducers, add blacklist: [reportApi.reducerPath] to persistConfig, and concat(reportApi.middleware) onto the existing getDefaultMiddleware({ serializableCheck: false }) chain. Extend the RootState interface to include [reportApi.reducerPath]: ReturnType<typeof reportApi.reducer>. Optionally add setupListeners(store.dispatch) in main.tsx for refetchOnFocus/refetchOnReconnect.

Refactor lookup loading in Frontend/src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx: replace the manual useEffect at lines 552-609 plus the lookupOptions/loadingLookupNames useState (511-516) with RTK Query. Drive one useGetLookupQuery(lookup.lookupName) per distinct lookup from reportLookupFilters (already computed at 542-550). Since the lookup count is dynamic, extract a small child component (e.g. <LookupLoader lookupName onLoaded>) rendered once per name, or a fixed-shape useReportLookups hook — never call the hook in a raw loop. RTK Query dedups identical lookupName requests across all mounted pages so countries/business-type lists are fetched once and shared.

Migrate the debounced company-name lookup (GenericReportPage.tsx:611-650) to useLazyGetCompanyNameQuery from reportApi: keep the 300ms setTimeout debounce but call the lazy trigger instead of axiosInstance.get('ReportLookups/company-name'). RTK Query caches by the companyRegistrationNo arg, so repeated lookups of the same reg-no are served from cache.

Fix the latent bug the verifier flagged in Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx: the useCallback deps are [] while the effect deps are [url] (lines 19 and 23), so fetchData closes over a stale url. Preferred: delete the hook and replace call sites with an RTK Query endpoint; otherwise add url to the useCallback dependency array. Confirm call sites first with grep -rn useFetchData Frontend/src.

Document the pattern (single createApi slice, axiosBaseQuery, providesTags/invalidatesTags) so the report-rows POST (fetchRows, GenericReportPage.tsx:652-662) and the async Excel job polling can be migrated onto reportApi incrementally rather than all at once.
pitfalls: redux-persist ordering: the api slice MUST be in persistConfig.blacklist, otherwise the serialized RTK Query cache rehydrates stale and PersistGate can desync the cache — keep it in memory only. Add reportApi.middleware to the chain before shipping, or queries silently never run. Keep using the existing axiosInstance inside axiosBaseQuery instead of fetchBaseQuery so JWT Authorization header injection and envConfig.baseUrl prefixing (AxiosInstance.ts) are not duplicated and cannot drift from auth behavior. Lookup count is dynamic per report, so you cannot call useGetLookupQuery in a raw loop (rules-of-hooks) — render one query hook per lookup via a child component or fixed-shape hook. This is incremental and reversible: migrating only lookups first leaves the old fetchRows/Excel paths untouched. Rollback = remove reportApi from combineReducers/middleware/blacklist and restore GenericReportPage's local lookupOptions useState.
verification: Run grep -rn 'createApi' Frontend/src and confirm reportApi.ts matches (it previously returned nothing).
npm run build (or tsc -b) in Frontend passes with RootState now including [reportApi.reducerPath].

In the running app, open DevTools Network, navigate to a report whose filters use a shared lookup (countries/business types), then navigate to a second report using the same lookup: confirm ReportLookups/<name> is requested only ONCE for the session (served from cache on the second page) instead of refetching on every navigation.
With Redux DevTools, confirm a 'reportApi' slice appears holding cached lookup entries keyed by lookupName, and that it is NOT present in localStorage under persist:root (verifies the blacklist).
Block ReportLookups in DevTools to force a transient failure and confirm RTK Query surfaces isError/error and that refetch recovers, replacing the inconsistent manual loading/error handling.
Verify the useFetchData fix: the hook re-fetches when url changes (url added to useCallback deps), or grep -rn useFetchData Frontend/src shows no remaining call sites if the hook was removed.
estimatedTime: 1 day for the RTK Query infra + lookup/company-name migration + useFetchData fix; full migration of all report fetching is multi-day and incremental

approach: Add a shared parseApiError helper and route all swallowed catches through it.
steps: Create Frontend/src/utils/parseApiError.ts. Export a `ParsedApiError` type ({ status: number | null; message: string; isClientError: boolean; raw: unknown }) and a `parseApiError(error: unknown, fallback?: string)` function. Use axios's `isAxiosError` type guard (import { isAxiosError } from 'axios'; axios ^1.9.0 supports it). Logic: if !isAxiosError -> return generic fallback with status null. If `error.response` exists, read `status = error.response.status` and `data = error.response.data`; derive a serverMessage by: if typeof data === 'string' && data.trim() -> use it; else if data?.detail (ProblemDetails) -> use it; else if data?.title -> use it; else if data?.errors (ASP.NET ModelState) -> join the first messages. For status 400/404/409/422 (isClientError=true) return { status, message: serverMessage || fallback, isClientError: true }. For 401 return a session-expired message (the App.tsx interceptor already calls logout, so just surface text). For 5xx return fallback like 'The server encountered an error. Please try again.' (isClientError=false). If no response (network/timeout) return 'Could not reach the server. Check your connection and try again.' Always `console.error('[API error]', error)` inside the helper so the raw error is never lost. Provide a sensible default `fallback` param value of 'Something went wrong. Please try again.'

In Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx: add `import { parseApiError } from '../../../utils/parseApiError';` (verify the relative depth from src/components/My Components/Table/ -> src/utils; it is three levels up). Change the grid load catch at line 226-227 from `} catch { setError('Failed to load table data.'); }` to bind the error and call the helper: `} catch (err) { setError(parseApiError(err, 'Failed to load table data.').message); }`. Do the same for the Excel catch at lines 257-258: `} catch (err) { setError(parseApiError(err, 'Failed to generate Excel file.').message); }`. The existing Alert at lines 334-341 already renders the `error` string, so no UI change is needed.

In Frontend/src/hooks/useFormActions.tsx: import the helper `import { parseApiError } from '../utils/parseApiError';`. Replace the static `error()` modal so it accepts a message. Change the `error` helper (lines 29-34) to `const error = (msg = 'Something went wrong...') => { Modal.error({ title: 'Error', content: msg }); };`. Then in the New catch (lines 45-48) and Edit catch (lines 57-60) of `onFinish`, change `} catch (ex) { setWriteLoading(false); error(); }` to `} catch (ex) { setWriteLoading(false); error(parseApiError(ex).message); }`. Apply the same to the ToggleActive catch (lines 101-105) and to the onFinishStepper New catch (line 136-140, which already does console.error) and Edit catch (line 164-168): pass `parseApiError(ex).message` to `error(...)`. The helper already logs internally, so the existing `console.error(ex)` lines can stay or be removed.

In Frontend/src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx: the company-name lookup catch at lines 639-643 intentionally blanks the field on lookup failure (a not-found registration is an expected, non-error outcome), so keep the field-blanking behaviour BUT bind and log the error so a real server/network fault is observable: change `} catch {` to `} catch (err) {` and add `parseApiError(err, 'Company name lookup failed.');` (called for its console.error side effect) before/after the `if (!isCancelled) { form.setFieldValue(companyNameFilter.name, ''); }`. Add the import `import { parseApiError } from '../../utils/parseApiError';` (verify depth: src/Report/Page -> src/utils is two levels up). Do not pop a modal here — blanking the field silently to the user is the correct UX for a debounced lookup.

Optionally centralise logging in the axios response interceptor in Frontend/src/layouts/app/App.tsx (lines 132-157). It currently only handles 401. Leave the 401 logout branch intact; you may add an `else { console.error('[axios]', error.response?.status, error.config?.url); }` for non-401 failures so every rejected request is logged once even if a caller forgets to use the helper. Keep `return Promise.reject(error);` so per-call catches still run.
Run the type-check/lint to confirm no unused-var or import-path errors: from Frontend/, `npx tsc --noEmit` and `npm run lint`. Fix any path-depth mistakes in the new imports.
verification: From Frontend/: `npx tsc --noEmit` and `npm run lint` pass with no unused `ex`/`err` or bad import-path errors.

Run the app (`npm run dev`). Trigger a 400: open a report page and submit a filter the backend rejects (e.g. ToDate before FromDate hits the BadRequest("ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.") path in the report controllers) — the BasicTable Alert / form Modal should now show that exact server sentence instead of 'Failed to load table data.' / 'Something went wrong...'.

Simulate a network failure: stop the backend or block the API base URL in DevTools, then trigger a load — the Alert shows 'Could not reach the server...' and the browser console shows '[API error]' with the raw axios error object.
Simulate a 500 (or hit an endpoint that throws server-side) — the message reads 'The server encountered an error. Please try again.' and the raw error is logged.
Confirm the company-name lookup still silently blanks the field for an unknown registration number, but now logs '[API error]' to the console on a real fault.
pitfalls: Import path depth differs per file: BasicTable.tsx is at src/components/My Components/Table/ so it needs `../../../utils/parseApiError`; GenericReportPage.tsx at src/Report/Page/ needs `../../utils/parseApiError`; useFormActions.tsx at src/hooks/ needs `../utils/parseApiError`. Verify each or tsc will fail.; Do NOT turn the company-name lookup (GenericReportPage 639-643) into a user-facing modal/alert — a not-found registration is an expected outcome of a debounced background lookup; only add logging while keeping the field-blanking behaviour.; ASP.NET BadRequest("...") returns the message as a raw JSON string, so `error.response.data` is a string in most 4xx cases here; the `typeof data === 'string'` branch must come first. Validation failures from model binding instead return a ProblemDetails/ModelState object — that's why the `detail`/`title`/`errors` branches exist. Don't assume one shape.; The 401 branch in App.tsx already calls auth.logout() and shows a message; parseApiError's 401 text is a fallback for callers and won't double-trigger logout — leave the App.tsx interceptor as the single place that logs out.; Keeping the existing `console.error(ex)` calls in onFinishStepper alongside the helper's internal console.error will double-log; harmless, but remove the redundant ones if clean logs matter.; Rollback is trivial: the change is additive (one new util file) plus localized catch edits; revert the edits and delete parseApiError.ts.

approach: Externalize the SA password and connection string into a git-ignored .env file referenced via ${SA_PASSWORD} interpolation, replace the developer-specific host bind mount with a named Docker volume, and rotate the leaked dev password.
steps: Pick a new strong dev SA password to replace the leaked `Saobran131994` (MSSQL requires >=8 chars with upper/lower/digit/symbol). This rotation is mandatory because the old value is in git history.

Create `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.env` (already git-ignored by lines 11-12 of `.gitignore`) containing the secret, e.g. a single line `SA_PASSWORD=<new-strong-password>`. Docker Compose auto-loads a `.env` file sitting next to the compose file.

Create a committed template `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.env.example` (allowed by `.gitignore` line 13 `!.env.example`) with `SA_PASSWORD=` (no value) so other operators know what to set.

In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 11, replace the hardcoded `SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994` under the `db` service with the interpolated, fail-fast form `SA_PASSWORD=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env}`.

In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 14, replace the host-specific bind mount `/Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB:/var/opt/mssql/data` with the named volume `mssql-data:/var/opt/mssql/data` so the file is portable across operators/CI and avoids cross-OS mssql data-dir corruption.

In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 29, change the `backend` service's `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection` to interpolate the same secret and stop trusting any server cert: `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env};MultipleActiveResultSets=true;` (drop `TrustServerCertificate=True`).
Add a top-level `volumes:` block at the bottom of `docker-compose.dev.yml` (there is currently none) declaring the named volume: `volumes:` / `  mssql-data:` so the `mssql-data` reference resolves.

Optional but recommended: purge the leaked value from history with `git filter-repo --replace-text` (or BFG) on `docker-compose.dev.yml`, since rotating alone does not remove `Saobran131994` from prior commits.

Separately file/flag the higher-severity sibling issue the verifier called out: the production SA password `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` is committed in plaintext in `Backend/appsettings.json` (and duplicated under `Backend/bin/...`); that is a distinct finding and should be rotated and externalized too.
patch:
# docker-compose.dev.yml

  db:
    ...
    environment:
      - ACCEPT_EULA=Y
-     - SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994
+     - SA_PASSWORD=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env}
      - MSSQL_PID=Express
    volumes:
-     - /Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB:/var/opt/mssql/data
+     - mssql-data:/var/opt/mssql/data
    ...

  backend:
    ...
    environment:
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
-     - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=Saobran131994;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;TrustServerCertificate=True;
+     - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env};MultipleActiveResultSets=true;
    ...

networks:
  app-network:
    external: true

+volumes:
+  mssql-data:

# .env  (NOT committed — already matched by .gitignore lines 11-12)
SA_PASSWORD=<new-strong-password>

# .env.example  (committed — allowed by .gitignore line 13 "!.env.example")
SA_PASSWORD=
verification: Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml config` from the repo root: the rendered output must show the resolved `SA_PASSWORD`/connection string with no literal `Saobran131994`, `TrustServerCertificate`, or `/Users/saobranaung/...` path.

Temporarily rename `.env` and run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml config` again — it must fail with `error while interpolating ... set SA_PASSWORD in .env`, proving the secret is required and not defaulted.
`grep -RnE 'Saobran131994|/Users/saobranaung/Database|TrustServerCertificate' docker-compose.dev.yml` returns nothing.
`git status` shows `.env` is untracked/ignored and `.env.example` is the only new tracked env file; `git check-ignore .env` prints `.env`.

Bring the stack up with `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d`, confirm `docker volume ls` lists the `mssql-data` named volume, and that the backend connects to SQL Server (db healthy, no login failures in `docker logs dev-sqlserver`).
pitfalls: Rotation is required, not optional: editing the compose file does not remove `Saobran131994` from git history — change the actual SQL SA password and consider a history rewrite (git filter-repo/BFG), which requires a coordinated force-push. Switching from the host bind mount to a named `mssql-data` volume starts with an EMPTY database; the prior data under `/Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB` is not migrated, so reseed/restore TemplateDB after first `up` (or do a one-time copy into the named volume) if existing data matters. Dropping `TrustServerCertificate=True` means SQL Server must present a cert the client trusts; the official mssql image uses a self-signed cert, so for local dev you may need `Encrypt=False` (acceptable on the internal compose network) rather than re-adding `TrustServerCertificate=True`, which silently disables TLS validation. The `app-network` is declared `external: true`, so `docker network create app-network` must exist before `up`. The MSSQL image enforces password complexity; a weak rotated value makes the `db` container exit on boot.

approach: Move the existing `app.UseSwagger()` / `app.UseSwaggerUI(...)` calls (Program.cs:109-115) inside an `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` block so the OpenAPI spec and interactive UI are never served in production. The real defect is purely in Program.cs; launchSettings.json only affects local launch and needs no change.
steps: Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Program.cs.

Locate the unconditional Swagger calls at lines 109-115: `app.UseSwagger();` followed by `app.UseSwaggerUI(options => { ... });`. These currently sit AFTER the existing `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); } else { app.UseHsts(); }` block (lines 100-107), so they run in every environment.

Wrap both calls in `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { ... }`. The cleanest option is to fold them into the existing development branch at line 100-103 (add them right after `app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();`), or add a new dedicated `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` block immediately below — keep the `SwaggerEndpoint`, `RoutePrefix = "swagger"`, and `DisplayRequestDuration()` options exactly as they are.

Do NOT remove `builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(...)` or `builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer()` (Program.cs:27-63) — registering the generators is harmless and keeps the dev experience intact; only the middleware that exposes the endpoint must be gated.

Leave Backend/Properties/launchSettings.json unchanged: its `launchUrl: "swagger"` (lines 6, 16, 24) and `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` only affect local browser launch, not the deployed/production host (which sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT via the deployment). Per the verifier, launchSettings:6 is not the real exposure vector.

Confirm the production host does NOT set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development (check the deployment/IIS/Docker env vars). If for some operational reason production must run as Development, instead gate Swagger behind authentication and a non-guessable RoutePrefix rather than the environment check.
Build the Backend project to ensure no compile errors: `dotnet build "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend"`.
verification: Run the app with the production environment: `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run --project "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend"`, then `curl -k https://localhost:8000/swagger/v1/swagger.json` and `curl -k https://localhost:8000/swagger/index.html` — both must return 404 (not the OpenAPI JSON / UI HTML).

Run with `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` (or the default `dotnet run` which uses launchSettings) and confirm /swagger still loads the UI and /swagger/v1/swagger.json still returns the spec, so the dev workflow is unaffected.
Confirm `dotnet build` succeeds with no warnings/errors after the edit.
After deploy, hit https://<prod-host>/swagger and /swagger/v1/swagger.json and confirm 404.
pitfalls: If the production deployment (IIS web.config, Docker env, or host) sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT to 'Development' or leaves it unset on a Development-defaulting host, the IsDevelopment() guard will still expose Swagger — verify the deployed env var is 'Production'/'Staging'. Per the verifier, this finding on its own is Medium because unauthenticated report/export endpoints already reject calls; the severe attacker-runs-every-endpoint scenario only materializes combined with the weak-JWT-key finding, so address that JWT key finding too. Rollback is trivial: move the two calls back outside the if-block. No data migration or ordering concerns with other middleware.

approach: Replace the SDK-based dev image as the deployable artifact with a real multi-stage production Dockerfile (sdk build/publish -> slim aspnet runtime) that runs as the built-in non-root `app` user on non-privileged port 8080, add a Backend/.dockerignore to keep secrets/bin/obj/EF tooling out of the context, and add a `USER nginx` directive to the Frontend nginx stage. Keep Dockerfile.dev for local dev only; move EF migrations out of container startup into a gated step.
steps: Create `Backend/.dockerignore` (does not currently exist) to shrink the build context and keep secrets/build artifacts out of the image. At minimum exclude: `bin/`, `obj/`, `**/bin/`, `**/obj/`, `.git`, `.gitignore`, `Dockerfile*`, `appsettings*.json` (the committed `appsettings.json` holds the live `sa` credentials), `*.user`, `App_Data/`, `.vs/`. Note: excluding appsettings*.json means config must be supplied at runtime via env vars (the docker-compose already passes `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection`), so do NOT rely on the baked-in file in prod.

Add a new file `Backend/Dockerfile` (production, separate from the existing `Dockerfile.dev`) as a multi-stage build. Stage 1 `build` uses `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0`, restores against `API.csproj` (the assembly/output is `API.dll`), and runs `dotnet publish API.csproj -c Release -o /app/publish --no-restore`. Stage 2 `final` uses the slim runtime `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0`, installs tzdata for the existing `TZ=Asia/Yangon` requirement, copies only `/app/publish` from the build stage, switches to the image's built-in non-root user with `USER app`, exposes 8080, and sets `ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://0.0.0.0:8080`. The entrypoint is `dotnet API.dll` only — NOT `dotnet watch run` and NOT `dotnet ef database update` (see step 4).

Do NOT install `dotnet-ef` or run `dotnet ef database update` in the production image (the dev image does this at `Dockerfile.dev:26-31`). The `app` user is non-root and the runtime image has no SDK/EF tooling. Run migrations as a separate, explicitly gated operational step (e.g. CI/CD job or one-off `dotnet ef database update` against a connection string, or generate an idempotent SQL script via `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` and apply it with DBA review) so a running app container can no longer mutate the live schema on boot.

Harden the Frontend image at `Frontend/dockerfile:20`. The final stage is `FROM nginx:alpine` and runs as root. Add a `USER nginx` directive after configuring nginx. The stock nginx config listens on port 80 which a non-root user cannot bind; either (a) keep root only for the master process by leaving USER as-is and document it, or (b) preferred: switch the listen port to 8080 in a custom nginx.conf, `COPY` it in, then add `USER nginx`. Also fix the latent path bug on `Frontend/dockerfile:21`: it copies `--from=0 /app/build/` but Vite outputs to `dist/` (confirmed: `Frontend/dist` exists, no `build.outDir` override in `vite.config.ts`), so change the source to `/app/dist/`.

Update `docker-compose.dev.yml` only if you want compose to build the prod image; otherwise leave it pointing at `Dockerfile.dev` for local development. If you add a production compose file, point `backend.build.dockerfile` at the new `Backend/Dockerfile`, drop the `volumes: - ./Backend:/app` bind mount (it would shadow the published app), and map the host port to container `8080` instead of `5000`.

Remove the hardcoded SA password from `docker-compose.dev.yml` (`SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994`, lines 11 and 29) by moving it to a `.env`/secret — this is the same credential the .dockerignore now keeps out of the image, so the two changes must be consistent. (Tracked under the hardcoded-secrets finding; mentioned here so the .dockerignore exclusion of appsettings*.json doesn't break the app while leaving the secret in compose.)
patch:
# ===== NEW FILE: Backend/.dockerignore =====
bin/
obj/
**/bin/
**/obj/
.git
.gitignore
.vs/
Dockerfile*
**/*.user
App_Data/
# keep secrets out of the image; supply config via env vars at runtime
appsettings*.json

# ===== NEW FILE: Backend/Dockerfile (production, multi-stage, non-root) =====
# ---------- Build stage ----------
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY API.csproj ./
RUN dotnet restore API.csproj
COPY . .
RUN dotnet publish API.csproj -c Release -o /app/publish --no-restore

# ---------- Runtime stage (slim, non-root) ----------
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS final
ENV TZ=Asia/Yangon
RUN apt-get update \
    && DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends tzdata \
    && ln -fs /usr/share/zoneinfo/$TZ /etc/localtime \
    && apt-get clean && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/publish .
# aspnet:8.0 ships a built-in non-root user named 'app'
USER app
EXPOSE 8080
ENV ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://0.0.0.0:8080
# NO dotnet watch, NO `dotnet ef database update` at startup
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "API.dll"]

# ===== EDIT: Frontend/dockerfile (final nginx stage) =====
# BEFORE
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=0 /app/build/ /usr/share/nginx/html
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
# AFTER (fix dist path + run as non-root nginx)
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=0 /app/dist/ /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf   # listen 8080; user dirs writable by nginx
EXPOSE 8080
USER nginx
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
verification: Build the prod image: `docker build -f Backend/Dockerfile -t tradenet-api:prod ./Backend`. Confirm it succeeds and is built on aspnet (not sdk): `docker image inspect tradenet-api:prod --format '{{.Config.Cmd}} {{.Config.Entrypoint}}'` shows `dotnet API.dll` and no `watch`/`ef`.
Confirm the container runs as non-root: `docker run --rm tradenet-api:prod id` (or `docker run --rm --entrypoint id tradenet-api:prod`) prints `uid=1654(app)` style output, NOT `uid=0(root)`.
Confirm secrets/tooling are gone: `docker run --rm --entrypoint sh tradenet-api:prod -c 'ls /app; which dotnet-ef || echo no-ef'` shows `API.dll` present, no `appsettings.json`, and `no-ef`.

Confirm context shrank: with `Backend/.dockerignore` in place, `docker build` output shows a small build context (not hundreds of MB including bin/obj), and the final image is materially smaller than the SDK-based dev image (`docker images` size comparison).

Run end-to-end with config injected via env: `docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 -e ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection='...' tradenet-api:prod` and hit a controller route (e.g. swagger or a report endpoint) on `http://localhost:8080` — verify it serves without the baked appsettings.json.

Frontend: `docker build -f Frontend/dockerfile -t tradenet-fe:prod ./Frontend`, then `docker run --rm --entrypoint id tradenet-fe:prod` shows the nginx (non-root) user, and the served HTML is non-empty (proves the `/app/dist/` COPY path fix worked).
pitfalls: The aspnet:8.0 image's built-in `app` user cannot bind to privileged port 80, which is why the runtime uses 8080 and `ASPNETCORE_URLS` is set explicitly — any compose/ingress port mapping must target 8080, not 5000/80.; Excluding `appsettings*.json` via .dockerignore means the app will fail to start if it relies on a baked connection string. Ensure all required config (ConnectionStrings, JWT signing key, AWS S3 settings) is provided at runtime via env vars before deploying; otherwise the container starts as non-root but crashes on missing config.; Removing `dotnet ef database update` from startup means schema changes will NOT auto-apply. Migrations must be applied as a deliberate step before/at deploy; coordinate so a new release doesn't run against an un-migrated DB. This is intentional (prevents a compromised container from mutating the live schema) but is an operational behavior change.; The Frontend `USER nginx` change requires the listen port to move to 8080 (custom nginx.conf) and the cache/run/pid dirs to be writable by the nginx user; the stock nginx:alpine config listens on 80 and writes pid to /run as root. If you skip the custom conf, nginx will fail to bind/write as non-root.; Keep `Backend/Dockerfile.dev` for local hot-reload development; do not delete it. The new `Backend/Dockerfile` is the deployable artifact. Make sure CI/deploy references the new `-f Backend/Dockerfile`, not `Dockerfile.dev`.; Rollback: the change is additive (new Dockerfile, new .dockerignore) plus a small Frontend edit; reverting is just removing the two new files and restoring the Frontend nginx stage lines.

approach: Replace the single inline `app.UseCors(builder => ...)` block in Backend/Program.cs with a config-driven, environment-aware named policy: production gets only the exact HTTPS production origins (no localhost, no trailing slash, no method/header wildcards) and no AllowCredentials (this API authenticates with JWT bearer tokens, not cookies); development keeps the localhost origins via appsettings.Development.json.
steps: In Backend/appsettings.json, add a top-level `Cors` section listing only the exact production HTTPS origins (no trailing slashes, no localhost). Top-level keys today are Logging, AllowedHosts, JWT, ExcelExport, ConnectionStrings; add: "Cors": { "AllowedOrigins": [ "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com", "https://www.mpuecomuat.com", "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com" ] }

In Backend/appsettings.Development.json, add a `Cors:AllowedOrigins` array that includes the dev origins (capacitor://localhost, http://localhost, http://localhost:3000, http://localhost:5173, http://localhost:8100, https://localhost) so local React/Vite (5173) and Ionic webviews still work without touching prod config.

In Backend/Program.cs, register a named CORS policy via builder.Services.AddCors(...) right before `var app = builder.Build();` (currently line 97). Read origins from config: `var corsOrigins = builder.Configuration.GetSection("Cors:AllowedOrigins").Get<string[]>() ?? Array.Empty<string>();` (Array.Empty is in System, already imported at line 14).

In the AddCors call, build one named policy 'TradeNetCors' that calls `.WithOrigins(corsOrigins)`, `.WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE")`, `.WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type")`. Do NOT call AllowAnyMethod(), AllowAnyHeader(), AllowCredentials(), or add the "*" method — the API uses bearer tokens (see AddJwtBearer at Program.cs:72), so credentialed CORS is unnecessary.

Replace the entire inline CORS block at Backend/Program.cs lines 118-156 (the `#region Cors` ... `#endregion` wrapping `app.UseCors(builder => { ... })`) with a single `app.UseCors("TradeNetCors");`, keeping it after app.UseHttpsRedirection() and before app.UseRouting()/UseAuthentication().

Delete the now-dead inline origin list entirely, including the literal "http://localhost:*" (a no-op: WithOrigins does exact string matching and never matches a real Origin header), every trailing-slash origin (e.g. "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com/", "http://localhost:3000/" — origins are sent without a trailing slash so these never match), and the redundant per-method WithMethods(...) chain plus the duplicate WithMethods("*").
Leave the pre-existing duplicate `app.UseHttpsRedirection()` calls (Program.cs:117 and :159) as-is; they are out of scope for this finding.
verification: Build: `dotnet build` in Backend/ succeeds with no CORS-related errors.

Run the API in Production environment (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production) and send a preflight from a disallowed origin: `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<host>/api/<any-controller> -H 'Origin: http://localhost:5173' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — the response must NOT contain an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.

Send the same preflight with an allowed prod origin: `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<host>/api/... -H 'Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — response includes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET,POST,PUT,DELETE`, and does NOT include `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
Run the app in Development and confirm the Vite frontend at http://localhost:5173 still reaches the API (browser console shows no CORS error on report calls).
Grep confirms cleanup: `grep -nE 'localhost:\*|AllowCredentials|AllowAnyMethod|WithMethods\("\*"\)' Backend/Program.cs` returns nothing.
pitfalls: Order matters: AddCors must be called on builder.Services BEFORE builder.Build(); keep UseCors before UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization as it already is. Origins in WithOrigins must have NO trailing slash and NO path — the browser Origin header is scheme+host+port only; a trailing slash silently never matches. Dropping AllowCredentials is safe ONLY because auth is bearer-token (Authorization header) per AddJwtBearer at Program.cs:72 — if cookie-based auth is later added, you'd need credentialed CORS with an explicit origin allowlist (never combined with AllowAnyOrigin). Ensure the Production deployment actually sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production so appsettings.json (not Development) supplies the origins; otherwise prod could pick up dev localhost origins. Rollback: revert Program.cs and the two appsettings files; no data/migration impact.

approach: Add repo-root CI that gates the main branch: secret scanning (gitleaks), dependency/SAST scanning (Dependabot + CodeQL for JS & C#), and real build/lint/test jobs for both the Node 20 frontend and the .NET 8 backend. Fix the broken existing test.yml (wrong branch gating, EOL Node 16/18, dead `npm test`) and relocate it to where GitHub will actually run it.
steps: FIRST, fix the workflow LOCATION bug. All workflows currently live in `Frontend/.github/workflows/` but this is a SINGLE git repo rooted at `tradenet-admin-report/` (confirmed: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` = repo root, no `Frontend/.git`). GitHub Actions ONLY reads `<repo-root>/.github/workflows/`, so test.yml/release.yml/commitlint.yml almost certainly never run today. Create `/.github/workflows/` at the repo root and place ALL new workflows there. Move (git mv) the three existing files from `Frontend/.github/workflows/` to the repo-root `.github/workflows/` as part of this work, adjusting their `working-directory`/paths since they now run from repo root.

Create `/.github/dependabot.yml` (repo root) with three ecosystems: `npm` rooted at `/Frontend`, `nuget` rooted at `/Backend` (and `/Backend.Tests`), and `github-actions` rooted at `/` — each on a `weekly` schedule. This wires automated dependency-update PRs (npm audit equivalent) for the React app, the .NET 8 API, and the Actions themselves.

Add `/.github/workflows/codeql.yml` for SAST. Use `github/codeql-action` v3 with a matrix of two languages: `javascript-typescript` (autobuild is fine for the Vite/TS frontend) and `csharp` (manual build mode: run `dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release` so CodeQL can trace the .NET 8 compilation). Trigger on `push` to `main`, `pull_request` into `main`, and a weekly `schedule`. Grant `security-events: write` permissions.

Add `/.github/workflows/gitleaks.yml` for secret scanning using `gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2` with `fetch-depth: 0` checkout so full history is scanned. Trigger on `push` to `main` and `pull_request`. This is the control that would have caught the committed `appsettings.json` sa credentials (a separate finding). Set `GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}` env. Note: gitleaks-action requires `GITLEAKS_LICENSE` only for GitHub orgs; for the personal `SakBran` repo it runs free.

Replace the broken frontend test workflow. In the relocated `/.github/workflows/test.yml`: (a) change the `on:` triggers from `branches-ignore: [main, master]` (which excludes the deploying branch) to `pull_request: { branches: [main] }` plus `push: { branches: [main] }`; (b) replace the Node `16.x`/`18.x` matrix with a single `node-version: 20.18.0` to match release.yml and the actual build target; (c) bump `actions/checkout@v3`/`actions/setup-node@v3` to `@v4` and add `cache: npm` with `cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json`; (d) add `working-directory: ./Frontend` (via `defaults.run.working-directory`) since the workflow now runs from repo root; (e) replace the dead `npm test` step with `npm run lint` (the real script that exists in Frontend/package.json — there is no `test` script) and keep `npm run build`; use `npm ci` instead of `npm install`.

Add a new `/.github/workflows/backend-ci.yml` for the .NET 8 backend (none exists today). Trigger on `pull_request`/`push` to `main`. Steps: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` with `dotnet-version: 8.0.x`, `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, `dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-restore`, and `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-build` (runs the existing xUnit `Backend.Tests` project).

After verifying the new root workflows run green, configure GitHub branch protection on `main` (Settings → Branches): require the new CodeQL, gitleaks, frontend test, and backend-ci checks to pass before merge. CI files alone do not block merges without this rule.

Optional cleanup: the relocated `release.yml` already correctly targets `push: branches: [main]` and Node 20.18.0 — only update its checkout/setup-node to `@v4` and add `working-directory: ./Frontend` for the npm steps. `commitlint.yml` needs no logic change beyond the relocation and a checkout bump to `@v4`.
patch:
name: Backend CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  build-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      - run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      - run: dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-restore
      - run: dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-build
---
# .github/workflows/codeql.yml
name: CodeQL
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request: { branches: [main] }
  schedule: [{ cron: '23 4 * * 1' }]
jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions: { security-events: write, actions: read, contents: read }
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - language: javascript-typescript
            build-mode: none
          - language: csharp
            build-mode: manual
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
        with: { languages: ${{ matrix.language }}, build-mode: ${{ matrix.build-mode }} }
      - if: matrix.build-mode == 'manual'
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with: { dotnet-version: 8.0.x }
      - if: matrix.build-mode == 'manual'
        run: dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release
      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3
---
# .github/workflows/test.yml  (BEFORE -> AFTER, key changes)
# - on.push/pull_request: branches-ignore [main, master]  ->  branches [main]
# - node-version matrix 16.x/18.x  ->  single 20.18.0
# - run: npm test (no such script)  ->  run: npm run lint
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request: { branches: [main] }
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults: { run: { working-directory: ./Frontend } }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20.18.0, cache: npm, cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm run build
---
# .github/dependabot.yml
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: npm
    directory: /Frontend
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
  - package-ecosystem: nuget
    directory: /Backend
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
  - package-ecosystem: github-actions
    directory: /
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
---
# .github/workflows/gitleaks.yml
name: gitleaks
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request:
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }
      - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        env: { GITHUB_TOKEN: '${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}' }
verification: Confirm location fix: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` returns the repo root and the new files exist under `<root>/.github/workflows/` — NOT under Frontend/. Push a no-op commit/PR to `main` and open the Actions tab: previously zero runs appeared for main; now CodeQL, gitleaks, Test, and Backend CI should all trigger.
Frontend test job: green run that executes `npm run lint` and `npm run build` on Node 20.18.0 (no more 'Missing script: test' failure that the old `npm test` produced).
Backend CI job: `dotnet build` and `dotnet test` both succeed against `tradenet-admin-report.sln` (net8.0); the xUnit Backend.Tests run is reported.
Secret scanning: temporarily add a fake high-entropy key to a branch and open a PR — the gitleaks job must fail. Remove the test key before merge.
CodeQL: after first run, Security tab → Code scanning shows results for both `javascript-typescript` and `csharp`.
Dependabot: Insights → Dependency graph → Dependabot shows the npm, nuget, and github-actions ecosystems enabled (Dependabot will open update PRs on its schedule).
Run locally before pushing to catch obvious failures: `cd Frontend && npm ci && npm run lint && npm run build` and `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release`.
pitfalls: "LOCATION IS THE #1 TRAP: workflows under `Frontend/.github/workflows/` do NOT run for this single repo — GitHub only reads `<repo-root>/.github/workflows/`. If you add CodeQL/backend CI under Frontend/ it will silently never execute. Put everything at the repo root .github/. CI files alone do not enforce anything until you add branch-protection required-status-checks on `main`. `dotnet build` for CodeQL csharp/manual mode and for backend-ci can be slow and may need NuGet restore to succeed in CI — if the API has any machine-specific build deps (SkiaSharp native libs), the ubuntu-latest runner usually has them but watch for native-lib failures; pin a known-good SDK if needed. gitleaks scanning full history (`fetch-depth: 0`) WILL flag the already-committed appsettings.json sa credentials — that is expected and is the point; treat it as the trigger to rotate those creds and purge them (a separate finding), and use a `.gitleaksignore` only for confirmed false positives, never to suppress the real secret. Use `npm ci` not `npm install`; it requires Frontend/package-lock.json to be committed and in sync. Rollback is trivial: the workflow files are additive — delete them and remove dependabot.yml to revert; no runtime/data impact."

approach: Add Serilog as the structured logging provider with a centralized/file (and optional Seq) sink, capture an audit trail of every report and Excel-export call via a single request-logging enricher middleware (so all 158 controllers are covered without per-controller edits, using the existing ClaimTypes.Name user id), and expose ASP.NET Core HealthChecks (DB + export worker) for external monitoring/alerting.
steps: Add the logging/health packages to Backend/API.csproj inside the existing <ItemGroup> (after the Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Debug line at :25): Serilog.AspNetCore (8.0.x), Serilog.Sinks.File, Serilog.Sinks.Seq (optional central sink), and AspNetCore.HealthChecks.SqlServer (8.0.x). The framework-provided Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks / Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore come transitively with the Web SDK, so no separate add is needed for the EF/self check.

In Backend/Program.cs, wire Serilog into the host BEFORE 'var app = builder.Build();' (currently line 97). Add 'using Serilog;' and 'using Serilog.Events;' at the top, then add 'builder.Host.UseSerilog((ctx, sp, cfg) => cfg.ReadFrom.Configuration(ctx.Configuration).ReadFrom.Services(sp).Enrich.FromLogContext());' right after 'var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);' (line 21). This replaces the default console logger with Serilog driven by the Serilog config block in appsettings.json.

Register health checks before Build(): 'builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddSqlServer(builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("TemplateDB")!, name: "templatedb", tags: new[] {"db"}).AddDbContextCheck<API.DBContext.TradeNetDbContext>("tradenetdb", tags: new[] {"db"});'. (AddDbContextCheck ships with the framework's HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore.)

In the HTTP pipeline (after 'app.UseRouting();' near line 160, before UseAuthentication), add Serilog's request logging: 'app.UseSerilogRequestLogging();'. Leave it early enough to time the whole pipeline.

Add the audit enrichment so the request log line includes who/what/params. Immediately after UseSerilogRequestLogging, add a lightweight inline middleware that pushes the authenticated user id (the existing ClaimTypes.Name claim used across the report controllers, e.g. ImportPermitVoucherReportController.cs:84) and the query string into Serilog's LogContext: see patch. This makes every POST to /api/<Report> and /api/<Report>/Excel produce an auditable structured line with UserId, path, status, elapsed ms — no edits to the 158 controllers needed.

Expose the health endpoints AFTER 'app.MapControllers();' (line 168): 'app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready", new Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = c => c.Tags.Contains("db") });' and 'app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = _ => false });' (liveness = process up, no dependency checks). Add 'using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks;'. Note: /health/* must be reachable for the monitoring probe — the report controllers are [Authorize] but MapHealthChecks endpoints are anonymous by default, which is what you want for an uptime/alert probe.

Add the Serilog config block to Backend/appsettings.json. Keep the existing top-level "Logging" block (Serilog ignores it once UseSerilog is active, but leaving it avoids breaking any non-Serilog reader) and add a sibling "Serilog" section: MinimumLevel Information with Microsoft.AspNetCore at Warning, WriteTo File (rolling daily, App_Data/logs/log-.json with a 31-day retention) using CompactJsonFormatter, and optionally WriteTo Seq with serverUrl from config/env for the centralized sink. Do NOT hardcode a Seq API key; read it from configuration/secret. See patch for the exact JSON.

Add the same "Serilog" block (or just an override raising console verbosity) to Backend/appsettings.Development.json so local runs still log to console; in Development also add a Console sink. For production, point WriteTo:Seq:Args:serverUrl at the real central log server (or swap the Seq sink for Serilog.Sinks.ApplicationInsights / an OTLP sink if the ministry already runs an APM).

Alerting (process/config, no code): configure the monitoring/uptime system (or a load-balancer health probe) to poll GET /health/ready every 30-60s and page on non-200, and in Seq (or the chosen sink's backend) create a signal/alert on '@Level = "Error"' or on the existing 'Excel export {JobId} ... failed' message from ExcelExportWorker.cs:197 so background-worker failures and unhandled errors raise notifications.
verification: Build: dotnet build Backend/API.csproj -- must restore the new packages and compile (confirms namespaces/APIs match .NET 8).

Run the API and curl GET http://localhost:5xxx/health/live -> 200 'Healthy' even with DB down; curl GET /health/ready -> 200 'Healthy' when both SQL connections succeed, 503 'Unhealthy' if you point TemplateDB at a bad server (confirms the DB check actually probes the connection).

Hit any report endpoint with a valid JWT, e.g. POST /api/ImportPermitVoucherReport, then tail Backend/App_Data/logs/log-<date>.json: confirm a structured line exists with the request method/path, StatusCode, Elapsed, and the enriched UserId (the user Id from the token's ClaimTypes.Name) and QueryString -- this is the audit trail of who ran which report.

POST /api/ImportPermitVoucherReport/Excel and confirm the corresponding audit line plus, when the background worker processes it, the existing 'Excel export {JobId} ... completed/failed' lines now appear in the file/Seq sink (proves worker logs flow into the centralized sink, not just console).
If Seq is configured, open the Seq UI and confirm events arrive and that a signal on @Level='Error' fires on a forced failure.
pitfalls: Order matters in Program.cs: UseSerilogRequestLogging and the audit middleware must come AFTER UseRouting/UseAuthentication so context.User is populated (place the audit Use() after UseAuthentication if you find UserId is 'anonymous' for authenticated calls -- the request-logging summary still captures it via LogContext as long as PushProperty wraps next()). The /health/* endpoints are intentionally anonymous; do not put them behind [Authorize] or the uptime probe can't reach them. The File sink writes to App_Data/logs -- ensure the container/app pool user has write permission and that this path is on a persisted volume in the Dockerfile.dev deployment, otherwise logs are lost on restart; for true centralization prefer the Seq/APM sink over File alone. Do NOT commit a real Seq apiKey -- read it from env/UserSecrets. This change does NOT remediate the hardcoded sa credentials in appsettings.json (separate finding); logging the QueryString/params could itself record sensitive trade filters, so restrict log file/sink access accordingly. Rollback is low-risk: remove the UseSerilog/UseSerilogRequestLogging/health lines and the packages to fall back to default console logging.
estimatedTime: 4-6 hours (code + config); +1-2 hours to stand up the central sink and wire alerting

approach: Bump react-router-dom (and its transitive react-router) from 7.6.2 to the patched line >=7.15.0 (latest 7.17.0) within the same major version, refresh the lockfile, and smoke-test routing. The audit range for react-router is 7.0.0–7.14.2, so the verifier-corrected floor of >=7.15.0 is required; >7.11.0 is insufficient.
steps: cd into the frontend project: the working directory is "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" (path contains spaces — quote it).

Run the upgrade so both package.json and package-lock.json update together: `npm install react-router-dom@^7.17.0`. This rewrites Frontend/package.json:41 from "^7.6.2" to "^7.17.0" and bumps the transitive react-router entry in Frontend/package-lock.json (currently 7.6.2 at line ~11980) up to 7.17.0. 7.17.0 is the latest 7.x (dist-tag latest), and >=7.15.0 is the patched floor since the react-router advisory range is 7.0.0–7.14.2.

If you prefer to edit declaratively, change Frontend/package.json line 41 to "react-router-dom": "^7.17.0", then run `npm install` to regenerate the lockfile — but verify afterward that the transitive react-router resolved version is also 7.17.0 (npm may keep an old pin otherwise; see verification).
Confirm React peer compatibility is satisfied: react-router-dom@7.17.0 requires react/react-dom >=18 and this repo has react 19.1.0 (package.json:34), so no React change is needed.

Do NOT add or change any vite.config SSR/prerender settings or convert routes to data-router loaders/actions. Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx uses createBrowserRouter with element-only routes (no loader:/action: keys) and the app is a pure client-side SPA, so the SSR/single-fetch/turbo-stream/__manifest advisories are not exercised — the bump is the entire fix.
Re-run `npm audit` and confirm there are no remaining react-router / react-router-dom advisories.
Build to catch any API breakage: `npm run build` (runs `tsc && vite build`).
verification: From Frontend/, run: `node -e "const l=require('./package-lock.json'); for(const k in l.packages){ if(k.includes('react-router')&&!k.includes('@types')) console.log(k, l.packages[k].version);}"` — both node_modules/react-router-dom and node_modules/react-router must report 7.17.0 (not 7.6.2).

Run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --json | grep -i react-router`) — the previously-flagged react-router (high, range 7.0.0–7.14.2) and react-router-dom (moderate, range 7.0.0-pre.0–7.11.0) advisories must no longer appear.
Run `npm run build` and confirm it completes with no TypeScript/Vite errors (no breaking-API regressions from the bump).

Smoke-test routing in `npm run dev`: load /auth/signin (root redirect via the single <Navigate> in routes.tsx:66), sign in to hit the ProtectedRoute tree, then exercise a /Report/* page, a /User/Edit/:id param route, and the ScrollToTop behavior on navigation — all should render without console errors.
pitfalls: "Verifier correction honored: do not stop at >7.11.0 — the react-router advisory range extends to 7.14.2, so the patched floor is >=7.15.0; this repo should land on 7.17.0. Use `npm install react-router-dom@^7.17.0` rather than hand-editing only package.json, because the vulnerable version is the transitive react-router pin in the lockfile (line ~11980); a stale lockfile can leave react-router@7.6.2 installed even after package.json is bumped. The unrelated `storybook-addon-react-router-v6@2.0.15` and `@types/react-router-dom@^5.3.3` are for React Router v5/v6 tooling/types and are out of scope — do not touch them. This is a same-major (7.x) bump, so it is low-risk, but the build/dev smoke-tests above are still required because no automated route tests exist. Rollback is trivial: revert the package.json line and lockfile and run `npm install`."

approach: Tighten the single inline `app.UseCors(...)` policy in Backend/Program.cs: keep an explicit production allow-list with AllowCredentials, gate all localhost/capacitor dev origins behind `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()`, drop the inert trailing-slash and non-functional `http://localhost:*` entries, and replace the redundant chain of `WithMethods(...)` calls plus `AllowAnyMethod()` with a single explicit method+header set.
steps: Open Backend/Program.cs and locate the #region Cors block at lines 118-157 (the inline `app.UseCors(builder => { ... })` middleware overload — note the lambda parameter is named `builder`, which shadows the outer `WebApplication.CreateBuilder` builder; it is a CorsPolicyBuilder here).

Delete the five redundant `builder.WithMethods("GET"|"PUT"|"POST"|"DELETE"|"*")` lines (122-126) and the standalone `builder.WithHeaders("Authorization")` line (127). They are overridden later by `.AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader()` on line 153 so they currently do nothing.

Build the origin list in two parts. Declare the production origins as an explicit array WITHOUT trailing slashes and WITHOUT any localhost entries: "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com", "https://www.mpuecomuat.com", "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com". Remove the duplicate trailing-slash form "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com/" (browsers never send a trailing-slash Origin, so it is dead config).

Gate every localhost/capacitor origin behind the dev check. Reuse the existing `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()` (already used at line 100) so the dev origins are only added when running in Development. Drop the non-functional "http://localhost:*" entry entirely (ASP.NET Core `WithOrigins` does exact string match in net8.0 — it is not a wildcard and never matched anything) and drop the trailing-slash duplicates ("http://localhost:5173/", "http://localhost/", "https://localhost/", "http://localhost:8100/", "http://localhost:3000/").

Replace `.AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader()` with explicit allowances scoped to what the SPA actually needs: `.WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS")` and `.WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type")`. Keep `.AllowCredentials()` — it is now safe because every remaining origin is an explicit, fully-qualified string (no wildcard).

Remove the now-redundant second `app.UseHttpsRedirection();` while you are here is OPTIONAL and out of scope for this finding — do not touch lines 159-160 unless separately requested. Keep the change confined to the #region Cors block (lines 118-157).
verification: dotnet build the Backend project succeeds (the inline-middleware overload still type-checks; the lambda param renamed to `policy` is still a CorsPolicyBuilder).

Production preflight rejects localhost: run the API with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production and send `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<api>/api/<anyendpoint> -H 'Origin: http://localhost:3000' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — the response must NOT contain an `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000` header (origin not echoed = blocked).

Production allows the real SPA: same curl with `-H 'Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com'` returns `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
Dev still works: run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development and repeat with `Origin: http://localhost:5173` — it is now echoed back.
Confirm the wildcard removal had no functional cost: previously `http://localhost:*` never matched any real browser Origin, so no legitimate caller loses access.
pitfalls: Do not pair `.AllowCredentials()` with `.AllowAnyOrigin()` / a `*` origin — ASP.NET Core throws at runtime ("AllowCredentials and AllowAnyOrigin cannot be used together"). The new code only uses explicit origins, so this is fine; just don't reintroduce a wildcard.; Add only origins, never paths or query strings — `WithOrigins` matches scheme://host:port exactly, so trailing slashes (the removed `.../`) never match a real Origin header. Re-adding them is harmless but dead config.; If the mobile Capacitor app is built/run in a non-Development environment and genuinely needs `capacitor://localhost` / `http://localhost`, the dev-only gate will block it. If that is a real production client, add those specific origins to the production `allowedOrigins` list explicitly (still no wildcard) rather than removing the IsDevelopment gate.; Environment is driven by ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT; verify the deployed hosting (IIS/systemd/container) actually sets it to Production, otherwise the dev origins stay enabled.; Rollback is trivial: revert the single #region Cors block; no data, migration, or schema impact.

approach: Set explicit, coherent pool ceilings so backpressure queues gracefully instead of throwing connection-pool-timeout: cap each AddDbContextPool at poolSize 128 in Backend/Program.cs and add matching Max/Min Pool Size + Connect Timeout to the TemplateDB and TradeNetDBTest connection strings. Leave MARS=False and ExcelExport.MaxConcurrency=1 unchanged (verifier confirmed no streaming path runs overlapping queries on one context, so MARS is not needed and concurrency=1 leaves ample headroom).
steps: Open Backend/Program.cs. Locate the two registrations at lines 64-67: AddDbContextPool<ApplicationDbContext> and AddDbContextPool<TradeNetDbContext>.

Add an explicit poolSize argument to both calls: poolSize: 128. The poolSize is the last positional argument of AddDbContextPool, after the options lambda (signature: AddDbContextPool<TContext>(Action<DbContextOptionsBuilder> optionsAction, int poolSize)). Keep the existing UseSqlServer(...) options exactly as-is.

Open Backend/appsettings.json. On the 'TemplateDB' connection string (line 26) and the 'TradeNetDBTest' connection string (line 27), append connection-pool keywords to each: 'Max Pool Size=128;Min Pool Size=5;Connect Timeout=30;'. Keep the existing MultipleActiveResultSets=False, Encrypt=True, and TrustServerCertificate=True untouched. Do NOT add pooling keywords to 'DefaultConnection' (line 25) unless that local fallback is actually used in production.

Keep Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportOptions.cs MaxConcurrency at 1 and the appsettings.json ExcelExport.MaxConcurrency at 1. With one streaming export holding at most one physical connection on TradeNetDBTest, a 128-connection budget leaves ~127 connections for concurrent analyst report requests. Document this relationship in a comment above the AddDbContextPool calls so future increases to MaxConcurrency are weighed against Max Pool Size.

Do NOT enable MultipleActiveResultSets on the production strings. Confirmed: in Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportPermitDetailReport_Fast.cs the lookup queries GetPortNamesAsync/GetCountryNamesAsync (lines 115-116) are fully awaited before the AsAsyncEnumerable() reader opens (line 118), so no second query runs against an open reader on the same TradeNetDbContext. MARS would only be required if that ordering changed.
Rebuild the backend (dotnet build from the Backend project directory) to confirm the AddDbContextPool overload with poolSize compiles cleanly under EF Core 9.

Separately (out of scope for this finding, flag to the team): the plaintext 'sa' password in appsettings.json:26-27 should be moved to user-secrets / environment variables / a secret store for this government system.
verification: dotnet build the Backend project succeeds with the poolSize: 128 overload (EF Core 9 AddDbContextPool(Action, int)).

Smoke test: start the API, hit any paginated report endpoint and trigger an Excel export concurrently — both succeed, no 'connection pool timeout' or 'timeout period elapsed prior to obtaining a connection' errors in logs.

Load test the bottleneck: fire >128 concurrent report requests against a TradeNetDB-backed endpoint. With the explicit Max Pool Size, excess requests should block up to Connect Timeout (30s) and then either complete or fail predictably with a clear pool-timeout, rather than the old behaviour of EF handing out far more contexts than there are physical connections. Confirm no requests get a SqlException for MARS ('There is already an open DataReader...') — there should be none, since lookups resolve before the stream opens.
Optionally observe SQL Server sys.dm_exec_connections / sp_who2 during the load test: the connection count for the app login should plateau at or below 128 per pool rather than climbing unbounded.
pitfalls: Connection-string Max Pool Size and Program.cs poolSize must stay numerically aligned (both 128) — if poolSize exceeds Max Pool Size, EF can still hand out contexts that then block waiting for a physical connection, which reintroduces timeouts under load. If you ever raise ExcelExport.MaxConcurrency above 1, subtract that from the analyst-request headroom (each concurrent export holds one connection for its full duration via the AsAsyncEnumerable cursor in sp_ImportPermitDetailReport_Fast.cs). Do NOT flip MultipleActiveResultSets to True as a workaround; it is unnecessary for current code paths and would mask any future accidental overlapping-query bug. Rollback is trivial and low-risk: remove the poolSize argument and the three pool keywords. Min Pool Size=5 keeps 5 warm connections open per pool at idle — acceptable here, but drop it to 0 if the DB server is connection-constrained across many app instances.

approach: Replace per-cell `PropertyInfo.GetValue` reflection in both Excel writers with compiled `Func<object, object?>` accessor delegates built once per type via expression trees and cached in a shared static `ConcurrentDictionary`, so the reflective dispatch cost is paid once per export type instead of once per cell.
steps: Create a new shared cache class `Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelPropertyAccessor.cs` in namespace `API.Service.ExcelExport`. It holds the exported column names and a parallel array of compiled getter delegates for a given runtime `Type`, and caches one instance per `Type` in a `static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<Type, ExcelPropertyAccessor>`.

In that class, port the existing column-selection rule verbatim from both files so behavior is identical: `type.GetProperties(BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance).Where(p => p.GetIndexParameters().Length == 0)`. Capture each property's `Name` into a `string[] ColumnNames` and compile each property's getter into a `Func<object, object?>[] Getters` using `System.Linq.Expressions` (ParameterExpression of `object` -> `Expression.Convert` to the declaring type -> `Expression.Property` -> `Expression.Convert` result to `object` -> `Expression.Lambda<Func<object,object?>>().Compile()`). Expose `IEnumerable<object?> GetValues(object row)` that yields `Getters[i](row)`.

In `Backend/Service/ExcelExport/StreamingExcelWriter.cs`: change the field `private PropertyInfo[]? _properties;` (line 35) to `private ExcelPropertyAccessor? _accessor;`. Remove `using System.Reflection;` only if no longer referenced (it is still used elsewhere? no — after this change `PropertyInfo`/`BindingFlags` are gone from this file, so the using can be removed).
