For intermediate crumbs, render `labelFor(segment)`.

Remove the `'...'` truncation and the hover-only `Tooltip`: replace `{x.length > 10 ? '...' : x}` with the actual text and let CSS handle overflow. The antd `Tooltip` import can be dropped if no longer referenced.

In Frontend/src/components/PageHeader/styles.css add an ellipsis rule for the last crumb, e.g. `.page-header-breadcrumbs .ant-breadcrumb li:last-child .ant-breadcrumb-link { display: inline-block; max-width: 360px; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; white-space: nowrap; vertical-align: bottom; }` so long titles clip visually but remain fully present in the DOM and the `title` tooltip.

Verify the other callers (ListOfDirectors.tsx, CompanyProfile.tsx, MemberRegistrationReport.tsx, ExportsDrive.tsx, UserList.tsx, etc.) still pass a meaningful `title` prop; they already do (e.g. GenericReportPage passes `config.title`). No call-site changes are required since the `Props` contract is unchanged.
verification: Run `npm run dev` in Frontend and navigate to /Report/BorderExportLicenceTotalValueLicencesReport. The breadcrumb should read 'Home / Reports / Border Export Licence Total Value Licences Report' (the config.title) instead of 'Home / Report / ...'.
Hover the last crumb (or shrink the window so the CSS clips it): the full title appears via the native `title` tooltip and the complete string is present in the DOM (inspect element).
Visit several distinct reports and confirm each breadcrumb shows its own title rather than an identical '...'.
Run `npm run build` (or `tsc --noEmit`) in Frontend to confirm no unused-import/type errors after removing useState/useEffect/Tooltip if they became unused.
pitfalls: The cited 'line 736' in verifierNotes is the GenericReportPage.tsx call site, not this component; inside PageHeader.tsx the title renders at line 71 — don't edit GenericReportPage.; If you remove the `useState`, `useEffect`, or `Tooltip` imports, make sure they aren't used elsewhere in the file (they currently are not) to avoid a leftover-import lint error.; config.title is capitalized via the existing `.page-header-breadcrumbs .ant-breadcrumb-link { text-transform: capitalize }` rule in styles.css; titles that are already proper-cased multi-word strings are unaffected, but be aware all-caps acronyms would be down-cased — keep the existing capitalize rule scoped to crumbs only, as it already is.; Pure cosmetic change, no data/state migration; rollback is reverting PageHeader.tsx and styles.css.

approach: Pass an AntD `locale` to the existing `ConfigProvider` in App.tsx so DatePicker/RangePicker, Pagination, Empty and Popconfirm chrome can render in Myanmar or English. Drive the choice from a new redux-persisted `locale` slice (mirroring the existing `themeSlice`), keep dayjs's locale in sync, and localize the one hardcoded English `showTotal` string in BasicTable. This is the verifier-endorsed low-priority fix; a full per-string i18n dictionary for filter labels/titles is noted as a follow-on, not done here.
steps: Create a locale slice mirroring src/redux/theme/themeSlice.ts. New file Frontend/src/redux/locale/localeSlice.ts exporting `LocaleState { lang: 'en' | 'my' }` with `initialState = { lang: 'en' }`, a `createSlice({ name: 'locale', reducers: { setLanguage(state, action: PayloadAction<'en'|'my'>) {...}, toggleLanguage(state) {...} } })`, and export the actions plus the reducer as default.

Register the slice in Frontend/src/redux/store.ts: import `localeReducer, { LocaleState } from './locale/localeSlice'`, add `locale: LocaleState` to the `RootState` interface, and add `locale: localeReducer` to the `combineReducers({...})` call. It is automatically persisted because the existing `persistConfig` persists the whole root reducer.

In Frontend/src/App.tsx add imports at top: `import { useEffect } from 'react';`, `import enUS from 'antd/locale/en_US';`, `import myMM from 'antd/locale/my_MM';`, `import dayjs from 'dayjs';`, `import 'dayjs/locale/en';`, `import 'dayjs/locale/my';`. (`useSelector` and `RootState` are already imported.)

In App.tsx `App()`, read the language next to the existing theme: `const { lang } = useSelector((state: RootState) => state.locale);`, compute `const antdLocale = lang === 'my' ? myMM : enUS;`, and sync dayjs in an effect: `useEffect(() => { dayjs.locale(lang === 'my' ? 'my' : 'en'); }, [lang]);`.

Add the `locale` prop to the existing `<ConfigProvider>` opening tag in App.tsx (line 32): add `locale={antdLocale}` while leaving the entire `theme={{...}}` block unchanged. This localizes DatePicker/RangePicker, Pagination page-size/jump labels, Empty, and Popconfirm.

Localize the hardcoded pagination summary in Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx (lines 494-496). Add `import enUS from 'antd/locale/en_US'; import myMM from 'antd/locale/my_MM';` and read the language inside the component (`const { lang } = useSelector((state: RootState) => state.locale);` — BasicTable already uses hooks). Replace the inline English template `showTotal` with the locale's `Pagination.total_text` template, filling its {range0}/{range1}/{total} placeholders, with the original English string as fallback.

(Optional, recommended) Add a language switch beside the existing theme toggle. Grep `toggleTheme` under Frontend/src to find where the theme dispatch lives, then add a sibling Segmented/Switch dispatching `setLanguage`/`toggleLanguage` from the new localeSlice so ministry staff can pick Myanmar vs English.

(Follow-on, larger, out of scope for this Low finding) For full RDLC parity introduce a static dictionary keyed off Frontend/src/Report/config/reportConfigs.ts so report `title`, filter labels, the hardcoded 'Filter'/'Reset' buttons in GenericReportPage.tsx (lines 775/778), the 'From Date'/'To Date' placeholders (lines 430-431), and the 'No'/'Yes' boolean options (lines 460-461) carry both English and Myanmar strings. Track scope in ReportColumnComparison.md.
verification: cd Frontend && npm run build (or npx tsc --noEmit) — confirms the new slice, store wiring, and ConfigProvider `locale` prop type-check against antd 5.26 (both antd/locale/en_US and antd/locale/my_MM resolve; verified present under node_modules/antd/locale/).

Run the app (npm run dev), open any report under GenericReportPage, click a DatePicker/RangePicker — with lang='en' the calendar/month names render in English; switch language to 'my' (dispatch setLanguage('my') or via the new toggle) and observe the same picker now shows Myanmar month/weekday labels and the Pagination 'x-y of N total' summary switches to the my_MM template.

Verify persistence: switch to Myanmar, refresh the browser — redux-persist restores state.locale.lang='my' so the UI stays Myanmar (the existing persistConfig already persists the whole root reducer).
Empty-state check: load a report that returns zero rows — the AntD Empty 'No data' text follows the selected locale instead of the built-in English string.
pitfalls: Do NOT modify the existing `theme={{...}}` object on ConfigProvider — only add the sibling `locale` prop; reformatting the large theme block risks merge noise. The dayjs locale must be set globally (dayjs.locale) AND the dayjs/locale/my data imported, otherwise RangePicker still shows English month names even with the AntD locale set — AntD's date components read dayjs's active locale, not ConfigProvider alone. Because persistConfig persists the whole root, existing users have a persisted root without `locale`; redux-persist merges, so the slice's initialState (lang:'en') fills the gap on first load — no migration needed. The my_MM Pagination.total_text uses {range0}/{range1}/{total} placeholders; the English fallback string keeps pagination working if a future antd minor renames them. The full filter-label/title/column i18n (step 8) is explicitly out of scope for this Low finding — don't attempt the per-report dictionary in the same PR or it balloons into the 'Large' effort the audit flagged.

approach: Strip the prefilled demo credentials and credential logging from the sign-in form, add email-format validation plus autoComplete attributes, and make AuthContext.login() return a discriminated result (or thrown error) so SignIn.tsx can show the server's real failure reason (bad credentials vs. server unavailable) instead of a generic "Login fail".
steps: In Frontend/src/context/AuthContext.tsx, change the login signature so it conveys the failure reason instead of a bare boolean. Define a result type above the interface: `type LoginResult = { ok: true } | { ok: false; reason: 'invalid' | 'server' | 'network' };` and update the interface member to `login: (name: string, password: string) => Promise<LoginResult>;`.

In AuthContext.tsx login(), import `axios` (add `import axios from 'axios';` at the top) so you can use `axios.isAxiosError`. Replace `return false;` (line 47) inside the `!temp.token` guard with `return { ok: false, reason: 'invalid' };`, and replace the success `return true;` (line 60) with `return { ok: true };`.

In AuthContext.tsx, rewrite the catch block (lines 61-64): remove `console.log(ex);`, and classify the error: `if (axios.isAxiosError(ex)) { const status = ex.response?.status; if (status === 401 || status === 400) return { ok: false, reason: 'invalid' }; if (status) return { ok: false, reason: 'server' }; return { ok: false, reason: 'network' }; } return { ok: false, reason: 'server' };`.
In Frontend/src/pages/authentication/SignIn.tsx, delete `console.log('Success:', values);` (line 39) and `console.log('Failed:', errorInfo);` (line 64) so submitted credentials are never logged.

In SignIn.tsx onFinish, update the result handling: `const result = await auth?.login(values['email'], values['password']);` then `if (result?.ok) { ... success ... } else { const reason = result?.reason; const content = reason === 'invalid' ? 'Invalid email or password.' : reason === 'network' ? 'Cannot reach the server. Check your connection.' : 'Login failed. Please try again later.'; message.open({ type: 'error', content }); }`. Keep the existing `setLoading(false)` calls in both branches.

In SignIn.tsx remove the demo prefill: delete the `initialValues={{ email: 'demo@email.com', password: 'demo123', remember: true }}` object (lines 121-125). If you want 'Remember me' checked by default without prefilling credentials, replace it with `initialValues={{ remember: true }}`; otherwise drop the prop entirely.

In SignIn.tsx, harden the Email Form.Item (lines 133-141): add an email-format rule and give the Input the proper type/autocomplete: change the `rules` to `[{ required: true, message: 'Please input your email' }, { type: 'email', message: 'Please enter a valid email address' }]` and change `<Input />` to `<Input type="email" autoComplete="username" />`.

In SignIn.tsx, add `autoComplete="current-password"` to the password field: `<Input.Password autoComplete="current-password" />` (line 151). Note the Form already has `autoComplete="off"`; leave that as-is or remove it so the field-level autoComplete is honored by the browser password manager (optional).
verification: Run the frontend dev server (cd Frontend && npm run dev) and open the login page: confirm the Email and Password fields render EMPTY (no demo@email.com / demo123 prefilled).
Type an invalid email like 'abc' and blur/submit: the field shows 'Please enter a valid email address' and the API is not called.
Submit valid-format but wrong credentials (backend returns 401/400 or no token): toast reads 'Invalid email or password.' not 'Login fail'.
Stop the API or point baseUrl at an unreachable host and submit: toast reads 'Cannot reach the server. Check your connection.' (network) or 'Login failed. Please try again later.' (5xx).
With DevTools Console open, submit the form: no 'Success:' / 'Failed:' log lines and no credential or exception objects appear in the console.
Run `cd Frontend && npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) to confirm the new LoginResult type compiles and no other caller of auth.login() relied on the boolean return.
pitfalls: "AuthContext.login() return type changes from Promise<boolean> to Promise<LoginResult>, which is a breaking contract change. Grep for other callers before committing: `grep -rn \"\\.login(\" Frontend/src` — currently only SignIn.tsx uses it, but any new caller doing `if (await login(...))` would now always be truthy (object is truthy even on failure), so all callers must switch to checking `.ok`. Do the AuthContext change and the SignIn change together in one commit. The Form already sets autoComplete=\"off\"; field-level autoComplete=\"username\"/\"current-password\" may be ignored by some browsers while that is present — acceptable, but if password-manager integration is desired, remove the form-level autoComplete=\"off\". Rollback is trivial (revert the two files); no data migration or backend change involved."

approach: Make the client-side fallback in BasicTable honest instead of silent: when no server-side `onExcel` is supplied, relabel the Excel button to "Export current page" and (when the data spans more than one page) confirm/warn before exporting only the visible rows. This removes the silent-data-loss trap while keeping the single live caller (UserList) working, with no backend change.
steps: Open Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx. The buggy fallback is `exportClientTableToExcel` (lines 236-244), which calls `XLSX.utils.table_to_book(document.getElementById(tableId))` — the DOM only holds the current server-side page. The button label is the hardcoded 'Excel' (line 330) regardless of whether the export is the full server queue export or the partial DOM export.
Add an import for `Modal` from 'antd' (the existing import block on lines 5-13 already pulls Alert/Button/Empty/Flex/Pagination/Skeleton/Typography — add `Modal` to that list).

Compute whether the current export path is the partial DOM path. Right after the existing `query` useMemo (around line 202), the component already knows `onExcel`, `data.totalCount`, and `pageSize`. Add a derived const: `const isClientExport = !onExcel;` and `const isPartialExport = isClientExport && data.totalCount > pageSize;`.

In `exportClientTableToExcel` (lines 236-244), keep the table_to_book/writeFile logic but guard it so a multi-page dataset triggers a confirmation modal first. Wrap the actual export in a helper `runClientExport()` and have `exportClientTableToExcel()` show `Modal.confirm` when `isPartialExport` is true, only calling `runClientExport()` on confirm; when not partial, call `runClientExport()` directly.

Relabel the button so the partial path is never disguised as a full export. Replace the hardcoded `Excel` on line 330 with `{isClientExport ? 'Export current page' : 'Excel'}` and add a matching `title` tooltip when `isClientExport` (e.g. `title={isClientExport ? 'Exports only the rows on the current page' : undefined}` on the Button at lines 323-331).

Leave UserList.tsx (the only fallback caller) as-is for the export wiring — the relabel and warning now make its behavior honest. Separately, the verifier flagged that UserList renders a 'password' column (line 29 `displayData={['name', 'password', 'permission', 'isActive', 'id']}`); that is out of scope for THIS finding but should be raised as its own ticket — do NOT silently fold it into this change.

Do NOT touch MemberRegistrationReport.tsx or ListOfDirectors.tsx — both pass `onExcel={generateExcel}` and use the server-side job queue, so they keep the 'Excel' label and are unaffected (the audit's location list incorrectly included MemberRegistrationReport).
verification: Run the frontend (cd Frontend && npm run dev) and open the User list page (UserList, route backed by the `User` API). Confirm the export button now reads 'Export current page' instead of 'Excel'.

Set the page size so totalCount > pageSize (e.g. 10 of many users). Click 'Export current page' and confirm a Modal.confirm appears stating it exports only the current page's rows out of the total; cancelling produces no file, confirming produces a workbook with exactly the visible rows.

Open any GenericReportPage-backed report (e.g. MemberRegistrationReport / ListOfDirectors). Confirm the button still reads 'Excel', no modal appears, and clicking it still enqueues/downloads the full server-side export (onExcel/generateExcel path unchanged).
Run `cd Frontend && npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) to confirm no TypeScript errors from the new Modal import and added consts.
pitfalls: Scope: do not 'fix' MemberRegistrationReport.tsx or ListOfDirectors.tsx — they use onExcel (server queue) and the audit location list is wrong about MemberRegistrationReport. Touching them risks regressing the working full-export path.; isPartialExport relies on `data.totalCount` and `pageSize` being current; both are component state already used by the Pagination control, so no extra fetch is needed. If a future caller uses the client fallback without server pagination (totalCount <= pageSize), the modal correctly does not appear and behavior is unchanged.; The UserList 'password' column is a separate security/privacy concern (sensitive field rendered in a grid and exportable). File it as its own finding; do not bundle it into this UX change or the fix's scope becomes ambiguous.; Rollback is trivial: revert BasicTable.tsx; no data migration, no API/contract change, no backend impact.

approach: Add semantic table-header associations (scope="col" on every column header <th> and a visually-hidden <caption> built from the report title) and make the <thead> sticky via CSS so headers stay visible while scrolling tall/wide report grids. All changes live in BasicTable.tsx (markup) and style.css (sticky rules + visually-hidden caption); no API or data changes.
steps: Edit `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx`. Right after the opening `<table id={tableId}>` on line 362, add a `<caption>` element that uses the existing `title` prop (already destructured at line 128, defaults to 'Table'): `<caption className="visually-hidden">{title}</caption>`. Place it before `<thead>` (caption must be the first child of the table per HTML spec).
In the same file, add `scope="col"` to the row-number header `<th>` at line 377: change `{showRowNumber && <th>{rowNumberTitle}</th>}` to `{showRowNumber && <th scope="col">{rowNumberTitle}</th>}`.
Add `scope="col"` to the mapped column-header `<th>` at lines 382-390: add the attribute alongside the existing `key` and inline `style={{ width: column.width }}`.
Add `scope="col"` to the Action header `<th className="action-column-header">Action</th>` at line 393.
Do NOT add `scope` to the `report-header-row` heading `<th>` at lines 368-373 (it is a spanning banner, not a column header); leave it as-is.

Edit `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/style.css`. Extend the existing `.table-container thead th` rule (lines 132-139) by adding `position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 2;` so the whole header band (both the report-header rows and the column-header row) sticks to the top while the body scrolls vertically. Sticky must be on the `th` cells (not `thead`) because the table uses `border-collapse: collapse` (lines 4 and 114), where sticky on `thead`/`tr` is ignored by most browsers.

Because `border-collapse: collapse` drops the th's own border when scrolled, replace the header's `border: 1px solid #a3a3a3;` with a `box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px #a3a3a3;` (or add `box-shadow: 0 1px 0 #a3a3a3;` as a bottom divider) so the gridline stays visible over scrolled rows. Keep the existing `background-color: #e4e4e4;` (an opaque background is required so body rows don't show through the sticky header).

Add a `.visually-hidden` utility class to `style.css` (it does not exist yet) so the caption is announced by screen readers but not painted: `.visually-hidden { position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; padding: 0; margin: -1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0 0 0 0); clip-path: inset(50%); white-space: nowrap; border: 0; }`.

Optional (defer unless requested): to also sticky the first/No column horizontally you would add `position: sticky; left: 0` to the first `td`/`th`; this is fragile with the existing colSpan report-header rows and variable column widths, so leave it out of this fix unless a follow-up explicitly asks for it (the recommendation lists it as optional).
verification: Run `npm run build` (or `npm run dev`) in `Frontend/` and confirm no TypeScript/JSX errors from the new caption/scope attributes.

Open any report (e.g. an Import Permit report) in the browser, set the page size to 1000 (BasicTable.tsx pageSizeOptions, line 497), and scroll the grid vertically: the gray column-header band (and the centered report-header lines) must remain pinned at the top of `.table-container`.

Inspect the rendered table in DevTools: every column `<th>` (including the 'No' and 'Action' headers) has `scope="col"`, and the table's first child is `<caption class="visually-hidden">` showing the report title; the caption must not be visible on screen.
Run an axe/Lighthouse accessibility pass (or `npx @axe-core/cli`) on a report page and confirm the prior 'th elements should have scope' / 'table has no caption' findings are gone.
With VoiceOver/NVDA, navigate a data cell and confirm the column header name is announced (proves the scope association works).
pitfalls: Order matters in HTML: `<caption>` must be the FIRST child of `<table>`, before `<thead>`. Placing it after thead will render but is invalid and some assistive tech ignores it.; Do not put `position: sticky` on `<thead>` or `<tr>` — the table uses `border-collapse: collapse` (style.css lines 4 and 114), under which sticky on thead/tr is ignored by Chrome/Safari. It must go on the `th` cells.; The sticky header background MUST stay opaque (#e4e4e4); if it were made transparent, scrolled body rows would bleed through. Note the `.report-viewer-container` override at line 184 sets the header background to #ffffff — that is still opaque, so it remains fine, but verify sticky also looks correct inside the ReportViewer skin.; Because all thead rows (the colSpan report-header banner rows AND the column-header row) get `top:0`, they all pin to the very top and stack on top of each other only if their heights differ; in practice the browser stacks them in document order so they appear as one sticky block. If a future requirement needs the banner to scroll away while only column headers stick, switch to per-row `top` offsets instead.; The change is purely additive/CSS; rollback is reverting the two files. No data migration, no API contract change.

approach: Remove the unreachable dark-theme code path entirely (no UI ever dispatches it, the report grids are an intentional SSRS white-paper facsimile that must stay light, and the persisted value is latent debt). Hardcode AntD to defaultAlgorithm, drop the unused themeSlice/selectors, and bump the redux-persist version so any stale `mytheme: 'dark'` in users' localStorage is purged. This is lower-risk than building a real dark mode for a sensitive gov system. (If product instead wants a real toggle later, see pitfalls.)
steps: In Frontend/src/App.tsx, delete the theme selector and the conditional algorithm. Remove the imports `theme as antdTheme` from 'antd' (keep `ConfigProvider`), `useSelector` from 'react-redux', and `RootState` from './redux/store' (verify none are used elsewhere in this file first — they are not). Delete line 28 `const { mytheme } = useSelector((state: RootState) => state.theme);`.

In the same ConfigProvider theme object (App.tsx:90-93), replace the conditional `algorithm: mytheme === 'dark' ? antdTheme.darkAlgorithm : antdTheme.defaultAlgorithm,` with the static `algorithm: theme.defaultAlgorithm,` (import `theme` from 'antd'), or simply delete the `algorithm` property entirely since defaultAlgorithm is AntD's default.

In Frontend/src/layouts/app/App.tsx, delete the dead read on line 55 `useSelector((state: RootState) => state.theme);`. Then check whether `useSelector` and `RootState` are still used elsewhere in that file — `RootState` import is on line 33 and `useSelector` on line 32; if no other usage remains, remove both imports.

Delete the now-unused Redux theme slice: remove the file Frontend/src/redux/theme/themeSlice.ts (it exports `toggleTheme`, `ThemeState`, and the reducer, none of which will be referenced after the edits above).

In Frontend/src/redux/store.ts, remove the theme reducer wiring: drop the `import themeReducer, { ThemeState } from './theme/themeSlice';` line, remove the `theme: ThemeState;` field from the `RootState` interface, and remove `theme: themeReducer` from `combineReducers`. Because `combineReducers` requires at least one reducer, either keep redux-persist for a future-proof empty root or remove the store entirely if nothing else consumes it — see pitfalls. Simplest: replace the slice with a tiny no-op `app` slice OR keep the store but add a placeholder reducer; if the Provider/store is still mounted in main.tsx/index, do NOT delete the store file.

Bump the redux-persist migration version in store.ts `persistConfig` from `version: 1` to `version: 2` so previously persisted state (including any `mytheme: 'dark'`) is discarded on next load instead of rehydrating a dead key.

In Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/style.css, leave the report-grid colors as-is. They are an intentional SSRS/ReportViewer white-paper facsimile (.table-container at 112-139 and .report-viewer-container at 146-193) and must render black-on-white for print/official parity — do NOT tokenize them to follow a (now-removed) dark algorithm.
Run a project-wide grep to confirm no dangling references remain: `grep -rn "toggleTheme\|themeSlice\|state.theme\|mytheme\|darkAlgorithm" Frontend/src/` should return nothing after the edits.
verification: Build the frontend: `cd Frontend && npm run build` (or `tsc -b`) — it must compile with no unused-import / missing-module errors, proving themeSlice/RootState.theme references are fully removed.
`grep -rn "toggleTheme\|themeSlice\|state.theme\|mytheme\|darkAlgorithm" Frontend/src/` returns zero matches.

Run the app (`npm run dev`), open any report grid (e.g. a BasicTable report). The table renders the expected SSRS white-paper look (white .container, gray #e4e4e4 header band, black text) — unchanged from before.

Regression for stale persisted state: in DevTools Application > Local Storage, set `persist:root` to include a theme with mytheme:'dark', reload — with version bumped to 2, redux-persist discards the old payload and the UI stays light (no half-dark chrome).
pitfalls: combineReducers must contain at least one reducer. If `theme` was the ONLY slice, removing it makes `combineReducers({})` invalid and an empty RootState. Either (a) keep the store but register a trivial placeholder reducer, or (b) if nothing else in the app reads the store/Provider, also remove the redux Provider + PersistGate from main.tsx/index.tsx and delete store.ts. Grep `grep -rn "redux/store\|<Provider\|PersistGate\|useSelector\|useDispatch" Frontend/src/` BEFORE deleting to confirm the store is otherwise unused.; Do not skip the `version: 1 -> 2` bump: without it, redux-persist will rehydrate the old persisted blob and (if you removed the theme reducer) may log a non-fatal reducer-mismatch warning while leaving the dead key in localStorage.; Order: remove the consumers (both App.tsx files) BEFORE deleting themeSlice.ts and editing store.ts, otherwise the build breaks mid-edit on missing imports.; Rollback is trivial (revert the commit); no DB/server changes and no API contract is touched — this is frontend-only.; If product later wants a REAL dark mode instead of removal: keep the slice, add a toggle control (e.g. in HeaderNav.tsx) that dispatches toggleTheme, and tokenize ONLY the chrome — but keep .table-container / .report-viewer-container hardcoded light, since those grids are an intentional print/official ReportViewer facsimile.

approach: Reuse the app's established Ant Design `Popconfirm` pattern (already used in `ExportsDrive.tsx`) for both destructive actions: gate the header Logout menu item behind a confirm, and wrap the report Reset button in a confirm that only triggers when filters have actually been applied (so the common case of an unfiltered/idle page stays one click). Add an undo-style success toast on Reset for extra safety.
steps: Reset button — open Frontend/src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx. The Reset handler `resetFilters` is at lines 717-722 and the button is rendered at lines 777-779 inside the `<Space wrap>` next to the primary Filter submit button. The `hasAppliedFilters` boolean state is already in scope (used at lines 713/720/727).

Add `Popconfirm` to the existing antd import block at the top of GenericReportPage.tsx (currently imports Button, Card, Col, DatePicker, Form, Input, InputNumber, Row, Select, Space, message on lines 2-14). Insert `Popconfirm,` into that destructured import list.

In `resetFilters` (lines 717-722), after the existing `setRefreshKey((current) => current + 1);` line, add `message.info('Filters reset.');` so the user gets feedback that the action happened (the `message` API is already imported).

Replace the bare Reset `<Button onClick={resetFilters} ...>` (lines 777-779) with a conditional render: when `hasAppliedFilters` is true, wrap the button in `<Popconfirm>` so applied filters cannot be wiped by a stray click; when false (idle / no applied filters), keep the plain one-click button calling `resetFilters` directly. Move the `onClick={resetFilters}` off the button when wrapped (Popconfirm fires the action via its `onConfirm`).

Logout confirmation — open Frontend/src/layouts/app/App.tsx. The logout menu item is the `items` array entry at lines 70-83 (key `user-logout-link`). Because this is a Dropdown menu item config (not JSX), use `Modal.confirm` rather than a Popconfirm wrapper — Ant Design menu items render as plain rows and cannot host a Popconfirm child cleanly.

In App.tsx add `Modal` to the existing antd import (lines 1-12, which currently import Avatar, Button, Dropdown, Flex, FloatButton, Layout, MenuProps, message, theme, Tooltip). Insert `Modal,` into that list.

Change the logout menu item's `onClick` (lines 75-82) so that instead of immediately calling `auth?.logout()` and navigating, it opens `Modal.confirm({ title: 'Log out?', content: 'You will need to sign in again to continue.', okText: 'Log out', okType: 'danger', cancelText: 'Cancel', onOk: () => { message.open({ type: 'loading', content: 'signing you out' }); auth?.logout(); navigate(PATH_AUTH.signin, { replace: true }); } })`. Keep the existing loading toast inside `onOk` so behaviour after confirmation is unchanged.
Run the Frontend lint/build to confirm no unused imports and types are valid (see verification).
verification: Frontend lint/build: from Frontend/ run `npm run lint` (or `npx tsc --noEmit`) and `npm run build` — must pass with no unused-import or type errors from the new Popconfirm/Modal symbols.

Reset (applied state): open any report (e.g. an Import Licence report), set filters and click Filter so the grid loads (hasAppliedFilters=true), then click Reset — a Popconfirm pops with Reset/Cancel; Cancel keeps filters, Reset clears them and shows the 'Filters reset.' info toast.
Reset (idle state): on a freshly opened report with no filters applied, click Reset — it should clear immediately with no popconfirm (one-click), confirming the gating logic.

Logout: click the avatar dropdown -> logout; a Modal.confirm appears ('Log out?'). Cancel keeps the session; confirming shows the 'signing you out' loading toast, calls auth.logout(), and redirects to PATH_AUTH.signin.
Regression: confirm the 401 interceptor logout path in App.tsx (lines 132-157) is untouched and still logs out automatically on expired session (it must NOT prompt).
pitfalls: Do not wrap the antd Dropdown/Menu logout item in a Popconfirm component — menu items are config objects, not JSX children, so Popconfirm won't render correctly there; Modal.confirm is the right tool for the menu-item case. Popconfirm is only appropriate for the Reset button because it is real JSX.; Keep the loading toast and navigate call INSIDE Modal.confirm's onOk; if left outside they will fire before the user confirms, defeating the purpose.; Only gate Reset with Popconfirm when hasAppliedFilters is true — wrapping it unconditionally would add friction to the common idle case and regress UX.; Do NOT add a confirm to the 401 response-interceptor logout (App.tsx lines 132-157); that path is automatic/session-expiry and must remain prompt-free.; Rollback is trivial: revert the two files; no state, API, or data-migration changes are involved.

approach: Fix the real hook defects in useFetchData.tsx — the stale-closure `useCallback([])` over `url`, the missing in-flight cleanup, and the `any` typing — by inlining the fetch inside `useEffect` with an `AbortController` and making the hook generic. Do NOT route through axiosInstance and do NOT add an Authorization header: per the verifier, every call site fetches static public `/mocks/*.json` assets, so a JWT would be inappropriate and axiosInstance would prepend the API baseUrl and break these relative URLs.
steps: Open Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx.

Remove the useCallback import (no longer needed); keep useEffect and useState. Change the signature to a generic: `const useFetchData = <T,>(url: string) => {` (the trailing comma in `<T,>` is required so the .tsx parser doesn't read it as a JSX tag).

Type the state: `useState<T | undefined>(undefined)` for data, `useState<unknown>(null)` for error, `useState(true)` for loading. Note this changes the initial `data` value from `[]` to `undefined` — see pitfalls for the consumer impact.

Delete the `fetchData` useCallback entirely. Move the fetch logic directly inside the existing `useEffect(() => { ... }, [url])` so it re-runs correctly whenever `url` changes (this is what eliminates the stale-closure bug — the empty `[]` deps array no longer exists).

Inside the effect, create `const controller = new AbortController();`, call `setLoading(true)` and `setError(null)` to reset state on each url change, then call `fetch(url, { signal: controller.signal })`. In `.catch`, early-return when `controller.signal.aborted` so an aborted request does not call `setError` after unmount; add the same guard around `setLoading(false)` in `.finally`. Return `() => controller.abort()` as the effect cleanup.

Keep raw `fetch` and do NOT import or use axiosInstance, and do NOT add an Authorization header. The 12 call sites (e.g. Frontend/src/pages/corporate/Pricing.tsx, Faqs.tsx, Team.tsx, About.tsx, Licence.tsx; userAccount/Activity.tsx, Help.tsx, Security.tsx; dashboards/Projects.tsx; dashboard default LatestOrdersCard.tsx, RecentUsersCard.tsx) all fetch static public mock JSON via constant relative URLs like '../mocks/Pricing.json' and '/mocks/CountryOrders.json'. axiosInstance (Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts) unconditionally prepends envConfig.baseUrl and a Bearer token, which would break these relative URLs and leak the JWT to static-asset requests.

Audit consumers that previously relied on `data` defaulting to `[]`. Grep the 12 call sites and confirm components handle `undefined` (most pass `data` straight into a presentational component that already guards via the `loading` flag). If any consumer does `data.map(...)` directly without a loading/empty guard, default it at the call site (e.g. `const rows = data ?? []`).
verification: Run lint from Frontend/: `npm run lint` (or `npx eslint src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx`) — the react-hooks/exhaustive-deps warning on the old useCallback([]) must be gone, and `npx tsc --noEmit` must pass.

Run the dev server (`npm run dev`) and load a page using the hook, e.g. the Pricing page (Frontend/src/pages/corporate/Pricing.tsx). Confirm the table still renders the mock data — proving fetch still resolves the static '../mocks/*.json' URL without axios baseUrl rewriting.
Open DevTools Network tab while navigating to a mock-backed page: confirm the request goes to the static /mocks/*.json path and carries NO Authorization header (verifier requirement).

Stale-closure regression check: temporarily render `useFetchData` with a url prop, switch the url, and confirm the new URL is actually fetched (Network tab shows the second URL), not the original — the previously-captured url no longer sticks.

Cleanup check: navigate to a mock page and immediately navigate away; confirm no 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' / setState-after-unmount warning appears (the AbortController + aborted guard suppresses it).
pitfalls: Initial `data` changes from `[]` to `undefined`; any consumer doing `data.map(...)` before `loading` resolves will throw. Grep all 12 call sites first and default with `data ?? []` where a consumer expects an array. The `<T,>` generic syntax needs the trailing comma in a .tsx file or it's parsed as JSX. This is correctly scoped as Low/latent per the verifier: no src/Report/ page uses this hook and all URLs are constant, so there is no functional defect in the live report system — if these template/demo pages are slated for removal, deleting them is a valid alternative to fixing the hook. Do not "improve" it by switching to axiosInstance: that would break the relative mock URLs and send the JWT to public assets.

approach: Make the six helpers in BasicHttpServices.ts generic and return resp.data directly, dropping the pointless JSON deep-clone and the misleading PaginationType/AnyObject annotations; also drop the duplicate clone in useFormload.tsx.
steps: Open Frontend/src/services/BasicHttpServices.ts. Rewrite all six exported helpers (Get, GetSingle, Post, Put, Delete, ToggleUserActive) so each is generic in the response shape, returns `resp.data` directly, and no longer does `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...))`. Remove the local `const data = await resp.data;` / `const responseData: PaginationType = ...` lines.

For `Get`, keep its concrete return shape since its only caller (UserList) expects a paginated list: declare it `export const Get = async <T extends AnyObject = AnyObject>(url: string): Promise<PaginationType<T>> => (await axiosInstance.get<PaginationType<T>>(url)).data;`. This keeps `PaginationType` imported and UserList.tsx's `transformUserData(response)` typing intact.

For `GetSingle`, `Post`, `Put`, `Delete`, `ToggleUserActive`, make each generic with a default of `AnyObject` so existing untyped callers still compile, e.g. `export const GetSingle = async <T = AnyObject>(url: string): Promise<T> => (await axiosInstance.get<T>(url)).data;`. Do NOT annotate the parsed result as `PaginationType` anymore.

Keep the `Put` body logic that injects the id: `const jsonObject = data as { [key: string]: unknown }; jsonObject.id = id;` then `return (await axiosInstance.put<T>(url + '/' + id, jsonObject)).data;`. Keep `ToggleUserActive`'s URL `${url}/ToggleActive/${id}`.
After the rewrite, `PaginationType` is still used (by `Get`) and `AnyObject` is still used (as the generic default), so leave both imports in place. Confirm no now-unused imports remain.

Open Frontend/src/hooks/useFormload.tsx (note: lowercase `hooks`). At lines 19-21 remove the redundant outer clone: replace `const resp = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(await GetSingle(url + '/' + id)));` with `const resp = await GetSingle(url + '/' + id);`. The result is already a fresh object from axios; the extra round-trip is dead work.

Leave the other two callers as-is — Frontend/src/hooks/useFormActions.tsx (Post/Put/Delete/ToggleUserActive) ignores or destructures the result generically, and Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx passes Get's result into transformUserData which expects PaginationType. No caller-side type annotations need to change because the generics default to AnyObject / PaginationType.
From Frontend/, run `npm run build` (or `npx tsc --noEmit`) to confirm the generic signatures compile against all three callers with no new TS errors.
verification: From Frontend/: `npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) completes with no new TypeScript errors — the generic defaults keep useFormActions.tsx, useFormload.tsx, and UserList.tsx compiling unchanged.

Run the app and exercise the User CRUD screens (the only consumers): User list loads and renders rows (Get -> transformUserData -> BasicTable); open a user Detail/Edit (GetSingle via useFormload populates the form via formRef.setFieldsValue); create/edit/delete a user and toggle active (Post/Put/Delete/ToggleUserActive via useFormActions) all succeed with the success/error modals as before.
Grep to confirm the clone is gone: `grep -rn "JSON.parse(JSON.stringify" Frontend/src/services/BasicHttpServices.ts Frontend/src/hooks/useFormload.tsx` returns no matches.
Smoke-test a report grid to confirm no regression even though it bypasses this file: GenericReportPage.tsx still fetches via axiosInstance.post directly (line 654) and is unaffected.
pitfalls: Use the exact lowercase path Frontend/src/hooks/useFormload.tsx — macOS is case-insensitive so `Hooks` may resolve too, but the repo's real directory is `hooks`. Keep `Get` returning `PaginationType<T>` (not bare `T`): UserList.tsx's transformUserData spreads `data.data` and would break if Get were typed as a flat object. Keep the AnyObject default on the other five helpers so the existing untyped callers (which destructure `{ id, applicationNo }` etc.) still compile without edits. Do not delete the PaginationType or AnyObject imports — both are still referenced. This is purely a type-hygiene/perf cleanup limited to User CRUD; the verifier confirmed the 'Dates become strings / corrupts report data' concern does not apply (axios has no custom transformResponse in AxiosInstance.ts and reports never route through this file), so don't expect or chase any report-data behavior change. Rollback is a single-file git revert with no data/migration impact.

approach: x
steps: Belt-and-suspenders prod strip: edit Frontend/vite.config.ts and add an `esbuild: { drop: ['console', 'debugger'] }` block. esbuild.drop runs in production builds (vite build); the dev server (vite serve) keeps console output so debugging still works. This alone guarantees the 51 statements never reach the deployed bundle.

Create a tiny logger so genuine error reporting survives the esbuild drop and has one place to wire monitoring later. Add Frontend/src/services/logger.ts exporting `logError(context: string, err: unknown)` and `logDebug(...)`. In dev it calls `console.error`/`console.log`; in prod (`import.meta.env.PROD`) it is a no-op stub (and the future hook point for remote/Sentry reporting). Using a named method here also means esbuild.drop won't accidentally remove your real error reporting once you add a remote sink — only the literal `console.*` calls are dropped.

Frontend/src/context/AuthContext.tsx line 62: replace `console.log(ex);` in the `login` catch with `logError('AuthContext.login', ex);` and import the logger. Keep `return false;` — SignIn.tsx (line 42) already branches on the boolean, so the caller still reacts; we are only fixing the swallowed/unstructured logging.

Frontend/src/layouts/app/App.tsx: delete the two debug `console.log(collapsed);` calls (lines 115 and 122) inside the `isMobile` collapse useEffect — they log nothing useful. For the 401 interceptor (line 139) remove the `console.log('401 Unauthorized: Logging out...')`; the user already gets the antd `message.open({ type: 'error', ... })` toast, so the console line is pure noise. If you want a trace, replace it with `logDebug('401 Unauthorized: logging out')` instead.

Frontend/src/hooks/useFormActions.tsx line 121: delete `console.log(response);` (it dumps the full API response object). At line ~139 the existing `console.error(ex);` in the catch should become `logError('useFormActions.onFinishStepper', ex);`. Grep the file for any other `console.` and apply the same treatment.

Frontend/src/components/My Components/StepForm/StepForm.tsx line 37: delete the `console.log(stepContext.applicationNo);` inside the useEffect. If the effect body becomes empty, remove the whole useEffect (and the now-unused import if applicable).

Delete the dead template file Frontend/src/layouts/app/SideNav copy.tsx entirely (it is an unused copy of SideNav and a source of leftover console calls). Confirm it is not imported anywhere first: `grep -rn "SideNav copy" Frontend/src`.

Sweep the remaining ~30 files for debug leftovers: run `grep -rn "console\." Frontend/src --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx"`. Delete pure-debug `console.log` lines (e.g. SignIn.tsx lines 39 and 64 `console.log('Success:'/'Failed:'...)`); convert any meaningful `catch` logging to `logError(...)`. The esbuild.drop in step 1 is the safety net for anything missed.
verification: Build the prod bundle and confirm no console statements remain: `cd Frontend && npm run build && grep -rc 'console\.' dist/assets/*.js` should return 0 (esbuild.drop applied).

Run the dev server (`npm run dev`), open devtools, exercise: a failed login (wrong password) -> network shows the Auth POST returning non-200, the form shows the error state, and in DEV the console shows `[AuthContext.login] ...` from logError (in a prod build it shows nothing); toggle the sidebar on a mobile viewport -> no `true/false` spam; trigger a 401 (expired token) -> the antd 'login session is expired' toast appears with no console line.

`grep -rn 'console\.' Frontend/src --include='*.ts' --include='*.tsx'` returns only intentional logger internals (Frontend/src/services/logger.ts) — the previous count of 51 across 36 files is down to the logger util.
Confirm SideNav still renders after deleting `SideNav copy.tsx` and that `npm run build` / type-check (`tsc -b` or `npm run lint`) passes with no unused-import errors from the removed console lines.
pitfalls: esbuild.drop:['console'] strips EVERY console call in prod, including any future real error reporting — that is exactly why genuine errors must go through logger.ts (which calls a named function, not `console.*`, at its monitoring hook point) so they survive once a remote sink is wired in. Do not remove the `return false;` in AuthContext.login: SignIn.tsx line 42 awaits the boolean and drives the UI off it; dropping it would break the login error path. After deleting StepForm.tsx's useEffect, also drop the `useEffect` import if it becomes unused (TypeScript noUnusedLocals/eslint will fail the build otherwise). Before deleting `SideNav copy.tsx`, grep for imports of it; if found, fix the import first. This is a low-risk, easily reverted change (git revert of the source edits + vite.config), no data migration.

approach: x
steps: From the Frontend directory, rename the folder preserving git history: `git mv 'src/components/My Components' src/components/common`. Because macOS is case-insensitive and the new name shares no characters issues, this is a clean single move. (The folder is git-tracked, confirmed via `git ls-files`.)
Update `src/main.tsx` line 9: change `'./components/My Components/StepForm/StepContext.tsx'` to `'./components/common/StepForm/StepContext.tsx'`.
Update `src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx` line 23: change the BasicTable import path `'../../components/My Components/Table/BasicTable'` to `'../../components/common/Table/BasicTable'`.
Update `src/Report/Page/ListOfDirectors.tsx` line 23: same change `'../../components/My Components/Table/BasicTable'` -> `'../../components/common/Table/BasicTable'`.
Update `src/Report/Page/MemberRegistrationReport.tsx` line 20: same change `'../../components/My Components/Table/BasicTable'` -> `'../../components/common/Table/BasicTable'`.
Update `src/pages/User/UserList.tsx` line 2: change `'../../components/My Components/Table/BasicTable'` -> `'../../components/common/Table/BasicTable'`.

Update `src/pages/User/UserPage.tsx`: line 3 `'../../components/My Components/AjaxButton/AjaxButton'` -> `'../../components/common/AjaxButton/AjaxButton'`, and line 8 `'../../components/My Components/Form/BasicForm'` -> `'../../components/common/Form/BasicForm'`.

Then the importers can use `'@common/Table/BasicTable'` instead of `'../../components/common/...'`. If the alias was added: confirm `import ... from '@common/...'` resolves in both `tsc` and the dev server.
pitfalls: Use `git mv` rather than a Finder/OS rename so history and the index stay correct; on case-insensitive macOS a plain `mv` to a name differing only in case can be lost, but `My Components` -> `common` differs entirely so it is safe. Update all 8 import lines (UserPage.tsx has two) before building or the dev server will throw resolve errors. If you also add the alias, you must edit BOTH tsconfig.json and vite.config.ts — adding `paths` to tsconfig alone makes the editor happy but Vite will still fail to bundle. Rollback is a single `git mv src/components/common 'src/components/My Components'` plus reverting the import edits. No data migration, no backend impact (this is frontend-only).

approach: Resolve each react-hooks/exhaustive-deps warning at its source by adding the genuinely-missing dependency (never blanket-suppressing), wrapping referenced functions so they stay stable, and treating ChatBox as the one real latent bug. This is cleanup that only becomes enforceable once the ESLint 9 flat-config gate is fixed (the repo still ships a legacy .eslintrc.cjs that ESLint 9 ignores, so npm run lint currently errors out instead of reporting these).
steps: Reproduce first (the gate is broken so CI shows nothing). From Frontend/, ESLint 9.28 cannot read the legacy .eslintrc.cjs, so create a TEMPORARY flat config eslint.config.tmp.mjs that imports eslint-plugin-react-hooks + @typescript-eslint/parser and enables react-hooks/exhaustive-deps:'warn', then run: npx eslint --config eslint.config.tmp.mjs src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx . Confirm the 4 warnings (708, 68, 19, 23), then rm eslint.config.tmp.mjs. (The permanent gate fix lives in the broken-eslint-gate finding.)

Fix Frontend/src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx:708 (generateExcel useCallback). It calls normalizeReportFilters(values) on line 677 but the dep array is [config.excelFileName, config.excelRoute, form]. normalizeReportFilters is itself a useCallback defined at line 498 (deps [config.filters, derivedFilterValues]), so it is stable enough to list. Add it to the dependency array: change to [config.excelFileName, config.excelRoute, form, normalizeReportFilters]. This is benign (config is static) but silences the warning correctly without suppression.

Fix Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx:68 (the only genuine latent bug). The useEffect at line 41 connects MQTT and, inside its message handler (line 55), fetches axiosInstance.get(`chat?room=${FixedRoomNo}`); it also uses the module-level `topic`. FixedRoomNo = id from useParams (line 30-31), so if the route :id changes while the component stays mounted, the handler keeps querying the stale room. Add the missing dependency: change the dep array from [] to [FixedRoomNo]. `topic` is a component-scoped const literal ('chat-app-room2', line 39) and is also flagged-stable; if ESLint additionally requests it, include it too — adding it is harmless. The cleanup callback already calls client.end(), so re-running the effect on FixedRoomNo change cleanly tears down and reconnects.

Fix Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx:19 and :23. The fetchData useCallback (line 8) reads `url` (line 10) but has deps []; the useEffect (line 21) calls fetchData() with deps [url]. Correct the indirection so the memoized function actually tracks url: add `url` to the useCallback deps (line 19 -> [url]) and make the effect depend on the memoized function (line 23 -> [fetchData]). Net behavior is identical for callers but the dependency graph is now honest and both warnings clear.

Do NOT add eslint-disable-next-line comments anywhere for these four cases; all are resolvable by adding the real dependency. Reserve suppression only for cases ESLint cannot reason about, which none of these are.

Re-run the temporary-config eslint command from step 1 against the three files and confirm 0 warnings, then delete the temp config again. Leave the permanent enforcement to the broken-eslint-gate remediation.
verification: From Frontend/: create the temporary flat config (eslint.config.tmp.mjs importing eslint-plugin-react-hooks and @typescript-eslint/parser with react-hooks/exhaustive-deps:'warn'), run `npx eslint --config eslint.config.tmp.mjs src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx`, expect '0 problems', then delete the temp config.
Build still type-checks: `npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) from Frontend/ succeeds.

ChatBox behavior: navigate between two chat routes with different :id values without a full page reload (e.g. /chat/1 -> /chat/2) and confirm the message list refetches for the new room rather than continuing to show room 1 data.

Report Excel path: open any report, enter filters without clicking Filter, click the Excel export action, and confirm the enqueue request body (config.excelRoute POST) reflects the currently-entered filters — proving generateExcel still uses the up-to-date normalizeReportFilters.
pitfalls: Verifier confirmed only the ChatBox case is a real latent bug; the GenericReportPage and useFetchData warnings are benign (static config / constant-literal URLs into mock data) and are pure hygiene — do not over-engineer or claim this fixes wrong report output.; These warnings are invisible until the broken-eslint-gate finding is fixed (ESLint 9 needs an eslint.config.js flat config; the current .eslintrc.cjs is ignored and `npm run lint` errors with ERR no config). Fix/sequence this alongside that gate or the change is unverifiable in CI.; Never commit the temporary eslint.config.tmp.mjs — it resolves plugins from Frontend/node_modules but is not part of the build; remove it after each run (git status must stay clean).; For ChatBox, adding FixedRoomNo causes the MQTT client to disconnect/reconnect on room change; this is intended and the existing cleanup (client.end()) handles it — do not also store FixedRoomNo in a ref to dodge the dep, that reintroduces staleness.

approach: Drop the unused `moment` runtime dependency, then centralize the duplicated date/boolean formatting into the existing `src/utils/index.ts` barrel as parameterized helpers (output format + empty-value fallback are arguments, since callers use three different formats and three different fallbacks), and replace the per-file copies with imports.
steps: Remove `moment` from Frontend/package.json: delete line 32 (`"moment": "^2.30.1",`) from the `dependencies` block. Note the verifier correction: the declared range is `^2.30.1`, not pinned `2.30.1`. There are zero `moment` references anywhere under `Frontend/src` (verified via grep), so nothing else needs touching for the removal.

Do NOT create a new `src/utils/format.ts` file — reuse the existing barrel to avoid a second utils entry point. The date helper must be parameterized on output format AND empty/invalid fallback because callers use three formats (`YYYY-MM-DD`, `DD/MM/YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm`) and three fallbacks (`'N/A'`, `''`, `'-'`). See patch for `formatReportDate` and `formatBoolean`.

Update the 6 files that inline a `formatDate`/`formatLegacyReportDate` copy. There is no path alias configured (no `@/` in tsconfig/vite), so use relative imports. In each file: (a) add `import { formatReportDate } from '<relative>/utils';`, (b) delete the local helper definition, (c) replace call sites preserving the original format and fallback.

GenericReportPage.tsx (Frontend/src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx): remove the local `formatDate` (lines 110-117) and `formatBoolean` (lines 119-129). Import `{ formatReportDate, formatBoolean }` from `../../utils`. Replace `formatDate(value)` call sites with `formatReportDate(value, 'YYYY-MM-DD', 'N/A')` (this file's helper falls back to the raw string when present and 'N/A' when empty — keep that behavior via the `fallback`/`onEmpty` args in the patch). `formatBoolean` is only used here (lines 186, 372) — move it but keep identical Yes/No logic.

reportConfigs.ts (Frontend/src/Report/config/reportConfigs.ts): remove `formatLegacyReportDate` (lines 327-330). Import `{ formatReportDate }` from `../../utils`. Replace its body usages and the 8 inline `dayjs(String(filters.X)).format('DD/MM/YYYY')` call sites (lines 6696/6699, 12285/12288, 12399, 13022/13025, 13076/13079) with `formatReportDate(filters.X, 'DD/MM/YYYY')`. Keep the existing `dayjs` import (line 1) — it is still used elsewhere (e.g. line 328 originally, plus any remaining direct calls).

CompanyProfile.tsx (Frontend/src/Report/Page/CompanyProfile.tsx): remove local `formatDate` (lines 122-129, fallback `''`/raw string, format `DD/MM/YYYY`). Replace with `formatReportDate(value, 'DD/MM/YYYY', '')`. Also the title-string call sites at 347-349 use `dayjs(filters.ToDate).format('DD/MM/YYYY')` — optionally convert to `formatReportDate`. Keep the `dayjs`/`Dayjs` import (line 24) since `dayjs()` is still used (line 221) and the `Dayjs` type is referenced.

ExportsDrive.tsx (Frontend/src/Report/Page/ExportsDrive.tsx): local `formatDate` (lines 63-64) uses format `YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm` and fallback `'-'` (no isValid guard). Run `npm run lint` and `npm run build` (which runs `tsc && vite build`) in Frontend to confirm no unused-import errors (eslint runs with `--max-warnings 0`) and the type check passes.

`cd Frontend && npm run build` succeeds (tsc + vite build) with no unused-variable / unused-import TS errors and no leftover references to the deleted local `formatDate`/`formatLegacyReportDate`/`formatBoolean` definitions.

Manually load each affected report in the running app and confirm date columns render exactly as before: Generic/Member reports show `YYYY-MM-DD` (empty -> N/A), Company Profile / Card Lists / Directors-by-CRN show `DD/MM/YYYY` (empty -> blank), Exports Drive shows `YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm` (empty -> -), and Import Licence subtitles still show the `DD/MM/YYYY` From/To range.
Toggle a boolean-rendered column in a Generic report and confirm it still shows Yes/No (formatBoolean behavior unchanged).
pitfalls: Do NOT collapse to a single fixed-format helper — callers use three formats (YYYY-MM-DD, DD/MM/YYYY, YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm) and three empty fallbacks ('N/A', '', '-'); a non-parameterized helper would silently change dates across reports. Preserve each call site's original format/fallback (and the invalid-value behavior: GenericReportPage/MemberRegistration fall back to the raw string, CompanyProfile/CardLists to '', ExportsDrive to '-'). When deleting per-file local helpers, only remove the file's `dayjs` import if no other `dayjs()` call remains (GenericReportPage line 203/208, MemberRegistration line 132, CompanyProfile line 221, ListOfDirectors, and reportConfigs all still use dayjs directly — keep those imports). The audit's "36 call sites" is overstated (actual: ~8 'YYYY-MM-DD' literals, ~26 total dayjs() calls); scope the change to the 6 files that duplicate the helper rather than chasing a phantom count. Run `npm install` (not just edit package.json) or the lockfile keeps moment in the tree. Rollback is a clean git revert — no data/runtime migration, purely build-time/UI-formatting.

approach: Keep `reportConfigs.ts` as a thin barrel that still exports the two public symbols (`reportConfigs` and `reportConfigList`) every consumer imports, but move the shared helpers into one `_shared.ts` module and split the 134-report object literal into per-domain modules (Border, Import, Export, Misc) plus fold the existing factory-style `newReportConfigs.ts` into the same scheme via spread. This is mechanical and changes no runtime values, so all 154 importing pages and the report-parity work are unaffected.
steps: Create folder `Frontend/src/Report/config/reports/`. This will hold the per-domain config modules. Leave `reportTypes.ts` and `newReportConfigs.ts` where they are.

Create `Frontend/src/Report/config/reports/_shared.ts` and CUT lines 1-361 of `reportConfigs.ts` into it (everything BEFORE `export const reportConfigs` on line 363) EXCEPT line 8 `import { newReportConfigs } from './newReportConfigs';`. That block is all the shared building blocks: `pathakaStateFilterOptions`, `pathakaStatusFilterOptions`, `registrationApplyTypeOptions`, `detailApplyTypeOptions`, `voucherPaymentTypeOptions`, every `importLicence*Filter` const, the composed filter arrays (`importLicenceAmendFilters`, `importLicenceSummaryDetailFilters`, `importLicenceSectionMethodFilters`, `importLicenceSellerCountryFilters`, `importLicenceCompanyListFilters`, `importLicenceDailyFilters`, `importLicenceTotalValueFilters`, `importLicenceHSCodeFilters`, `importLicenceNewFilters`, `importLicencePendingFilters`, `importLicenceVoucherFilters`, `importLicenceExtensionFilters`), `importLicenceVoucherHeaders`, `formatLegacyReportDate`, `importLicenceRangeSubtitle` (used 42x), and `resolveImportLicenceVoucherColumns`. Keep its top import block (`dayjs` + the `ReportColumnConfig`/`ReportFilterConfig`/`ReportFilterOption`/`ReportPageConfig` imports from `../reportTypes` — note the path becomes `../reportTypes` since the file is one level deeper). Add `export` to every const/function in `_shared.ts` (they are currently file-private).

Split the big `reportConfigs` object literal (lines 363-13789) by key prefix into domain modules under `reports/`. Based on the actual key distribution, create: `borderReports.ts` (54 keys, prefix `Border*`), `importReports.ts` (28 keys, prefix `Import*`), `exportReports.ts` (26 keys, prefix `Export*`), and `miscReports.ts` (the ~26 remaining: `AccountSummaryReport`, the `WholeSale*`/`Retail*` registration group, `MPUReport`, `MPUReportV3`, `ListOf*`, `EIRCardBindReport`, `ChequeNoReport`, `OnlineFeesReport`, `MemberRegistrationReport`, `CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumber`, `PaThaKa*`, `RegistrationBy*`, `Company*`). Each module: `import { ReportPageConfig } from '../reportTypes';`, then `import { ... } from './_shared';` pulling only the helper symbols that module's entries reference (e.g. `borderReports.ts`/`importReports.ts` need `importLicenceRangeSubtitle`, the import-licence filter arrays, and `resolveImportLicenceVoucherColumns`; `miscReports.ts` needs `pathakaStateFilterOptions`/`pathakaStatusFilterOptions`). Export each as a typed map, e.g. `export const borderReports: Record<string, ReportPageConfig> = { ... };`.

Move the cut object entries verbatim into their domain module's map — do not retype the objects; cut/paste the exact `Key: { ... }` blocks so values, titles, columns and `subtitle` functions are byte-identical (this protects the report-parity behavior the repo cares about).

Reduce `reportConfigs.ts` to a barrel. Replace its whole body with: import the four domain maps plus `newReportConfigs`, spread them into the public `reportConfigs` map, and re-derive `reportConfigList`. Keep BOTH named exports (`reportConfigs`, `reportConfigList`) with identical names and shapes — 153 page components do `reportConfigs.SomeKey` and `Frontend/src/Report/reportNavItems.tsx` imports `reportConfigList`, so the public surface must not change.

Grep to confirm no page imports a helper symbol from `reportConfigs` directly (`grep -rn "from '.*config/reportConfigs'" Frontend/src` shows all 154 consumers import only `reportConfigs`/`reportConfigList`), so no back-compat re-export shim of `_shared` symbols is needed from the barrel.

From `Frontend/`, run `npx tsc --noEmit` and `npm run build` (Vite) to confirm zero type/build errors, then `npx eslint src/Report/config` (if the repo lints) to catch any unused-import leftovers in the split modules.
verification: From `Frontend/`, run `npx tsc --noEmit` — must report 0 errors (proves all moved helper references resolve across the new module boundaries).
Run `npm run build` (Vite) — build succeeds with no 'reportConfigs has no exported member' errors, confirming the two public exports are intact.

Assert the public surface is unchanged by count: before refactoring, capture the key count, then add a temporary vitest/log `expect(reportConfigList.length).toBe(<prev count>)`; the spread of the four domain maps + newReportConfigs must equal it (no key dropped or duplicated).

Load the app and open one report from each split domain (a Border*, an Import*, an Export*, and a WholeSale*/Retail* page) — tables, filters, and RDLC-style subtitles render identically to before (proves `importLicenceRangeSubtitle`/`resolveImportLicenceVoucherColumns` still produce the same output).
Run `git diff -M --stat` — `reportConfigs.ts` shrinks to ~15 lines, new files appear, and verbatim cut/paste lets git detect the entries as moves rather than rewrites.
pitfalls: Do the moves as cut/paste, never retype: the report-parity work depends on exact titles, column header text (English/Myanmar), and subtitle functions, so a typo here is a parity regression even though the finding is Low. Ordering: create `_shared.ts` and add `export` to its symbols FIRST, otherwise the domain modules won't compile. Keep `...newReportConfigs` LAST in the barrel spread to match the original ordering; the AlcoholicBeverages/DutyFree/ShowRoom/SaleCenter/BusinessServiceAgency/ReExport/EV keys live ONLY in `newReportConfigs.ts` today — keep them there, do NOT duplicate them into `miscReports.ts`, or last-spread-wins will silently re-establish which definition is canonical. `formatLegacyReportDate` is referenced only inside `importLicenceRangeSubtitle` (0 standalone uses), so keep it in `_shared.ts` next to that function, do not delete it. Rollback is trivial: this is a pure move with no behavior or data change, so `git revert` of the single refactor commit fully restores the prior state — keep it to one commit so `git blame` can follow the move.

approach: x
steps: Open Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx. Delete the import on line 19: `import * as XLSX from 'xlsx';` — this is the ONLY place the library is imported anywhere in src/ (confirmed by grep; all other `.xlsx` hits are filename strings).

Replace the body of `exportClientTableToExcel` (lines 236-244) so it no longer calls `XLSX.utils.table_to_book` / `XLSX.writeFile`. Build a CSV from the rendered DOM table (`document.getElementById(tableId)`) and trigger a download via a Blob + anchor. Name the file off `excelFileName` but swap the `.xlsx` extension for `.csv` (Excel opens CSV natively). See the patch.
In Frontend/package.json, remove the `"xlsx": "^0.18.5"` line from `dependencies` (currently line 44).

Regenerate the lockfile cleanly: from Frontend/, delete node_modules/xlsx and run `npm install` so package-lock.json no longer contains the `node_modules/xlsx` entry resolving to `https://registry.npmjs.org/xlsx/-/xlsx-0.18.5.tgz`.

Optional hardening for the one client-fallback consumer: Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx renders <BasicTable> WITHOUT an `onExcel` prop, so its Excel button is the only live caller of `exportClientTableToExcel`. The new CSV exporter keeps that button working. If a CSV (rather than true .xlsx) is unacceptable for the User list, instead pass `excelEnabled={false}` to that <BasicTable> to hide/disable the button, since user lists are not a business report. The three report consumers (GenericReportPage.tsx:794, ListOfDirectors.tsx:478, MemberRegistrationReport.tsx:257) all pass `onExcel={generateExcel}` and never touched the client path.
Run `npm run build` (or `tsc -b`) in Frontend/ to confirm there are no remaining references to the removed `XLSX` symbol.
Run `npm audit` in Frontend/ and confirm GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6 (Prototype Pollution) and GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9 (ReDoS) for `xlsx` no longer appear.
verification: From Frontend/: `npm install` then `npm audit` — confirm xlsx and its advisories (GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6, GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9) are gone.
Confirm the dependency is fully removed: `grep -n xlsx package.json` returns nothing under dependencies, and `grep node_modules/xlsx package-lock.json` returns nothing.
Confirm no dangling code reference: `grep -rn "from 'xlsx'" src/` and `grep -rn "XLSX\." src/` return zero hits.
`npm run build` succeeds (no TS error about an undefined `XLSX`).

Manual smoke test: open the User list page (UserList.tsx), click the Excel button — a file downloads and opens in Excel with the table contents. Then open any server-backed report (e.g. via GenericReportPage), apply filters, click Excel — it still produces the server-generated file (unchanged path through onExcel/generateExcel).
pitfalls: The client-side export now produces a .csv rather than a true .xlsx. The only consumer affected is UserList.tsx (the sole <BasicTable> without an onExcel prop); all business reports use server-side onExcel and are unaffected. If a real .xlsx is mandatory for the User list, the correct path is the existing server-side job queue, not re-adding the npm tarball — if SheetJS must be retained, pin the patched CDN build (https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-latest/...) in package.json instead of the registry tarball. Include the UTF-8 BOM in the Blob (as in the patch) or Myanmar/Unicode column data will be mojibake in Excel. Rollback is trivial: restore the import line and the original two-line function body, re-add the package.json entry, and run npm install.

approach: Add a single top-level React class `ErrorBoundary` (the only way to catch render throws in React 19 — there are no hooks for `getDerivedStateFromError`/`componentDidCatch`) and wrap `RouterProvider` with it in `Frontend/src/App.tsx`, rendering an antd `Result` fallback with a reload action. Also close the residual gap by adding `errorElement: <ErrorPage />` to the two `/Certificate` routes in `routes.tsx`, which currently have no error handling. This is defense-in-depth: per-route `errorElement` already covers the daily report pages.
steps: Create the boundary component at Frontend/src/components/ErrorBoundary/ErrorBoundary.tsx as a React class component. It is the only React-supported way to catch render-time throws (no hook equivalent exists in React 19). Implement static getDerivedStateFromError to flip a hasError flag, componentDidCatch(error, info) to console.error the error and component stack, and render() that shows an antd Result with a RefreshBtn fallback when hasError is true, otherwise this.props.children. Reuse the existing RefreshBtn component (Frontend/src/components/RefreshBtn/RefreshBtn.tsx, which already calls window.location.reload()) so the UX matches ErrorPage.

Export it from the components barrel Frontend/src/components/index.ts: add `export { ErrorBoundary } from './ErrorBoundary/ErrorBoundary.tsx';` next to the existing BackBtn (line 1) / RefreshBtn (line 15) exports.

In Frontend/src/App.tsx, import ErrorBoundary from './components' (add to the import block near lines 1-8) and wrap `<RouterProvider router={routes} />` at line 114 with `<ErrorBoundary>...</ErrorBoundary>`. Keep it inside StylesContext.Provider so the fallback still renders inside ConfigProvider/HelmetProvider and inherits the antd theme.

Close the residual per-route gap: in Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx, add `errorElement: <ErrorPage />` to both `/Certificate` route objects (lines 100-103 `path: 'Certificate'` and lines 104-107 `path: 'Certificate/:id'`). ErrorPage is already imported at line 9 of routes.tsx, so no new import is needed. This brings them in line with the other 8 routes that already declare errorElement.

Do NOT change the existing per-route `errorElement: <ErrorPage />` on /, /auth, /dashboards, /User, /Report, /Timeline, /Test, errors — they already handle router/loader errors and remain the primary mechanism for report-page throws. The new ErrorBoundary is a backstop only.
verification: Build/typecheck the frontend: from Frontend/ run `npm run build` (or `npx tsc --noEmit`) — it must compile, confirming the class component, the barrel export, and the App.tsx import all resolve under React 19 / TS.

Run the dev server (`npm run dev` in Frontend/) and temporarily add `throw new Error('boom')` to a chrome/layout component rendered above the router error boundaries — the page should show the antd Result 'Oops!' fallback with a working 'Refresh page' button instead of a blank white screen, and the error should appear in the browser console via componentDidCatch.
Navigate to /Certificate/123 and force a render throw in Certificate.tsx — confirm React Router now renders ErrorPage (the per-route errorElement) instead of unmounting to a blank screen.
grep -rn 'ErrorBoundary' Frontend/src — should now return the new file, the barrel export, and the App.tsx wrapper (previously zero hits).
pitfalls: A React error boundary only catches errors thrown during render/lifecycle of its descendants in the same render pass; it does NOT catch errors in event handlers, async callbacks, or setTimeout — those still need try/catch or the existing axios error handling. With react-router-dom 7, errors thrown inside a route element are caught by that route's `errorElement` first and never bubble to the top-level ErrorBoundary, so the ErrorBoundary is purely a backstop for provider/chrome-level throws (ConfigProvider, StylesContext, RouterProvider bootstrap) — do not expect it to replace the per-route errorElements. Do NOT reuse ErrorPage (Error.tsx) as the class-boundary fallback: it calls useRouteError and only works as a router errorElement, which is why the new boundary uses its own Result + RefreshBtn. Rollback is trivial: remove the App.tsx wrapper, the barrel line, and the two routes.tsx additions; no data or state migration involved.

approach: Rewrite `useFetchData` to run its async work inline in the effect (eliminating the empty-deps `useCallback` stale closure), route the request through the shared `axiosInstance` so JWT auth and `envConfig.baseUrl` apply, and add an `AbortController` for cancellation + out-of-order protection. Separately quarantine the 12 `*/mocks/*.json` consumers so placeholder data is not silently shipped to the production admin (and so the new axios path does not get `baseUrl` prepended onto a mock path).
steps: Open Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx. Add `import axiosInstance from '../services/AxiosInstance';` (the default export of src/services/AxiosInstance.ts) plus `import axios from 'axios';` (for isCancel), and remove `useCallback` from the react import — it is no longer needed.

Delete the `const fetchData = useCallback(async () => { const response = await fetch(url); const json = await response.json(); setData(json); ... }, [])` block entirely. The empty deps array is the stale-closure source and `fetch` is what bypasses auth/baseUrl.

Move the async logic inline into the existing `useEffect`. Create `const controller = new AbortController()` at the top of the effect, set `setLoading(true)` so re-fetches on url change reset loading, call `await axiosInstance.get(url, { signal: controller.signal })`, and `setData(response.data)` (axios already parses JSON — do NOT call `.json()`). In `catch`, guard with `if (!axios.isCancel(error) && !controller.signal.aborted) setError(error)`, and only `setLoading(false)` when not aborted. Return `() => controller.abort()` as the cleanup so unmount/url-change aborts the in-flight request and prevents setState-after-unmount and out-of-order writes. Keep `[url]` as the effect dependency.

Quarantine the mock consumers so dead placeholder data is not shipped, and because the axios request interceptor in src/services/AxiosInstance.ts prepends `envConfig.baseUrl` to `config.url`, which would corrupt a `../mocks/*.json` path. The 12 call sites are: src/components/dashboard/default/LatestOrdersCard/LatestOrdersCard.tsx:50 ('/mocks/CountryOrders.json'), src/components/dashboard/default/RecentUsersCard/RecentUsersCard.tsx:41 ('/mocks/ChannelUsers.json'), src/pages/corporate/Licence.tsx:12, src/pages/corporate/Faqs.tsx:14, src/pages/userAccount/Help.tsx:79, src/pages/dashboards/Projects.tsx:153 & 158, src/pages/userAccount/Activity.tsx:9, src/pages/userAccount/Security.tsx:46, src/pages/corporate/Pricing.tsx:9, src/pages/corporate/Team.tsx:13, src/pages/corporate/About.tsx:25 — each passing a static `*/mocks/*.json` literal.

For the quarantine, prefer the lowest-risk fix that matches how these template/marketing pages are actually used: replace each `useFetchData('../mocks/X.json')` with a direct static import of the JSON fixture (e.g. `import PricingData from '../../../public/mocks/Pricing.json'` — the same pattern already used in the *.stories.tsx files and in src/components/dashboard/projects/ProjectsCountCard/ProjectsCountCard.tsx). This removes the runtime fetch of placeholder data entirely. If a page is genuinely dead (corporate/marketing demo pages not reachable from the admin's routes), confirm against the route config and delete it instead.

After the consumers no longer call `useFetchData`, decide its fate: if no real-endpoint consumer remains, either remove the export from src/hooks/index.ts (line 4 `export { usePageContext, useFetchData }`) and delete the file, OR keep the hardened axios version as the canonical real-API fetch hook. Do not leave the old raw-fetch version exported as a trap.
verification: In Frontend, run `npx tsc --noEmit` and the lint step to confirm no unused-import (`useCallback`) or type errors after the rewrite.

grep to prove the trap is gone: `grep -rn "fetch(" Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx` returns nothing, and `grep -rn "useFetchData(" Frontend/src` shows no remaining `*/mocks/*.json` literals (either quarantined to static imports or removed).

Manual / behavior check: point the hardened hook at a real API path in a scratch component, open DevTools Network — confirm the request hits `envConfig.baseUrl + url` with an `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header (proving it now goes through axiosInstance). Navigate away mid-request and confirm the request shows as `(canceled)` and no "Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" warning appears in the console.

Render the previously-affected dashboard/marketing pages (LatestOrdersCard, RecentUsersCard, Pricing, Team, Faqs, Activity, Security, Projects, About, Help, Licence) and confirm they still display their fixture data from the static import and make no `/mocks/*.json` network request in the Network tab.
pitfalls: Do NOT swap `fetch` for `axiosInstance` while leaving the `../mocks/*.json` consumers calling the hook at runtime: the request interceptor in src/services/AxiosInstance.ts unconditionally prepends `envConfig.baseUrl` to `config.url`, so a mock path would become `<baseUrl>/../mocks/Pricing.json` and 404. That is why the consumer quarantine (static imports) must land in the same change, not later. Also: axios returns parsed data on `response.data` — calling `.json()` like the old code did will throw. The interceptor rejects when `config.url` is falsy, so never call the hook with an empty/undefined url. Static JSON imports require `resolveJsonModule` (Vite/TS default) — already used by existing *.stories.tsx so it is enabled. Rollback is trivial (revert the single hook file + consumer edits); no DB or API changes involved. Note: there is no eslint-disable in this file, so nothing to remove on that front.
