approach: Axios's default instance already parses the response body into `resp.data` as a live JS object, so the `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...))` deep-clone in each `BasicHttpServices` helper (and the duplicate clone in `useFormLoad`) is pure overhead. Return `resp.data` directly from each helper and drop the redundant re-parse in the hook.
steps: Open Frontend/src/services/BasicHttpServices.ts. There are SIX exported helpers, not five — the audit listed Get/GetSingle/Post/Put/Delete (lines 10-11, 17-18, 24-25, 37-38, 44-45) but ToggleUserActive at lines 51-52 has the identical pattern and must be fixed too.

In `Get` (lines 8-11): collapse the body to `const resp = await axiosInstance.get(url); return resp.data;` removing the `await resp.data` and the `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(data))` clone. (resp.data is not a Promise, so `await` on it is unnecessary.)
In `GetSingle` (lines 15-18): replace the `const data = await resp.data;` + clone with `return resp.data;`.
In `Post` (lines 22-25): replace `const temp = await resp.data;` + clone with `return resp.data;`. Keep the `await axiosInstance.post(url, data)` call unchanged.
In `Put` (lines 35-38): keep the `jsonObject.id = id` mutation and the `axiosInstance.put(url + '/' + id, jsonObject)` call; replace the temp+clone with `return resp.data;`.
In `Delete` (lines 42-45): replace the temp+clone with `return resp.data;`.
In `ToggleUserActive` (lines 49-52): replace the temp+clone with `return resp.data;`.

Note a pre-existing type inconsistency: GetSingle/Post/Put/Delete/ToggleUserActive are declared `Promise<AnyObject>` but currently annotate the local as `: PaginationType`. By returning `resp.data` directly you let the function's declared return type apply — keep each function's existing `Promise<...>` signature; do not re-introduce the `PaginationType` local annotation.

Open Frontend/src/hooks/useFormload.tsx. At lines 19-21 replace `const resp = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(await GetSingle(url + '/' + id)));` with `const resp = await GetSingle(url + '/' + id);`. GetSingle now already returns a parsed object, so the second clone is redundant. Everything downstream (`const { applicationNo } = resp;`, `formRef.current?.setFieldsValue(resp)`, `Object.assign(result, resp)`) works unchanged on the plain object.

If a defensive clone is genuinely wanted anywhere later, use the built-in `structuredClone(resp.data)` instead of the JSON round-trip — but it is not needed for these single-record form-load / user-CRUD flows.
verification: From Frontend/, run `npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) to confirm no type errors after removing the `PaginationType` local annotations.
Run `npx eslint src/services/BasicHttpServices.ts src/hooks/useFormload.tsx` to confirm no new lint issues (unused `data`/`temp` vars are gone).

Manually exercise the 3 consuming files in the running app: load a record-edit form (useFormload.tsx path), perform a user create/update/delete and the active-toggle in pages/User/UserList.tsx (useFormActions.tsx). Confirm the form fields populate and CRUD round-trips behave identically — same data appears, applicationNo still resolves.
Optional regression check: `grep -rn 'JSON.parse(JSON.stringify' Frontend/src/services Frontend/src/hooks` should return no matches for these files.
pitfalls: Scope/impact caveat per the verifier: these helpers are only imported by hooks/useFormload.tsx, hooks/useFormActions.tsx and pages/User/UserList.tsx — single-record form-load and user-CRUD flows with small payloads. The large report grids (BasicTable.tsx / reportConfigs) do their own fetching and never route through BasicHttpServices, so the real perf win is negligible; this is a clean-up, not a hot-path optimization. Functional risk: the JSON round-trip silently dropped any non-serializable fields (undefined, Date instances, functions). If any consumer relied on a field being absent/undefined after the clone, behavior could change subtly — but these endpoints return plain JSON DTOs, so this is not expected. Do not add `transformResponse` to AxiosInstance.ts; the default already parses JSON. No data migration or ordering constraints; rollback is reverting the two files.

approach: Two small, independent changes in the React grid: (1) lower the max interactive page size from 1000 to 200 in the shared `BasicTable` Pagination so no single page render/fetch is unbounded, and (2) stop the silent DOM-scrape Excel fallback from producing partial/sensitive exports — the only caller without a server `onExcel` is `UserList.tsx`, so either give it a server export or hide the Excel button when no `onExcel` is supplied.
steps: Open `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx`. In the `<Pagination>` element (line 497) change `pageSizeOptions={[10, 20, 50, 100, 1000]}` to `pageSizeOptions={[10, 20, 50, 100, 200]}` so the largest interactive page is 200 rows. This is the shared table used by all reports (GenericReportPage, ListOfDirectors, MemberRegistrationReport, UserList), so it caps every report's synchronous fetch+render at 200 rows; full exports already go through the server-side async `onExcel` job.

Decide how to handle the DOM-scrape fallback `exportClientTableToExcel` (lines 236-244), which is only reached when a caller passes no `onExcel`. The single such caller is `Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx` (it passes `api='User'` + `fetch`, no `onExcel`). Recommended minimal fix: stop rendering the Excel button when there is no server export, so the partial/DOM-scrape path can never run.

In `BasicTable.tsx`, gate the Excel `<Button>` (lines 323-331) on `onExcel` being present. Wrap it in `{onExcel && ( ... )}`. This removes the only way to trigger `exportClientTableToExcel`. After doing this you can also delete the now-dead `exportClientTableToExcel` function (236-244) and simplify `handleExcel` (246-262) to drop the `if (!onExcel)` branch, and remove the now-unused `import * as XLSX from 'xlsx'` (line 19) — only if no other code in this file references XLSX (grep confirms it's used solely by `exportClientTableToExcel`).

If product actually wants a User-list Excel export, instead of removing the button: add a server-side async export for User (mirror the `generateExcel` pattern used in `Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx:794`, `ListOfDirectors.tsx:478`, `MemberRegistrationReport.tsx:257`) and pass it as `onExcel={generateExcel}` to the `BasicTable` in `UserList.tsx`. Critically, the User export query/columns must NOT include the `password` field that `UserList` currently lists in `displayData` (line 29) — exclude it server-side.
Do not change `defaultPageSize`/`initialPageSize` (defaults to 10) or the `BasicTableQuery.pageSize` plumbing; the only edit needed for the page-size concern is the `pageSizeOptions` array.
verification: Build the frontend: from `Frontend/` run `npm run build` (or `npx tsc --noEmit`) — must compile with no unused-import/unused-var errors (especially after removing the XLSX import and exportClientTableToExcel).
Run `npm run lint` in `Frontend/` to confirm no new ESLint errors from the removed symbols.

In the running app, open any report page (e.g. GenericReportPage / Member Registration) and confirm the page-size dropdown now offers 10/20/50/100/200 and no longer offers 1000; selecting 200 renders without freezing.

Open the User list page (`/pages/User/UserList`): confirm the Excel button is gone (no DOM-scrape export), so it can no longer export only the current page or leak the `password` column. If you took the server-export route instead, click Excel and confirm the downloaded file contains ALL users across pages and has NO password column.
Reports that pass onExcel (GenericReportPage:794, ListOfDirectors:478, MemberRegistrationReport:257) still show the Excel button and trigger the server-side async job unchanged.
pitfalls: Removing `import * as XLSX from 'xlsx'` will break the build if any other code in BasicTable.tsx still references XLSX — grep confirms `exportClientTableToExcel` is the only user, so remove the import only together with that function.; Gating the Excel button on `onExcel` removes Excel export from UserList entirely. If that capability is required, you MUST take the server-export route (step 4) instead of just hiding the button — and that server export must exclude the sensitive `password` field that UserList currently surfaces in `displayData`.; Lowering the max page size to 200 changes behavior for any operator who relied on the 1000-row page; full datasets are still available via the server-side async Excel export, so this is acceptable, but mention it in release notes.; Rollback is trivial and per-change: revert the `pageSizeOptions` array and/or restore the unconditional Excel button + XLSX import. The two changes are independent and can be shipped separately.
estimatedTime: 30 min (button-gating route); ~2 hours if adding a password-safe server-side User export

approach: Add a DB-aware /health endpoint in the .NET backend, then add HEALTHCHECKs, service_healthy gating, a consistent restart policy, and resource limits across the compose services — prioritizing backend-on-DB readiness because the backend CMD runs an EF migration at boot.
steps: Add health-check packages to Backend/API.csproj. Add `<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.SqlServer" Version="8.0.2" />` (this version targets net8.0). The EF-context check API (`AddDbContextCheck`) is part of `Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore` — add `<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore" Version="8.0.17" />` if you prefer the EF-context approach over the raw SqlServer check.

In Backend/Program.cs, register health checks during service configuration. Insert immediately after the Excel export queue line (`builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);`, currently line 95) and before `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97). Register a DbContext check against ApplicationDbContext so /health reflects DB reachability: `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>(name: "templatedb");` (using the EF package). ApplicationDbContext is already pooled (registered at line 64 against the TemplateDB/DefaultConnection string), so the DI scope resolves correctly.

In Backend/Program.cs, map the health endpoint. Add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health");` right after `app.MapControllers();` (line 168). Map it as anonymous/unauthenticated so the container probe needs no JWT — `MapHealthChecks` is not affected by `app.UseAuthorization()` unless you add `.RequireAuthorization()`, so leaving it bare keeps it public. If you want a liveness probe that does NOT hit the DB, also add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = _ => false });` (requires `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks;`).

Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `db` service (lines 4-17): keep `restart: unless-stopped`, and add a HEALTHCHECK using the sqlcmd inside the mssql image plus resource limits. Add under the service:
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "/opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P \"$$SA_PASSWORD\" -No -Q 'SELECT 1' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    mem_limit: 2g
    cpus: 2.0
(Note: the 2022-latest image ships sqlcmd under /opt/mssql-tools18/bin and requires the -No/-C flag for the self-signed cert; verify the path with `docker exec dev-sqlserver ls /opt/mssql-tools18/bin`.)

Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `backend` service (lines 19-33): change the bare `depends_on: - db` (lines 32-33) to gate on DB readiness, add a HEALTHCHECK hitting the new /health endpoint on the in-container port 5000, add a restart policy, and add resource limits:
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 15s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 60s
    mem_limit: 1g
    cpus: 1.5
(The sdk:8.0 Debian image includes wget; if not, swap to `curl -fsS`. Use port 5000, not the host-mapped 5001, since the probe runs inside the container.)

Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `frontend` service (lines 35-46): gate it on backend health, add restart + resource limits. Change `depends_on: - backend` (lines 43-44) to:
    depends_on:
      backend:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
    mem_limit: 512m
    cpus: 1.0
For the Vite dev server (node:lts-alpine) add a wget-based check on 5173:
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5173 || exit 1"]
      interval: 20s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 30s

Add a HEALTHCHECK to the production frontend image. In Frontend/dockerfile (final nginx:alpine stage, after `EXPOSE 80`), add `HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost/ || exit 1` (nginx:alpine includes wget via busybox). Optionally also add resource limits + the same healthcheck to Frontend/docker-compose.production.yml `react-app` service which already has `restart: unless-stopped`.

Optional but recommended: add a HEALTHCHECK directly in Backend/Dockerfile.dev after `EXPOSE 5000 5001` so the image is self-describing even when run outside compose: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s --timeout=5s --start-period=60s --retries=5 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1`.
patch:
// Backend/Program.cs — register + map health checks
// after line 95 (AddExcelExportQueue), before line 97 (builder.Build())
builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>(name: "templatedb");   // <-- ADD

var app = builder.Build();
...
app.MapControllers();
app.MapHealthChecks("/health");                                      // <-- ADD (anonymous)
app.UseStaticFiles();
app.Run();

# docker-compose.dev.yml — backend service (was lines 19-33)
  backend:
    build:
      context: ./Backend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    ports:
      - "5001:5000"
    volumes:
      - ./Backend:/app
    environment:
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
      - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=Saobran131994;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;TrustServerCertificate=True;
    networks:
      - app-network
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy      # was:  - db   (ordering only)
    restart: unless-stopped             # ADD (was missing -> inconsistent)
    healthcheck:                        # ADD
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 15s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 60s
    mem_limit: 1g                       # ADD
    cpus: 1.5                           # ADD

# docker-compose.dev.yml — db service healthcheck (add under existing restart: unless-stopped, line 17)
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "/opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P \"$$SA_PASSWORD\" -No -Q 'SELECT 1' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    mem_limit: 2g
    cpus: 2.0
verification: Build the backend: `dotnet build` in Backend/ must succeed after adding the NuGet packages (confirms AddHealthChecks/AddDbContextCheck/MapHealthChecks resolve).

Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build`. With the SQL volume cold-starting, the backend container should now WAIT (status 'created'/'health: starting') until `dev-sqlserver` reports healthy, instead of failing `dotnet ef database update` and crash-looping.
Run `docker ps` and confirm each container shows a `(healthy)` status column after start_period elapses. `docker inspect --format '{{json .State.Health}}' <container>` should show Status: healthy.

Hit the endpoint: `curl -i http://localhost:5001/health` should return HTTP 200 with body `Healthy` while the DB is up, and a 503 `Unhealthy` if you stop the db container (`docker stop dev-sqlserver`) — confirming the probe is DB-aware and unauthenticated (no JWT required).
Confirm resource caps applied: `docker inspect --format '{{.HostConfig.Memory}} {{.HostConfig.NanoCpus}}' <backend-container>` returns non-zero values.
pitfalls: Inside-container probes must target the container port (backend 5000, not host 5001; frontend 5173, not 3000). The mssql healthcheck uses $$SA_PASSWORD (double $ escapes compose interpolation) and the tools path differs by image: mssql/server:2022-latest uses /opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd and needs -No (or -C) for the self-signed cert — verify with `docker exec dev-sqlserver ls /opt/mssql-tools18/bin` before committing. `mem_limit`/`cpus` are Compose v2 spec keys honored by `docker compose` for non-swarm runs (do not use `deploy.resources.limits` here, which is ignored outside swarm). depends_on: condition: service_healthy makes startup slower (backend now waits on DB start_period); keep start_period generous on first cold-volume migration. The /health endpoint is intentionally public — do not chain `.RequireAuthorization()` or the container probe will get 401 and report unhealthy. Rollback is config-only: revert the compose/Program.cs/csproj edits; no data migration involved.

approach: Harden transport config in Backend/Program.cs and Backend/appsettings.json: prioritize removing TrustServerCertificate=True from the two remote, internet-exposed DB connections (203.81.66.111) and switching to validated TLS; add a tuned HstsOptions policy; tighten AllowedHosts; drop the duplicate UseHttpsRedirection; and document that the nginx frontend image relies on an external TLS-terminating reverse proxy. The verifier flagged the SQL DB-channel item as the only substantive fix here, so do that first.
steps: PRIORITY 1 (real security item) — fix DB-channel TLS in Backend/appsettings.json lines 26-27. Both 'TemplateDB' and 'TradeNetDBTest' point at the internet-exposed host 203.81.66.111,14330 with Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;. Remove TrustServerCertificate=True so the server certificate's chain/name is actually validated. If the SQL Server presents a cert issued by a private/internal CA, install that CA into the app host's trust store and add the host's real DNS name (matching the cert CN/SAN) to the Server= value instead of the bare IP; if it presents a self-signed cert that cannot be replaced short-term, pin it via HostNameInCertificate=<cert-subject> rather than blanket-trusting. Leave the local 'DefaultConnection' (Server=localhost;Trusted_Connection=True) as-is for dev.

Note for the report (do NOT fix here): the same lines 26-27 hardcode User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022 in source control, and appsettings.json line 10 has a weak hardcoded JWT:Key. Those are secrets-management defects tracked under a separate finding (verifier note 4); flag them but keep them out of this transport change.

PRIORITY 2 — tune HSTS in Backend/Program.cs. Today the else branch at lines 104-107 calls bare app.UseHsts() (default 30-day max-age, no preload). Register a tuned policy in the service-configuration section (before line 97 'var app = builder.Build();'), e.g. after the AddExcelExportQueue call on line 95, add a builder.Services.AddHsts(...) block setting MaxAge to 365 days, IncludeSubDomains, and Preload. Keep the existing app.UseHsts() inside the non-development else branch (lines 104-107) — UseHsts then picks up the configured options.

PRIORITY 3 (cosmetic, per verifier note 1) — remove the duplicate app.UseHttpsRedirection(). It is called at both line 117 and line 159. Delete the first one (line 117) and keep the call at line 159 so redirection runs in correct pipeline order alongside UseRouting/UseForwardedHeaders. This is copy-paste cleanup, not a security fix.

PRIORITY 4 (low/info hygiene, per verifier note 2) — narrow AllowedHosts in Backend/appsettings.json line 8 from "*" to the explicit production hostnames already trusted by CORS in Program.cs lines 135-153, e.g. "reportuat.myanmartradenet.com;myanmartradenet.com". Provide the dev override in appsettings.Development.json (currently only has a Logging block) so local hosts/localhost still work without editing the committed prod file.

PRIORITY 5 (documentation/codify TLS termination) — the Frontend nginx image (Frontend/dockerfile line 20 'FROM nginx:alpine', EXPOSE 80 only) ships the stock nginx default.conf and serves plain HTTP on :80; docker-compose.production.yml maps 3000:80 with no TLS. Document in the dockerfile header and docker-compose.production.yml that HTTPS/TLS is terminated by an external reverse proxy (the same proxy that must forward X-Forwarded-Proto, which Program.cs lines 161-165 already honor via UseForwardedHeaders). Optionally codify it: add a custom nginx.conf (listen 443 ssl; with cert/key mounts) and COPY it in the dockerfile, or commit the reverse-proxy config so TLS termination is no longer undocumented/implicit.
Build and confirm: dotnet build the Backend project to ensure the AddHsts/connection-string edits compile and the app still starts.
verification: dotnet build on Backend succeeds and the app starts in Production environment (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run).

DB-channel TLS: with TrustServerCertificate=False, the two TemplateDB/TradeNetDBTest connections still open successfully against 203.81.66.111 (hit any report endpoint that queries them, e.g. via Swagger at /swagger). A failure here means the server cert chain/name isn't trusted yet — install the CA / fix the Server= DNS name rather than reverting to TrustServerCertificate=True.
HSTS: curl -I https://<prod-host>/ shows 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload' (only in Production, since UseHsts is in the non-development branch).
HTTPS redirect: curl -I http://<prod-host>/ returns 307/308 to https. grep -c 'UseHttpsRedirection' Backend/Program.cs returns 1 (no longer 2).
AllowedHosts: a request with a spoofed Host header (curl -H 'Host: evil.example.com' http://<host>/swagger) returns 400 Bad Request in Production, while localhost still works in Development.
pitfalls: "Order/priority: do PRIORITY 1 (DB TLS) first — it is the only item the verifier considers substantive. Removing TrustServerCertificate=True will BREAK DB connectivity if the SQL Server's cert is self-signed or its CN/SAN does not match the connection's Server= value; have the validated cert / CA trust (or HostNameInCertificate pinning) ready before deploying, or staging will fail to reach the DB. Roll back by temporarily restoring TrustServerCertificate=True only if connectivity is lost and re-prioritize the cert fix. Do NOT touch the local DefaultConnection. Tightening AllowedHosts will 400 any host not listed — include every prod hostname and the load-balancer/proxy host name, and add the dev override in appsettings.Development.json or local debugging breaks. The duplicate UseHttpsRedirection removal is cosmetic (verifier note 1) — keep the SECOND occurrence (line 159) which sits in correct order with UseForwardedHeaders so X-Forwarded-Proto from the proxy is respected. Keep the hardcoded sa password and JWT key untouched here; they are a separate secrets-management finding (verifier note 4)."
estimatedTime: 2 hours (most of it coordinating the DB server certificate / CA trust with infra)

approach: Make deploy.ps1 deploy atomically and reproducibly: publish to a versioned/timestamped staging folder, set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production explicitly, swap the live folder by repointing a directory junction (keeping the previous build for rollback), add a post-deploy health check with auto-rollback, and fix the misspelled frontend target. Drop -ExecutionPolicy Bypass in deploy.bat. (Secret/appsettings leakage is the separate secrets-in-source finding; keep the /XF exclusion so a local publish never overwrites prod config.)
steps: Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/deploy.ps1. Add params for base deploy roots and a health-check URL, and compute a unique release tag. After the existing param(...) block (lines 10-13) add `[string]$BackendBase = 'P:\WEBSITES\tradenet-admin-backend'`, `[string]$FrontendBase = 'P:\WEBSITES\tradenet-admin-frontend'`, and `[string]$HealthUrl = 'http://localhost/health'`. Right after `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` (line 15) add `$ReleaseTag = Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss'` and `$Sha = (git rev-parse --short HEAD)`.

Set the environment explicitly before publishing. After `Set-Location $backendDir` (line 27) add `$env:ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT = 'Production'` so the publish step and any local tooling are unambiguous about the target environment (currently it is implicit).

Replace the single in-place backend robocopy (line 38) with an atomic-swap to a release folder. Keep publishing to `$publishOutput` (line 33). Then robocopy `$publishOutput` into `Join-Path $BackendBase "releases\$ReleaseTag-$Sha"` (NOT directly onto the live `current` folder), keeping the existing `/XF appsettings.json appsettings.*.json` exclusion. Check robocopy success with `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8) { throw }` (robocopy exit codes <8 mean success). Then repoint a `current` directory junction to the new release and retain the prior target as `previous` for rollback.

Copy the unversioned prod config forward into the new release before swap so the live site keeps its appsettings/web.config. After the robocopy into the release folder, loop over `appsettings.json` and `appsettings.Production.json`, and for each `Copy-Item (Join-Path "$BackendBase\current" $f) (Join-Path $releaseDir $f) -Force` guarded by Test-Path so the first-ever deploy still works.

Apply the same atomic-swap pattern to the frontend block (lines 46-50), and FIX THE TYPO: change `'P:\WEBSITES\tradenenet-admin-frontend'` to use `$FrontendBase` ('...\tradenet-admin-frontend'). Keep the `/XF web.config` exclusion and copy the existing web.config forward.

Add a post-swap health check with auto-rollback. After the backend `current` junction is repointed, call `Invoke-WebRequest -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 30 -Uri $HealthUrl`; on non-200 or exception, repoint `current` back to `previous` and `throw`. This requires a /health endpoint: Backend/Program.cs has none today (it only has the `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()` check at line 100 and uses minimal-hosting `app`), so add `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks();` near the other service registrations and `app.MapHealthChecks("/health");` after the app is built. If you cannot add it now, point $HealthUrl at an existing known-200 route and leave a TODO.

Edit deploy.bat line 4: remove `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass`. Sign deploy.ps1 (or set the target machine policy to RemoteSigned once) and launch with `powershell -NoProfile -File "%SCRIPT_DIR%deploy.ps1" %*`. Interim: use `-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned` (never Bypass).

Stop shipping prod secrets in the repo (compounding context owned by the secrets finding): `git ls-files` confirms Backend/appsettings.json is still tracked even though .gitignore:162 lists it. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json`, commit a sanitized `Backend/appsettings.Template.json`, keep prod config only on the target via the copy-forward in step 4, and rotate the leaked sa password and JWT key.

Document rollback: add a header comment in deploy.ps1 (and/or AGENTS.md) and optionally a small rollback.ps1 that repoints `current` to the `previous` release via `cmd /c rmdir current` then `New-Item -ItemType Junction -Path current -Target (Get-Item previous).Target`.
verification: Dry run on a non-prod share: `.\deploy.ps1 -BackendBase C:\temp\be -FrontendBase C:\temp\fe -NoGit` twice; confirm two folders under releases\, `current` junction points at the newest and `previous` at the first — check with `(Get-Item C:\temp\be\current).Target`.
Env isolation: temporarily add `Write-Host $env:ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT` and confirm it prints Production during the run.

Health/rollback: with AddHealthChecks/MapHealthChecks added, `curl -i http://localhost/health` returns 200; then point $HealthUrl at a down URL and confirm the script logs 'rolling back', repoints `current` to `previous`, and exits non-zero.
Typo fix: confirm frontend lands in ...\tradenet-admin-frontend (single 'net'), not tradenenet-...
Config preservation: place an appsettings.json in `current`, deploy a new release, confirm it is present in the new release and was NOT overwritten by publish (the /XF + copy-forward).
grep deploy.bat and confirm `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass` is gone.
pitfalls: Directory junctions require the path NOT already exist; always `cmd /c rmdir` the junction (removes the link, not the target) before recreating — never `Remove-Item -Recurse` on `current` or you delete the real release. The first-ever deploy has no current/previous, so every swap/rollback step must be Test-Path-guarded (the patch does this). robocopy returns 0-7 on success and >=8 on failure, and $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' does NOT catch native exit codes — rely on the explicit `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8)` check. Health-check/rollback only works once Program.cs actually exposes /health (it currently does not) — ship the AddHealthChecks/MapHealthChecks change in the same PR or the check will false-fail and trigger needless rollbacks. Carrying appsettings.json forward only protects prod config if the secrets fix (step 8) keeps config on the target; until `git rm --cached` is done and the sa/JWT secrets rotated, the committed copy still leaks. Removing -ExecutionPolicy Bypass breaks the .bat on any box whose policy is Restricted until the script is signed or the machine is set to RemoteSigned once.
estimatedTime: half a day (scripted atomic swap + health endpoint); 2-3 days if migrating to a true CI/CD pipeline

approach: Stop sourcing xlsx from the npm registry (which can only ever serve the vulnerable 0.18.5) and re-pin it to the patched SheetJS CDN tarball 0.20.3 in package.json, then regenerate the lockfile. The single consumer (BasicTable.tsx) needs no code change because the patched build keeps the same `XLSX.utils.table_to_book` / `XLSX.writeFile` API.
steps: In Frontend/package.json, change the dependency on line 44 from "xlsx": "^0.18.5" to the patched CDN tarball URL: "xlsx": "https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz". The semver-range form must be removed entirely — a URL dependency cannot carry a `^` prefix.

Delete the existing resolved npm entry so the new source actually takes effect: remove the node_modules/xlsx block at Frontend/package-lock.json:13695-13718 by regenerating the lockfile rather than hand-editing. From the Frontend directory run: rm -rf node_modules/xlsx && npm install (npm will fetch xlsx from the cdn.sheetjs.com URL and rewrite the package-lock.json `node_modules/xlsx` entry with `resolved` pointing at the CDN tarball and a new `integrity` hash).

Leave Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx unchanged: line 19 `import * as XLSX from 'xlsx';` and lines 242-243 (`XLSX.utils.table_to_book(table, { sheet: 'Report' })` then `XLSX.writeFile(workbook, excelFileName)`) are API-compatible between 0.18.5 and 0.20.3, so no source edit is required. This is the sole consumer — confirmed via `grep -rn XLSX Frontend/src` returns only BasicTable.tsx.

Verify the lockfile now records the CDN source: open Frontend/package-lock.json and confirm the `node_modules/xlsx` entry shows `"version": "0.20.3"` and `"resolved": "https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz"` (it must NOT say registry.npmjs.org anymore).

Commit BOTH package.json and package-lock.json together so CI installs from the pinned CDN tarball and the integrity hash is enforced. If the project's CI runs `npm ci` behind a registry mirror/proxy that blocks cdn.sheetjs.com, allowlist that host (or, see pitfalls, fall back to the exceljs swap which stays on npm).

Optional hardening / alternative path (pick one — not both): if outbound access to cdn.sheetjs.com from CI or developer machines is not acceptable, instead remove xlsx entirely (`npm uninstall xlsx`, drop line 19 import), add a maintained npm package (`npm install exceljs`), and rewrite exportClientTableToExcel() at BasicTable.tsx:236-244 to read rows out of the DOM table by `tableId` and build the workbook with exceljs (`new ExcelJS.Workbook()`, `addWorksheet('Report')`, `addRow(...)`, then `workbook.xlsx.writeBuffer()` piped to a Blob + anchor download named by `excelFileName`). This avoids the SheetJS-not-on-npm problem altogether.
verification: From Frontend/, run `npm ls xlsx` — it must report xlsx@0.20.3 (not 0.18.5).
Run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --omit=dev`) in Frontend/ — the GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6 (CVE-2023-30533) and GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9 (CVE-2024-22363) advisories against xlsx should no longer appear.
grep the lockfile: `grep -A2 'node_modules/xlsx' Frontend/package-lock.json` shows `version 0.20.3` and a `cdn.sheetjs.com` resolved URL, with no remaining `registry.npmjs.org/xlsx`.

Functional smoke test: `npm run dev`, open a report rendered by BasicTable that has no `onExcel` handler (e.g. the route backed by Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx), click the Excel export button, and confirm a valid Report.xlsx (or the configured excelFileName) downloads and opens in Excel/LibreOffice with the table contents intact.
`npm run build` (tsc && vite build) completes with no type or bundling errors for the xlsx import.
pitfalls: Do NOT keep a `^` or any semver range on the CDN URL value — a tarball-URL dependency in package.json must be the bare URL, otherwise npm install fails.; Hand-editing package-lock.json is fragile: change package.json then let `npm install` rewrite the `node_modules/xlsx` block (and its integrity hash). A stale lockfile integrity hash will cause `npm ci` to fail.; CI / locked-down government build agents often install only from an internal npm mirror and may block cdn.sheetjs.com. If that host is unreachable, the install breaks — either allowlist cdn.sheetjs.com or take the exceljs swap (stays on npm) described in the optional step. Mirror the CDN tarball into the internal artifact repo if neither is allowed.; Severity is Low and exploitability here is effectively nil: the two CVEs live in the READ/parse path (XLSX.read / SSF) and this codebase has zero parse calls — it only does table_to_book + writeFile. So this is a hygiene fix, not an urgent one; do not let it block a release if the CDN pin is awkward.; Re-escalate immediately: if anyone later adds an Excel/CSV IMPORT feature that calls XLSX.read(...) on user-supplied files, this jumps back to High and the 0.20.3+ pin (or exceljs) becomes mandatory before that feature ships.; Rollback: revert the two changed lines/blocks in package.json + package-lock.json and run `npm install` to restore 0.18.5.
estimatedTime: 30 min (CDN pin); ~2 hours if switching to exceljs

approach: Clear the transitive form-data <4.0.4 advisory (GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4) by bumping the direct axios dependency in Frontend/package.json to >=1.16.0 (which pulls form-data >=4.0.4). This is npm-audit hygiene only: the browser SPA uses the native FormData object, not the npm package, so the vulnerable path is not exercised at runtime (per verifier).
steps: Work in /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend (the lockfile, package.json, and node_modules all live here).

Primary fix: edit Frontend/package.json line 26 and change the axios dependency from "axios": "^1.9.0" to "axios": "^1.16.0". axios 1.16.0+ declares form-data "^4.0.4", so the transitive resolution moves off the vulnerable 4.0.x range.

Regenerate the lockfile: run `npm install` from the Frontend directory. This rewrites Frontend/package-lock.json so node_modules/axios resolves to >=1.16.0 and node_modules/form-data (line ~8872) resolves to >=4.0.4.

If the axios major-minor bump is judged too risky to ship right now, use the interim fallback instead: add a top-level `overrides` block to Frontend/package.json (a sibling of the existing dependencies/devDependencies keys, e.g. after line 81) forcing form-data, leaving axios at ^1.9.0. There is currently NO overrides key in this package.json, so it must be added.
After whichever fix, run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --omit=dev`) from Frontend and confirm GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4 / the form-data Critical no longer appears.
Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together so CI installs the patched tree.
verification: From Frontend/: run `npm ls form-data` — the resolved version under axios must be >=4.0.4 (previously antd-multi-dashboard -> axios@1.9.0 -> form-data@4.0.2).
From Frontend/: run `npm audit` and confirm the form-data Critical (GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4) is gone (0 critical from this advisory).
Grep the regenerated Frontend/package-lock.json: the node_modules/form-data entry (~line 8872) shows version 4.0.4 or higher; if axios was bumped, node_modules/axios (~line 6563) shows >=1.16.0.
Build still works: from Frontend/ run `npm run build` (tsc && vite build) to confirm the axios bump introduced no type/runtime breaks.
Smoke-test the app's HTTP layer (any axios call, e.g. a report fetch via the configured axios instance) to confirm requests still succeed after the axios upgrade.
pitfalls: "Always edit package.json AND re-run npm install so package-lock.json is regenerated; editing only the lockfile (or only package.json) leaves the tree inconsistent and CI's `npm ci` will fail or restore the vulnerable version. The `overrides` fallback also requires `npm install` to take effect (npm 10.9.8 here supports overrides). The two approaches are mutually exclusive in spirit — prefer the axios bump; if you keep the override long-term, remember to drop it once axios is upgraded so it doesn't silently pin form-data. Rollback is a clean `git checkout -- package.json package-lock.json`. Low priority: the verifier confirmed the SPA uses native browser FormData and has no multipart-upload code in src, so this is audit-cleanliness, not an exploitable runtime path — sequence it after higher-severity findings."

approach: firebase is entirely unused in this app — the only file touching it is Frontend/src/firebase/firebaseConfig.ts, whose `analytics`/`messaging` exports are imported nowhere. Remove firebase to eliminate the whole @firebase/firestore -> @grpc/proto-loader -> protobufjs@7.5.3 subtree. If product wants to keep firebase (future messaging), instead pin protobufjs via an npm `overrides` entry to a patched 7.x.
steps: Confirm firebase is dead code: from Frontend/, run `grep -rn "firebase" src` — the only hits are the three imports inside src/firebase/firebaseConfig.ts; `analytics` and `messaging` are exported but never imported anywhere else (verified). No VITE_APP_API_KEY-style env keys are consumed elsewhere either.
PRIMARY FIX (recommended) — remove the unused dependency. In Frontend/package.json delete line 29: `"firebase": "^11.9.1",` from the dependencies block.
Delete the dead module Frontend/src/firebase/firebaseConfig.ts (and the now-empty Frontend/src/firebase/ directory). Since nothing imports it, no other source edits are needed.
From Frontend/, regenerate the lockfile cleanly: `rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json && npm install`. This drops @firebase/firestore, @grpc/proto-loader and protobufjs from the tree entirely.

Verify the vulnerable package is gone: `npm ls protobufjs` should print `(empty)` / not found, and `npm audit` should no longer report the protobufjs Critical advisories (GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg, GHSA-66ff-xgx4-vchm, GHSA-fx83-v9x8-x52w).

FALLBACK (only if firebase must stay) — instead of steps 2-4, add an npm `overrides` block to Frontend/package.json pinning protobufjs to the newest patched 7.x (7.6.2, which still satisfies @grpc/proto-loader's `^7.2.5` range), then run `npm install` to rewrite package-lock.json.
After either path, run `npm run build` (tsc + vite build) from Frontend/ to confirm the app still compiles and bundles.
verification: From Frontend/: `npm ls protobufjs` returns empty / 'not found' after the primary fix (or shows 7.6.2 deduped under @grpc/proto-loader if the fallback overrides path was used).
`npm audit` no longer lists the protobufjs Critical advisories.
`npm run build` succeeds (no TypeScript errors from the deleted firebaseConfig.ts, no missing-module errors — confirms nothing imported it).
`git grep -n firebase -- Frontend/src` returns no results after deleting firebaseConfig.ts.
pitfalls: Severity in the audit is Low / not-reachable: @firebase/firestore (the only path to protobufjs) is never imported by this app, so this is hardening, not an active exploit fix — don't treat it as an emergency hotfix. Regenerate package-lock.json with a full `rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json && npm install` rather than a partial install so the lockfile is internally consistent; commit the updated package-lock.json alongside package.json. If you choose the fallback `overrides` path, do NOT pin protobufjs to 8.x — @grpc/proto-loader@0.7.15 declares `^7.2.5`, so an 8.x pin can break grpc codegen at runtime; stay on 7.6.2. Before removing firebase, double-check there's no out-of-tree consumer (e.g. a service worker like firebase-messaging-sw.js in Frontend/public) that loads the firebase compat SDK separately — none was found in src, but the public/ dir is worth a glance if push notifications are planned. Rollback is trivial: re-add the package.json line and run npm install.

approach: Bump lodash to the patched line (>=4.18.0 / >4.17.23, per CVE-2026-4800) and refresh the lockfile so npm audit goes clean. The caret range in package.json already permits the patched version, so the real fix is regenerating package-lock.json; add an `overrides` pin as belt-and-suspenders. Severity is genuinely Low here: only `_.sumBy/_.chain/_.sortBy/_.toLowerCase` are used (no `_.template/_.unset/_.omit`), so there is no reachable exploit path — this is hygiene, not an active vuln.
steps: In Frontend/package.json line 31, change the lodash pin from "^4.17.21" to "^4.18.0" (the patched line per CVE-2026-4800). Leave "@types/lodash": "^4.17.17" on line 59 alone — the types package versioning is independent and unaffected.

Add an `overrides` block to Frontend/package.json (top-level, e.g. right after the "dependencies" object) to force any transitive copies onto the patched version: "overrides": { "lodash": "^4.18.0" }. This guarantees the single node_modules/lodash entry at package-lock.json:10086 resolves above 4.17.23.

From the Frontend/ directory, run `npm install` to resolve and rewrite package-lock.json. Confirm the node_modules/lodash block (currently lines 10086-10091, version 4.17.21) now shows version >=4.18.0 with a new integrity hash.

Run `npm audit` (from Frontend/) and confirm the lodash GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh / GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg (prototype pollution) and GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc (_.template) advisories no longer report against lodash.

Optional hardening (recommended by the verifier since the lodash files are template boilerplate): the only methods used are `_.sumBy`, `_.chain`, `_.sortBy`, and `_.toLowerCase` across 6 files (EarningsCard.tsx, CampaignsAdsCard.tsx, StudyStatisticsCard.tsx, DeliveryAnalyticsCard.tsx, Faqs.tsx, Help.tsx). These are trivially replaceable with native JS (Array.prototype.sort / reduce / String.prototype.toLowerCase), after which lodash and @types/lodash can be removed from package.json entirely, eliminating the advisory surface for good. Note `_.toLowerCase()` in CampaignsAdsCard.tsx:42 is called with no argument and is almost certainly a pre-existing bug — verify intended behavior if you refactor that file.
Commit both Frontend/package.json and the regenerated Frontend/package-lock.json together so CI installs the patched tree.
verification: From Frontend/: `npm install` then `npm ls lodash` shows a single resolved version >=4.18.0.
`npm audit` from Frontend/ reports zero lodash advisories (no GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg, or GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc).
grep node_modules/lodash in package-lock.json shows version >=4.18.0 with a fresh integrity hash, not 4.17.21.
`npm run build` (tsc && vite build) succeeds — confirms the upgraded lodash API surface (sumBy/chain/sortBy/toLowerCase) is unchanged and nothing broke.
pitfalls: Do not regenerate package-lock.json by hand; run npm install so the integrity hash matches the published tarball or CI/installs will fail.; Verify a patched lodash >4.17.23 (4.18.0) is actually published on the registry when you run this; if not yet released, the `overrides` pin to ^4.18.0 will fail to resolve — in that case keep 4.17.21, add a documented npm audit exception noting no reachable exploit path (verified: no _.template/_.unset/_.omit usage), and revisit when the patch ships.; If you take the optional removal path, the `_.toLowerCase()` call at CampaignsAdsCard.tsx:42 has no argument and looks broken — don't blindly translate it; confirm intended behavior first.; Commit package.json and package-lock.json in the same change; committing only one leaves the lockfile and manifest out of sync.
estimatedTime: 15 min (lockfile bump); ~1 hour if also removing lodash entirely

approach: Remove the dead `moment` direct dependency from the Frontend project and standardize date handling on `dayjs` (already required transitively by AntD 5 / rc-picker and imported in 10 source files). This is pure dependency hygiene — no source code change is needed because nothing in `src` imports `moment`.
steps: Confirm there are truly zero `moment` usages before removing: from the Frontend dir run `grep -rn "moment" src/` — it must return nothing (verified empty during audit). Also confirm no config reference: `grep -rn "moment" vite.config.* tsconfig*.json .storybook/` (also empty).

Uninstall the package so both package.json and package-lock.json are updated atomically. From `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend` run: `npm uninstall moment`. This removes the `"moment": "^2.30.1"` entry at Frontend/package.json:32 and the root-level `"moment": "^2.30.1"` entry at Frontend/package-lock.json:24.

Verify package.json no longer lists moment: `grep -n moment Frontend/package.json` should return nothing. The remaining lockfile hits (package-lock.json lines ~5869, ~11476, ~11490) are optional/peer deps of unrelated libraries and must be left untouched — do NOT hand-edit those.
Do NOT add `@types/moment` or any moment shim, and do NOT touch the existing `dayjs` entry (package.json:28). The 10 files importing dayjs continue to work unchanged.
Run the production build to confirm nothing broke: `npm run build` (runs `tsc && vite build`).
Run the Storybook build since this app ships Storybook: `npm run build-storybook`.
Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together (single dependency-removal commit).
verification: `grep -rn "moment" Frontend/package.json` returns nothing (entry removed).
`grep -n '"moment"' Frontend/package-lock.json` no longer shows the root entry at line ~24 (only the unrelated nested optional/peer-dep hits remain).
`npm run build` succeeds (tsc type-check + vite build) with no missing-module errors — proves no source file depended on moment.
`npm run build-storybook` succeeds.
`npm run lint` still passes (eslint with --max-warnings 0).
pitfalls: Use `npm uninstall moment` rather than hand-editing JSON so package-lock.json stays consistent; if you edit package.json by hand you must also run `npm install` to regenerate the lockfile, otherwise CI installs will drift. Do not delete the nested `moment` references deeper in package-lock.json (they belong to other packages' optional/peer deps and removing them will corrupt the lockfile). Rollback is trivial: `npm install moment@^2.30.1`. Note the audit verifier's correction — the production bundle impact is negligible (unused, so Rollup/Vite never bundled it); the real wins are smaller node_modules, fewer audit/vulnerability surfaces, and a single canonical date library (dayjs). Do not overstate bundle-size savings in the PR description.

approach: Both `firebase` and `mqtt` are confirmed non-production: `firebaseConfig.ts` is fully unreferenced dead code, and `mqtt` is used only by ChatBox under the `/Test` scratch route pointing at a public broker. Delete the dead/scratch code and drop both deps from `Frontend/package.json`, which removes the protobufjs Critical subtree and the prod-side `ws` path in one move.
steps: Confirm the dead/scratch status one more time (already verified): `cd Frontend && grep -rn "firebaseConfig\|firebase/app\|firebase/messaging\|firebase/analytics" src --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx" | grep -v firebaseConfig.ts` returns nothing, and `grep -rn mqtt src` only hits ChatBox.tsx. If a teammate later says Firebase push or chat IS a shipped feature, STOP and instead do the lazy-import variant in pitfalls; otherwise proceed to remove.

Delete the Firebase dead code: remove the file `Frontend/src/firebase/firebaseConfig.ts` and the now-empty `Frontend/src/firebase/` directory (`git rm Frontend/src/firebase/firebaseConfig.ts`). Its exports `analytics`/`messaging` are imported nowhere, and no `.env`/code consumes the `VITE_APP_API_KEY`/`VITE_APP_AUTH_DOMAIN`/`VITE_APP_PROJECT_ID`/`VITE_APP_STORAGE_BUCKET`/`VITE_APP_MESSENGER_SENDER_ID`/`VITE_APP_APP_ID`/`VITE_APP_MEASUREMENT_ID` keys outside this file.

Remove the MQTT/chat scratch feature. The dependency chain is `mqtt` -> `Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx` -> `Frontend/src/components/Chat/Chat.tsx` -> `Frontend/src/pages/Test.tsx` -> the `/Test` route in `Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx`. Delete the whole `Frontend/src/components/Chat/` directory (`Chat.tsx`, `ChatBox.tsx`, `ChatList.tsx`, `ChatListItem.ts`, `ChatListMobile.tsx`, `ChatList.css`) since it is the only `mqtt` consumer and is purely scratch UI.

Remove the `/Test` route block in `Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx` (the object with `path: '/Test'` at lines ~215-233) and delete its import `import Test from '../pages/Test.tsx';` (line 23). Also delete the now-orphaned scratch page `Frontend/src/pages/Test.tsx`. Verify nothing else navigates to `/Test` after removing ChatList/ChatListMobile (their `navigate('/Test/Edit/...')` calls go away with the Chat dir).

In `Frontend/package.json`, delete line 29 `"firebase": "^11.9.1",` and line 33 `"mqtt": "^5.13.1",` from `dependencies`. Leave `devDependencies` untouched (storybook keeps its own `ws`, which is acceptable as a dev-only dep).

Reinstall to regenerate the lockfile and prune the transitive trees: `cd Frontend && npm install`. This drops `firebase`, `mqtt`, the Critical `protobufjs` subtree, and the prod-side `ws`/`follow-redirects` brought in by mqtt. Commit the updated `Frontend/package.json` and `Frontend/package-lock.json` together.
verification: `cd Frontend && grep -rn "firebase\|mqtt" src` returns no matches (no remaining importers).
`cd Frontend && npm run build` (tsc + vite build) succeeds with no missing-module or unresolved-import errors for firebase/mqtt/Test/Chat.

`cd Frontend && grep -E '\"firebase\"|\"mqtt\"' package.json` returns nothing; `grep -c 'node_modules/protobufjs' package-lock.json` and `grep -c 'node_modules/firebase' package-lock.json` both return 0 after `npm install`.
`cd Frontend && npm audit` shows the protobufjs Critical (and the mqtt-side ws/follow-redirects) findings gone; remaining `ws` is dev-only via storybook.
App still loads and all real report routes work; navigating to `/Test` now hits the ErrorPage/404 (intended — it was a scratch route).
pitfalls: Ordering: delete the source consumers (firebaseConfig.ts, Chat/, Test.tsx, the /Test route + import) BEFORE running `npm install`, otherwise `npm run build` will fail on unresolved `firebase`/`mqtt` imports. ChatList.tsx and ChatListMobile.tsx call `navigate('/Test/Edit/...')` — they live inside the deleted Chat/ dir, so no dangling links remain; just double-check no other file links to `/Test`. Rollback is trivial: `git revert` restores both files and the package.json/lock entries. If a stakeholder confirms Firebase Cloud Messaging push or the MQTT chat is actually a planned production feature, do NOT delete — instead keep the deps but (a) convert the consumer to a dynamic `import()` (e.g. `const mqtt = await import('mqtt')` / `await import('../firebase/firebaseConfig')`) so they code-split out of the main bundle, (b) move the public `wss://broker.emqx.io` broker to a configured private broker, and (c) bump `firebase`/`mqtt` to the latest patched releases before shipping.

approach: Align the entire Storybook dev toolchain on the 9.x major. Because Storybook 9 folded addon-essentials, addon-interactions, blocks, manager-api, and theming into the core `storybook` package, the fix is not a simple version bump: remove those standalone packages, update `.storybook/main.ts` addons and the config-file imports, then run the official upgrade/dedupe so the lockfile resolves a single tree.
steps: Run the official migration from Frontend/ to let Storybook rewrite config and deps: `cd "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" && npx storybook@latest upgrade`. This handles most of the steps below automatically; verify the results against the manual steps and finish anything it skips.

In Frontend/package.json devDependencies, remove the four packages that no longer exist as standalone modules in Storybook 9 (folded into core): delete `@storybook/addon-essentials` (line 49), `@storybook/addon-interactions` (line 50), `@storybook/blocks` (line 53). Also remove `@storybook/manager-api` (line 54) and `@storybook/theming` (line 58) — in v9 these are re-exported from the `storybook` package.

Bump the remaining mixed-major entries to 9.x and drop the exact pin on addon-links: change `@storybook/addon-links` from `"9.0.9"` to `"^9.0.9"` (line 51). Confirm `@storybook/addon-onboarding`, `@storybook/react`, `@storybook/react-vite`, `eslint-plugin-storybook`, and `storybook` are all `^9.0.9` (they already are).

Also remove the stale, deprecated test helper `@storybook/testing-library` (line 57, pinned 0.2.2) — it is superseded by `storybook/test` in v9 and is unused in the codebase (no source imports it). If you prefer to keep test utilities, replace its usages with `storybook/test` instead; a repo grep shows zero `@storybook/testing-library` imports, so removal is safe.

Edit Frontend/.storybook/main.ts: drop `'@storybook/addon-essentials'` and `'@storybook/addon-interactions'` from the `addons` array (these are built into v9 core). Keep `'@storybook/addon-links'`, `'@storybook/addon-onboarding'`, and `'storybook-addon-react-router-v6'`. If you still want docs/controls behavior, no addon entry is needed in v9.
Edit Frontend/.storybook/manager.ts: change `import { addons } from '@storybook/manager-api';` to `import { addons } from 'storybook/manager-api';`.
Edit Frontend/.storybook/theme.ts: change `import { create } from '@storybook/theming/create';` to `import { create } from 'storybook/theming/create';`.
Edit Frontend/.storybook/preview.tsx: change `import { themes } from '@storybook/theming';` to `import { themes } from 'storybook/theming';`.

Edit Frontend/src/stories/Configure.mdx (auto-generated docs page): replace the `@storybook/blocks` import with `@storybook/addon-docs/blocks` (the v9 location for Meta/doc blocks). If that file is not used as a docs entry, you may delete it instead.

Delete the existing lockfile and reinstall to collapse the duplicated 8.6.x/9.0.9 trees: `rm Frontend/package-lock.json && cd "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" && npm install`. Then confirm no 8.6.x @storybook packages remain in the new lockfile.
verification: From Frontend/: `grep -E '8\.6\.' package-lock.json | grep storybook` returns nothing (no 8.6.x Storybook packages remain).
`npm ls storybook @storybook/react @storybook/react-vite @storybook/addon-links @storybook/addon-onboarding` shows every entry at 9.x with no 'invalid'/'deduped to 8.x' warnings.
`npm run storybook` boots the dev server on :6006 and renders existing stories (e.g. Card, Logo) without 'Cannot find module @storybook/manager-api' or addon-resolution errors.
`npm run build-storybook` completes successfully.
`npm audit --omit=dev` (prod-only) is unaffected; `npm audit` no longer lists the `storybook` (High) or `@storybook/addon-essentials`/`addon-actions` (Moderate) advisories tied to the 8.6.x tree.
`npm run lint` passes (eslint-plugin-storybook 9.x rules load cleanly).
pitfalls: Do not just bump @storybook/addon-essentials, addon-interactions, and blocks to ^9.0.9 — those packages do not exist on the 9.x line (they were merged into core `storybook`), so npm install would fail. They must be removed, not version-bumped. Update .storybook/main.ts (addons array) and the manager-api/theming/blocks imports in the SAME change as the package.json edit; otherwise the dev server throws module-not-found at startup. Regenerate package-lock.json (delete + npm install) rather than hand-editing it, so the duplicated 8.6.x transitive deps (addon-actions, addon-docs, test, instrumenter, csf-plugin) get pruned. This is dev-only tooling (devDependencies) with zero production runtime exposure, so there is no app/data-migration risk; rollback is `git checkout` of package.json, package-lock.json, and the .storybook/*.ts(x) + Configure.mdx files.

approach: Add NuGet vulnerability scanning at three layers (Directory.Build.props audit props, a CI gate, and a Dependabot nuget entry); no dynamic-LINQ code change is required because the sort/filter columns are already allowlisted.
steps: Create a new file `Directory.Build.props` at the REPO ROOT (next to `tradenet-admin-report.sln`, the dir that contains the Backend/ and Backend.Tests/ folders). MSBuild auto-imports it into every project under that folder, so both `Backend/API.csproj` and `Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj` inherit it without per-project edits. Put a single PropertyGroup enabling restore-time auditing of ALL (direct + transitive) packages at warning level 'low' and treat NU1901-NU1904 vulnerability warnings as errors so a vulnerable restore fails the build (see patch).

Do NOT also add these props to `Backend/API.csproj` / `Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj` — keep them only in Directory.Build.props to avoid duplicate/conflicting values. The existing `<PackageReference>` lines in API.csproj (lines 11-31) stay untouched.

Create the CI workflow directory `.github/workflows/` at the REPO ROOT (this repo currently has NO root `.github`; only `Frontend/.github/workflows/*` exists and is node-only). Add `.github/workflows/backend-audit.yml` that: checks out the repo, runs `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` pinned to `dotnet-version: 8.0.x` (the projects target net8.0), runs `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, then runs `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` and fails the job if any vulnerability is reported. `dotnet list package` returns exit 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so grep the output and explicitly exit 1 on a hit (see patch).

Create `.github/dependabot.yml` at the REPO ROOT with two ecosystems: a `nuget` entry rooted at `/` (Dependabot discovers `tradenet-admin-report.sln` and scans both csproj files) and an `npm` entry rooted at `/Frontend` to cover the existing React app. Schedule weekly. (See patch.)

Audit the System.Linq.Dynamic.Core call sites in `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs` for the record — confirmed safe but worth a code comment: `ApplySort` (line 251) and `ApplyFilter` (line 209) both gate `sortColumn`/`filterColumn` through `IsValidProperty` (line 299), which resolves the name against `typeof(T)` reflection, so only real model properties reach the parser. The user value is passed as the parameterized `@0` placeholder in every `source.Where(...)` call (lines 226, 230, 237-240, 244-247), never string-concatenated. Add a one-line `// security: column allowlisted via IsValidProperty; value bound as @0 parameter` comment above the Where calls so future edits don't break the invariant. No behavioral code change.

Run `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln` locally once to confirm the new audit props don't surface an already-known vulnerable transitive package that would now break the build; if one appears, bump that package before merging so main stays green.
patch:
Directory.Build.props (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Supply-chain: audit direct + transitive NuGet packages on every restore -->
    <NuGetAudit>true</NuGetAudit>
    <NuGetAuditMode>all</NuGetAuditMode>
    <NuGetAuditLevel>low</NuGetAuditLevel>
    <!-- Fail the build (not just warn) when a vulnerable package is restored -->
    <WarningsAsErrors>$(WarningsAsErrors);NU1901;NU1902;NU1903;NU1904</WarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

.github/workflows/backend-audit.yml (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
name: Backend NuGet Audit
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'   # weekly Monday 06:00 UTC catches new advisories
jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: '8.0.x'
      - name: Restore
        run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      - name: List vulnerable packages
        run: |
          dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive 2>&1 | tee audit.txt
          if grep -qiE 'has the following vulnerable packages|Critical|High|Moderate|Low' audit.txt; then
            echo "::error::Vulnerable NuGet packages detected"
            exit 1
          fi

.github/dependabot.yml (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "npm"
    directory: "/Frontend"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
verification: Local audit: from the repo root run `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` — output should list each project and report no vulnerable packages (or surface real ones to fix).

Props effective: run `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln` and confirm it completes; to prove auditing is active, temporarily add a reference to a known-vulnerable package (e.g. an old `System.Net.Http`) and confirm restore now FAILS with an NU1903/NU1902 error rather than only warning — then revert.
CI: open a PR; the new `Backend NuGet Audit` check must appear and pass. Confirm it fails when a vulnerable package is present (the grep-based `exit 1`).
Dependabot: after merge to main, check the repo Insights > Dependency graph > Dependabot tab shows both `nuget` (root) and `npm` (/Frontend) ecosystems enabled and that it has scanned the manifests.
pitfalls: Ordering: create Directory.Build.props BEFORE wiring the CI gate, and run a local `dotnet restore` first — if any current transitive dependency already has an open advisory, `WarningsAsErrors` (NU1901-1904) will immediately break the build for everyone, so fix/upgrade that package in the same PR or main goes red. NuGetAuditMode=all + AuditLevel=low is intentionally strict; if it proves too noisy for a transitive package with no available fix, you can add a `<NuGetAuditSuppress Include="https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-..." />` ItemGroup rather than weakening the level globally. Note Directory.Build.props auto-applies to ALL projects under the repo root, including Backend.Tests — that is desired here. The `dotnet list package --vulnerable` grep can false-positive if a column header literally contains one of the severity words; the current .NET 8 output format does not, but pin a comment so a future SDK output change is reviewed. Rollback is clean: delete the three new files (no source code changes), since the IsValidProperty/parameterized-@0 audit in step 5 requires no behavioral edit.

approach: This is an Info-level caching-correctness cleanup, not a bug fix. Add an explicit `Invalidate()` hook to the singleton `CountryCache` so reference-data edits can force-refresh on demand, and reduce drift by having `ReportLookupCache.GetCountryNamesAsync` delegate to the same singleton instead of maintaining a second independent 1-day copy of the identical Countries query. Do NOT delete `GetCountryNamesAsync` (verifier confirmed it is actively used by several `_Fast` reports) and do NOT collapse the dropdown's `GetCountries` query, which has a genuinely different shape.
steps: In Backend/Service/Reports/CountryCache.cs, add `void Invalidate();` to the `ICountryCache` interface (after the `EnsureLoadedAsync` declaration around line 37) with a doc comment: 'Drops the cached snapshot so the next EnsureLoadedAsync reloads from the database. Call after Countries reference data is edited.'

Implement `Invalidate()` on the `CountryCache` class: set `Interlocked.Exchange(ref _loadedAtTicks, 0);`. This forces `IsFresh()` (line 82-88) to return false on the next call, triggering a reload. Do NOT null/replace `_countries` here so concurrent readers of the `Countries` property keep seeing the last good snapshot until the reload completes (lock-free swap is preserved by `LoadCoreAsync`).

Optionally also expose an `Invalidate()` overload that immediately empties the snapshot only if a hard reset is desired; the ticks-only reset above is the safer default for a read-heavy singleton, so prefer it.

Reduce the duplicate Countries query: keep the public static `ReportLookupCache.GetCountryNamesAsync(TradeNetDbContext, IMemoryCache)` signature so the four `_Fast` reports that call it (sp_ExportPermitDetailReport_Fast.cs, sp_ImportPermitDetailReport_Fast.cs, sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs, sp_ImportLicencePendingDetailReport_Fast.cs) are untouched, but make it delegate to the singleton. Add an `ICountryCache` parameter only where you can thread it; since these are static report methods that already receive `IMemoryCache cache`, the lower-risk move is to leave them on the existing `IMemoryCache` path for now and ONLY add the invalidation hook — flag the full delegation as a follow-up unless you are also wiring `ICountryCache` into those report method signatures.

If you do pursue full consolidation in this pass: change `GetCountryNamesAsync` so the cache entry it builds shares the same projection already used by `CountryCache.LoadCoreAsync` (Countries.AsNoTracking().OrderBy(Id).Select(Id, Name)). The two are already byte-identical today, so extract that projection into a single private helper (e.g. `static IQueryable<ReportLookupEntry> CountriesProjection(TradeNetDbContext db)`) referenced by both `CountryCache.LoadCoreAsync` and `ReportLookupCache.GetCountryNamesAsync` to prevent future drift.

Leave ReportLookupsController.GetCountries (lines 154-160) as-is: its query filters `IsActive && !IsDeleted`, orders by `Name`, and projects `Code` into `ReportLookupOption` — a different shape and contract from the report-body cache, so it must not be merged into the report Countries cache.

Wire the new hook for the future: there is currently NO Countries write endpoint in this repo (reference data is edited in the legacy Tradenet 2.0 admin / DB directly), so add a short XML-doc note on `Invalidate()` stating it must be called from whatever admin save path edits the Countries table, and (optionally) expose an internal admin endpoint or a cache-bust query param later. Do not invent a fake call site.
Update the class summary comment in CountryCache.cs (lines 12-23) to mention that staleness can now be cut short via `Invalidate()` rather than only the 1-hour TTL.
verification: dotnet build the Backend project to confirm the new interface member and Invalidate() compile (the interface change forces CountryCache to implement it).

Unit/integration check: call `ICountryCache.EnsureLoadedAsync()`, read `Countries`, then call `Invalidate()`, then `EnsureLoadedAsync()` again and assert `LoadCoreAsync` re-ran (e.g. via a DB hit counter or by observing a freshly edited country name appears). Confirm that between Invalidate() and the reload, the `Countries` property still returns the previous non-empty snapshot.
Smoke-test an affected report (e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReport via its controller) before and after Invalidate() to confirm country names still resolve and no regression in output.
Confirm ReportLookupsController GET /api/ReportLookups/countries dropdown is byte-for-byte unchanged (still IsActive/IsDeleted-filtered, Name-ordered, Code populated).
pitfalls: Severity is Info — this is cleanup, not a fix for a live defect; do not over-engineer. Do NOT delete ReportLookupCache.GetCountryNamesAsync: the verifier confirmed it is used by sp_ExportPermitDetailReport_Fast / sp_ImportPermitDetailReport_Fast / sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast / sp_ImportLicencePendingDetailReport_Fast and removing it breaks those reports. Do NOT merge the dropdown's GetCountries query into the report cache — different filter (IsActive/IsDeleted), order (Name), and projection (Code). If you implement Invalidate() by replacing _countries with an empty array, concurrent _Fast report requests racing between Invalidate() and the reload could momentarily resolve country ids to blanks — that is why the patch only resets the ticks marker and leaves the snapshot intact. The two caches still have independent TTLs (1h for CountryCache, 1d for the static cache and the dropdown); Invalidate() only covers the singleton, so a fully consistent bust would also require clearing the IMemoryCache keys (ReportLookups:Countries:AllNames:v1 and ReportLookups:countries) — note this if a single source of truth is required.

approach: Remove the hardcoded demo credentials from the SignIn form's `initialValues` so the login form ships empty on production, and stop logging submitted credential values to the browser console. Pair the code change with an operational check that no real `demo@email.com` weak-credential account exists in any deployed database.
steps: Open Frontend/src/pages/authentication/SignIn.tsx and locate the antd <Form> at lines 116-130, specifically the initialValues prop at lines 121-125.

Replace the initialValues object that hardcodes email: 'demo@email.com', password: 'demo123', remember: true. Either drop the email/password defaults entirely (keeping only remember: true if you want the checkbox checked by default), or remove the initialValues prop completely. Recommended: keep `initialValues={{ remember: true }}` so the email and password fields render blank but 'Remember me' stays checked.

In the same file, remove the credential-leaking console.log in onFinish at line 39 (`console.log('Success:', values);`) — `values` contains the plaintext email and password and should not be written to the browser console in a government portal. Also remove the console.log at line 64 in onFinishFailed for consistency (optional, but it is dead debug logging).

Operational verification (no in-repo code creates this account — confirmed by grep): have ops query each deployed/live database for a user with email 'demo@email.com' and, if present, either delete it or rotate it to a strong unique password. The repo Backend directory contains no seeding tied to these creds, so this is purely a runtime/database check, not a code change.
Rebuild the frontend (npm run build in Frontend/) and confirm the bundled output no longer contains the literal strings 'demo@email.com' or 'demo123'.
verification: Run `grep -rn "demo@email.com\|demo123" Frontend/src` from the repo root — it must return no matches (currently only SignIn.tsx:122-123 match).

Run the frontend dev server (npm run dev in Frontend/), open the /sign-in page, and confirm the Email and Password fields render empty (no prefilled demo@email.com / demo123) while 'Remember me' is still checked if you kept remember: true.
After `npm run build`, grep the dist/ output for 'demo@email.com' and 'demo123' to confirm the strings are not bundled into the production artifact.
Open the browser devtools console, submit the login form, and confirm no 'Success:' line printing the submitted values appears.

Ops check: query each deployed DB (e.g. SELECT * FROM <users table> WHERE Email = 'demo@email.com') and confirm either no row exists or the account has been removed / its password rotated to a strong value.
pitfalls: Do not delete the `remember` Form.Item or its valuePropName='checked' binding (lines 154-158) — only the initialValues defaults change. If you remove initialValues entirely instead of keeping `{ remember: true }`, the 'Remember me' checkbox will start unchecked, which is fine but is a minor behavior change. The database cleanup is an operational task outside this repo; the code fix alone removes the hint but a pre-existing weak demo account on a server would remain a foothold until ops verifies/removes it, so do not mark the finding fully closed until that check is done.

approach: This is Info/code-quality, not a live vulnerability: the cited hook `Frontend/src/hooks/useFileUpload.tsx` is dead code (zero importers, not in the hooks `index.ts` barrel), and its `document.write(image.outerHTML)` writes a DOM-encoded `Image` whose `src` carries a fixed `envConfig.imageUrl` scheme prefix. The correct, lowest-risk action is to delete the unused file outright, eliminating the `document.write` sink entirely.
steps: Confirm the hook is unreferenced before deleting. From Frontend/, run: grep -rn "useFileUpload'" src --include=*.ts --include=*.tsx | grep -v useFileUploadWithoutCrop  (expect no output) and grep -rn "hooks/useFileUpload\b" src  (expect no output). The only file with a similar name in use is Frontend/src/hooks/useFileUploadWithoutCrop.tsx (exports useFileUploadNoCrop), consumed by Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx — leave that one alone.

Delete the dead file: git rm "Frontend/src/hooks/useFileUpload.tsx". No edits to Frontend/src/hooks/index.ts are required because that barrel only re-exports usePageContext and useFetchData (it never exported useFileUpload).

Harden the surviving live preview path in Frontend/src/hooks/useFileUploadWithoutCrop.tsx (onPreview at lines 77-82). It already avoids document.write (uses window.open(src, '_blank')), but add rel-style hardening: open with window.open(src, '_blank', 'noopener,noreferrer') so the opened tab cannot reach back via window.opener.

Optional defense-in-depth in the same onPreview: before opening, validate the extension off fileList.name against a whitelist (e.g. const ok = /\.(png|jpe?g|webp|gif)$/i.test(fileList.name)) and bail out if it fails. Note beforeUpload at line 53 already forces a '.webp' extension on uploaded names, so live previews are normally .webp.

Run the TypeScript build to confirm nothing referenced the removed file: from Frontend/ run npx tsc --noEmit (or npm run build). It must compile with no 'Cannot find module' errors pointing at useFileUpload.
verification: From Frontend/: grep -rn "document.write" src returns no hits in the hooks directory after deletion.
From Frontend/: npx tsc --noEmit completes with no errors (proves no module imported the deleted useFileUpload.tsx).
From Frontend/: git ls-files src/hooks/useFileUpload.tsx returns empty (file removed from tracking).
Manual: in the Chat upload UI (ChatBox.tsx), click preview on an uploaded image — it still opens in a new tab via useFileUploadWithoutCrop.onPreview, confirming the live feature is unaffected.
pitfalls: Do NOT delete or rename Frontend/src/hooks/useFileUploadWithoutCrop.tsx — it exports useFileUploadNoCrop and is actively used by Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx. Only useFileUpload.tsx (the crop variant) is dead. The hooks index.ts barrel does not export useFileUpload, so no barrel edit is needed; do not add/remove unrelated exports there. Rollback is trivial (git restore the deleted file) since nothing depends on it. The optional extension whitelist must allow .webp, since beforeUpload rewrites upload names to .webp.

approach: This is an Info-level, not-currently-exploitable finding: xlsx is used only on the export path (XLSX.utils.table_to_book + XLSX.writeFile in BasicTable.tsx), never on a read/parse path, so CVE-2023-30533 / CVE-2024-22363 are unreachable. The fix is version hygiene plus a preventive guardrail: pin to the maintained SheetJS 0.20.x distribution (published on the SheetJS CDN, not the npm registry) and add an ESLint no-restricted-syntax rule banning XLSX.read/readFile/sheet_to_json so any future import feature fails lint until it parses with a patched version on validated input.
steps: Confirm scope first (1 command): from Frontend/, run `grep -rnE "XLSX\.(read|readFile)|sheet_to_json|readFileSync" src --include=*.ts --include=*.tsx`. It returns nothing today, which is what makes this Info/not-exploitable. The only usage is the export path in `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx`: line 19 `import * as XLSX from 'xlsx';`, line 242 `XLSX.utils.table_to_book(table, { sheet: 'Report' })`, line 243 `XLSX.writeFile(workbook, excelFileName)`. Do NOT change this code path; it is benign.

Move off the EOL npm `xlsx@0.18.5` to the maintained SheetJS 0.20.x distribution. Per the verifier note, there is NO drop-in npm `xlsx@0.20.x` — the npm `xlsx` package is frozen at 0.18.5. The maintained build is served from the SheetJS CDN, so this is an install-config change, not a plain version bump.

Edit `Frontend/package.json` line 44: replace the dependency `"xlsx": "^0.18.5"` with the CDN-hosted SheetJS tarball `"xlsx": "https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz"` (a direct tarball URL; this is the officially documented install method and avoids needing a custom registry). Keep the import specifier `'xlsx'` unchanged so `BasicTable.tsx` needs no code edit.

Regenerate the lockfile so `Frontend/package-lock.json` resolves to the CDN tarball instead of the npmjs.org 0.18.5 entry: run `npm install` from `Frontend/`. Verify the new lockfile `node_modules/xlsx` block points at `https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/...` and version `0.20.3` (it currently shows `version: 0.18.5`, resolved from registry.npmjs.org).

Add a preventive ESLint guardrail so any future import/parse usage is blocked at lint time. Edit `Frontend/.eslintrc.cjs` and add a `no-restricted-syntax` rule to the existing `rules` object (shown in the patch). Because usage is via the namespace `XLSX.*`, target the call expressions `XLSX.read`/`XLSX.readFile` and any `sheet_to_json` call — a plain `no-restricted-imports` would not catch namespace member calls.

Run `npm run lint` from `Frontend/` to confirm the new rule does not flag the existing export-only code (must stay green, since only write-path APIs are used), then `npm run build` to confirm the SheetJS swap compiles.

Since there are no CI workflows in the repo (`.github/workflows` does not exist), document the guardrail in team conventions instead of CI: add a one-line note to `AGENTS.md` / code-review checklist stating that adding any spreadsheet-import (`XLSX.read*` / `sheet_to_json` on uploaded files) requires a patched SheetJS version and explicit input validation. This satisfies the 'add a guard/code-review rule' part of the recommendation.
