verification: From Frontend/: `npm install`, then inspect package-lock.json — the `node_modules/xlsx` entry must show `version: 0.20.3` and `resolved: https://cdn.sheetjs.com/...` (no longer 0.18.5 from registry.npmjs.org).
`npm run lint` passes green: the new no-restricted-syntax rule does NOT flag BasicTable.tsx (it uses only table_to_book/writeFile), proving the export path is unaffected.
Negative test for the guardrail: temporarily add `const wb = XLSX.read(buf);` to a .tsx file and run `npm run lint` — it must fail with the CVE message; then remove the line.

`npm run build` succeeds (tsc + vite build) and the Excel export button in any report (e.g. MemberRegistrationReport) still downloads a valid .xlsx, confirming the SheetJS swap is functionally identical.
Repo-wide confirmation the CVE path stays absent: `grep -rnE "XLSX\.(read|readFile)|sheet_to_json" Frontend/src` returns no matches.
pitfalls: Severity is Info / not-exploitable — treat as hygiene, not an emergency; do not refactor the working export path in BasicTable.tsx. The npm registry has no 0.20.x for `xlsx` (frozen at 0.18.5), so a naive `npm install xlsx@latest` or a Dependabot bump will NOT work and may silently keep 0.18.5 — you must use the CDN tarball URL. Direct-URL/tarball deps can trip locked-down corporate registries or air-gapped CI; confirm the build environment can reach cdn.sheetjs.com (or vendor/mirror the tarball to the internal registry) before merging. The `integrity` hash in package-lock.json will change — commit the regenerated lockfile. Rollback is trivial: revert package.json line 44 to `\"xlsx\": \"^0.18.5\"`, restore the old lockfile entry, run `npm install`; the ESLint rule is independent and safe to keep regardless.

approach: Change the one-line scroll behavior in the existing ScrollToTop component from animated smooth scrolling to instant ('auto'), so each route change no longer fires a multi-frame scroll animation that competes with paint after a large report table renders. This is an Info-level cleanup contained to a single file.
steps: Open Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx.

In the ScrollToTop component (lines 28-40), inside the useEffect's window.scrollTo({...}) call, change the property `behavior: 'smooth'` to `behavior: 'auto'`. Leave `top: 0` and `left: 0` and the `[pathname]` dependency array unchanged.

Do not change the PageWrapper structure or where ScrollToTop is mounted — keeping it at the layout level (per /Report, /User, /Timeline group) is fine; the only issue was the animated behavior, and the useEffect correctly still resets scroll on each pathname change.

(Optional, only if a future cleanup is desired) react-router-dom v7.6.2 is installed, so the component could later be replaced with the built-in <ScrollRestoration /> placed in the layout, but this is NOT required to resolve this Info finding and would change behavior (it restores prior scroll positions on back/forward rather than always scrolling to top).
verification: Run the frontend (npm run dev in Frontend/) and navigate between report routes (e.g. /Report/... pages with large tables). Confirm the viewport jumps to the top instantly on each navigation with no visible animated scroll.
Grep to confirm the change landed and no smooth behavior remains: `grep -rn "behavior:" Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx` should show only `behavior: 'auto'`.
Run `npx tsc --noEmit` (or `npm run build`) in Frontend/ to confirm no type errors were introduced.
pitfalls: No data migration or ordering constraints. Rollback is a one-line revert back to 'smooth'. Note this changes the visible behavior from an animated scroll to an instant jump — if any stakeholder specifically wanted the animation, flag it, but the audit explicitly recommends 'auto'. Do not swap in react-router's <ScrollRestoration /> as a drop-in replacement without realizing it restores previous scroll positions on back/forward navigation rather than always returning to top.

approach: Replace the `||`-based Alert description in both dashboard cards with optional chaining and a nullish-coalescing fallback so neither the parent `error` prop nor the `useFetchData` error (which is `null` on success) is ever dereferenced unguarded. This is a trivial two-line change matching the audit recommendation.
steps: Open Frontend/src/components/dashboard/default/LatestOrdersCard/LatestOrdersCard.tsx. On line 57, change the Alert's `description` from `error?.toString() || ordersDataError.toString()` to `error?.toString() ?? ordersDataError?.toString() ?? 'Unknown error'`. The `?.` on `ordersDataError` prevents the null deref, and the trailing fallback guarantees a defined string.

Open Frontend/src/components/dashboard/default/RecentUsersCard/RecentUsersCard.tsx. On line 48, change the Alert's `description` from `error?.toString() || usersDataError.toString()` to `error?.toString() ?? usersDataError?.toString() ?? 'Unknown error'`.

Do NOT touch the guard expressions on line 54 (`ordersDataError || error`) and line 45 (`usersDataError || error`) — those `||` checks are intentional truthiness tests for whether to render the Alert at all; only the `description` operand is the bug.
Leave the shared hook Frontend/src/hooks/useFetchData.tsx unchanged — it correctly initializes `error` to `null`; the bug is solely the unguarded deref in the cards.
From Frontend/, run `npx tsc --noEmit` (or the project lint/typecheck script) to confirm the edited TSX still compiles.
verification: Run `npx tsc --noEmit` from Frontend/ — no new type errors.

Manual/repro: temporarily render <LatestOrdersCard error={''} /> (truthy guard via the `error` prop on line 54 path, but empty-string toString) — before the fix this throws `Cannot read properties of null (reading 'toString')` and blanks the page; after the fix the Alert renders with description 'Unknown error' instead of crashing.
In the running dashboard, the Latest Orders and Recent Users cards still show their tables on the mock-data happy path (no Alert), confirming the guard logic was not altered.
pitfalls: Use `??` (nullish coalescing), not `||`, for the description operands so a legitimately empty-but-present error string isn't swallowed — but more importantly so the chain reaches the literal fallback rather than re-evaluating. Keep the line 54/45 guard expressions as `||`; converting those to `??` would change when the Alert shows (e.g. an empty-string error prop would no longer trigger it). This finding is Info severity and the components are dead UI-template code wired to mock JSON, so if the team scopes out template components this can be deferred; the fix itself is safe and has no runtime/data dependencies, so rollback is just reverting the two one-line edits.

approach: Make redux-persist opt-in by adding an explicit `whitelist` to the existing `persistConfig` in store.ts, so only intended slices (currently just `theme`) are ever written to localStorage and future slices (auth/user/report data) don't get silently persisted.
steps: Open Frontend/src/redux/store.ts.

In the `persistConfig` object (currently lines 17-21), add the line `whitelist: ['theme'],` after `version: 1,`. The string `'theme'` must exactly match the key used in `combineReducers({ theme: themeReducer })` at line 13 and the field in the `RootState` interface.
Leave `PersistConfig<RootState>` typing as-is; it already type-checks the whitelist entries against `RootState` keys, so a typo'd slice name will fail compilation.

Going forward, when new slices are added to `rootReducer`, only add a slice name to `whitelist` if it is genuinely safe and useful to persist. Keep sensitive slices (auth tokens, user PII, fetched report rows) OUT of the whitelist. If a future slice needs persistence, prefer a nested persist config on that slice (with its own `storage`/transforms) rather than the root config.
verification: Run the frontend (`npm run dev` in Frontend/) and confirm the app boots and theme persistence still works: toggle the theme, reload the page, and verify the chosen theme is retained.
Open DevTools > Application > Local Storage and inspect the `persist:root` key. Its JSON value should contain only `theme` (plus redux-persist's internal `_persist` field) — no other slices.
Re-run `grep -rn "whitelist" Frontend/src/` and confirm `whitelist: ['theme']` now appears in store.ts.
Run `npm run build` (or `tsc`) in Frontend/ to confirm the typed `PersistConfig<RootState>` still compiles with the added whitelist.
pitfalls: This is an Info-severity, latent-pattern fix; there is no current data exposure because the store only holds `theme` today. No data migration is needed.; The whitelist value must match the reducer key (`'theme'`), not the slice's internal name or file name. If they ever diverge, TypeScript will flag it because of `PersistConfig<RootState>`.; Rollback is trivial: remove the `whitelist` line to restore the prior persist-everything behavior. No persisted-state version bump is required since the persisted shape (theme only) is unchanged.
